records.title: Custom Computer Programming Services

caveats: a: OEWS is for NAICS 541200, includes establishments outside CPA offices, excludes self-employed owners, and is May 2023 vintage.
a: The exposure paper measures technical time-saving potential on occupation tasks, not wage-weighted firm labor, deployment feasibility, or labor removal.
a: Small firms may already automate portions of bookkeeping and tax workflow, reducing the remaining opportunity relative to gross task exposure.
rho: The Thomson Reuters samples cover broader tax, audit, and accounting populations, multiple countries, and firms outside the EBITDA lens.
rho: Reported adoption does not establish sustained production use or monetizable labor avoidance.
rho: The five-year horizon includes substantial uncertainty in model reliability, regulation, professional standards, and software integration.
e: No source measures the eligible share of the frozen LMM firm population directly.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms near the EBITDA boundaries.
e: State ownership, independence, and firm-licensure rules vary, and attest work may require a split alternative-practice structure.
s5: The succession survey is from 2016, covers firms of various sizes, and reports discussions rather than closings.
s5: Published deal counts are incomplete, may include non-control deals, and lack a consistent eligible-firm denominator.
s5: High transaction activity among larger firms and PE platforms may not represent the frozen LMM population.
q: Survey respondents can offer several pricing models, so the percentages are not revenue shares and cannot be summed.
q: The pricing survey covers tax and accounting firms beyond NAICS 541211 and the LMM lens.
q: Observed fee growth includes inflation, demand, scope, and labor scarcity rather than isolated retention of AI benefits.
d5: BLS occupation employment is not six-digit industry service demand and covers a ten-year horizon.
d5: AICPA revenue growth is nominal and mixes price, scope, acquisitions, and quantity.
d5: IRS filing statistics cover individual returns only and professional preparers include non-CPA firms.
o: Legal responsibility varies by service: tax preparation, bookkeeping, advisory, review, and audit have different operator requirements.
o: The GAO statistic covers all paid individual-return preparers, not CPA firms or business clients.
o: Future standards may permit greater machine execution while retaining only a thin layer of licensed sign-off.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring annual tax and assurance engagements and repeat advisory projects for businesses, nonprofits, and individuals, with a mix of hourly or time-based, flat-fee, project, value-based, and retainer billing.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive accounting departments, non-CPA bookkeeping or stand-alone tax-preparation establishments classified outside NAICS 541211, shell firms, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the specified EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US CPA firms in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that provide recurring or repeat audit and assurance, tax compliance and advisory, financial-statement preparation, client accounting, and related accounting advice to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS OEWS measures the adjacent four-digit NAICS 541200 workforce rather than six-digit CPA offices: accountants and auditors are 31.73% of employment, tax preparers 7.13%, office and administrative support 28.76%, and management 10.54%. The Eloundou task study rates accountants and auditors at 100% exposure under its most expansive GPT-powered-software rubric, where exposure means at least a 50% task-time reduction, not job replacement. CPA.com identifies concrete exposure in bookkeeping, tax preparation, audit analysis, advisory modeling, and back-office workflows. I translate those occupation and task signals into a much lower wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated work because professional judgment, client interaction, final review, and already-automated work are not substitutable opportunity. The four-digit mix likely has more clerical and payroll work than CPA offices, which may overstate low-complexity exposure, while the technical exposure rubric likely overstates feasible substitution.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 541200, includes establishments outside CPA offices, excludes self-employed owners, and is May 2023 vintage.
The exposure paper measures technical time-saving potential on occupation tasks, not wage-weighted firm labor, deployment feasibility, or labor removal.
Small firms may already automate portions of bookkeeping and tax workflow, reducing the remaining opportunity relative to gross task exposure.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit NAICS 541211 establishment-level occupation-and-payroll extract combined with time studies from 20-30 LMM CPA firms that tag hours by workflow, current automation status, reviewer level, and AI substitutability would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix places most payroll dollars in professional, managerial, tax, and administrative knowledge work. Document intake, data extraction, classification, reconciliation, research, first-draft returns, sampling, summarization, correspondence, and workflow coordination are materially exposed. Partner judgment, attest conclusions, complex tax positions, client development, supervision, and accountability remain less substitutable, so the estimate is far below the headline technical-exposure measure.

d5.bridge: BLS projects accountants-and-auditors employment across all industries to grow 5% from 2024 to 2034, while saying complex tax and regulation support demand and automation should shift duties rather than reduce overall demand. AICPA's mostly small-firm MAP sample reported 6.7% nominal net-client-fee growth in 2024, and IRS data show professionally filed individual returns rose 1.7% between comparable 2024 and 2025 filing-season dates. I use these as directional indicators, not a direct constant-price, constant-quality quantity measure, and set five-year demand near modest growth after excluding price and quality expansion. Employment and nominal revenue can overstate service quantity when productivity or prices rise; tax-return counts omit assurance and advisory.
caveats: BLS occupation employment is not six-digit industry service demand and covers a ten-year horizon.
AICPA revenue growth is nominal and mixes price, scope, acquisitions, and quantity.
IRS filing statistics cover individual returns only and professional preparers include non-CPA firms.
d5.change_evidence: BLS or BEA five-year real-output projections for six-digit NAICS 541211, supplemented by service-line unit volumes for audit reports, business and individual returns, and recurring CAS engagements from an LMM firm panel, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: The current basket is supported by recurring filing, assurance, reporting, and regulatory obligations, business formation and economic activity, and rising complexity. Basic compliance faces self-service and software substitution, but assurance, complex tax, and client accounting demand should offset much of it. The midpoint therefore represents modest real quantity growth rather than extrapolating recent nominal fee gains.

caveats: No source measures the eligible share of the frozen LMM firm population directly.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms near the EBITDA boundaries.
State ownership, independence, and firm-licensure rules vary, and attest work may require a split alternative-practice structure.
e.change_evidence: A representative broker or transaction database for $1-10M EBITDA CPA firms showing screened opportunities, rejection reasons, revenue recurrence, client concentration, quality-control findings, partner dependence, and state ownership constraints would materially change the range.
e.rationale: Most firms counted in this NAICS and EBITDA band serve external clients through repeat tax, assurance, or accounting relationships, and the sector is described as fragmented with stable, recurring revenue. Eligibility is reduced for owner-dependent books, weakly documented practices, unacceptable client concentration or quality risk, independence conflicts, and firms whose licensed attest ownership cannot be transferred in a compliant control structure. Alternative practice structures make many otherwise constrained firms transferable, but not frictionlessly so.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $68,045.5M / sectoral output (T30) $134,726.2M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $263,807 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541211); class <5: 35,096 firms, avg revenue/firm $300,013 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $46,952 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 9,158 firms, avg revenue/firm $944,405 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $147,799 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4,257 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,000,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $313,036 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,015 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,602,856 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,033,347 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 271 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,129,554 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,810,775 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 88 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,137,349,114 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $177,995,136 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,286

o.bridge: BLS expects routine-task automation to raise efficiency and make advisory and analytical duties more prominent without reducing overall accountant demand, while NASBA documents that attest reports remain under licensed-CPA control and GAO reports that more than half of individual taxpayers used a paid preparer in fiscal 2024. These sources measure employment expectations, legal accountability, and current tax-preparer use rather than the exact year-five share of CPA-firm service quantity requiring an accountable operator. I reduce the share for straightforward returns, bookkeeping, data entry, and reporting that can move to self-service or software. Occupational demand can overstate operator-required quantity if software enables fewer professionals to serve more clients; tax self-service can understate the operator requirement for complex business work.
caveats: Legal responsibility varies by service: tax preparation, bookkeeping, advisory, review, and audit have different operator requirements.
The GAO statistic covers all paid individual-return preparers, not CPA firms or business clients.
Future standards may permit greater machine execution while retaining only a thin layer of licensed sign-off.
o.change_evidence: Service-line client surveys and engagement records tracking migrations from CPA firms to self-preparation or software, together with state-board and AICPA changes to attest-signature, supervision, and AI-accountability standards, would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: A high share of the current basket should still require a CPA-firm operator because attest opinions, independence, professional judgment, signed tax positions, quality control, exception handling, and client accountability cannot be supplied entirely by unaccountable software. Operator need is weaker for simple individual tax, routine bookkeeping, document processing, and standardized financial reporting, so the estimate is below full retention.

q.bridge: The 2024 Thomson Reuters tax-professional survey measures offered pricing models, not discounted benefit retention: 66% of respondents' firms use hourly or time-based pricing, 62% offer flat fees, 48% offer value-based pricing, and 29% offer retainers, with overlapping responses. AICPA's 2025 MAP survey shows that mostly small firms were able to grow net client fees, but it does not isolate AI savings. I infer that hourly work initially leaks efficiency through fewer billable hours unless capacity is redeployed, whereas fixed, project, retainer, and value pricing retain more until renewal repricing and competition. Overlap among pricing modes and client pressure make the direction uncertain; using model availability as revenue weights would overstate precision.
caveats: Survey respondents can offer several pricing models, so the percentages are not revenue shares and cannot be summed.
The pricing survey covers tax and accounting firms beyond NAICS 541211 and the LMM lens.
Observed fee growth includes inflation, demand, scope, and labor scarcity rather than isolated retention of AI benefits.
q.change_evidence: Cohort-level engagement data from LMM CPA firms comparing pre- and post-AI hours, realized fees, renewal repricing, win-loss rates, client churn, and contribution margin by billing model over at least three renewal cycles would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: The mix of billing models gives operators a real but partial path to retain implemented savings. Recurring fixed fees and value pricing can preserve margin, and constrained labor capacity can be redeployed into more clients or advisory work. Retention is eroded as clients expect faster delivery, hourly engagements generate fewer billable hours, annual renewals reset fees, commoditized tax work faces software and peer competition, and sellers or employees may capture part of the benefit.

rho.bridge: Thomson Reuters surveys tax, audit, and accounting firms rather than measuring the fraction of exposed wage opportunity implemented. Enterprise GenAI use rose from 8% in 2024 to 21% in 2025, but only about one in seven firms reported a comprehensive AI strategy; CPA.com reports rapid deployment in bookkeeping and tax but methodical audit adoption because of regulatory and liability complexity. I map current adoption, strategic readiness, and workflow-specific maturity to five-year implementation of exposed work. Survey optimism may overstate implementation, while current enterprise-use measures may understate five-year diffusion.
caveats: The Thomson Reuters samples cover broader tax, audit, and accounting populations, multiple countries, and firms outside the EBITDA lens.
Reported adoption does not establish sustained production use or monetizable labor avoidance.
The five-year horizon includes substantial uncertainty in model reliability, regulation, professional standards, and software integration.
rho.change_evidence: Annual AICPA PCPS MAP or CPA.com panel data linking firm size to production hours, AI-enabled workflow penetration, realized time savings, error or rework rates, and headcount avoided by service line would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years is enough for embedded tax, document, research, bookkeeping, and workflow tools to diffuse across a meaningful share of firms, especially under staffing pressure. Implementation will remain incomplete because client-data security, fragmented practice systems, review controls, hallucination risk, audit evidence requirements, change management, and partner accountability slow conversion from technical exposure to avoided labor or avoided hiring.

s5.bridge: An AICPA PCPS survey of roughly 850 responding CPA firms found 49% of multiowner-firm leaders had been in active merger discussions during the prior 24 months, but discussions are not completed qualifying control transfers and the 2016 sample is old and not limited to the LMM band. More recent market sources document 115 CPA-firm deals in 2024 and record sector equity-financing volume, confirming active consolidation without providing a denominator. I discount the discussion rate for noncompletion, internal reorganizations, minority financings, repeat add-ons, and non-LMM firms, then allow five years for retirement and succession events. The old survey may understate today's transaction appetite, while deal lists may overstate unique qualifying transfers.
caveats: The succession survey is from 2016, covers firms of various sizes, and reports discussions rather than closings.
Published deal counts are incomplete, may include non-control deals, and lack a consistent eligible-firm denominator.
High transaction activity among larger firms and PE platforms may not represent the frozen LMM population.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed US CPA-firm control transactions matched to a census of eligible $1-10M EBITDA firms, with seller age, transaction type, ownership percentage, and internal-versus-external succession flags, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Aging owners, weak internal succession benches, and active strategic and sponsor-backed consolidation support a meaningful five-year transfer probability. However, many firms will execute internal partner buyouts, delay retirement, take minority capital, remain too owner-dependent to sell, or enter discussions that never close.

records.principal_driver: A labor-intensive base of repeat compliance and advisory work contains substantial document, research, preparation, and workflow effort that can be standardized and AI-assisted.
records.principal_weakness: Mixed hourly pricing and licensed-quality constraints create a wide gap between technical task exposure and durable owner-retained economics.

caveats: The a base is an honest but judgment-heavy translation from four-digit employment shares, a technical task-exposure rubric, and workflow examples; no source measures the wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated work in six-digit LMM CPA firms.

The rho range maps current adoption and strategy indicators to a five-year implemented share without longitudinal evidence on production penetration, realized hours saved, rework, or avoided headcount in the target firm band.

The e estimate is not directly measured: the cited sources establish ownership constraints, alternative practice structures, fragmentation, and recurring revenue, but not the share of $1-10 million EBITDA firms that pass target-specific eligibility screens.

The s5 proxy combines an old survey of merger discussions with broad recent deal compilations that lack a qualifying-control definition and eligible-firm denominator; the resulting five-year transfer probability remains weakly identified.
The q estimate is inferred from overlapping pricing-model offerings and nominal fee growth, neither of which measures discounted owner retention of AI benefits by engagement or revenue mix.

The d5 estimate relies on occupation employment, nominal firm revenue, and individual-return filing counts rather than a constant-price, constant-quality quantity measure for the full CPA-firm service basket.

The o estimate extrapolates from attest-control rules, accountant employment expectations, and all-paid-preparer usage to the year-five operator-required share across heterogeneous CPA services; none of the sources directly measures that share.

sources_audited.note: BLS May 2023 NAICS 541200 table reports accountants and auditors at 31.73%, tax preparers at 7.13%, management at 10.54%, and office and administrative support at 28.76% of employment; it is a disclosed four-digit, employment-mix proxy rather than six-digit wage-weighted task evidence.

The paper's expansive model-zeta measure lists accountants and auditors at 100.0 and defines exposure as at least a 50% task-time reduction with LLM-powered software, while explicitly warning that this does not imply full task automation.

The report describes agentic bookkeeping, near-full tax-preparation automation subject to final review, methodical audit adoption due to regulatory and liability complexity, advisory modeling, and back-office workflow tools; it is directional industry evidence rather than a representative LMM outcome study.
The page states that enterprise-level GenAI use among tax, audit, and accounting firms increased from 8% in 2024 to 21% in 2025.
The article reports that one in seven respondents had a comprehensive AI strategy and identifies 462-plus responses across 25 countries, about half from US firms.

BLS projects 5% accountant-and-auditor employment growth from 2024 to 2034, cites economic and regulatory demand drivers, and says routine-task automation should raise efficiency and shift duties toward advisory and analytical work rather than reduce overall demand.

The NASBA paper states that most states require majority licensed-CPA equity and voting rights, at least 44 jurisdictions require active individual non-CPA owners, and alternative practice structures keep attest services under licensed-CPA control and oversight.

S&P describes a fragmented accounting, auditing, and taxation sector with stable customer bases and predictable recurring revenues and reports 24 global PE/VC-backed deals in the first 11 months of 2024; the deal evidence is broader than US LMM CPA firms.

The 2016 article reports roughly 850 survey respondents, 49% of multiowner-firm leaders in active merger discussions during the prior 24 months, one-fourth with current succession challenges, and another 48% expecting them within five years.
The compiled market article reports upwards of 115 CPA-firm deals in 2024; it does not provide a qualifying-control definition or an eligible-firm denominator.

The 2024 report states that 66% of respondents' firms offer hourly or time-based pricing, 62% flat fees, 48% value-based pricing, and 29% retainers; these are overlapping firm-level offerings, not revenue shares or measured AI-benefit retention.

AICPA reports that 81% of responses came from firms with revenue of $5 million or below, median net client fees grew 6.7%, and revenue growth occurred in audit and assurance, tax, and client accounting advisory services.
The IRS/National Taxpayer Advocate table shows professionally filed individual returns rising from 71.3 million in 2024 to 72.5 million in 2025, a 1.7% increase.

GAO states that more than half of individual taxpayers used a paid preparer in fiscal 2024 and discusses credentials, errors, and oversight; this covers all paid preparers rather than CPA firms or business-client services.

caveats: a: BLS occupational data cover NAICS 541800 rather than only 541810.
a: Tool use does not imply full task automation.
a: Exposure varies materially by client category, channel mix, data access, and seniority.
rho: Survey populations include brands and publishers as well as agencies.
rho: Stated plans may not be realized.
rho: Integration can mean assistance rather than end-to-end automation.
e: No representative firm-level dataset directly measures the share meeting every control, transferability, recurrence, concentration, and normalized-EBITDA screen.
e: The frozen firm count is itself bridged from revenue size classes using a 15.65% margin.
s5: Gallup covers all U.S. employer businesses, not agencies, and plans are not completed sales.
s5: M&A reports cover a broader marketing-services universe and include deals outside the target EBITDA band.
q: Evidence is not representative of private lower-middle-market contract terms.
q: Capture depends on renewal timing, channel mix, differentiation, measurable outcomes, and whether the agency sells deliverables, capacity, or performance.
d5: BLS covers broader NAICS 541800 and a ten-year horizon.
d5: IAB spending is nominal buyer media spend rather than agency net revenue.
d5: WPP is a large global company rather than a U.S. lower-middle-market sample.
o: Buyer surveys skew toward larger advertisers and rapidly changing digital-video workflows.
o: Smaller clients may lack internal talent, while large clients may have stronger in-housing economics.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Outsourced service for business, nonprofit, and public-sector advertisers, monetized through recurring retainers, project or scope fees, media commissions, and performance incentives; the qualifying lens requires recurring or repeat revenue and normalizes pass-through billings.
lens.deviation_from_default: None; the default U.S. lower-middle-market recurring outsourced-service lens is coherent for full-service NAICS 541810 agencies.
lens.excluded_activities: Shell, captive, and non-control situations; pure media representatives, graphic-design studios, direct-mail operators, marketing consultancies, and ad-tech hosting businesses outside NAICS 541810; project-only, founder-dependent, highly client-concentrated, or nonassignable-contract firms without transferable recurring economics; and media or production pass-through amounts when assessing normalized agency economics.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market full-service advertising agencies with $1-10 million of normalized EBITDA and recurring or repeat outsourced client work across strategy, creative, account management, content production, and media planning or buying.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: occupation mix for NAICS 541800 and documented AI use cases across the advertising campaign lifecycle. Transformation: mapped research, analytics, planning, content generation, design iteration, reporting, optimization, and routine administration to exposed hours; applied lower exposure to relationship management, negotiation, strategic judgment, and accountable approval; then bounded for the narrower full-service-agency population. Error direction: the broader industry includes public relations and media-representation roles that may differ in exposure, so bias can run either way; observed AI use cases may overstate autonomous substitution and therefore tend to bias the estimate upward.
caveats: BLS occupational data cover NAICS 541800 rather than only 541810.
Tool use does not imply full task automation.
Exposure varies materially by client category, channel mix, data access, and seniority.
a.change_evidence: IAB reports that AI can already build media plans, create audience segments, select partners, forecast performance, and support attribution; video advertisers also use generative AI for rapid, lower-cost creative variation. BLS occupation shares show that the broader advertising and public-relations services workforce is concentrated in arts/design/media, sales, management, business analysis, and office support, enabling an hours-based mapping rather than treating every job as equally exposed.
a.rationale: A large share of agency hours sits in research, reporting, media planning, creative production, copy and design iteration, campaign versioning, optimization, and administration. Those activities are increasingly machine-assistable, while brand strategy, senior creative judgment, client stewardship, negotiation, and final approval remain more human-intensive.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS real-output projection for NAICS 541800, IAB nominal advertising-spend outlook, and recent large-agency organic revenue performance. Transformation: converted the BLS 2.7% real annual output projection into an approximate five-year multiplier, then discounted the center for the narrower agency population's in-housing and automation risk and used spend growth and recent revenue contraction to set asymmetric bounds. Error direction: the broader category and aggregate output projection may overstate demand retained by independent agencies; using real rather than nominal output limits upward inflation bias.
caveats: BLS covers broader NAICS 541800 and a ten-year horizon.
IAB spending is nominal buyer media spend rather than agency net revenue.
WPP is a large global company rather than a U.S. lower-middle-market sample.
d5.change_evidence: BLS projects real output for NAICS 541800 to grow 2.7% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying roughly 1.14 over five years, while employment grows only 0.2% annually. IAB expects continued ad-spend growth, but WPP reported recent organic revenue declines and client assignment losses, directly supporting downside dispersion.
d5.rationale: Underlying advertising demand and real industry output can grow over five years, but agency service demand will lag headline media-spend growth where platforms automate buying, brands internalize production, or clients reduce intermediaries. The base therefore sits below the broader industry's mechanical real-output projection.

caveats: No representative firm-level dataset directly measures the share meeting every control, transferability, recurrence, concentration, and normalized-EBITDA screen.
The frozen firm count is itself bridged from revenue size classes using a 15.65% margin.
e.change_evidence: The official industry definition confirms an integrated outsourced scope, while large-agency disclosures document recurring retainers as well as fee, commission, and incentive arrangements.
e.rationale: NAICS 541810 is intrinsically an outsourced-service category, and full-service agencies commonly combine repeat account management, planning, creative, production, and media work. Eligibility is reduced for project-only work, severe customer concentration, founder dependence, nonassignable relationships, and economics dominated by pass-through billings.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $33,005.0M / sectoral output (T30) $68,557.8M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $303,923 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541810); class <5: 9,620 firms, avg revenue/firm $521,704 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $81,647 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,690 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,849,936 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $289,515 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,101 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,849,742 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $602,485 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 977 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,049,911 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,885,811 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 198 firms, avg revenue/firm $48,263,556 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,553,246 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $267,062,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,795,242 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,175

o.bridge: Source population/quantity: advertiser self-service and in-housing plans, demand for human partner support, and brand preferences for internal, external, or hybrid agentic deployment. Transformation: translated the balance of in-housing signals and continued partner demand into the share of AI-enabled work expected to remain with an external full-service agency rather than move directly to clients or platforms. Error direction: large-advertiser survey samples likely overstate in-housing relative to lower-middle-market clients, biasing orchestration retention downward; agency self-reporting can bias retained relevance upward.
caveats: Buyer surveys skew toward larger advertisers and rapidly changing digital-video workflows.
Smaller clients may lack internal talent, while large clients may have stronger in-housing economics.
o.change_evidence: Half of surveyed agencies feared brands would use AI to reduce agency reliance, and 74% of video buyers had built or planned internal teams for self-service connected-TV activation. Countervailing evidence is that more than 80% still wanted human assistance, and 2026 survey data show brands favoring hybrid or external-partner agentic deployment alongside internal builds.
o.rationale: AI enables brands to internalize more creative variation, planning, activation, and analytics, but full-service agencies retain an orchestration role across brand strategy, cross-channel execution, specialized talent, governance, partner coordination, and accountable client service. Hybrid delivery is therefore more likely than complete bypass for many target firms.

caveats: Evidence is not representative of private lower-middle-market contract terms.
Capture depends on renewal timing, channel mix, differentiation, measurable outcomes, and whether the agency sells deliverables, capacity, or performance.
q.change_evidence: Agency disclosures show retainers, commissions, fees, and performance incentives, but also state that labor costs are the primary input for many fee arrangements. Industry surveys show both growing AI capability and client interest in reducing agency reliance.
q.rationale: Agencies can retain part of AI-created value through broader scope, faster iteration, improved performance, and fixed or value-linked fees. Retainers and incentive structures support capture, but labor-based pricing, procurement, competitive rebids, in-housing, and transparent tool costs pass a substantial share of efficiency gains to clients.

rho.bridge: Source population/quantity: current and planned AI integration reported by U.S. advertising buyers, agencies, publishers, and executives, plus agentic deployment preferences. Transformation: converted current full-lifecycle adoption, near-term plans, use-case breadth, and governance barriers into the share of technically exposed agency hours expected to be practically automated or materially augmented within five years. Error direction: stated adoption plans and vendor-era enthusiasm tend to bias realization upward; rapid improvement in tools and competitive pressure could make the estimate conservative.
caveats: Survey populations include brands and publishers as well as agencies.
Stated plans may not be realized.
Integration can mean assistance rather than end-to-end automation.
rho.change_evidence: Only 30% of surveyed organizations had fully integrated AI across the campaign lifecycle in 2025, but about half of those not fully integrated expected to do so by 2026. Generative-video adoption is already broad, while data-quality and governance gaps and frequent AI incidents provide direct evidence of adoption friction.
rho.rationale: Five years is sufficient for agencies to diffuse proven AI tools through planning, targeting, content production, reporting, and workflow orchestration, but realized adoption will remain below technical exposure because client approvals, fragmented data, rights, governance, brand safety, and integration constrain deployment.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners and observed marketing-services M&A activity. Transformation: used the 22% all-industry transfer intention as an anchor, adjusted for active sector buyer demand and agency owner demographics, and widened bounds for completion rates, internal transfers, minority deals, and target-quality screens. Error direction: intentions and broad deal counts tend to overstate completed qualifying control sales; an aging owner base and strong strategic demand could make the low case conservative.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer businesses, not agencies, and plans are not completed sales.
M&A reports cover a broader marketing-services universe and include deals outside the target EBITDA band.
s5.change_evidence: Gallup finds that 22% of employer-business owners plan to sell or transfer within five years and 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older. Capstone reports rising marketing-services deal activity through early 2026, with strategic buyers representing most transactions and buyer preference for recurring, low-concentration, AI-capable businesses.
s5.rationale: Owner aging and stated five-year transfer plans create a meaningful seller pool, and current marketing-services transaction activity confirms buyer depth. Realized control sales remain below stated intentions because some owners delay, transfer internally, sell minorities, or fail quality and valuation screens.

records.principal_driver: The principal driver is the agency's ability to convert automation of routine delivery into more client scope and throughput while preserving its role as the trusted, accountable orchestrator.
records.principal_weakness: The principal weakness is limited evidence that independent lower-middle-market agencies can retain AI-driven savings when clients can demand lower fees, self-serve through platforms, or bring production and activation in-house.

caveats: The a mapping is directionally supported but not reproducible as an hours calculation: BLS covers the broader NAICS 541800 population, O*NET covers one occupation, and the packet does not provide task weights. PROXY is nevertheless the honest state, and the range already spans an H low case of 5.08, so this does not establish a base-tier or action change.

Most AI adoption and orchestration evidence comes from broad advertising-industry or digital-video buyer samples rather than U.S. lower-middle-market full-service agencies and their clients. This population gap is disclosed and appropriately treated as PROXY evidence.

S7 directly supports agentic deployment choices and advertising-spend growth but only indirectly informs q; no cited source measures the share of AI-created value retained by private agencies. q is honestly labeled ESTIMATE, and the MODEL_CONDITIONED action already reflects that weakness.

The s5 base of 0.25 sits above Gallup's all-industry five-year employer-owner transfer intention of 0.22 even though the rationale says realized qualifying control sales fall below intentions. Sector deal activity is buyer-demand evidence rather than an agency-specific completion rate. This inconsistency is not material under the required test because the registered low s5 of 0.16 still leaves F at 7.67 and does not change the HIGHEST_PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The d5 bridge discounts the BLS demand anchor partly for in-housing and platform automation, while o separately discounts work that moves to clients or platforms. That creates some conceptual overlap within D. Removing the overlap would tend to raise d5 and D from the registered base rather than lower the tier, and would not change the action.

The strict eligibility estimate e and value-capture estimate q lack representative lower-middle-market firm or contract data. Their estimate labels, ranges, narrative weakness, and MODEL_CONDITIONED action make the uncertainty visible rather than presenting it as observed evidence.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS Manual defines 541810 as full-service advertising agencies covering advice, creative, account management, content production, media planning, and buying, and confirms the stated adjacent-industry exclusions. It supports the lens and scope used in the affected primitives, not their numerical values by itself.

The exact BLS May 2023 NAICS 541800 page was retrievable through its indexed official result despite a direct page-render cache error. It reports industry occupational employment shares, including substantial arts/design/media and sales employment, and clearly identifies the broader 541800 population.

O*NET lists market-research tasks including data collection and analysis, reporting, forecasting, effectiveness measurement, and advertising-needs identification, supporting the task mapping used for a.

IAB reports a survey of more than 500 buy- and sell-side experts, 30% full campaign-lifecycle AI integration, half of non-integrators expecting integration by 2026, the cited planning and measurement use cases, and agency concern about client in-housing.

The IAB article reports a 125-executive U.S. survey, broad current GenAI use, 70% experiencing at least one AI incident, and substantial governance and accountability gaps, supporting adoption friction in rho.

IAB reports 86% of buyers using or planning GenAI video creative, rapid and lower-cost variation, 74% building or planning in-house self-serve CTV teams, and more than 80% wanting human sell-side assistance. The last statistic concerns sell-side platform partners rather than advertising agencies, a population limitation disclosed in the record.

The 2026 IAB Outlook supports the stated U.S. ad-spend outlook, agentic use cases, and hybrid or external deployment preferences among 205 buy-side decision-makers. It is only indirect evidence for the numerical value-retention estimate q and does not measure private-agency capture.
BLS Table 2.11 reports NAICS 541800 employment growth of 0.2% annually and real output growth of 2.7% annually for 2024-2034, matching the d5 anchor and its disclosed broader-industry limitation.

WPP's 2025 annual report documents commissions, fees, incentives, retainers, labour as the primary input for most fee arrangements, pass-through treatment, short and cancellable contracts, and 2025 organic declines tied partly to client losses and spending cuts.

Capstone's April 2026 update reports rising marketing-services and advertising transaction activity, 68.6% strategic-buyer share, and sponsor preference for low concentration, recurring revenue, and AI-enabled offerings. It covers a broader marketing-services deal universe, as disclosed.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years and Census data showing 52.3% of employer businesses owned by people 55 or older. The source is all-industry and reports intentions, not completed agency control sales.

caveats: a: BLS does not publish the cited occupational mix at the six-digit 541214 level; NAICS 541200 is materially affected by accountants and tax preparers.
a: The task allocation is analyst judgment rather than a measured AI-substitution rate.

a: The frozen labor input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, includes a 1.4294 benefits multiplier, and is harmonized by a cross-code median divisor; the vintage and harmonization can distort the economic weight even though they do not change this task-share estimate.
rho: The PayrollOrg survey is global, appears weighted toward larger and multinational organizations, and reports use or plans rather than realized labor displacement.
rho: Vendor-embedded deterministic automation and generative AI are difficult to separate in payroll operations.
rho: Security, privacy, auditability, and state-by-state compliance may slow LMM implementation more than technical capability suggests.
e: Eligibility is not directly reported in public establishment data.

e: The frozen n of 297 is itself an ESTIMATE bridged from SUSB size classes using a sector EBITDA margin; firm-specific margins, multi-establishment ownership, and high industry concentration can materially change the true LMM count.
e: NAICS assignment follows primary activity and may not reflect current revenue mix after HCM, benefits, or software expansion.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and based on plans, not completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: The public payroll transaction is a very large strategic deal and only demonstrates buyer appetite, not LMM incidence.
s5: Family gifts, management succession without a control event, and closures must not be counted.
q: Public-company retention and client-life metrics may overstate switching costs for independent LMM bureaus.
q: Revenue retention is not the same as retention of an implemented gross benefit; pass-through is not publicly disclosed.
q: Savings may be reinvested in service, compliance, cybersecurity, and sales rather than appearing as retained operating profit.
d5: The BLS output projection is for NAICS 541200 and combines payroll with accounting, tax preparation, and bookkeeping.
d5: Employment is an input, not service quantity, and can fall while output rises through automation.
d5: Public payroll-company revenue is unsuitable as a direct demand measure because it includes HCM modules, PEO services, pricing, acquisitions, and interest on client funds.
o: Employer legal responsibility can support demand for expert service but can also motivate direct monitoring and self-service; the net effect is not directly measured.
o: The BLS occupational projection covers payroll clerks across all industries rather than outsourced 541214 operators.
o: Large vendors' hybrid service models may not describe the minimum operator layer needed by LMM clients.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring outsourced business-to-business service, generally billed per payroll period and often per employee, with implementation fees and optional compliance, HR, timekeeping, and reporting services.
lens.excluded_activities: CPA firms, bundled bookkeeping or accounting practices classified elsewhere, PEO co-employment, captive payroll departments, pure software publishers, pure data-processing vendors, non-control financing vehicles, and shells.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market payroll service bureaus that repeatedly collect client hours, pay rates, deductions, and related data and produce payroll, reports, payments, and tax filings for external employer clients.

a.bridge: The source population is NAICS 541200 employment across accounting, tax, bookkeeping, and payroll firms plus the cross-industry payroll-clerk occupation, not 541214 LMM payroll bureaus. I mapped structured data intake, payroll calculation, reconciliation, report preparation, tax-form drafting, routine support, and internal administration to high or medium AI exposure; sales, management, final approval, and novel compliance exceptions to lower exposure; and calibrated the wage-weighted result to 0.52. The broad 541200 mix overweights accountants and tax preparers and therefore likely understates pure-payroll clerical exposure, while O*NET includes work already partly automated and therefore can overstate the remaining not-yet-automated opportunity; the range covers both errors.
caveats: BLS does not publish the cited occupational mix at the six-digit 541214 level; NAICS 541200 is materially affected by accountants and tax preparers.
The task allocation is analyst judgment rather than a measured AI-substitution rate.

The frozen labor input uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, includes a 1.4294 benefits multiplier, and is harmonized by a cross-code median divisor; the vintage and harmonization can distort the economic weight even though they do not change this task-share estimate.
a.change_evidence: A 541214-specific payroll by occupation from a private labor-data vendor, combined with time-and-motion studies from 15-20 LMM payroll bureaus that separate already automated work from manual intake, reconciliation, exception handling, support, compliance updates, sales, and management, would materially move this estimate.
a.rationale: BLS shows office and administrative support at 28.76% and business and financial operations at 47.31% of NAICS 541200 employment, while O*NET describes payroll-clerk work as verifying hours, computing wages and deductions, entering records, reconciling payroll, preparing reports, and filing payroll tax returns. These are structured information workflows well suited to extraction, anomaly detection, drafting, and assisted resolution. Exposure is not higher because client implementation, sales, cybersecurity, changing-law interpretation, complex exceptions, and final release of funds require judgment and accountability. BLS's projected 17% decline in payroll and timekeeping clerks provides directional confirmation that productivity technology can substitute for part of this work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for broad NAICS 541200, not six-digit 541214 service quantity, at a 2.1% annual compound rate from 2024 to 2034; five-year compounding implies about 1.11. I temper that to 1.09 using 541214 payroll employment's modest rise from 240,600 in July 2024 to 242,700 in June 2025 and the risk that software productivity raises measured output without equal growth in the current service basket. The broad-output proxy likely overstates 541214 quantity if adjacent accounting and tax lines grow faster or if quality-enhancing software is counted as output, so the base is below the raw transformation.
caveats: The BLS output projection is for NAICS 541200 and combines payroll with accounting, tax preparation, and bookkeeping.
Employment is an input, not service quantity, and can fall while output rises through automation.
Public payroll-company revenue is unsuitable as a direct demand measure because it includes HCM modules, PEO services, pricing, acquisitions, and interest on client funds.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain 541214-specific five-year histories and forecasts for active employer clients, paid employees, payroll runs, tax filings, and constant-price service revenue from Census service-product data, payroll processors, and industry vendors, with software-license revenue and interest on client funds removed.
d5.rationale: The number of workers paid, employers served, filing obligations, and regulatory complexity sustain payroll demand, while broad BLS real-output projections point to moderate growth. Counterweights are client consolidation, per-employee billing sensitivity to employment, self-service, and software automation. The result is modest five-year real quantity growth rather than either rapid expansion or structural collapse.

caveats: Eligibility is not directly reported in public establishment data.

The frozen n of 297 is itself an ESTIMATE bridged from SUSB size classes using a sector EBITDA margin; firm-specific margins, multi-establishment ownership, and high industry concentration can materially change the true LMM count.
NAICS assignment follows primary activity and may not reflect current revenue mix after HCM, benefits, or software expansion.
e.change_evidence: Review a stratified sample of 75-100 firms from the SUSB-derived LMM population using tax returns or confidential financials, customer invoices, ownership records, employee rosters, and product mix to classify recurring external payroll revenue, standalone operations, owner dependence, and software-only or PEO exposure.
e.rationale: NAICS 541214 is already defined around an external-client service: collecting client payroll data and generating paychecks, reports, and tax filings. Most LMM-band firms in the code should therefore satisfy the recurring outsourced-service lens. The haircut excludes software-dominant businesses, misclassified bundled accounting or PEO operations, captive units, firms without a transferable operating team, and entities whose economics are inseparable from one owner or a reseller relationship.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541214 ($22,102,632,622) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541214 ($27,322,843,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $98,054 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541214); class <5: 2,110 firms, avg revenue/firm $581,493 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $91,004 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 662 firms, avg revenue/firm $919,816 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $143,951 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 480 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,588,508 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $248,602 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 741 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,599,022 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $719,747 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 297 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,560,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,122,240 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 194 firms, avg revenue/firm $89,119,103 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,947,140 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 297

caveats: Employer legal responsibility can support demand for expert service but can also motivate direct monitoring and self-service; the net effect is not directly measured.
The BLS occupational projection covers payroll clerks across all industries rather than outsourced 541214 operators.
Large vendors' hybrid service models may not describe the minimum operator layer needed by LMM clients.
o.change_evidence: Survey and interview 100 US SMB payroll buyers on planned self-service versus managed-service use, switching triggers, required human support, and willingness to accept software-only filing and payment; pair this with processor ticket, exception, amended-return, tax-notice, and manual-approval volumes by client cohort.
o.rationale: Much routine calculation and self-service can move into software, and BLS expects payroll-clerk employment to decline 17% by 2034 because of productivity technology. Yet the current outsourced-service basket also includes onboarding, data correction, reconciliation, tax filing, payment custody, changing-law interpretation, audit trails, security, and accountable exception resolution. The IRS emphasizes that employers remain liable for tax deposits even when duties are outsourced, encouraging them to retain a trusted operator and controls rather than rely on unaccountable software alone. ADP's combination of cloud platforms with 24/7 payroll-professional support illustrates the durable hybrid model. On balance, a clear majority of year-five quantity still needs a service operator, though with fewer human touches per payroll.

caveats: Public-company retention and client-life metrics may overstate switching costs for independent LMM bureaus.
Revenue retention is not the same as retention of an implemented gross benefit; pass-through is not publicly disclosed.
Savings may be reinvested in service, compliance, cybersecurity, and sales rather than appearing as retained operating profit.
q.change_evidence: Analyze five years of anonymized contracts, renewal quotes, discounts, churn reasons, and gross margins from 15-20 LMM payroll bureaus, then compare realized price and service-level changes before and after measurable automation releases on matched client cohorts.
q.rationale: Payroll fees are generally charged each payroll period and often vary with employee count, so labor savings are not mechanically passed through through cost-plus billing. Long client relationships and high retention support near-term capture: ADP reported 92.1% Employer Services revenue retention and an estimated 13-year Employer Services relationship, while Paycom reports a 10-year estimated client life. Capture is not permanent because agreements can be terminable on 30 days' notice, prices are periodically reassessed, and capable rivals can share automation savings through lower prices or richer service. Discounting that repricing and competition over five years yields a retained share modestly above one-half.

rho.bridge: PayrollOrg measures organization-level technology use among more than 500 global payroll professionals, not the fraction of exposed labor opportunity implemented by US LMM payroll bureaus. I treated the 21% already using AI plus 30% planning near-term integration as evidence that roughly half the market has entered the adoption funnel, then allowed five years for wider diffusion but discounted for incomplete workflow coverage, data integration, review controls, security, and compliance. The global respondent base likely overstates LMM US implementation capacity, while its near-term planning horizon understates cumulative five-year adoption; the base gives more weight to the former and the range spans both.
caveats: The PayrollOrg survey is global, appears weighted toward larger and multinational organizations, and reports use or plans rather than realized labor displacement.
Vendor-embedded deterministic automation and generative AI are difficult to separate in payroll operations.
Security, privacy, auditability, and state-by-state compliance may slow LMM implementation more than technical capability suggests.
rho.change_evidence: Obtain implementation cohort data from payroll-platform vendors and 15-20 LMM bureaus showing pre/post labor hours, error and rework rates, percentage of clients on standardized integrations, manual-review rates, and production AI coverage by workflow over at least four payroll quarters.
rho.rationale: A majority of exposed work should be implementable through bounded copilots, exception triage, document and data ingestion, anomaly detection, knowledge retrieval, customer-service assistance, and workflow orchestration. Full autonomous payroll release is less plausible: PayrollOrg identifies compliance and inbound/outbound integration as leading challenges, and O*NET shows reconciliation, tax-law monitoring, employee advice, and error correction among core duties. Implementation therefore captures more than current adoption but materially less than all technical exposure.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year plans across all US employer-business owners: 22% planned to sell or transfer ownership. I reduced that starting point for gifts, internal transfers, rare public offerings, abandoned plans, and closures that do not become qualifying control transfers, then added a smaller offset for payroll firms' recurring revenue, long client lives, and demonstrated strategic-buyer appetite. The raw 22% likely overstates qualifying transfers because its outcome definition is broader; payroll transferability and unplanned sales partly offset that upward error, producing a 20% base.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and based on plans, not completed qualifying control transfers.
The public payroll transaction is a very large strategic deal and only demonstrates buyer appetite, not LMM incidence.
Family gifts, management succession without a control event, and closures must not be counted.
s5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and payroll-specialist broker data filtered to US payroll-service targets in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, deduplicated by closing and matched to estimated eligible firm stock; supplement with owner-age and succession-plan interviews across at least 50 independent bureaus.
s5.rationale: Succession supply is meaningful: Gallup reports 52.3% of US employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older, and 22% of employer owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. Payroll client books are more transferable than owner-personal labor, but not every intention closes or changes control. Paychex's 2025 acquisition of Paycor confirms acquirer appetite for payroll and HCM assets, although that transaction is far above the LMM band and cannot establish a small-firm transfer rate.

records.principal_driver: Structured recurring payroll workflows offer substantial assisted-automation potential while clients still value an accountable compliance and payment operator.
records.principal_weakness: The evidence for LMM transfer incidence and durable operator-level value capture is proxy-heavy, and software competition can pass savings to customers.

caveats: S1 is a 1997 NAICS archive. It reproduces the cited payroll-services definition, but a current 2022 NAICS source would be preferable for a population scored with 2022-coded data.

The a and rho bases are analyst calibrations from a four-digit occupational mix, a cross-industry occupation, and a global organization-level adoption survey; none directly measures remaining AI-removable labor or five-year implementation coverage in US LMM payroll bureaus. The PROXY labels, bridges, and ranges disclose this limitation.

The s5 base of 0.20 is close to Gallup's raw 0.22 intention rate even though gifts, internal transfers, abandoned plans, and non-control outcomes require a downward adjustment. The offset for payroll transferability and unplanned sales is not measured, and the only industry transaction is far above the LMM band; the low case captures meaningful downside but F is the base-tier weakest link.

The frozen n of 297 is an ESTIMATE derived from establishment-size classes, class-average revenue, and a broad sector EBITDA margin. Because the scenario envelope holds n fixed, uncertainty in actual independently owned LMM payroll-firm stock is not reflected in the attainable tier interval.

ADP retention and estimated client life and Paycom contract terms establish recurring relationships and repricing mechanics, but they do not measure retention of automation benefits. The q value is therefore appropriately an ESTIMATE rather than an observed capture rate.

The d5 and o evidence establishes broad real-output growth, modest six-digit employment movement, legal accountability, and a large-vendor hybrid service model, but it does not directly measure year-five service quantity or the operator-required share for independent LMM bureaus. The proxy and estimate treatment is honest, and the wide D range reflects the gap.

sources_audited.note: The page contains the quoted 541214 definition and cross-references, but it is explicitly the 1997 NAICS archive rather than the current 2022 definition used by the scored population.
The May 2023 NAICS 541200 table reports the cited 1,100,840 employment total and the stated major-occupation shares; its four-digit, mixed-industry population is disclosed.

O*NET lists the cited payroll tasks, including hours verification, wage and deduction computation, record entry, reconciliation, discrepancy handling, tax-law monitoring, employee guidance, and payroll-tax-return preparation.
BLS reports payroll and timekeeping clerk employment of 161,100 in 2024 and 134,200 in 2034, a 17% decline, and attributes limited demand partly to productivity-enhancing technology.

PayrollOrg reports more than 500 global payroll respondents, 21% current AI use, 30% near-term integration plans, 57% citing local compliance, and inbound and outbound automation as the next challenges.
Gallup reports the cited 52.3% owner-age statistic and that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer in the next five years; the survey ran in fall 2024.

Paychex documents completion of the Paycor acquisition on April 14, 2025 for approximately $4.1 billion of enterprise value; the record correctly limits this to evidence of large strategic-buyer appetite.

ADP's 2025 Form 10-K reports 92.1% Employer Services revenue retention, an estimated 13-year Employer Services relationship, recurring service arrangements, and 24/7 payroll-professional support for RUN.

Paycom's 2025 Form 10-K states that recurring fees are recognized and charged during each payroll period, most contracts effectively have one-month terms and 30-day termination rights, prices are periodically assessed, and estimated client life is 10 years.
The BLS 2024-2034 table reports broad NAICS 541200 employment growth of 2.4% and constant-dollar output rising from $210.0 billion to $258.6 billion at 2.1% annually.
The IRS states that employers remain ultimately responsible for federal tax deposits and liable for taxes, penalties, and interest when payroll duties are outsourced.
The archived July 2025 CES table reports seasonally adjusted 541214 employment of 240,600 in July 2024 and 242,700 in June 2025.
