caveats: a: BLS publishes the occupational mix at broader NAICS 541500, not exact NAICS 541519, and employment shares are not payroll shares.
a: The software-developer experiment and Claude usage data cover task populations that overlap only part of this residual service category.
a: The estimate explicitly discounts work already automated, but no exact-industry baseline separates conventional automation from remaining tasks.
a: The injected labor-intensity input combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and the specified IPS harmonization; that vintage mismatch is not direct evidence about task exposure.
rho: Both implementation sources survey MSPs globally and may not represent disaster-recovery, installation, or residual consulting firms classified in 541519.
rho: Self-reported use and workload automation do not independently verify quality, durability, or labor-hour savings.
rho: The respondents are reached through industry and vendor channels that may select for more technology-active firms.
e: The official definition and survey form identify included and neighboring activities but do not measure the eligible share of firms.
e: The residual category is unusually heterogeneous and classification practices may vary among similar providers.
e: The injected count of 705 firms is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% EBITDA margin, so its band assignment is uncertain even though it is not re-estimated here.
s5: The succession survey is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: The transaction report covers the global MSP market, a narrower business model that only partially overlaps NAICS 541519.
s5: Deal-count definitions and small private transaction disclosure are not standardized.
s5: The estimated 705-firm denominator inherits uncertainty from size-class receipts and the 15.65% margin bridge.
q: The billing survey is vendor-sponsored, global, and specific to MSPs rather than exact NAICS 541519.
q: Predominant billing model does not reveal revenue weights, contract duration, or post-automation repricing behavior.
q: Recent MSP evidence reports shrinking deal sizes and strong competition, which may erode retention faster than historical pricing data imply.
d5: BLS output is projected only at broader NAICS 541500 and includes custom programming, systems design, and facilities management excluded from 541519.
d5: Vendor-survey revenue growth is self-reported, global, and reflects MSP service lines rather than constant-quality demand for the exact current basket.
d5: Cybersecurity and AI can create new services while simultaneously automating legacy work, making category-level net demand unusually uncertain.
o: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share for NAICS 541519.
o: The category mixes physically grounded installation and recovery with remote services that may be more readily self-served or software-supplied.
o: A major security failure by an autonomous tool could raise operator demand, while reliable vendor-native agents could lower it sharply.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include small and midsize businesses, larger enterprises, and public-sector organizations. Revenue can combine monthly managed-service subscriptions, per-user or per-device charges, fixed-fee bundles, disaster-recovery and backup retainers, incident-response fees, hourly consulting, installation projects, and software or hardware pass-throughs.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal IT units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, one-person practices without transferable operations, and firms whose primary activity belongs in custom programming, systems integration or design, computer facilities management, data processing or hosting, software publishing, training, repair, telecommunications, or hardware and software wholesaling. Product-resale-only operations and nonrecurring installation practices without repeat outsourced service are outside the lens.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms in NAICS 541519 that deliver recurring or repeat outsourced computer-related services to external customers, including computer disaster recovery, software or hardware installation, technical support, monitoring, backup, cybersecurity, domain-related services, and residual computer consulting that does not belong in programming, systems design, facilities management, hosting, publishing, training, repair, or distribution codes.

a.bridge: The source population is the broader NAICS 541500 occupational workforce, where computer and mathematical occupations represent 50.57% of employment and management represents 14.77%, combined with adjacent computer-support duties and measured AI effects in software-development and customer-support-like work. I translate those employment shares and task studies into a wage-weighted share for residual 541519, raising weight for relatively well-paid technical roles and discounting physical installation, high-stakes security response, customer communication, novel diagnosis, and work already automated through conventional IT tools. This may overstate exposure if 541519 contains more field installation and support than the broader group, but may understate it if the residual category concentrates remote support and automation-ready technical work.
caveats: BLS publishes the occupational mix at broader NAICS 541500, not exact NAICS 541519, and employment shares are not payroll shares.
The software-developer experiment and Claude usage data cover task populations that overlap only part of this residual service category.
The estimate explicitly discounts work already automated, but no exact-industry baseline separates conventional automation from remaining tasks.
The injected labor-intensity input combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and the specified IPS harmonization; that vintage mismatch is not direct evidence about task exposure.
a.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541519 payroll by detailed occupation, firm-level time studies by ticket category and installation workflow, and production measurements of technician hours eliminated rather than merely accelerated would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Ticket classification, documentation, knowledge retrieval, routine troubleshooting, scripting, monitoring review, patch workflows, reporting, and portions of coding and analysis are strongly exposed to generative AI and agents. The broader computer-services workforce is heavily technical, and randomized software-developer trials found higher completed-task output with an AI assistant. Exposure remains bounded because support specialists also diagnose ambiguous failures, repair or install equipment, train users, handle incidents, communicate with frustrated customers, and remain accountable for security and recovery outcomes.

d5.bridge: The source quantity is BLS projected real output for broader NAICS 541500, growing 3.2% annually from 2024 to 2034; five years of that rate compound to approximately 1.17, used as the base for the current 541519 service basket. I widen the range because exact 541519 mixes defensive cybersecurity and disaster recovery with vulnerable routine support, installation, consulting, and resale-related activity. The broader category may overstate exact-category growth if programming and systems design capture more AI demand, but may understate it if security, recovery, compliance, and small-business outsourcing expand faster.
caveats: BLS output is projected only at broader NAICS 541500 and includes custom programming, systems design, and facilities management excluded from 541519.
Vendor-survey revenue growth is self-reported, global, and reflects MSP service lines rather than constant-quality demand for the exact current basket.
Cybersecurity and AI can create new services while simultaneously automating legacy work, making category-level net demand unusually uncertain.
d5.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541519 constant-price revenue history and forecasts segmented into cybersecurity, backup and recovery, support, installation, consulting, and resale, plus provider backlog and churn data, would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: BLS expects strong output growth for computer systems design and related services, while the 2026 MSP survey reports revenue growth in cybersecurity and business continuity and disaster recovery. Digital complexity, security threats, compliance needs, cloud and AI adoption, and customer skill gaps support continued service demand. Routine support automation, client self-service, vendor-direct offerings, procurement pressure, and macroeconomic sensitivity create a nearly flat downside case.

caveats: The official definition and survey form identify included and neighboring activities but do not measure the eligible share of firms.
The residual category is unusually heterogeneous and classification practices may vary among similar providers.
The injected count of 705 firms is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% EBITDA margin, so its band assignment is uncertain even though it is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census or commercial database classifying the estimated lower-middle-market population by recurring-service revenue, customer concentration, owner dependence, primary NAICS accuracy, and share of product resale would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The exact code is service-oriented and explicitly includes disaster recovery and software installation, while the screen's repeat-service requirement admits managed support, backup, monitoring, cybersecurity, installation follow-on, and recurring technical consulting. Eligibility is reduced by project-only installers, product-pass-through-heavy firms, one-person consulting practices with key-person dependence, and establishments whose reported activities drift toward neighboring programming, design, hosting, repair, publishing, training, or distribution categories.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541519 ($19,543,540,823) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541519 ($42,029,844,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $279,051 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541519); class <5: 8,483 firms, avg revenue/firm $429,327 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $67,190 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 841 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,781,555 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $278,813 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 505 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,678,558 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $575,694 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 526 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,815,287 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,692,592 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 179 firms, avg revenue/firm $40,627,151 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,358,149 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 116 firms, avg revenue/firm $190,265,517 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,776,553 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 705

caveats: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share for NAICS 541519.
The category mixes physically grounded installation and recovery with remote services that may be more readily self-served or software-supplied.
A major security failure by an autonomous tool could raise operator demand, while reliable vendor-native agents could lower it sharply.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal data on fully self-resolved ticket share, vendor-direct displacement, client insourcing, autonomous remediation failure rates, cyber-insurance and compliance requirements, and revenue retained by human-accountable providers would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Automated chatbots, remote-management platforms, vendor self-service, and AI agents can eliminate some routine troubleshooting, monitoring review, documentation, patching, and installation guidance. BLS nevertheless describes complex diagnosis, physical setup and repair, user training, disaster-recovery maintenance, communication, and escalation, and it expects some small organizations to outsource network support rather than staff it internally. Security, recovery, compliance, and cross-vendor accountability preserve a substantial operator role even as the operator uses much more software.

q.bridge: The source population is a global MSP benchmark rather than all U.S. 541519 firms. The reported predominant billing mix includes 26% combining per-user and per-device pricing, 21% per-user, 14% fixed-fee value subscriptions, 13% per-device, 10% tiered bundles, 12% a la carte, and 3% incident response. I translate the large contracted and unit-priced share into above-half retention of implemented labor benefits, discounted for renewal repricing, competitive bids, service-level commitments, vendor-cost pass-through, falling deal sizes, and hourly or project work. This may overstate retention for government contractors and project installers, but may understate it for mature fixed-fee providers that keep productivity gains until repricing.
caveats: The billing survey is vendor-sponsored, global, and specific to MSPs rather than exact NAICS 541519.
Predominant billing model does not reveal revenue weights, contract duration, or post-automation repricing behavior.
Recent MSP evidence reports shrinking deal sizes and strong competition, which may erode retention faster than historical pricing data imply.
q.change_evidence: Exact-industry contract data linking billing model, renewal cadence, price changes, churn, service-level penalties, pass-through costs, and gross-margin changes after automation would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Per-user, per-device, subscription, and bundled contracts decouple revenue from technician hours in the short run, allowing automation to improve service capacity and margin. Over five years, customers can demand better service levels, benchmark prices, reduce supported seats or devices, switch providers, or negotiate at renewal; product and license costs also limit gross-benefit retention. The result is meaningful but incomplete commercial capture.

rho.bridge: The source populations are global managed service providers rather than the complete U.S. NAICS 541519 lower-middle-market population. Kaseya reports that 53% already use AI for ticketing, patching, and monitoring, while more than half have automated only about one-quarter of workload; MSP Global separately reports planned expansion and material integration, quality, staffing, and vendor-dependency challenges. I use current use as a readiness signal, not as realized coverage, and project deeper but incomplete workflow penetration over five years. Vendor-sponsored and technology-forward respondents may overstate implementation, while the category's technical workforce may implement faster than ordinary small businesses.
caveats: Both implementation sources survey MSPs globally and may not represent disaster-recovery, installation, or residual consulting firms classified in 541519.
Self-reported use and workload automation do not independently verify quality, durability, or labor-hour savings.
The respondents are reached through industry and vendor channels that may select for more technology-active firms.
rho.change_evidence: A representative U.S. NAICS 541519 survey measuring production deployment, percentage of tickets or technician hours automated, exception rates, security incidents, and realized headcount avoidance by firm size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Providers already use AI in core operational workflows and face hiring pressure that strengthens the incentive to scale without proportional headcount. Realization will still require standardized service catalogs, clean ticket and asset data, security controls, human approval paths, integration across PSA and RMM tools, customer consent, and reliable handling of edge cases. These constraints support a central case where most, but not all, exposed opportunity becomes operational.

s5.bridge: The source populations are U.S. employer-business owners across industries and global managed service provider transactions, not eligible NAICS 541519 firms. Gallup reports that 22% of employer owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; I haircut intentions for noncompletion, family or internal transfers, and firms lacking transferable operations, then offset part of the reduction for the active MSP acquisition market, where an advisory report counted 466 deals in 2025. The Gallup intention measure likely overstates qualifying closes, while global deal flow may overstate U.S. 541519 activity and also omits undisclosed small transactions.
caveats: The succession survey is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed qualifying control transfers.
The transaction report covers the global MSP market, a narrower business model that only partially overlaps NAICS 541519.
Deal-count definitions and small private transaction disclosure are not standardized.
The estimated 705-firm denominator inherits uncertainty from size-class receipts and the 15.65% margin bridge.
s5.change_evidence: Closed U.S. transaction counts mapped to NAICS 541519, a verified denominator of eligible independent firms, owner-age and succession-plan data, and broker-level outcomes for listed MSP and disaster-recovery firms would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: General employer-owner succession intentions put a meaningful transfer pool into the relevant horizon, and concentrated MSP deal activity shows that strategic and financial buyers are actively completing transactions. Not every planned transition becomes a qualifying control sale, and recurring revenue quality, customer retention, cybersecurity liability, owner dependence, and clean operating data determine whether a computer-services firm is actually transferable.

records.principal_driver: A large technical work share, contracted recurring pricing, and already-deployed automation in ticketing, patching, and monitoring create credible labor leverage.
records.principal_weakness: Residual-category heterogeneity and fast vendor-led automation make it difficult to know which firms retain differentiated, operator-required demand rather than commoditized support or pass-through revenue.

caveats: The labor-opportunity bridge combines broader NAICS 541500 employment shares with software-developer evidence and then converts them into a wage-weighted 541519 task share without exact-industry payroll or workflow data; the PROXY label and wide H range disclose, but do not resolve, that uncertainty.

The rho base projects 65% five-year realization from current adoption and implementation signals in global, vendor- or industry-channel samples. Those sources do not directly measure durable technician-hour elimination, exception rates, or U.S. lower-middle-market deployment.
S7's survey population is materially broader than pure MSPs and is heavily EMEA-weighted, so it is only partial support for a U.S. 541519 implementation bridge.

The q bridge observes predominant billing models, not revenue weights, renewal cadence, competitive pass-through, or retained automation economics. Because every registered q scenario exceeds the factor cap, C remains 10 throughout the envelope despite this unmeasured retention uncertainty.
The s5 bridge combines cross-industry five-year owner intentions with global MSP deal counts; neither source measures completed qualifying control transfers among eligible U.S. 541519 firms.

The demand factor combines a broader 541500 output forecast, vendor-reported MSP service-line growth, and an explicit operator-required-share estimate. Programming and systems-design activities excluded from the lens may account for a different share of broader-category growth than residual 541519 services.

The eligibility and operator-required shares are honest ESTIMATE inputs, but the residual code's broad classification and unknown mix of recurring managed services, project installation, consulting, and pass-through revenue make the base thesis strongly model-conditioned.

sources_audited.note: The Census NAICS page defines 541519 as other computer-related services, explicitly includes disaster recovery and software installation, and excludes programming, systems-design, and facilities-management services. It supports the boundary but does not measure eligibility or recurring-revenue prevalence.

The 2022 Economic Census form distinguishes 541519 computer-related consulting and installation from neighboring activities and enumerates technical support, recovery, security, domain, and consulting product lines, supporting the claimed heterogeneity and classification boundary.

The BLS NAICS 541500 table reports computer and mathematical occupations at 50.57% of employment and management occupations at 14.77%, exactly supporting the broader-industry inputs described in the bridge.

BLS describes diagnosis, documentation, equipment setup or repair, hardware and software installation, user training, automation pressure from chatbots, complex-case work, and outsourcing by smaller organizations.

Anthropic reports high automation-mode use in Claude Code and frequent human feedback or validation, supporting the directional software-work exposure claim while remaining limited to observed Claude interactions rather than 541519 production work.
The Management Science abstract reports three randomized field experiments involving 4,867 developers and a combined 26.08% increase in completed tasks with an AI coding assistant.

The report supports AI expansion plans, service customization, mixed delivery tooling, and integration, quality, staffing, and vendor-dependency challenges. Its 290-person worldwide member sample is broader than pure MSPs (only 45% identify their business type as MSP) and is 70% EMEA-focused, weakening representativeness for U.S. 541519.

Kaseya states that its 2026 report surveyed more than 1,000 MSPs worldwide, that 53% use AI for ticketing, patching, and monitoring, that more than half have automated only about one-quarter of workload, and that cybersecurity and disaster-recovery revenue grew for many respondents.

The Kaseya pricing page reports the cited predominant billing mix: 26% combined per-user/per-device, 21% per-user, 14% fixed-fee value subscription, 13% per-device, 12% a la carte, 10% tiered bundles, and 3% incident response.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years, and clearly identifies the measure as cross-industry owner intentions rather than completed qualifying sales.
The advisory PDF reports 466 global MSP deals in 2025, up about 20%, with $4.3 billion in disclosed value, supporting active MSP consolidation but not a U.S. 541519 transfer rate.
The BLS projection table reports a 3.2% annual real-output growth rate for broader NAICS 541500, supporting the stated five-year compounding proxy and its disclosed population mismatch.

caveats: a: The occupational distribution is for broader NAICS 541700 and includes biotechnology and social-science R&D outside this code.
a: Employment shares are not wage shares, although the estimate accounts directionally for different occupational wages.
a: Published AI research examples may select unusually data-rich or automation-friendly scientific problems.
a: The injected labor-intensity input combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and the specified IPS harmonization, so its vintage mismatch is not evidence of task exposure.
rho: The researcher survey spans countries, institutions, and scientific fields and measures use and perceptions rather than implemented labor savings.
rho: Autonomous-lab evidence demonstrates technical feasibility in specific materials workflows, not scalable economics across the industry.
rho: Regulated medical, defense, and safety-critical projects may require slower validation and approval than the central case.
e: No source measures the share of exact-industry firms that earn recurring or repeat external service revenue.
e: The code spans radically different scientific fields, capital requirements, customer types, and intellectual-property models.
e: The injected count of 1,716 firms is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% EBITDA margin and may misclassify research firms with unusually volatile or nonstandard margins.
s5: The succession source measures intentions across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: The M&A source combines CROs, CDMOs, and suppliers globally and overlaps only part of exact NAICS 541715.
s5: Asset purchases of patents, programs, or laboratories may not constitute qualifying transfers of an eligible operating firm.
s5: The estimated 1,716-firm denominator inherits uncertainty from SUSB size classes and the 15.65% margin bridge.
q: Federal contracting rules establish permissible and typical structures, not revenue weights for all 541715 firms.
q: Fortrea is a large global clinical CRO whose customer, scale, and regulatory profile differs from much of the exact industry.
q: No source directly measures commercial sharing of AI benefits in outsourced scientific R&D.
d5: BLS output is available at broader NAICS 541700 and employment at 541710, both including nanotechnology and biotechnology or adjacent R&D.
d5: NSF total R&D spending includes captive business, government, higher-education, and nonprofit performance rather than outsourced services only.
d5: Current-dollar and modeled spending estimates require care when inferring constant-price service quantity.
d5: Government funding, commercial research programs, and venture-backed customer demand can change abruptly.
o: No direct measure exists for the year-five operator-required share of exact-industry demand.
o: Computational R&D may become software-supplied faster than wet-lab, field, hardware, agricultural, or safety-critical research.
o: The accountable entity could shift from a lower-middle-market provider to a platform, equipment vendor, hyperscaler, or customer-owned laboratory.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include manufacturers, pharmaceutical and medical-device companies, aerospace and defense organizations, technology companies, agriculture and food businesses, and federal or other public agencies. Revenue may come from cost-reimbursement and cost-plus contracts, fixed-price or milestone projects, fee-for-service and per-unit work, time and materials, full-time-equivalent arrangements, multi-year programs, and reimbursed pass-through costs.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive manufacturer or corporate R&D units, firms principally developing their own intellectual property or products rather than serving external customers, shells, non-control financing vehicles, nontransferable owner-only practices, nanotechnology and biotechnology R&D, routine analytical testing, engineering design, prototype production, and social-science or humanities research.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market firms in NAICS 541715 that provide recurring or repeat outsourced research and experimental-development services to external commercial or government customers in physical, engineering, agricultural, environmental, computer, chemical, food, geological, health, medical, pharmaceutical, robotics, aerospace, and other covered sciences.

a.bridge: The source population is broader NAICS 541700, where management is 20.28% of employment, business and financial operations 12.14%, computer and mathematical work 14.29%, architecture and engineering 12.29%, and life, physical, and social science work 23.16%. I map those employment shares and their wage levels to task exposure in 541715, assigning high exposure to literature review, search, coding, modeling, data analysis, documentation, project administration, and candidate selection, while discounting laboratory setup, specimen and equipment handling, fieldwork, safety, physical prototyping, validation, and work already automated. This may overstate exact-industry exposure because the broader source includes less experimental social-science R&D, but may understate it because 541715 includes AI, computer, engineering, and data-intensive research and the estimate is wage-weighted rather than headcount-weighted.
caveats: The occupational distribution is for broader NAICS 541700 and includes biotechnology and social-science R&D outside this code.
Employment shares are not wage shares, although the estimate accounts directionally for different occupational wages.
Published AI research examples may select unusually data-rich or automation-friendly scientific problems.
The injected labor-intensity input combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and the specified IPS harmonization, so its vintage mismatch is not evidence of task exposure.
a.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541715 payroll by detailed occupation, time-use studies separating computational and documentation work from physical experiments, and audited before-and-after labor hours for deployed AI and laboratory automation would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The industry concentrates highly paid computational, engineering, scientific, managerial, and project work, much of which contains information-intensive subtasks accessible to AI. Researcher surveys show broad use, bibliometric evidence associates AI-augmented work with materially higher output, and autonomous-lab demonstrations extend exposure into experiment selection and execution. The remaining share reflects the irreducibly physical, safety-critical, tacit, novel, and validation-heavy work that makes experimental R&D different from pure desk research.

d5.bridge: The source quantities are BLS projections for broader NAICS 541700 and 541710 and NSF measures of total U.S. R&D, not constant-quality outsourced demand for 541715. BLS projects broader scientific R&D real output at 2.0% annually and physical, engineering, and life-science R&D employment growth of 8.7% over the decade; compounding the output rate for five years gives approximately 1.10. I use that as the base and widen for business-funded R&D expansion, flat or declining agency budgets, field mix, customer capital cycles, and possible AI-driven self-performance. Broader measures may overstate outsourced growth if captive R&D captures spending, but may understate specialized external demand when sponsors outsource scarce capabilities.
caveats: BLS output is available at broader NAICS 541700 and employment at 541710, both including nanotechnology and biotechnology or adjacent R&D.
NSF total R&D spending includes captive business, government, higher-education, and nonprofit performance rather than outsourced services only.
Current-dollar and modeled spending estimates require care when inferring constant-price service quantity.
Government funding, commercial research programs, and venture-backed customer demand can change abruptly.
d5.change_evidence: Exact NAICS 541715 constant-price revenue history and forecast, external-versus-captive R&D spending, government contract backlog, sponsor cancellations, and field-level outsourcing budgets would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: U.S. R&D spending remains large and growing, with business funding leading the recent increase, while BLS expects continued scientific R&D expansion. Demand is supported by defense, aerospace, electronics, medicine, energy, agriculture, materials, robotics, and environmental innovation. A downside remains plausible because federal obligations are uneven, sponsor programs can be canceled, capital markets affect research customers, and AI may let customers conduct more analysis internally.

caveats: No source measures the share of exact-industry firms that earn recurring or repeat external service revenue.
The code spans radically different scientific fields, capital requirements, customer types, and intellectual-property models.
The injected count of 1,716 firms is margin-bridged from SUSB size classes using a 15.65% EBITDA margin and may misclassify research firms with unusually volatile or nonstandard margins.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Census or commercial data identifying external-customer revenue, sponsor concentration, contract backlog, captive ownership, proprietary-product R&D, nonprofit status, and transferable operating assets for the estimated lower-middle-market population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition covers establishments conducting R&D, not only external contract-research providers. The Census survey form explicitly includes manufacturer R&D laboratories supporting their own manufacturing establishments, and the industry also contains firms developing proprietary intellectual property or products for themselves. The eligible subset consists of independent organizations with repeat external sponsor work, transferable teams and facilities, and an outsourced service model rather than captive or speculative proprietary R&D.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No direct measure exists for the year-five operator-required share of exact-industry demand.
Computational R&D may become software-supplied faster than wet-lab, field, hardware, agricultural, or safety-critical research.
The accountable entity could shift from a lower-middle-market provider to a platform, equipment vendor, hyperscaler, or customer-owned laboratory.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal data on sponsor insourcing, fully autonomous project share, software-only substitution, autonomous-lab reliability, regulatory acceptance, and revenue retained by human-accountable contract-research organizations would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: AI can self-serve literature search, computational screening, code, analysis, writing, and some experiment selection, and robotics can automate bounded laboratory loops. Most outsourced R&D still requires an accountable organization to own scientific protocols, instruments, facilities, safety, quality systems, physical samples, regulatory or government compliance, reproducibility, sponsor communication, intellectual-property controls, and expert validation. Even advanced autonomous laboratories combine specialized hardware, curated data, domain knowledge, maintenance, and human-designed objectives rather than eliminating the operator.

q.bridge: The source quantities are federal R&D contracting rules and a global clinical CRO's disclosed contract structures rather than an exact-industry billing distribution. Federal rules say uncertain R&D specifications usually make cost reimbursement appropriate, while the CRO reports fixed-price, fee-for-service, volume, time-and-materials, and scope-adjusted arrangements. I map cost-reimbursable and time-based work to low retention, fixed-price and per-unit work to higher short-run retention, and then discount all structures for renewal repricing, competitive bidding, customer ownership of results, scope changes, and pass-through costs. Government-heavy providers may retain less than the estimate, while differentiated fixed-price labs with scarce facilities may retain more.
caveats: Federal contracting rules establish permissible and typical structures, not revenue weights for all 541715 firms.
Fortrea is a large global clinical CRO whose customer, scale, and regulatory profile differs from much of the exact industry.
No source directly measures commercial sharing of AI benefits in outsourced scientific R&D.
q.change_evidence: Exact-industry contract-level revenue weights by cost reimbursement, cost plus, fixed price, milestone, per-unit, full-time-equivalent, and time and materials, linked to renewal repricing and post-automation gross margin, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: R&D uncertainty often ties customer payment to cost, effort, or scope, directly sharing labor savings with sponsors. Fixed-price, milestone, and per-unit contracts can retain automation benefits through lower delivery cost or greater capacity, but customers can renegotiate at renewal and sophisticated sponsors compare bids. The mixed contract architecture therefore supports retention below one-half in the central case, with meaningful variation by field and customer.

rho.bridge: The source population is a global survey of academic and corporate researchers plus published autonomous-materials laboratories, not representative U.S. lower-middle-market 541715 firms. The survey reports 84% overall AI use but only 62% use for research or publication tasks and 25% use of specialized research assistants; corporate researchers have better organizational access, while researchers report hallucination, privacy, guidance, and training concerns. I translate broad current use into substantial five-year implementation of exposed tasks, but discount for shallow general-purpose usage, validation requirements, integration with scientific instruments, reproducibility, safety, data rights, and capital needs. Survey respondents may overstate readiness, whereas technology-native contract labs may move faster than the average researcher.
caveats: The researcher survey spans countries, institutions, and scientific fields and measures use and perceptions rather than implemented labor savings.
Autonomous-lab evidence demonstrates technical feasibility in specific materials workflows, not scalable economics across the industry.
Regulated medical, defense, and safety-critical projects may require slower validation and approval than the central case.
rho.change_evidence: A representative U.S. 541715 survey measuring production AI workflow coverage, autonomous-instrument utilization, validation failure rates, capital expenditure, approved-use policies, and verified labor-hour avoidance by scientific field and firm size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Research organizations have strong incentives to shorten search, analysis, reporting, and experiment cycles, and corporate researchers have comparatively good access to approved tools. Implementation will remain uneven because specialized scientific models and autonomous equipment are far less common than general chat tools, and robust laboratory automation must manage stochastic and epistemic errors, interoperability, reproducibility, and physical failure modes.

s5.bridge: The source populations are U.S. employer-business owners across all industries and life-sciences CRO, CDMO, and supplier transactions, not eligible NAICS 541715 service firms. Gallup reports that 22% of employer owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; I reduce that for noncompletion, family or internal transfers, nonprofits, institution-owned labs, and research businesses whose value is not transferable without founders or sponsors. Active life-sciences services dealmaking partly offsets the haircut, although its concentration in CRO and CDMO assets may overstate activity in engineering, physical-science, agricultural, and environmental R&D.
caveats: The succession source measures intentions across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers.
The M&A source combines CROs, CDMOs, and suppliers globally and overlaps only part of exact NAICS 541715.
Asset purchases of patents, programs, or laboratories may not constitute qualifying transfers of an eligible operating firm.
The estimated 1,716-firm denominator inherits uncertainty from SUSB size classes and the 15.65% margin bridge.
s5.change_evidence: Closed U.S. transactions mapped to NAICS 541715, owner-age and succession data, a verified denominator of eligible independent firms, and broker or database outcomes segmented by contract-research field would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner succession creates a general transfer pool, and outsourced life-sciences services continue to attract strategic and financial buyers despite a slower 2025 market. Qualifying transfers are constrained by founder dependence, customer and grant concentration, intellectual-property ownership, security clearances, specialized facilities, scientific key-person risk, and institutional or nonprofit control structures.

records.principal_driver: AI addresses high-cost computational, analytical, documentation, and experiment-selection work while specialized facilities and expertise preserve outsourced delivery needs.
records.principal_weakness: A large share of the NAICS universe may be captive or proprietary R&D, and cost-based contracting can pass labor savings to sophisticated sponsors.

caveats: S1's cited URL is reachable but its rendered page does not expose the claimed 541715 definition; S2 supplies the relevant exact-industry scope, making this a non-material citation defect.

The a bridge starts from correctly quoted broader-industry occupation shares but does not show reproducible occupation-level task-exposure weights or a full wage-weight calculation; the broad-code and directional nature of the mapping are disclosed.

The rho sources establish researcher adoption, perceptions, and bounded autonomous-lab feasibility rather than verified production labor-hour implementation in U.S. lower-middle-market contract-research firms; the five-year base remains a wide, honestly labeled proxy.

No cited source measures the eligible external-service share e. The 0.48 base is an explicit estimate, and the resulting MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action correctly prevent it from being presented as observed target availability.

The s5 bridge reduces a 22% cross-industry five-year sale-or-transfer intention only modestly to a 20% qualifying-transfer base despite noncompletion and internal-transfer exclusions, while S11 reports sharply weaker CRO/CDMO dealmaking in 2025. This likely biases s5 upward, but a correction large enough to be plausible from these sources does not establish a change in the base PRIORITY tier or the already restricted action.

The q sources demonstrate available contract forms but provide neither exact-industry revenue weights nor observed sharing of AI savings, so the 0.47 retention base is judgment-heavy despite its appropriate PROXY label and broad range.

The d5 bridge uses broader-industry output and total national R&D, while o is an unsupported bounded estimate; possible AI-driven customer self-performance also appears in both discussions, creating some conservative overlap in downside framing without changing the base conclusion.

sources_audited.note: The cited parameterized Census URL reopened to a generic NAICS search page whose rendered text did not contain 541715 or its definition. Exact-industry scope is independently supported by S2, so this source-level defect does not change any primitive's already honest evidence state, the base tier, or the action.

The Census survey form expressly lists 541715 physical/engineering and life-science research, including manufacturer R&D laboratories supporting their own manufacturing establishments, and distinguishes adjacent nanotechnology, biotechnology, social-science, engineering-design, prototype, and IP activities.

The BLS NAICS 541700 table reports the quoted employment shares and wages for management, business/financial, computer/mathematical, architecture/engineering, and life/physical/social-science occupations.

The Wiley release reports worldwide researcher AI use of 84%, research/publication-task use of 62%, research-assistant use of 25%, stronger corporate access, and the stated accuracy, privacy, guidance, and training barriers.

The Nature paper describes an autonomous materials laboratory integrating robotics, databases, machine learning, text-mined literature heuristics, active learning, synthesis, characterization, and explicit failure modes.

The Nature Reviews Materials article directly states that automation will not easily remove humans and identifies robust operation, epistemic and stochastic error handling, reproducibility, reconfigurability, and interoperability as requirements.

The Nature article reports observational associations between identified AI-augmented natural-science research and higher paper and citation output, along with a narrowing of collective scientific focus; the packet appropriately describes these as associations.
FAR 35.006 says uncertain R&D specifications and costs usually make cost-reimbursement appropriate, while permitting fixed-price arrangements for sufficiently defined or limited work.

Fortrea's filing documents an outsourced clinical-development customer model, long-term work, fixed-price and fee-for-service arrangements, transaction-volume and time-and-materials pricing, scope adjustments, pass-through costs, cancellations, and customer funding risk.

Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years; it measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers, as disclosed in the packet.

The Deloitte report supports a broad life-sciences transaction market and buyer selectivity, but it also says the CRO/CDMO subsector stalled in 2025 and its deal value fell by more than 70%; it is therefore only a directional, partial signal for eligible 541715 service-firm availability.

The BLS projection table reports 2.0% annual real-output growth for broader NAICS 541700 and 8.7% decade employment growth for 541710, matching the demand bridge and its disclosed broader-code limitations.

NCSES reports $937 billion of U.S. R&D in 2023, an estimated $993 billion in 2024, inflation-adjusted totals, and that most of the 2023-to-2024 increase came from business funding; it also labels recent values preliminary or estimated.

records.title: Research and Development in the Physical, Engineering, and Life Sciences (except Nanotechnology and Biotechnology)

caveats: a: The only public industry occupation table is for broader management, scientific, and technical consulting and includes work unlike administrative management consulting.
a: The BCG experiments involve a large, highly selective global consultancy and bounded tasks, not end-to-end lower-middle-market engagements.
a: The supplied labor ratio combines more recent payroll with older receipts and may overstate or understate the current labor intensity paired with this task share.
rho: Reported tool use is not the same as implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: Experimental productivity on bounded tasks may not persist across discovery, diagnosis, recommendation, and implementation.
rho: Client data restrictions and errors that look persuasive can constrain implementation more than raw model capability.
e: No source measures eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
e: Repeat projects are not contractually recurring and may disappear when a principal leaves.
e: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged from older Census size classes and may include firms unlike today's normalized EBITDA population.
s5: Gallup measures owner intentions across industries, not completed consulting-firm transfers.
s5: Equiteq covers specialty consulting globally and does not provide the denominator required for a probability.
s5: A sale can destroy client and employee value if relationships are concentrated in the departing founder.
q: No public source measures the national contract mix or retained AI benefit for NAICS 541611.
q: Public-sector contract structures may differ materially from private consulting engagements.
q: Released capacity creates value only when firms can sell additional work or avoid hiring without impairing quality.
d5: Occupational employment includes internal analysts and adjacent consulting industries and is not service quantity.
d5: The outlook predates much of the latest AI-enabled client self-service and productivity change.
d5: General strategy and transformation spending is cyclical, while regulated and implementation-heavy work is more durable.
o: Management consulting has no universal licensing or statutory sign-off requirement that guarantees operator demand.
o: Routine generalist work is more exposed to client self-service than specialized, political, or implementation-heavy engagements.
o: The operator share depends on client trust, proprietary knowledge, and willingness to accept AI-generated advice, all of which can change quickly.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External clients buy expert capacity, independent judgment, analysis, recommendations, and change support through projects, task orders, retainers, or recurring managed advisory. Pricing may be hourly or time-and-materials, fixed price, cost reimbursement, performance based, or a hybrid; repeat revenue usually depends on relationships, specialization, and continuing transformation needs rather than mandatory subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Internal corporate strategy teams, captive shared-service units, software-only products, staffing without substantive management advice, one-person practices without a transferable operating organization, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing repeat outsourced advice and implementation assistance on strategy, organization, financial planning and budgeting, records and office management, site selection, new-business formation, operational improvement, and related administrative or general management issues.

a.bridge: BLS reports the occupation mix for broader NAICS 541600, not wage-weighted tasks for NAICS 541611, and the consulting experiment studies large-firm BCG consultants rather than lower-middle-market firms. I mapped research, data analysis, modeling, drafting, presentation production, project administration, knowledge retrieval, and first-pass recommendations into exposure; applied lower exposure to interviews, stakeholder persuasion, executive judgment, sales, and implementation; and excluded automation already embedded in templates and conventional analytics. The broader industry includes scientific and environmental field roles, which likely understates exposure for administrative management consulting, while selected experimental tasks and elite-firm workflows likely overstate exposure across the full current service basket.
caveats: The only public industry occupation table is for broader management, scientific, and technical consulting and includes work unlike administrative management consulting.
The BCG experiments involve a large, highly selective global consultancy and bounded tasks, not end-to-end lower-middle-market engagements.
The supplied labor ratio combines more recent payroll with older receipts and may overstate or understate the current labor intensity paired with this task share.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-motion study of NAICS 541611 firms that joins payroll by role to task frequencies, current automation, client-facing time, and validated AI feasibility would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Broader consulting employment is concentrated in management, business and financial operations, computer, market research, project management, sales, and administrative occupations. Management analysts gather information, analyze financial and operational data, develop alternatives, and present recommendations, all of which contain substantial digital work. In a randomized experiment, BCG consultants using GPT completed suitable knowledge tasks faster and completed more of them, but AI also reduced correctness on a task beyond its capability frontier. The task mix therefore supports high but not comprehensive current exposure. [S2, S3, S4, S5]

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for the management-analyst occupation across all industries, not constant-price service quantity for NAICS 541611, while Equiteq describes specialty-consulting market conditions and buyer preferences. I translate the positive occupational outlook and stated consulting demand into five-year service quantity, allow productivity to separate output from employment, and widen for economic cycles and discretionary project deferral. Employment growth can understate quantity when AI raises output per worker, while BLS's cross-industry occupation and Equiteq's transaction-market lens can overstate demand for the frozen general-consulting service basket.
caveats: Occupational employment includes internal analysts and adjacent consulting industries and is not service quantity.
The outlook predates much of the latest AI-enabled client self-service and productivity change.
General strategy and transformation spending is cyclical, while regulated and implementation-heavy work is more durable.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census revenue for NAICS 541611, combined with representative firm backlogs, proposal conversion, utilization, client budgets, and service-line mix, would materially change the year-five quantity ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS projects management-analyst employment growth of 9 percent from 2024 to 2034 and expects organizations to seek consulting to improve efficiency and control costs, with especially strong growth at smaller specialist firms. Demand also benefits from transformation and operational complexity. The downside is meaningful because generalist consulting is discretionary, clients can defer projects in weak economies, and Equiteq reports a more selective market with better resilience in regulated and specialized work. [S3, S10]

caveats: No source measures eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
Repeat projects are not contractually recurring and may disappear when a principal leaves.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged from older Census size classes and may include firms unlike today's normalized EBITDA population.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence on the supplied population showing repeat-client revenue, customer concentration, contract duration, delivery-team depth, founder dependence, and transferability would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently an external advisory business and the supplied firm count is already sized to established lower-middle-market operations, so most firms should satisfy the outsourced-service condition. Management analysts commonly work for clients on contract, and consulting relationships often generate repeat projects or task orders. I exclude practices whose revenue is isolated, founder-personal, nonrepeat, captive, or inseparable from an individual rainmaker, as well as software-only models. [S1, S3, S11]

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541611 ($114,205,171,465) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541611 ($205,640,681,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $260,722 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541611); class <5: 79,966 firms, avg revenue/firm $344,991 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $53,991 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,880 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,598,754 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $250,205 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,308 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,199,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $500,710 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,992 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,214,358 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,442,047 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 861 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,354,620 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,063,498 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 507 firms, avg revenue/firm $202,449,895 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $31,683,409 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 3,853

o.bridge: BLS describes management-analyst duties, the experiments measure AI-assisted consultant performance, and Anthropic surveys AI users rather than measuring the share of consulting demand requiring an external lower-middle-market operator. I map interviews, contextual diagnosis, independent challenge, stakeholder alignment, accountable recommendation, sales, and change implementation to operator-required quantity, and map generic research, synthesis, drafting, and routine analysis toward client self-service or software supply. BLS may overstate operator need because it observes the existing occupation, while AI-user and elite-consultant evidence may overstate self-service because users are selected and experiments omit full client engagements.
caveats: Management consulting has no universal licensing or statutory sign-off requirement that guarantees operator demand.
Routine generalist work is more exposed to client self-service than specialized, political, or implementation-heavy engagements.
The operator share depends on client trust, proprietary knowledge, and willingness to accept AI-generated advice, all of which can change quickly.
o.change_evidence: A revenue-weighted client study that tracks which current consulting scopes were renewed with human firms, internalized with AI, bought as software, or eliminated, split by task and client size, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Clients can increasingly self-serve generic research, summaries, benchmarks, presentations, and preliminary analysis. However, BLS defines the work around gathering organization-specific evidence, interviewing personnel, developing alternatives, presenting recommendations, and checking whether changes work. The experiments show both strong assistance and persuasive errors, while Anthropic's management respondents identify judgment and management as capabilities AI lacks. This leaves a substantial but reduced role for accountable consulting operators. [S3, S4, S5, S8]

q.bridge: Equiteq describes time-and-materials as the consulting industry's pricing foundation, BLS notes that self-employed analysts are commonly paid by the hour or project, and GSA documents multiple contract types for a federal vehicle that includes NAICS 541611. I assign low retention to hourly, labor-hour, and cost-reimbursement work; higher initial retention to fixed-price, retainer, and performance-based work; and then discount for rebids, renewal repricing, client scrutiny, scope expansion, and competitor adoption. The sources do not measure revenue weights: federal contracting may overstate formal pass-through, while the specialty-consulting report may overstate industry movement toward platforms and recurring services.
caveats: No public source measures the national contract mix or retained AI benefit for NAICS 541611.
Public-sector contract structures may differ materially from private consulting engagements.
Released capacity creates value only when firms can sell additional work or avoid hiring without impairing quality.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract audit showing revenue by pricing model, renewal cadence, utilization treatment, post-AI scope and rate changes, realized project margins, and client concentration would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Hourly and time-and-materials engagements directly expose fewer delivery hours, and federal rules explicitly note that such contracts provide no positive profit incentive for labor efficiency. Fixed-price projects can preserve savings through the contract term, while recurring managed advisory and outcome pricing can retain more value. Equiteq also reports that AI-compressed timelines are making hourly billing harder to defend, so competition and client expectations should dissipate a substantial share of benefits within five years. [S3, S10, S11, S12]

rho.bridge: Gallup measures AI use across US employees and organizations, while OECD surveys mostly medium and large AI-adopting firms in manufacturing and information technology and the experiments cover BCG consultants. I translate current use, demonstrated task gains, and the industry's digital work into five-year implementation of the exposed opportunity, then discount for client confidentiality, hallucination risk, weak workflow redesign, inconsistent proprietary data, review requirements, and smaller-firm change capacity. General workforce adoption likely understates consulting uptake, while large-enterprise and BCG evidence likely overstates lower-middle-market governance and integration capacity.
caveats: Reported tool use is not the same as implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
Experimental productivity on bounded tasks may not persist across discovery, diagnosis, recommendation, and implementation.
Client data restrictions and errors that look persuasive can constrain implementation more than raw model capability.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of lower-middle-market NAICS 541611 firms measuring production deployment, task coverage, accepted-output rates, labor hours, rework, client restrictions, and workflow redesign would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Consulting work is already digital and the experiment shows that trained consultants can realize immediate gains on suitable tasks. US workplace AI use is widespread, but Gallup finds that workflow transformation lags individual productivity, and OECD reports lower adoption by smaller firms plus persistent skills and return-on-investment barriers. The five-year range assumes copilots become routine in research, analysis, drafting, and administration while high-stakes recommendations retain structured human review. [S4, S5, S6, S7]

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year ownership plans among US employer-business owners across industries, and Equiteq counts broader global specialty-consulting deals without an eligible-firm denominator. I use the employer-owner transfer intention as an anchor, check that consulting has an active transaction market, and discount gifts, internal succession, non-control recapitalizations, plans that never close, firms outside the frozen size band, and owner-dependent practices that wind down. Gallup's inclusion of all employer sizes and planned rather than completed transfers likely biases the anchor upward; unannounced private consulting transactions in Equiteq's data may bias visible deal activity downward.
caveats: Gallup measures owner intentions across industries, not completed consulting-firm transfers.
Equiteq covers specialty consulting globally and does not provide the denominator required for a probability.
A sale can destroy client and employee value if relationships are concentrated in the departing founder.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year NAICS 541611 transaction census linking announced and private control changes to the eligible firm denominator, with seller EBITDA, buyer type, ownership percentage, and internal versus external succession, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while specialty-consulting deal activity remained substantial but became more selective. Eligible firms in this EBITDA band should be more transferable than microbusinesses, yet management consulting is unusually dependent on senior relationships and expertise. The base is below the stated general employer transfer intention because only completed qualifying control transfers count. [S9, S10]

records.principal_driver: Digitally produced analysis and deliverables account for a large share of high-cost work, and controlled experiments show that suitable consulting tasks can be completed materially faster with AI.
records.principal_weakness: Hourly economics and easy client self-service can pass productivity gains away, while the relationship-heavy remainder is difficult to automate and fragile in a control transfer.

caveats: S1's cited Census URL was reachable but did not render the claimed NAICS 541611 definition, leaving that citation unsupported as reopened; e is already an ESTIMATE and the correction does not change the base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

S11 was unreachable during repeated audit attempts, so the claimed GSA contract-pool and contract-type details remain unverified; S3, S10, and S12 still support the pricing mechanism, and removing S11 does not change the base tier or action.

The 0.52 exposure share and 0.62 implementation share are judgmental mappings from broader occupation, large-firm experiment, general-workforce, and mostly medium/large adopting-firm evidence rather than measurements of lower-middle-market NAICS 541611 firms.

The 0.42 retention share is not based on a revenue-weighted national contract-mix study; federal contract structures and an M&A advisor's global specialty-consulting observations are broad proxies for private US LMM consulting economics.

The 1.06 demand ratio and 0.59 operator-required share translate occupational growth, current duties, experimental results, and selected AI-user perceptions into service-quantity constructs that none of the sources measures directly.

The attainable tier interval holds the observed labor ratio and estimated firm-count anchor fixed, so it does not capture uncertainty from the wage/receipts vintage mismatch or the margin-bridged firm population.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census page loaded, but its rendered search results did not display the 541611 title or definition, so the claimed definition was not verifiable from the cited page during this audit.

The BLS NAICS 541600 table reports the cited broader-industry occupation shares, including 16.21% management, 33.12% business and financial operations, 10.92% computer and mathematical, and 14.98% office support.

BLS supports the cited management-analyst duties, contractual consulting context, hourly or project payment for self-employed analysts, 9% 2024-2034 outlook, and stronger expected growth for smaller specialist consultancies.

The randomized BCG experiment reports 12.2% more tasks completed, 25.1% faster completion on in-frontier tasks, improved quality, and a 19-percentage-point reduction in correct solutions on an out-of-frontier task.

BCG describes a controlled study completed by 480 consultants on coding, statistical understanding, and predictive modeling, documents strong task gains, and reports goal-misunderstanding and over-reliance risks; it also explicitly excludes a full end-to-end workflow.
Gallup reports the 23,717-employee survey, 50% occasional workplace AI use, 28% frequent use, reported productivity gains, and limited evidence of fundamental organizational work redesign.

The OECD report supports lower adoption by small firms, low use in core processes among small enterprises, and skills, readiness, use-case, cost, and ROI barriers; the packet appropriately discloses the survey's medium/large adopter emphasis.

Anthropic reports that respondents, especially experienced respondents, frequently identify judgment, management, contextual reasoning, trust, and interpersonal work as AI limitations and explicitly says the survey is not population-representative.
Gallup reports a fall 2024 survey based on 1,264 owners and shows that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years, while 5% planned closure.

The Equiteq report supports 166 H2 2025 transactions, more selective buyer demand, uneven AI adoption, time-and-materials as the industry's pricing foundation, and pressure on hourly billing as AI compresses delivery timelines.
The cited GSA PDF returned an internal retrieval error on repeated attempts, so its claims about NAICS 541611 pool inclusion and contract-type coverage could not be verified.
FAR Subpart 16.6 defines time-and-materials and labor-hour contracts and states that a time-and-materials contract provides no positive profit incentive for cost control or labor efficiency.

run_meta.title: Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services

sources.supports: Defines the industry around operating advice and assistance on administrative and general management issues such as financial planning, records, organizational planning, site selection, start-up, and process improvement.
sources.title: NAICS 541611 Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services

sources.supports: Reports the broader consulting occupation mix, including management occupations at 16.21 percent of employment, business and financial operations at 33.12 percent, computer and mathematical occupations at 10.92 percent, and office support at 14.98 percent.
sources.title: Management, Scientific, and Technical Consulting Services Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates

sources.supports: Describes information gathering, interviews, data analysis, solution development, recommendations, presentations, and implementation follow-up; reports 9 percent projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and common hourly or project payment for self-employed analysts.
sources.title: Management Analysts

sources.supports: Randomized BCG-consultant evidence showing more completed work and faster performance on suitable tasks, alongside significantly lower correctness on a strategic task outside the AI capability frontier.
sources.title: Navigating the Jagged Technological Frontier: Field Experimental Evidence of the Effects of Artificial Intelligence on Knowledge Worker Productivity and Quality

sources.supports: Reports a controlled experiment with 480 BCG consultants on coding, statistical, and predictive tasks, finding strong gains with AI on some tasks and risks from misunderstanding goals or inadequate supervision.
sources.title: GenAI Increases Productivity and Expands Capabilities

sources.supports: A representative survey of 23,717 US employees found half used AI at work at least occasionally, 28 percent used it frequently, and workflow transformation remained limited despite commonly reported individual productivity gains.
sources.title: Rising AI Adoption Spurs Workforce Changes

sources.supports: Synthesizes firm-adoption evidence, finding generally low and uneven diffusion, lower use among small firms, weak use in core business processes, and constraints involving skills and uncertain returns.
sources.title: The Adoption of Artificial Intelligence in Firms

sources.supports: Reports observed AI-use and survey evidence from Claude users, including that management respondents were overrepresented but often identified judgment and management as capabilities AI lacks; explicitly warns the survey is not population-representative.
sources.title: Anthropic Economic Index Report: Cadences

sources.supports: A fall 2024 survey of 1,264 US business owners found that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and 5 percent planned to close.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports 166 specialty-consulting transactions in the second half of 2025, describes more selective buyer demand, identifies time-and-materials as the pricing foundation, and discusses uneven AI adoption and pressure on hourly economics.
sources.title: Management Consulting M&A Activity: Second Half 2025

sources.supports: Places NAICS 541611 in a federal management-consulting contract pool and documents fixed-price, cost-reimbursement, time-and-materials, labor-hour, hybrid, incentive, and performance-based task-order structures.
sources.title: Human Capital and Training Solutions Ordering Guide Summary

sources.supports: Defines time-and-materials and labor-hour contracting and states that time-and-materials contracts provide no positive profit incentive for contractor cost control or labor efficiency.
sources.title: Federal Acquisition Regulation Subpart 16.6

records.title: Administrative Management and General Management Consulting Services
