d5: Occupation growth includes in-house PR staff and workers outside agencies.
d5: IPRN is a global member network rather than a representative US LMM sample.
d5: The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-visibility services.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of PR service demand.
o: Agency survey statements about human judgment and strategic trust may reflect provider optimism.
o: Rapid improvement in autonomous monitoring, content generation, journalist targeting, sentiment analysis, and crisis simulation could push the value below the range.
o: Lobbying and political consulting are more operator-dependent than routine publicity, widening intra-code variation.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Businesses, institutions, nonprofits, public bodies, political interests, and individuals buy ongoing counsel and campaign execution through monthly or annual retainers, hourly fees against budgets, fixed project fees, and hybrids. Repeatability is strongest in corporate reputation, media relations, public affairs, crisis preparedness, executive positioning, and integrated communications.
lens.excluded_activities: Advertising agencies, media buying and representation, direct-mail and display advertising, standalone market-research firms, software vendors, captive in-house communications teams, shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo practitioners below the LMM band, and firms limited to isolated nonrepeat publicity projects.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market public relations agencies providing recurring or repeat outsourced communications campaigns, media relations, reputation and crisis counsel, public affairs, lobbying, political consulting, executive communications, stakeholder engagement, and related PR consulting to external clients.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541800, not six-digit 541820, and O*NET describes representative PR occupations rather than every agency role. I map high exposure to brainstorming, research, media monitoring, first drafts, editing, releases, speeches, social posts, reporting, lists, and routine administration; I map lower exposure to relationship building, executive coaching, live media response, crisis judgment, lobbying, negotiation, and final reputational accountability. The broader industry contains more advertising, design, media-buying, and distribution work, so it may overstate visual-production exposure while understating 541820's concentration in PR specialists and senior counsel.
caveats: The occupation mix is for NAICS 541800 and includes advertising and related services with different workflows.
O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or existing automation.
The Muck Rack survey reports individual tool use rather than the share of agency labor technically substitutable.
The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may be distorted where agency receipts include third-party campaign or event pass-through.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541820 payroll occupation table combined with agency time-and-motion data by seniority, workflow, wage, and current AI use would materially change the wage-weighted estimate.
a.rationale: PR agencies perform large volumes of language, research, monitoring, and document work, and the reported use cases show AI already fits those workflows. Public relations specialists also spend substantial time maintaining external relationships, advising management, coaching spokespeople, and responding under time pressure, while managers own crisis plans and trusted relationships. Those human-intensive duties hold the current not-yet-automated exposed share near half at base.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 541800 to grow 2.7% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 1.14 over five years, while PR-specialist employment grows 5% over the decade. I center below the broad industry output implication because 541800 includes advertising, media buying, and related services, while retaining modest growth due to continuing reputation, outreach, social-media, crisis, and stakeholder needs. IPRN's global member-agency optimism supports direction but likely overstates typical US LMM conditions.
caveats: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541800, not public relations agencies alone.
Occupation growth includes in-house PR staff and workers outside agencies.
IPRN is a global member network rather than a representative US LMM sample.
The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-visibility services.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit BLS or Census real-output forecast for 541820, supported by inflation-adjusted US PR-agency billings, client retention, backlog, and budget surveys by end market, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Fast-moving public opinion, fragmented media, social platforms, crises, political and regulatory complexity, and reputation management sustain demand for the current service basket. PR budgets remain discretionary and cyclical, traditional media access is harder, and clients may reduce or postpone programs in downturns. AI self-service is reserved for the operator-required share rather than deducted again from demand quantity.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin counsel boutiques or lower-margin execution-heavy agencies into the EBITDA band.
The code includes lobbying and political consulting as well as conventional PR, creating customer and cyclicality variation.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment census or commercial database segmented by independence, normalized EBITDA, contract duration, retainer share, repeat-client revenue, and principal service would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is intrinsically an external campaign and consulting service, and LMM-scale employer agencies commonly maintain ongoing client relationships through retainers and repeat programs. Ineligibility remains for project-only publicity shops, election-cycle entities without repeat commercial service, misclassified advertising or event businesses, captive units, and founder-personal practices whose work is not organizationally repeatable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541820 ($9,020,215,177) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541820 ($16,903,608,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $271,021 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541820); class <5: 6,209 firms, avg revenue/firm $468,889 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,381 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 927 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,829,861 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $286,373 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 551 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,315,470 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $518,871 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 348 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,294,121 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,611,030 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 73 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,745,397 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,063,655 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 49 firms, avg revenue/firm $82,824,510 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,962,036 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 421

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of PR service demand.
Agency survey statements about human judgment and strategic trust may reflect provider optimism.
Rapid improvement in autonomous monitoring, content generation, journalist targeting, sentiment analysis, and crisis simulation could push the value below the range.
Lobbying and political consulting are more operator-dependent than routine publicity, widening intra-code variation.
o.change_evidence: Client cohort data showing five-year agency-spend retention, insourcing, AI self-service, software substitution, and service unbundling by PR engagement type would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Clients can self-serve or buy software for basic releases, social copy, monitoring summaries, research, contact lists, and routine reporting. Most complex LMM-agency quantity should still require an accountable operator because media and government relationships, crisis response, political judgment, executive coaching, stakeholder negotiation, factual validation, brand risk, and trusted counsel depend on context and responsibility. The operator-required share is highest in crisis, public affairs, lobbying, regulated sectors, and reputation strategy and lowest in commodity content and distribution.

q.bridge: PRSA documents hourly fees against monthly or annual budgets, fixed project fees, and flat monthly retainers, but not the five-year retained share of AI benefit. I map fixed projects and flat retainers to higher initial retention, hourly budget work to lower retention because fewer hours reduce billings, and annual review or renewal to gradual customer sharing across all models. The guide is US and industry-specific but old and nonquantitative, so it may miss current performance pricing and stronger procurement pressure; the direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: The PRSA guide establishes fee structures but does not measure their prevalence or commercial retention.
The guide predates current AI adoption and modern procurement practices.
Retention varies sharply between hourly media-relations execution, fixed projects, crisis availability retainers, lobbying, and strategic counsel.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of current US 541820 contracts and account P&Ls showing fee model, utilization, realization, renewal pricing, scope expansion, client concessions, and margin before and after AI deployment would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Flat retainers and fixed projects allow agencies to retain near-term productivity through higher throughput or margin, especially when clients buy access, judgment, or outcomes rather than hours. Hourly-budget arrangements pass benefits through mechanically, and periodic retainer reviews, competitive pitches, AI-native entrants, and client transparency demands should share additional gains over five years. Differentiated crisis, public-affairs, and reputation counsel can retain more than commodity content work.

rho.bridge: Muck Rack surveyed 1,013 PR professionals across agency and in-house settings in late 2024, while IPRN surveyed a self-selected global network of independent agencies in 2026. I translate widespread individual use and regular member-agency deployment into the share of remaining exposed work operationalized within five years, discounting for incomplete policies, limited disclosure, privacy, plagiarism, output quality, client transparency demands, training, and workflow integration. IPRN members are likely more internationally connected and innovation-oriented than typical US LMM agencies, biasing adoption upward; smaller agencies may nevertheless implement faster than large governed organizations.
caveats: Tool use and self-reported speed or quality are not audited implementation or labor savings.
Muck Rack mixes agency and in-house professionals and does not isolate LMM firms.
IPRN covers member agencies across more than 22 countries and does not state a respondent count on the fetched page.
Current high adoption reduces the remaining not-yet-implemented opportunity even as it supports future workflow acceptance.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal US PR-agency survey reporting production hours, utilization, rework, client approvals, policy coverage, and headcount avoided by workflow before and after AI deployment would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: Three-quarters of surveyed PR professionals already used GenAI in 2025, with common use in brainstorming, drafting, editing, and research, and IPRN reported regular use across most member agencies in 2026. The behavioral barrier is therefore low. Implementation will deepen, but confidential client information, factual error, brand voice, disclosure, legal and political sensitivity, and the need for senior review prevent full conversion of exposure into dependable labor avoidance.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while PRovoke Media identified 99 PR-firm transactions in 2024, including many small targets. I use the owner-plan rate as a broad anchor and the observed sector activity as evidence that agency transfers clear, then discount gifts, internal transfers, minority investments, non-US transactions, sub-LMM micro deals, unexecuted intentions, and founder-dependent firms that cannot complete a qualifying control sale. The deal count has no eligible-firm denominator and may bias upward because it is global and includes firms with only a minor PR component.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than PR agencies or the LMM band.
PRovoke's transaction universe is not restricted to the United States, control deals, or the frozen EBITDA band.
Many observed targets below $6 million in fees are likely below the LMM EBITDA threshold.
Founder and rainmaker dependence can make nominal agencies difficult to transfer without long earn-outs.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, PRovoke, or Davis+Gilbert data isolating completed US 541820 control transactions and failed sale processes by eligible-firm denominator over five years would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: PR agency M&A is demonstrably active, and the concentration of 2024 deals among smaller firms shows a functioning buyer market below the large holding companies. Employer agencies can transfer client rosters, sector expertise, teams, and methods, but founder relationships, change-of-control churn, earn-outs, political affiliations, and key-person reputational capital reduce the qualifying five-year probability.

records.principal_driver: Large volumes of writing, research, monitoring, editing, and reporting create implementable AI leverage inside recurring agency relationships.
records.principal_weakness: Commodity PR production is easy for clients and AI-native competitors to self-serve, constraining both operator-required demand and retained pricing benefit.

caveats: The labor-opportunity primitives are directionally grounded but not quantitatively identified: the four-digit occupation mix and O*NET tasks do not establish a wage-weighted six-digit exposure share, and adoption surveys do not measure dependable labor avoidance.

The rho bridge uses current tool adoption as a proxy for five-year operationalization of remaining exposed work; the researcher discloses the gap, but audited implementation, rework, utilization, and headcount evidence is absent.

The q base is an assumption-heavy proxy. The PRSA guide verifies fee structures and renewal mechanics but provides no current fee-model prevalence or empirical pass-through, so the base case's capped C factor should not be read as substantiated retention.

The availability factor rests on an honest estimate of eligible firms and a broad succession proxy: Gallup includes gifts and transfers across all employer businesses, while PRovoke's global transaction universe includes sub-LMM, non-control, and partly PR-adjacent deals without an eligible denominator.

Demand evidence is broader than the lens. The BLS output series is four-digit 541800, the occupation outlook includes in-house roles, and the IPRN survey is a self-selected global member network rather than representative US LMM evidence.

The code definition spans conventional PR, lobbying, political consulting, and PR consulting. Marking the lens non-heterogeneous understates meaningful differences in cyclicality, human relationship intensity, fee models, and AI self-service risk, although the packet acknowledges this variation and provides wide ranges.

The central e and o values remain explicitly labeled estimates with no direct population measurement, and the margin-bridged firm count is also an estimate; the resulting MODEL_CONDITIONED readiness and VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action are therefore appropriate.

sources_audited.note: The Census manual supports the 541820 definition and expressly includes lobbying, political consulting, and PR consulting, but it does not measure the scored eligibility or operator-required shares.

The May 2023 BLS table supports the stated occupation and wage mix for NAICS 541800, including PR managers and specialists, writers and editors, market-research analysts, project managers, and office support; it does not isolate six-digit 541820 or measure AI exposure.

O*NET supports the cited specialist tasks, including media response, releases, social content, relationship maintenance, management advice, coaching, and strategy, but task importance is not time, wage weight, substitutability, or operator-required demand.

O*NET supports the cited manager duties, including crisis plans, executive speeches and interviews, client-government-media relationships, budgets, supervision, and public response, but it does not quantify automation exposure or future operator-required demand.

Axios reports Muck Rack's late-2024 survey of 1,013 PR professionals, 75% GenAI use, and the cited use cases and policy/training concerns. The mixed agency/in-house survey measures use and perceptions, not labor avoidance or five-year implementation.

The IPRN page reports 82% regular AI use, 67% forecasting growth, conditional client acceptance and transparency, and human judgment and strategic trust as differentiators. It is a global member-agency survey across more than 22 countries and does not disclose a respondent count on the page.

The PRSA guide supports ongoing and project relationships, annual renewal, hourly fees against monthly or yearly budgets, project fees, and flat monthly retainers. It is old and supplies neither fee-model prevalence nor a measured retained share of AI benefits.

PRovoke supports 99 transactions in 2024, 62% involving firms below $6 million in revenue or fees, and the prominence of independent and private-equity buyers. Its global, partly PR-adjacent transaction count has no eligible-firm denominator and is not limited to qualifying US control sales.

Gallup supports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned within five years to sell or transfer ownership. The measure spans all employer businesses and includes transfers rather than completed qualifying sales.
BLS supports the cited 2024-2034 real-output projection for NAICS 541800 from $197.3 billion to $257.3 billion in chained 2017 dollars, or 2.7% annually, but not six-digit 541820 demand.

BLS supports 5% projected PR-specialist employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and the cited reputation, online-news, community/customer-relations, and social-media drivers. The occupation includes in-house roles and workers outside PR agencies.

records.title: Public Relations Agencies

caveats: a: No six-digit occupation mix for the narrowed 541990 lens was found; the published 541900 occupation table also contains photographic and veterinary services.
a: The DOE examples are site-specific demonstrations and do not establish average labor savings across commercial firms.
a: The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, so its level has a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.
rho: Research pilots and large global testing companies have more technical resources than typical firms in the lens.
rho: Implementation rates will differ sharply by asset accessibility, inspection modality, and customer regulation.
e: The Census industry definition lists activities but does not measure their establishment or revenue shares.
e: The injected count of 863 is estimated using a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge and may not represent the margin profile of inspection firms.
e: Firms may use 541990 for government contracting even when their underlying activity fits another industry, increasing classification noise.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than inspection firms and measures plans rather than completed transactions.
s5: EY covers the global testing, inspection, and certification sector, which includes larger laboratories and certification businesses outside the lens.
s5: The eligible population is only an estimated subset of the injected industry count.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for independent US inspection providers.
q: Pricing power varies between emergency or specialized work and competitively bid routine patrols.
q: The global large-company cost pass-through evidence may overstate lower-middle-market bargaining power.
d5: Pipeline mileage is an installed-base measure, not purchased outsourced inspection quantity.
d5: The EY outlook is global, broader than the lens, and nominal rather than constant-price demand.
d5: The narrowed lens spans end markets with different construction and maintenance cycles.
o: Construction and building inspectors are not the same population as 541990 infrastructure and commodity inspectors.
o: Some routine visual patrol quantity may become fully automated or self-served by asset owners.
o: Operator need is highest in safety-critical regulated work and lower in low-consequence repeat observations.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Infrastructure owners, industrial operators, utilities, commodity owners, insurers, and other businesses purchase documented inspections or verification under recurring service agreements or repeat work orders, commonly priced per visit, asset, route, project, or labor period.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 541990 is a residual code combining physical inspection, appraisal, dispute resolution, consumer counseling, brokerage, forecasting, and other unrelated services. The lens is narrowed to recurring business-to-business physical-asset inspection and technical verification because these activities share a coherent field-data, expert-review, and accountable-report workflow; the narrowing is for analytical coherence rather than attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Appraisal, arbitration, conciliation, consumer credit counseling, credit repair, business and patent brokerage, weather forecasting, handwriting analysis, one-off expert assignments, captive internal inspection units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced physical-asset inspection and technical verification, including visual pipeline and power-line inspection, commodity inspection, and fire-suppression equipment testing or inspection without repair or installation.

a.bridge: BLS describes the tasks of construction and building inspectors rather than the narrowed 541990 population, and DOE and PHMSA document drone and machine-learning applications at selected infrastructure sites. I map remote data collection, image triage, planning, scheduling, log preparation, report drafting, and anomaly classification to the inspection workflow while leaving physical access, sensor setup, nuanced diagnosis, validation, and customer accountability largely unexposed. The BLS occupation is more building-focused and may overstate code review work, while demonstration projects may overstate feasible automation; the net direction of error is uncertain.
caveats: No six-digit occupation mix for the narrowed 541990 lens was found; the published 541900 occupation table also contains photographic and veterinary services.
The DOE examples are site-specific demonstrations and do not establish average labor savings across commercial firms.
The injected compensation ratio uses 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, so its level has a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across independent pipeline, utility, commodity, and fire-system inspection firms that separates current manual work from already-deployed drones, computer vision, and workflow software would materially change this value.
a.rationale: The exposed opportunity is concentrated in labor-intensive access and observation that drones can reduce, plus digital-image review, document comparison, scheduling, and report production that AI can accelerate. It is limited by field presence, testing-device operation, mechanical judgment, exception handling, and certification-grade review. The estimate excludes remote and sensor workflows already deployed today.

d5.bridge: EY forecasts the broader global testing, inspection, and certification market, while PHMSA documents a large, stable US pipeline installed base and stronger integrity-management inspection requirements. I convert that evidence to modest real growth in the current US physical-asset inspection basket, reducing the global nominal outlook for inflation, differences in geography and service scope, and the maturity of domestic pipeline mileage. The proxy may overstate growth if customers internalize inspections or infrastructure spending slows, and may understate it if regulation, extreme weather, grid expansion, and aging assets accelerate inspection frequency.
caveats: Pipeline mileage is an installed-base measure, not purchased outsourced inspection quantity.
The EY outlook is global, broader than the lens, and nominal rather than constant-price demand.
The narrowed lens spans end markets with different construction and maintenance cycles.
d5.change_evidence: Segment-level US revenue and inspection-unit histories for pipeline, power-line, commodity, and fire-system providers, deflated by service prices and paired with customer maintenance budgets and regulatory inspection calendars, would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: A persistent installed base and safety regulation support repeat inspection demand even without rapid construction growth. PHMSA requires post-extreme-weather inspections and stronger integrity assessments, while broader testing and inspection demand benefits from regulation, infrastructure investment, and quality requirements. Cyclicality in energy, construction, and commodity volumes keeps the downside close to current real demand.

caveats: The Census industry definition lists activities but does not measure their establishment or revenue shares.
The injected count of 863 is estimated using a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge and may not represent the margin profile of inspection firms.
Firms may use 541990 for government contracting even when their underlying activity fits another industry, increasing classification noise.
e.change_evidence: A commercial establishment roster matched to 541990 tax records and coded by primary activity, recurring revenue, customer type, ownership independence, normalized EBITDA, and contract transferability would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition confirms that visual pipeline and power-line inspection, commodity inspection, and fire-suppression testing belong in 541990, but the same code also contains many unrelated and often episodic services. A material minority of the estimated lower-middle-market population is therefore expected to fit the narrowed recurring inspection lens, with the wide range reflecting the absence of a firm-level activity mix.

o.bridge: BLS describes a related inspector occupation in which remote inspection reduces headcount but certification, worksite examination, testing-device use, mechanical knowledge, detailed findings, and written accountability remain. PHMSA and DOE show drones and AI changing how evidence is collected and reviewed rather than eliminating the inspection obligation. I translate this to the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable inspection provider. Public-sector licensing evidence may overstate formal accountability in some private 541990 niches, while safety-critical utility work may require even more operator oversight.
caveats: Construction and building inspectors are not the same population as 541990 infrastructure and commodity inspectors.
Some routine visual patrol quantity may become fully automated or self-served by asset owners.
Operator need is highest in safety-critical regulated work and lower in low-consequence repeat observations.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement policies, regulator guidance, insurer requirements, and service contracts specifying whether automated outputs may be accepted without human review or third-party responsibility would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Sensors, drones, and computer vision can replace travel, access labor, and first-pass image review, but customers still need a party to plan the inspection, validate sensor coverage and model output, investigate anomalies, apply standards, maintain records, and stand behind the conclusion. BLS expects remote inspection to reduce inspector demand rather than eliminate the occupation.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for independent US inspection providers.
Pricing power varies between emergency or specialized work and competitively bid routine patrols.
The global large-company cost pass-through evidence may overstate lower-middle-market bargaining power.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and invoice sample showing pricing unit, renewal timing, escalation and benchmarking clauses, customer concentration, technician compensation, rebid outcomes, and gross-margin changes after technology deployment would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Per-visit, per-asset, route, and fixed-work-order pricing can let an operator retain near-term savings from faster data capture, image triage, and report production. Retention decays as customers rebid contracts, demand productivity concessions, compare technology-enabled providers, or bring remote monitoring in-house. High labor intensity and technician incentive needs also share part of the benefit internally.

rho.bridge: DOE and PHMSA demonstrate remote inspection, multisensor data capture, and deep-learning image processing in public infrastructure settings, while EY discusses AI adoption in the broader global testing, inspection, and certification sector. I translate demonstrated technical feasibility into a five-year implementation share for smaller US inspection firms, then haircut for equipment integration, data quality, aviation and site access, false-positive risk, customer approval, and workforce change. Demonstration and large-company evidence likely overstates near-term implementation for lower-middle-market firms.
caveats: Research pilots and large global testing companies have more technical resources than typical firms in the lens.
Implementation rates will differ sharply by asset accessibility, inspection modality, and customer regulation.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of lower-middle-market inspection firms reporting workflow-level deployment, capital cost, model validation, customer acceptance, realized hours saved, and exception rates after a full inspection cycle would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Remote capture and automated image analysis are operational rather than purely speculative, and inspection providers face strong safety and productivity incentives. Implementation remains constrained by fragmented asset data, heterogeneous field conditions, sensor capital, proof-of-accuracy requirements, and the need to integrate outputs into regulated customer workflows.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions for US employer-business owners across industries, while EY documents sustained acquisition activity in the broader global testing, inspection, and certification market. I haircut the employer-owner intention rate because intentions may not close, transfers may be internal or non-control, and small specialist firms can depend on owner credentials and relationships. Active sector consolidation partly offsets that haircut. The proxy likely overstates completed qualifying transfers if the narrowed firms are especially owner-dependent.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than inspection firms and measures plans rather than completed transactions.
EY covers the global testing, inspection, and certification sector, which includes larger laboratories and certification businesses outside the lens.
The eligible population is only an estimated subset of the injected industry count.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, and specialist brokers, matched to the eligible US firm roster and distinguishing control acquisitions from internal transfers, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner succession creates a plausible supply of firms, and inspection markets have strategic and sponsor acquisition interest. Actual control availability is lower than stated intent because credentials, customer approvals, contract assignment, and key-person knowledge can prevent a qualifying close.

records.principal_driver: Remote sensing and AI can remove substantial access, review, and reporting effort while safety and compliance obligations keep inspection demand recurring.
records.principal_weakness: The residual NAICS code is so heterogeneous that the eligible inspection population and the relevance of the injected industry-wide inputs are unusually uncertain.

caveats: The cited Census URL did not expose the claimed 541990 activity detail in its current rendered page, so the factual membership basis for the narrowed lens was not independently verifiable from S1 during this audit; e is already labeled ESTIMATE, and no alternative value supported a tier or action change.

The a, rho, and o bases translate one federal-site drone example, one pipeline R&D project, and a different inspector occupation into the narrowed commercial population. Those bridges and likely overstatement direction are disclosed, but the sources do not quantify representative workflow exposure, adoption, or operator-required share.
S8 is broad global TIC evidence dominated by larger players and supports direction rather than the quantitative rho, s5, q, or d5 values for US lower-middle-market physical-inspection firms.
The q base is an honest estimate: major-company pass-through of inflationary cost increases is not direct evidence that independent inspection firms retain 53% of AI-derived benefits over five years.

Pipeline mileage and integrity rules support recurring need but not outsourced purchased quantity across the full lens; the PHMSA gathering series also has a visible reporting break beginning in 2022.
Gallup measures intended sales or transfers across all employer businesses, not completed external control acquisitions of qualifying inspection firms; the haircut and this limitation are disclosed.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census NAICS page was reachable, but its current rendered content exposed only the generic NAICS search interface and 2022 manual link, not the asserted 541990 activity list. This weakens source verification for lens membership but does not independently establish a different eligible-share estimate.
The BLS table is explicitly for NAICS 541900 and lists photographic services and veterinary services among the industries within that broader aggregate, supporting the stated limitation.

BLS supports the cited inspection, testing-device, logging, reporting, physical, and judgment tasks and says remote inspections reduce inspector need while safety and quality sustain demand. It describes construction and building inspectors, not the narrowed 541990 population.

DOE documents Savannah River Site drone inspections across tanks, piping, power distribution, and other assets, with reduced labor, access risk, time, and cost and continued personnel review. It is one federal-site example and does not quantify typical commercial workflow exposure or five-year adoption.

The PHMSA project supports development and field validation of multisensor UAS collection, image processing, condition classification, and deep-learning video review for routine pipeline inspection. It is a completed research project, not evidence of representative lower-middle-market deployment or realized labor savings.
PHMSA directly states that the rule requires post-extreme-weather pipeline inspections and strengthens integrity-management assessment requirements.

PHMSA reports a large transmission and gathering mileage base through 2025 and a roughly stable transmission total. Mileage is an installed-base measure and does not establish outsourced inspection volume; gathering-series comparability also changes sharply beginning in 2022.

EY supports global TIC growth of 4-5% annually, major-player cost pass-through, AI as a strategic imperative, and nearly 1,000 global acquisitions over twelve years. It does not directly measure US lower-middle-market physical-inspection implementation, AI-benefit retention, completed qualifying transfers, or real demand growth.

Gallup directly reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years in its fall 2024 survey. The measure is cross-industry intent and includes transfers rather than completed qualifying control sales.

sources.supports: Defines the residual industry and identifies commodity inspection, visual pipeline and power-line inspection, fire-suppression testing, appraisal, arbitration, counseling, brokerage, forecasting, and other disparate activities.

sources.supports: Shows that the nearest published occupation table is a broader aggregate containing photographic and veterinary services as well as other professional services, limiting its use for the narrowed lens.

sources.supports: Describes inspection, testing-device, logging, reporting, physical, knowledge, licensing, and judgment tasks and states that remote inspection is expected to reduce but not eliminate inspector demand.
sources.title: Construction and Building Inspectors - Occupational Outlook Handbook

sources.supports: Documents drone inspections of tanks, piping, power distribution, and other assets that reduced labor, time, access risk, and cost while personnel reviewed imagery and verified regulatory condition.

sources.supports: Documents PHMSA-sponsored development of automated aerial data collection, signal and image processing, condition classification, and deep-learning review for routine pipeline inspection.

sources.supports: Describes structural testing-and-inspection demand, mid-single-digit global growth expectations, customer cost pass-through, AI as an operating imperative, and sustained acquisition activity.

records.title: All Other Professional, Scientific, and Technical Services

caveats: a: The available occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541600 and includes several consulting businesses with very different staffing.
a: O*NET importance scores do not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or current automation.
a: McKinsey's marketing-function value estimate is not a task-exposure measure and includes client-side marketing rather than marketing consultants.
a: Because AI is already widely used in marketing, some technically exposed work is no longer part of the current not-yet-automated opportunity.
rho: The surveys measure marketing organizations and decision-makers, not the frozen US LMM consulting-firm population.
rho: Adoption and self-reported efficiency are not the same as audited operational labor avoidance.
rho: Gartner's campaign-oriented sample may overweight creative execution that NAICS 541613 excludes.
rho: McKinsey's maturity survey is European and includes very large companies.
e: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
e: The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin boutiques or lower-margin implementation-heavy firms into the EBITDA band.
e: Boundary errors with advertising, public relations, market research, custom software, and general management consulting are plausible.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses, not marketing consulting or the LMM band.
s5: The Ciesco population is global media and marketing, including large media, agency, technology, and minority transactions.
s5: Founder dependence may require long transition periods or make apparent firms nontransferable.
q: No source directly measures the discounted five-year retained share of implemented AI benefit.
q: Monadnock confirms negotiated time-based consulting rates but does not report their prevalence versus fixed fees or retainers in current US LMM marketing consulting.
q: Value capture varies sharply between strategic advice, recurring fractional leadership, analytics, and implementation-heavy work.
d5: BLS real-output projections are for all management, scientific, and technical consulting services, not marketing consulting.
d5: Occupation growth includes in-house market researchers and workers in other industries.
d5: The estimate holds service quality and the current basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-transformation offerings.
o: No source directly measures the future share of marketing-consulting quantity requiring an accountable external operator.
o: The boundary between advice and execution is fluid, and AI may move work to software vendors, agencies, or in-house teams rather than eliminate it.
o: Rapid progress in autonomous research, first-party-data analysis, scenario modeling, and campaign planning could push the result below the range.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External businesses and organizations buy project-based strategy engagements, ongoing retainers, time-based advisory work, and sometimes implementation support. Revenue is primarily professional fees under fixed-scope, hourly, daily, weekly, team-rate, retainer, or hybrid arrangements; repeatability comes from continuing planning, measurement, product, pricing, customer, and go-to-market needs.
lens.excluded_activities: Advertising campaign creation and media placement, public-relations agencies, direct-mail agencies, standalone market-research and polling firms, software vendors, captive in-house marketing teams, shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo practitioners below the LMM band, and firms limited to nonrepeat one-off advice.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced marketing operating advice and assistance, including marketing objectives and policy, market and sales forecasting, product and pricing strategy, customer-service and sales-management consulting, licensing or franchise planning, and marketing planning and strategy.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541600 rather than six-digit 541613, and O*NET describes representative marketing occupations rather than all firm employees. I map high exposure to information gathering, competitive analysis, forecasting support, survey design, data processing, report and presentation drafting, campaign evaluation, and routine administration; I map lower exposure to executive discovery, pricing judgment, stakeholder alignment, relationship development, negotiation, and final accountable recommendations. The broader 541600 mix includes environmental, scientific, and operations consultants plus more technical staff, so it likely understates 541613's concentration in marketing knowledge work but may overstate exposure if marketing consultants spend more time in senior client interaction.
caveats: The available occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541600 and includes several consulting businesses with very different staffing.
O*NET importance scores do not measure time, wage weight, feasibility, or current automation.
McKinsey's marketing-function value estimate is not a task-exposure measure and includes client-side marketing rather than marketing consultants.
Because AI is already widely used in marketing, some technically exposed work is no longer part of the current not-yet-automated opportunity.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541613 payroll occupation table combined with representative time-and-motion studies by consultant level and workflow, including current AI use, would materially change the wage-weighted exposed share.
a.rationale: Marketing consulting is dominated by digital information, analysis, language, and presentation workflows. Market-research specialists prepare reports, collect and analyze customer and competitor data, forecast trends, and assess program effectiveness, while marketing managers develop strategies, pricing, and plans. Generative and analytical AI can substitute or avoid hiring across much of the production layer, but human judgment and client-facing work keep the base below two-thirds.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 541600 to grow 2.6% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 1.14 over five years, while employment of market research analysts and marketing specialists grows 7% over the decade. I center modestly below the broad output implication because 541600 includes faster-growing technical and environmental specialties, while retaining a positive base due to expanding data, measurement, pricing, customer, and strategy complexity. The occupation series spans the whole economy and the industry series is broader than 541613, so error is two-sided.
caveats: BLS real-output projections are for all management, scientific, and technical consulting services, not marketing consulting.
Occupation growth includes in-house market researchers and workers in other industries.
The estimate holds service quality and the current basket constant, excluding wholly new AI-transformation offerings.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit BLS or Census real-output forecast for 541613, supported by inflation-adjusted US consulting bookings, backlog, and marketing-budget surveys by client size, would materially change the year-five ratio.
d5.rationale: Organizations face continuing pressure to understand customers, measure marketing effectiveness, set prices, launch products, and adapt channels, supporting modest real demand growth for the current advisory basket. Demand is cyclical and marketing budgets are readily cut in downturns, while AI may create new advisory needs even as it changes delivery. AI self-service displacement is reserved for the operator-required share rather than deducted again here.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
The injected firm count uses a 15.65% broad Business & Consumer Services margin and may misclassify high-margin boutiques or lower-margin implementation-heavy firms into the EBITDA band.
Boundary errors with advertising, public relations, market research, custom software, and general management consulting are plausible.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit firm census or commercial database segmented by independence, normalized EBITDA, revenue model, repeat-client share, and principal service would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition is inherently an outsourced B2B advisory service, and firms large enough to reach the LMM EBITDA band generally need multiple employees and repeat clients. Ineligibility remains for misclassified agencies or software-led businesses, captive units, founder-only practices, and consultancies whose revenue is dominated by isolated one-off engagements without a repeat service relationship.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541613 ($34,007,872,680) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541613 ($79,535,987,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $282,178 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541613); class <5: 41,556 firms, avg revenue/firm $395,427 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,884 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,444 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,727,383 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $270,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,925 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,595,461 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $562,690 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,662 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,903,738 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,393,435 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 274 firms, avg revenue/firm $47,364,223 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,412,501 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $120,739,011 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,895,655 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,936

caveats: No source directly measures the future share of marketing-consulting quantity requiring an accountable external operator.
The boundary between advice and execution is fluid, and AI may move work to software vendors, agencies, or in-house teams rather than eliminate it.
Rapid progress in autonomous research, first-party-data analysis, scenario modeling, and campaign planning could push the result below the range.
o.change_evidence: Client cohort data comparing five-year consulting spend, project insourcing, AI self-service, and vendor replacement by engagement type and client size would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Clients can increasingly self-serve routine competitive scans, draft plans, summaries, forecasts, content ideas, and baseline analysis using AI and integrated marketing platforms. A substantial share should still require an accountable external operator for proprietary-data interpretation, market and pricing judgment, independent challenge, executive alignment, cross-functional facilitation, implementation design, and ownership of consequential recommendations. Operator requirement is higher in complex product, pricing, channel, and organizational engagements than in templated small-business marketing plans.

caveats: No source directly measures the discounted five-year retained share of implemented AI benefit.
Monadnock confirms negotiated time-based consulting rates but does not report their prevalence versus fixed fees or retainers in current US LMM marketing consulting.
Value capture varies sharply between strategic advice, recurring fractional leadership, analytics, and implementation-heavy work.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of US 541613 contracts and project P&Ls showing fee structure, utilization, realization, renewal pricing, gross margin, and client concessions before and after AI deployment would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Fixed-scope projects and retainers can initially retain AI productivity through higher throughput or margin, while hourly, daily, weekly, and team-rate work shares gains mechanically through fewer billable units. Over five years, sophisticated client procurement, rate negotiations, new AI-native competitors, lower entry barriers, and renewal repricing should transfer a meaningful portion of gross benefit to customers. Proprietary benchmarks, trusted advice, and outcome-oriented scopes preserve some capture.

rho.bridge: Gartner surveyed 418 marketing leaders about client-side campaign use, and McKinsey surveyed European marketing decision-makers rather than US LMM marketing consultancies. I translate broad adoption, use in creative and strategy development, and reported mature-user efficiency into the share of the remaining exposed consulting opportunity implemented within five years, discounting for immature operating models, weak data foundations, confidentiality, quality review, change management, and client acceptance. Client-side leaders may have larger technology budgets but slower governance than LMM consultancies, making the net direction of error uncertain.
caveats: The surveys measure marketing organizations and decision-makers, not the frozen US LMM consulting-firm population.
Adoption and self-reported efficiency are not the same as audited operational labor avoidance.
Gartner's campaign-oriented sample may overweight creative execution that NAICS 541613 excludes.
McKinsey's maturity survey is European and includes very large companies.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of US marketing consultancies reporting workflow-level production hours, utilization, rework, client acceptance, and headcount avoided before and after AI deployment would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: Marketing organizations already use GenAI for creative work, evaluation, reporting, campaign planning, and strategy, so adoption barriers are lower than in many services. However, only a small minority in McKinsey's European sample described their use as mature, and both sources identify ROI, data, technology, and operating-model constraints. Five years should enable broad bounded deployment without converting every exposed task into dependable labor avoidance.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while Ciesco observed active global media-and-marketing M&A and rising Agency Services & Strategy activity. I use Gallup's 22% as an upper anchor and discount gifts, internal transfers, unexecuted plans, non-US and non-consulting deals, minority investments, and founder-dependent firms that cannot complete a qualifying control sale. Active sector deal flow supports a nontrivial probability but does not supply a usable denominator.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses, not marketing consulting or the LMM band.
The Ciesco population is global media and marketing, including large media, agency, technology, and minority transactions.
Founder dependence may require long transition periods or make apparent firms nontransferable.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or broker data isolating completed US marketing-consulting control transactions and failed sale processes by eligible-firm denominator over five years would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: LMM employer consultancies can be transferable through client contracts, recurring retainers, data assets, methods, and multi-person teams, and broad marketing M&A demonstrates buyer interest. Still, stated intentions do not equal completed control transactions, and founder relationships, key-person selling, earn-outs, and client-change-of-control risk can prevent or delay qualification.

records.principal_driver: High-wage research, analysis, forecasting, writing, and presentation workflows create broad AI leverage in a labor-intensive advisory model.
records.principal_weakness: The same tools that improve consultant productivity enable client self-service and AI-native competition, limiting durable demand and commercial retention.

caveats: The labor-exposure base is a judgmental translation from a four-digit consulting occupation table and representative O*NET occupations; no source measures wage-weighted task exposure for six-digit 541613, but the PROXY label, bridge, and 0.42-0.71 range disclose this limitation.

The implementation base translates client-side campaign adoption and self-reported European marketing efficiency into five-year labor avoidance for US LMM consultancies; this is directionally informative but population- and construct-distant, and is honestly labeled PROXY.

The transferability estimate combines broad employer-owner intentions with global media-and-marketing deal counts that include agencies, technology, large transactions, and non-control deals; neither source supplies a qualifying completed-sale denominator.

The retention primitive is an ESTIMATE supported only by the existence of negotiated time-based rate structures; the source does not establish contract-mix prevalence, repricing, or realized post-AI value capture.

The demand projection bridges from four-digit consulting output and an economy-wide occupation outlook, so faster-growing scientific or technical consulting and in-house marketing employment may bias the 541613 base upward.

The operator-required share is an ESTIMATE without direct client-cohort evidence, and continuing improvement in self-service research, analysis, and planning tools could move it below the registered range.
The injected LMM firm count is itself margin-bridged and estimate-classified; its uncertainty is excluded from the published scenario envelope because dataset anchors are held fixed.

The lens is coherent with the stated recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM objective, but boundary leakage among marketing consulting, advertising, public relations, market research, software, and broader management consulting remains substantial.

sources_audited.note: The Census 2022 NAICS manual is reachable and is the authoritative source for the 541613 industry definition and adjacent-industry boundaries used by the lens.

The BLS table supports the cited May 2023 occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541600, including management analysts, market research specialists, marketing managers, project managers, and office support, but it does not establish the six-digit 541613 mix.

O*NET lists report preparation, customer and competitor data collection, forecasting, survey design, effectiveness measurement, analysis, and client-facing communication tasks for market research analysts and marketing specialists.
O*NET lists marketing strategy, policy, pricing, financial evaluation, forecasting, research, coordination, and advisory tasks for marketing managers.

Gartner reports the cited 418-leader survey, 27% limited or no campaign GenAI adoption, 77% creative-development use and 48% strategy-development use among adopters, plus ROI concerns; the population is client-side marketing organizations and campaign work rather than US LMM marketing consultancies.

McKinsey supports the cited 5%-15% of marketing-spend value estimate and general knowledge-work use cases, but that functional value estimate does not measure wage-weighted task exposure in marketing consulting.

McKinsey reports a survey of 500 European senior marketing decision-makers, 6% mature GenAI use, 22% average efficiency gains among leaders, and data, technology, strategy, and operating-model barriers; the mixed European population is not US LMM marketing consultancies and the gains are self-reported.

Gallup reports that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, but intentions across all employer businesses do not establish completed qualifying control transfers for LMM marketing consultancies.

Ciesco reports 2,306 global media-and-marketing transactions in 2024 and 32% year-over-year growth in Agency Services & Strategy activity, supporting active broad deal flow but not an eligible-firm denominator or completed US marketing-consulting control-sale probability.

BLS reports NAICS 541600 real output rising from $493.3 billion in 2024 to $639.7 billion in 2034, a 2.6% compound annual increase, but the projection covers all management, scientific, and technical consulting rather than six-digit 541613.

Monadnock documents negotiated US consulting master-service-agreement data and hourly, daily, and weekly rates, with marketing consulting coverage, but the page does not establish the present prevalence of fee structures or the five-year retained share of AI benefit.

BLS projects 7% employment growth for market research analysts and marketing specialists from 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to data use, customer understanding, strategy measurement, and product-demand analysis; the occupation spans the whole economy and is not a 541613 output forecast.

records.title: Marketing Consulting Services

caveats: a: OEWS reports employment and wages but not task time or current automation penetration.
a: The exact-industry table excludes self-employed practitioners and may not mirror lower-middle-market group practices.
a: AI can assist clinical preparation without substituting the therapeutic encounter or licensed judgment.
rho: The AMA survey covers physicians, not this industry's practitioner population.
rho: Professional guidance establishes feasible use cases and constraints but does not measure production adoption.
rho: Privacy, consent, malpractice, and documentation-integrity requirements can turn gross time savings into review work.
e: No source measures eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
e: Corporate-practice and fee-splitting rules can separate clinical ownership from management economics.
e: A group practice can meet the revenue test yet remain economically dependent on one clinician-owner or a few payor contracts.
s5: Gallup measures intentions among employer-business owners across all industries, not completed mental health practice transfers.
s5: LifeStance is much larger than screened firms and includes physician services outside the exact code.
s5: Management-service transactions or licensed-owner substitutions may not represent clean control transfers of the clinical practice.
q: LifeStance's payor mix and bargaining position are not representative of smaller practices.
q: No source measures how AI benefits are shared through mental health reimbursement or clinician compensation.
q: Capacity gains have little value where clinician supply, appointment demand, supervision, or payor panels bind.
d5: Occupational employment growth is not the same quantity as constant-quality demand for the current service basket.
d5: The principal BLS projection is for one major practitioner occupation and does not cover every profession in the code.
d5: Coverage expansions can raise billed demand while fee cuts, network closures, or workforce scarcity suppress realized visits.
o: No source directly measures year-five operator-required share for this exact industry.
o: Low-acuity coaching and structured interventions may migrate to employer, primary-care, or software channels.
o: Regulation preserves licensed accountability but does not guarantee that incumbent group practices retain the patient relationship.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients receive scheduled in-person or virtual clinical visits; practices collect negotiated fee-for-service reimbursement from commercial and government payors and patient payments, with each counseling session generally constituting a separate service obligation.
lens.excluded_activities: Solo practices without transferable operations, physician psychiatry, inpatient and residential facilities, general outpatient centers classified elsewhere, nonclinical coaching, captive employee-assistance units, and software-only mental health products.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market private and group practices of nonphysician psychologists, counselors, marriage and family therapists, social workers, and related licensed mental health practitioners providing recurring or repeat diagnosis, psychotherapy, assessment, and behavioral treatment to external patients and payors.

a.bridge: BLS measures the exact industry's employment and wages rather than task hours: counselors and social workers, psychologists, other health practitioners, and office-administrative staff dominate the mix. I map administrative work, intake, scheduling, billing, record review, measurement, note drafting, care-plan drafting, and patient messaging to high exposure; clinical interpretation and live therapy to partial exposure; and crisis judgment, empathy, diagnosis, safeguarding, and accountability to low exposure. ACA confirms plausible administrative uses while explicitly preserving human diagnosis and treatment decisions. This may overstate exposure if privacy-safe tools cannot handle session content, and understate it if measurement-based care and documentation automation absorb more clinician time than the occupation titles reveal.
caveats: OEWS reports employment and wages but not task time or current automation penetration.
The exact-industry table excludes self-employed practitioners and may not mirror lower-middle-market group practices.
AI can assist clinical preparation without substituting the therapeutic encounter or licensed judgment.
a.change_evidence: A representative time-and-motion study of group mental health practices, wage-weighted by role and separating live care, documentation, intake, billing, scheduling, supervision, and care coordination, paired with audited preimplementation and postimplementation hours, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The practice has a meaningful clerical layer and substantial clinician documentation and synthesis, but most wage expense remains tied to licensed therapeutic interaction whose context, alliance, risk assessment, and judgment are not safely substitutable end to end.

d5.bridge: BLS projects national employment of substance abuse, behavioral disorder, and mental health counselors to grow over the coming decade, while CMS expanded independent Medicare billing to mental health counselors and marriage and family therapists and continues to pay behavioral telehealth and technology-supported services. I convert the occupation-growth signal to a moderated five-year constant-quality service-demand ratio, allowing for access expansion, unmet need, and reimbursement support but discounting clinician shortages, patient affordability, payor utilization controls, macroeconomic stress, and possible substitution by digital self-service. Employment may understate demand when vacancies persist and overstate service quantity if documentation or staffing intensity rises.
caveats: Occupational employment growth is not the same quantity as constant-quality demand for the current service basket.
The principal BLS projection is for one major practitioner occupation and does not cover every profession in the code.
Coverage expansions can raise billed demand while fee cuts, network closures, or workforce scarcity suppress realized visits.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry constant-price receipts and visit counts, commercial claims volumes by therapy modality, new-patient wait times, clinician vacancy rates, and payer-approved utilization per patient would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Underlying need and coverage expansion support demand, but realized visits depend on licensed supply, reimbursement, patient engagement, and the ability of group practices to fill schedules without diluting care quality.

caveats: No source measures eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
Corporate-practice and fee-splitting rules can separate clinical ownership from management economics.
A group practice can meet the revenue test yet remain economically dependent on one clinician-owner or a few payor contracts.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Census product-line receipts or a commercial provider directory matched to ownership, clinician count, payor contracts, repeat-patient revenue, legal entity structure, and founder dependence for the supplied population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The exact code is built around independent private and group practices serving external patients, so most firms in the supplied lower-middle-market population fit the service lens. The discount covers nontransferable founder practices, misclassification, mixed clinics, captive arrangements, and practices whose contracts, credentials, or patient panels cannot survive a control change.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621330 ($12,409,594,231) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621330 ($20,889,493,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $95,447 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621330); class <5: 31,706 firms, avg revenue/firm $202,692 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,025 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,993 firms, avg revenue/firm $724,532 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $114,476 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,996 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,229,971 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $194,335 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,664 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,887,960 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $456,298 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 321 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,232,732 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,142,772 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 139 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,511,748 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,082,856 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 460

caveats: No source directly measures year-five operator-required share for this exact industry.
Low-acuity coaching and structured interventions may migrate to employer, primary-care, or software channels.
Regulation preserves licensed accountability but does not guarantee that incumbent group practices retain the patient relationship.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal claims and outcomes comparing clinician-led, hybrid, and software-only mental health care, including escalation, adverse-event, retention, and payer-coverage data by acuity, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Software can support intake, psychoeducation, measurement, reminders, and low-acuity self-management, but covered diagnosis and psychotherapy remain licensed services with human responsibility. ACA explicitly preserves human diagnosis, treatment planning, contextual interpretation, and direct involvement, and CMS describes digital treatment devices as adjuncts used under an ongoing behavioral treatment plan.

q.bridge: LifeStance reports negotiated fee-for-service reimbursement and recognizes each counseling session as a performance obligation; CMS pays newly eligible mental health counselors and marriage and family therapists under the Physician Fee Schedule at a fixed relationship to psychologist payment. I translate per-service pricing into moderate retention of implemented administrative savings because payment is not mechanically cost-plus, then discount for payor renegotiation, utilization management, fee-schedule changes, clinician productivity compensation, review costs, and competitive pressure. A national platform may have better contracting leverage than lower-middle-market practices, while small specialist groups may retain savings that payors cannot observe.
caveats: LifeStance's payor mix and bargaining position are not representative of smaller practices.
No source measures how AI benefits are shared through mental health reimbursement or clinician compensation.
Capacity gains have little value where clinician supply, appointment demand, supervision, or payor panels bind.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of group-practice payor contracts and clinician compensation agreements linked to reimbursement, visit length, denial rates, documentation labor, and margins before and after AI implementation would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Per-visit reimbursement allows near-term cost savings and capacity gains to accrue to the practice, but payors set or negotiate rates and clinician compensation commonly follows visits, limiting durable capture of technology-enabled productivity.

rho.bridge: ACA identifies counseling-specific opportunities in note transcription, billing, paperwork, and intake but requires licensed oversight and rejects autonomous diagnosis or treatment planning. AMA surveyed physicians rather than nonphysician mental health practitioners; respondents strongly favored documentation and administrative uses, and operating health systems reported ambient-scribe deployment. I translate tool availability and adjacent-provider adoption into five-year implementation of exposed mental-health workflows, then discount for sensitive session data, consent, hallucinations, EHR integration, payer audits, state licensure, bias, procurement capacity, and mandatory clinician review. Physician-system evidence may overstate small-practice implementation resources, while the counseling profession's documentation burden may speed adoption.
caveats: The AMA survey covers physicians, not this industry's practitioner population.
Professional guidance establishes feasible use cases and constraints but does not measure production adoption.
Privacy, consent, malpractice, and documentation-integrity requirements can turn gross time savings into review work.
rho.change_evidence: A nationally representative survey of nonphysician mental health group practices measuring approved AI use by workflow, practice size, verified hours avoided, clinician review time, error rates, privacy incidents, and payer audit outcomes would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Ambient documentation, intake, scheduling, coding support, chart summarization, and routine communication have direct workflow fits and should diffuse, but highly sensitive data and the consequences of erroneous clinical language prevent frictionless automation.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that U.S. employer-business owners across industries planned a sale or transfer within five years, while LifeStance reports three completed outpatient mental health practice acquisitions in 2023 and none in 2024. I haircut the cross-industry intention rate for noncompletion, internal or family transfers, licensed-ownership constraints, clinician departures, nontransferable patient relationships, and the absence of acquisitions at the observed consolidator in 2024. The large-platform experience confirms transferability but may overstate buyer access to small practices; its 2024 pause may understate wider strategic and sponsor demand.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions among employer-business owners across all industries, not completed mental health practice transfers.
LifeStance is much larger than screened firms and includes physician services outside the exact code.
Management-service transactions or licensed-owner substitutions may not represent clean control transfers of the clinical practice.
s5.change_evidence: Closed transaction records from a healthcare deal database matched to U.S. outpatient mental health practice size, control percentage, seller rollover, clinician retention, and failed listings, plus owner-age data, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Group mental health practices have transferable payor contracts, clinician teams, referral channels, scheduling infrastructure, and patient access, but clinician retention and state ownership rules make a qualifying control transfer less reliable than an owner's stated intention.

records.principal_driver: Recurring reimbursed visits create a large base of documentation and administrative work around a durable licensed clinical service.
records.principal_weakness: The service and enterprise value depend on clinicians whose relationships, licenses, judgment, and continued employment are difficult to automate or guarantee after a sale.

caveats: S1 could not be reopened because the cited Census manual repeatedly timed out; its scope claim was therefore not independently verified from that source, although S2 corroborates the exact industry identity.

The a and rho bases remain judgment-heavy transformations: occupational mix and physician or professional-guidance evidence do not measure mental-health workflow hours, production adoption, or net time avoided. The PROXY labels, bridges, and broad ranges disclose this limitation.

The e and s5 estimates are not measured for lower-middle-market mental-health groups. Gallup provides broad owner intentions and LifeStance demonstrates transferability at platform scale, but neither establishes the eligible or completed-transfer shares used here.

The q base is not directly measured: fee-for-service reimbursement supports the direction of near-term retention, but the cited sources do not quantify how savings are divided among practices, clinicians, payors, and implementation or review costs.

The d5 and o inputs translate counselor employment growth and payment-policy evidence into constant-quality demand and operator-required share; neither cited source directly measures those constructs for this industry. Their PROXY and ESTIMATE treatment is appropriate but leaves substantial uncertainty.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census PDF timed out on repeated reopening attempts, so its exact text could not be independently verified in this review. The exact-industry identity is independently visible in S2, limiting the consequence of this access failure.

The exact NAICS 621330 OEWS table reports the stated clinician, psychologist, social-worker, managerial, and office-administrative employment and wage mix and discloses that self-employed workers are excluded.

The ACA submission expressly identifies transcription, billing, paperwork, and intake uses while requiring licensed review, human treatment decisions, continued direct involvement, and privacy safeguards.

The AMA article reports a physician survey emphasizing documentation and administrative uses and describes deployed ambient-scribe examples. It is adjacent-provider evidence, as the packet discloses, rather than direct evidence for nonphysician mental-health practices.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years and provides the survey population and weighting details. This is intention evidence across industries, not completed mental-health transactions.

The LifeStance 10-K supports outpatient mental-health delivery, negotiated fee-for-service reimbursement, counseling sessions as performance obligations, visit-linked clinician productivity and compensation, acquisition strategy, three completed practice acquisitions in 2023, and none in 2024.

BLS projects 17% employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and 48,300 annual openings for substance-abuse, behavioral-disorder, and mental-health counselors, supporting the disclosed occupation-to-demand proxy.

CMS states that MFTs and MHCs may bill Medicare independently from January 1, 2024, subject to licensing and experience criteria, at 75% of clinical-psychologist payment under the Physician Fee Schedule.

The CY 2025 final-rule fact sheet supports behavioral-health coding and payment, limited telehealth flexibilities, and payment for qualifying digital mental-health treatment devices used with ongoing practitioner-led treatment plans.

run_meta.title: Offices of Mental Health Practitioners (except Physicians)

sources.supports: Exact-industry scope, independent private and group practice structure, covered clinical activities, and cross-references.
