records.title: Testing Laboratories and Services

caveats: a: There is no direct wage-weighted task-exposure measure for this six-digit industry.
a: Adobe samples span graphic design, illustration, photography, video, and other creative roles rather than only LMM graphic-design firms.
a: The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment; that vintage mismatch may distort the labor base even though it is not re-estimated here.
rho: Tool use and faster output do not prove durable labor substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: Digital agencies include development and marketing firms whose workflows differ from pure graphic design.
rho: Commercial-use rights, client restrictions, training, and inconsistent outputs may slow implementation.
e: The frozen n of 370 is already a margin-bridged estimate and is not re-estimated here.
e: NAICS self-classification may include mixed creative or marketing agencies.
e: No fetched source directly measures repeat revenue or transferability among EBITDA-band graphic-design firms.
s5: Openness to selling is not observed deal incidence and the survey population is broader than graphic design.
s5: Marketing-services M&A includes advertising, martech, digital marketing, and large transactions outside the lens.
s5: Founder-dependent firms may close or lose clients rather than complete a transferable sale.
q: Pricing evidence covers digital agencies broadly rather than only NAICS 541430.
q: Fixed-fee projects retain savings only if scope and revision demands remain controlled.
q: Demand volume and self-service are reflected in d5 and o, and implementation friction is reflected in rho, not double-counted here.
d5: Employment is an input proxy and embeds productivity and outsourcing shifts rather than directly measuring output quantity.
d5: Adobe's content-demand evidence spans marketing and creative work beyond the current NAICS service basket.
d5: Print decline, recessions, in-housing, and AI-generated asset proliferation can move different submarkets in opposite directions.
o: Graphic design has no licensure or statutory accountability barrier comparable with regulated professions.
o: Creator preference for a human final decision is not proof that customers will continue buying an external firm.
o: Operator-required share should be higher for brand systems and packaging than for routine social, presentation, and promotional assets.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business and institutional customers buy discrete projects, fixed-scope campaigns and brand systems, hourly production, or ongoing retainers for recurring creative needs; repeat demand is generated by new campaigns, channels, products, variants, and brand maintenance rather than a mandatory subscription.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house creative departments, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, standalone advertising agencies classified elsewhere, software-only products, individual art sales without an outsourced service relationship, and practices whose client relationships or delivery capability are not transferable.
lens.included_activities: US graphic-design firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that repeatedly provide outsourced visual-communication planning, brand identity, layout, illustration, production design, packaging, digital content, and related design management to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS describes graphic-designer duties, Adobe measures AI use and productivity among broader creative-professional samples, and Promethean reports that production roles are about two-thirds of digital-agency talent; none measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure in NAICS 541430. I map exposure across ideation, first drafts, image generation and editing, resizing, layout alternatives, copy assistance, asset adaptation, proofing, and routine production, while retaining human time for briefs, client communication, brand judgment, selection, rights review, and final accountability. Adobe's vendor-selected respondents and self-reported speed gains may overstate exposure, while rapid model improvement may make the estimate conservative.
caveats: There is no direct wage-weighted task-exposure measure for this six-digit industry.
Adobe samples span graphic design, illustration, photography, video, and other creative roles rather than only LMM graphic-design firms.
The frozen compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and a harmonization adjustment; that vintage mismatch may distort the labor base even though it is not re-estimated here.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study of NAICS 541430 firms that reports hours and wages by brief development, concepting, production, revision, client service, project management, and administration before and after AI adoption would materially change a.
a.rationale: Graphic-design work is digitally mediated and generative tools already accelerate first drafts and production. BLS lists software-based illustration, image creation, layout, revisions, and error review among core duties, and Adobe reports 94% of surveyed creative professionals produce content faster with AI, with a majority saying at least 50% faster. A 51% base reflects broad task exposure but excludes the relational, strategic, and evaluative portions of the workflow and work already automated today.

d5.bridge: BLS projects graphic-designer employment and Adobe surveys perceived content demand, while d5 is constant-price, constant-quality quantity demanded for the current graphic-design service basket. I use BLS's 2% 2024-34 employment growth as a conservative baseline, then allow demand quantity to outgrow labor as customers require more digital-channel assets, variants, and faster campaign cycles. Adobe respondents may overstate demand growth because they are selected from AI-using creative and marketing populations, while BLS employment may understate output demand when productivity rises.
caveats: Employment is an input proxy and embeds productivity and outsourcing shifts rather than directly measuring output quantity.
Adobe's content-demand evidence spans marketing and creative work beyond the current NAICS service basket.
Print decline, recessions, in-housing, and AI-generated asset proliferation can move different submarkets in opposite directions.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of US outsourced graphic-design spend deflated by a design-services price index and segmented across brand identity, packaging, print, web, social, and production adaptation would materially change d5.
d5.rationale: BLS expects companies' expanding digital presence to support demand for website and social-media design, although total graphic-designer employment grows only 2% over a decade. Adobe finds rapid campaign cycles and heavy pressure for more content, with almost three-quarters of creative professionals worried that expected output will become impossible even with AI. The base therefore assumes modest real growth in design-output demand, with a wide range for macroeconomic and channel-mix uncertainty.

caveats: The frozen n of 370 is already a margin-bridged estimate and is not re-estimated here.
NAICS self-classification may include mixed creative or marketing agencies.
No fetched source directly measures repeat revenue or transferability among EBITDA-band graphic-design firms.
e.change_evidence: A representative survey or CRM/accounting-data panel of EBITDA-band NAICS 541430 firms reporting external-customer revenue, repeat-client revenue, retainers, top-client and founder concentration, team delivery, and post-founder client retention would materially change e.
e.rationale: The Census definition is inherently an external visual-communication service, and firms large enough to fall within the frozen EBITDA band are more likely than freelancers to have teams, repeat business customers, and transferable delivery systems. Eligibility is reduced for founder-led boutiques whose creative reputation and customer relationships cannot transfer, firms dominated by one-off identity projects, and mixed agencies classified as graphic design but economically dependent on non-design activities.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541430 ($5,155,227,877) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541430 ($9,873,226,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $191,989 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541430); class <5: 13,359 firms, avg revenue/firm $259,220 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $40,568 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,416 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,112,405 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $174,091 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 656 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,308,331 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $361,254 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 318 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,449,481 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,165,844 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 30 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,280,067 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,860,830 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,342,682 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,870,630 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 370

o.bridge: BLS states that automated design tools may reduce companies' need to contract freelance graphic designers, while Adobe measures which creative tasks remain collaborative or evaluative and reports broad creator preference for human final decisions; neither directly measures the year-five share requiring an accountable LMM operator. I deduct commodity asset generation, template adaptation, simple layouts, and self-service work, but retain complex briefs, brand systems, multi-channel consistency, client facilitation, selection, rights review, production readiness, and accountability. Evidence from freelancers and social creators may overstate elimination for LMM agency engagements, while rapid self-service improvement may make the base too high.
caveats: Graphic design has no licensure or statutory accountability barrier comparable with regulated professions.
Creator preference for a human final decision is not proof that customers will continue buying an external firm.
Operator-required share should be higher for brand systems and packaging than for routine social, presentation, and promotional assets.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement studies tracking which graphic-design categories move to in-house AI or self-service platforms, together with churn and scope data from design agencies by project complexity and client size, would materially change o.
o.rationale: Software can increasingly generate and edit serviceable assets without an agency, and BLS explicitly expects AI to reduce some freelance contracting. Yet Adobe finds collaboration has lower AI adoption, highlights selection, comparison, rights review, and collaboration as enduring needs, and reports 85% of surveyed creators believe the final creative decision should remain with the creator. A 67% base implies major self-service displacement while preserving an operator role for strategically and operationally complex design work.

q.bridge: Promethean measures pricing practices and margin pressure in digital agencies, not discounted five-year retention of AI-generated gross benefit in NAICS 541430. I treat fixed-fee projects and fixed retainers as allowing meaningful initial capture, hourly and time-and-materials work as passing savings through more directly, and then discount all models for renewals, competitive bids, higher client output expectations, and AI-driven commoditization. Digital-agency evidence may overstate retention for pure design because Promethean finds design shops skew project-based but commodity design faces especially strong self-service competition.
caveats: Pricing evidence covers digital agencies broadly rather than only NAICS 541430.
Fixed-fee projects retain savings only if scope and revision demands remain controlled.
Demand volume and self-service are reflected in d5 and o, and implementation friction is reflected in rho, not double-counted here.
q.change_evidence: Five years of project-level contracts, estimates, time records, realized margins, revision counts, renewal prices, client output demands, and win/loss data from AI-adopting LMM graphic-design firms would materially change q.
q.rationale: Design agencies lean toward project pricing, which can preserve an efficiency gain within an agreed scope, while most agencies use a mix of time-and-materials, fixed bids, and retainers. However, Promethean reports only 20% raised rates in 2026, a 13% average agency net margin in 2025, and client pricing pressure tied to expected AI savings. Nearly three-quarters of Adobe's creative respondents also fear that clients or managers will raise content expectations beyond what AI can satisfy. A 47% base reflects substantial initial capture followed by giveback through price and scope.

rho.bridge: Adobe measures individual creative-professional use and perceived speed, while Promethean measures AI implementation in the broader digital-agency sector. I translate high current use, roughly one-third of agencies already fully implemented by the second quarter of 2026, and AI embedded in incumbent creative software into five-year workflow penetration of the remaining exposed opportunity. Self-reported implementation may overstate sustained, quality-adjusted labor substitution; conversely, native tool integration may accelerate deployment beyond the base.
caveats: Tool use and faster output do not prove durable labor substitution or avoided hiring.
Digital agencies include development and marketing firms whose workflows differ from pure graphic design.
Commercial-use rights, client restrictions, training, and inconsistent outputs may slow implementation.
rho.change_evidence: Longitudinal Adobe, AIGA, or Promethean microdata for US design agencies showing AI-active seats, percentage of billable hours AI-assisted, realized cycle-time reduction, rework, quality failures, and net hiring avoidance by firm size would materially move rho.
rho.rationale: Implementation barriers are lower than in regulated or physical service industries because core work already occurs in digital creative suites. Adobe reports most creative professionals spend substantial time working with AI and 81% report surging time savings, while Promethean reports about one-third of digital agencies fully implemented AI by the second quarter of 2026. Quality control, copyright, brand consistency, client approval, and workflow redesign prevent complete realization.

s5.bridge: Promethean surveys broader digital-agency leaders about openness to selling or buying in 2025, not completed five-year control transfers for eligible NAICS 541430 firms. I translate 36% openness to selling into a lower completed-transfer probability by allowing for exploratory intent, failed processes, minority deals, internal reorganizations, and nontransferable founder dependence. The proxy may overstate actual closings because openness is cheap, but it may understate unplanned succession and sales later in the five-year window.
caveats: Openness to selling is not observed deal incidence and the survey population is broader than graphic design.
Marketing-services M&A includes advertising, martech, digital marketing, and large transactions outside the lens.
Founder-dependent firms may close or lose clients rather than complete a transferable sale.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, or GF Data transactions coded to NAICS 541430 and matched to seller revenue and EBITDA, combined with owner-age and exit-intention data from AIGA or Promethean, would materially change s5.
s5.rationale: Promethean reports that 36% of digital-agency leaders were open to selling in 2025 and that larger agencies commonly acquire smaller firms strategically. Marketing-services transaction volume also rose each year from 2023 through 2025. A 20% base recognizes an active consolidation market but discounts heavily for graphic-design specificity, completion risk, and the requirement for a qualifying control transfer.

records.principal_driver: Digitally native creative production exposes a large share of design and adaptation work to AI embedded directly in existing software.
records.principal_weakness: Low switching barriers and improving self-service tools make it difficult to retain productivity gains or preserve external-firm demand for commodity design.

caveats: S1 could be reopened only as a JavaScript-required Census shell, so its stated definition and establishment count were not directly verifiable; the decorative count does not affect the base tier or action.

S5's reopened text supports the ten-hours-per-week statement but not the cited 81% time-savings statistic or the stated sample description. S3 and S6 independently support the direction of rho, and removing the unverified S5 details does not establish a correction that changes the base tier or action.

S6 is a broad digital-agency source and its reopened page did not directly show the narrower claim that design agencies lean project-based. The packet discloses the population mismatch and treats the affected inputs as proxies.

The numeric bases for a, rho, q, d5, and o are judgmental transformations of broad creative-professional, creator, occupational, and digital-agency evidence rather than direct NAICS 541430 measurements. Their PROXY labels, bridges, error directions, and ranges are candid, but Stage 2 should not treat the point estimates as observed facts.

Eligibility e and five-year transfer incidence s5 lack direct evidence for EBITDA-band graphic-design firms. The record handles e as ESTIMATE and s5 as a disclosed proxy, which appropriately keeps readiness MODEL_CONDITIONED and action VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

sources_audited.note: The exact Census URL resolved, but the returned page was only a JavaScript-required shell, so the stated NAICS definition and 15,828-establishment statistic could not be directly verified from the reopened content. The establishment count is decorative and does not drive a researched primitive.
The BLS page supports the listed duties, 265,900 jobs in 2024, 2% projected 2024-34 growth, digital-presence demand, and possible reduction in freelance contracting from automated design tools.

The April 2026 Adobe article reports a March survey of more than 400 creative professionals and more than 400 marketers, 94% faster production, a majority at least 50% faster, rising content demand, and nearly three in four concerned about impossible output expectations.

The June 2026 Adobe research page documents the two job-posting waves, 1,433-person US/UK survey, lower use in collaboration, self-service loss of some small deliverables, and growing importance of selection, direction, rights review, collaboration, and accountability.

The exact Adobe resource page supports that most creative professionals spend at least ten hours weekly collaborating with AI and identifies the March 2026 survey, but its reopened text does not expose the claimed 81% time-savings figure or the stated sample of more than 800 US-based respondents.

The report supports its 1,452-leader and 3,172-position bases, roughly two-thirds production staffing, one-third AI implementation by 2Q 2026, mixed pricing, 20% raising rates in 2026, 13% average 2025 net margin, AI-related pricing pressure, and strategic acquisition activity. The reopened page does not directly expose the narrower claim that design shops skew project-based, and its population is digital agencies rather than NAICS 541430 firms.

The product page reports 151 agency leaders, a primarily North American sample fielded in February 2025, and 36% open to selling, 34% open to buying, with nearly half planning possible M&A participation.

The April 2026 Capstone update reports marketing-services M&A rising each year since 2023 at a 4.7% average annual rate through 2025 and digital-marketing targets at 39.5% of YTD transactions after 9.7% growth.

The June 2026 Adobe release reports more than 16,000 creators across eight countries, defines them as emerging and professional social-first creators, and states that 85% want the final creative decision to remain with the creator.

records.title: Graphic Design Services

caveats: a: No published source directly measures wage-weighted AI exposure for six-digit NAICS 541620.
a: O*NET describes an important occupation rather than the industry's full occupation and service mix.
a: Current use of software in baseline workflows is not separately measured, so the not-yet-automated adjustment is judgmental.
rho: Architecture and engineering firms overlap with but are not identical to environmental consulting firms.
rho: Reported tool use does not establish enterprise-wide deployment or realized labor substitution.
rho: Survey respondents may be more digitally engaged than the full small-firm population.
e: Public sources do not measure the eligible share of the frozen firm population.
e: NAICS classification can lag business-model changes and does not reveal repeat revenue or owner dependence.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries rather than environmental consulting specifically.
s5: The industry M&A source is advisory commentary and does not provide a denominator-based transfer rate.
s5: High deal visibility among larger or sponsor-backed firms may not represent all lower-middle-market firms.
q: Exponent is a diversified scientific consultancy, not a pure-play sample of NAICS 541620.
q: The pure-play environmental filing is older and reflects one public company.
q: Retention through incremental volume when staff are constrained is plausible but not directly measured.
d5: The BLS projection is occupation-based, broader than NAICS 541620, and spans ten rather than five years.
d5: Employment is an imperfect proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service demand.
d5: PFAS policy is changing: the current EPA maintains some requirements while proposing deadline extensions and rescission of others.
o: The estimate extrapolates from representative regulated workflows rather than measuring every service line.
o: Low-risk reporting and screening may become more self-service than the base assumes.
o: State-specific licensing and sign-off rules vary.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based and recurring advisory work sold to businesses and public entities under time-and-materials, cost-plus, fixed-price, unit-price, and task-order arrangements; labor hours are the main economic input and reimbursable field or subcontract costs may be passed through.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house environmental departments, solo nonemployers, environmental remediation contractors classified in NAICS 562910, environmental engineering establishments classified in NAICS 541330, testing laboratories, and firms outside the lower-middle-market size lens.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced environmental assessment, compliance, permitting, risk analysis, waste and pollution-control advice, and remediation planning to external commercial or public customers.

a.bridge: O*NET task descriptions for environmental scientists are used as a task-content proxy for the broader wage-weighted industry. Data synthesis, standards review, monitoring analysis, technical communication, and report preparation are treated as substantially AI-exposed; site inspection, sampling, interviews, stakeholder work, and consequential professional judgment are treated as less exposed. This likely overstates exposure for field-heavy firms and understates it for permitting, reporting, and data-heavy firms.
caveats: No published source directly measures wage-weighted AI exposure for six-digit NAICS 541620.
O*NET describes an important occupation rather than the industry's full occupation and service mix.
Current use of software in baseline workflows is not separately measured, so the not-yet-automated adjustment is judgmental.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study or anonymized timesheet extract from a representative sample of 541620 firms, mapped by task to field, analysis, documentation, review, and client-facing work, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: A large portion of consultant time is spent searching records, structuring environmental data, checking requirements, drafting reports, preparing permit material, and communicating technical findings, all amenable to AI assistance or avoided junior hiring. The ceiling remains well below full automation because the service basket also contains physical reconnaissance, sampling oversight, client interaction, and expert interpretation.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of environmental scientists and specialists to grow 4% from 2024 to 2034 and specifically expects businesses to continue using environmental consultants. A five-year geometric interpolation is about 1.02; the base is set slightly above that because active PFAS investigation, monitoring, liability, and compliance work adds demand, while the wide range recognizes rule changes, timing delays, public-budget risk, and that occupational employment is not constant-price service quantity.
caveats: The BLS projection is occupation-based, broader than NAICS 541620, and spans ten rather than five years.
Employment is an imperfect proxy for constant-price, constant-quality service demand.
PFAS policy is changing: the current EPA maintains some requirements while proposing deadline extensions and rescission of others.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit real-revenue forecast decomposed by compliance, assessment, permitting, remediation planning, and sustainability services, together with final PFAS rule outcomes and federal/state environmental spending plans, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: The service basket is supported by recurring compliance, transactions and liability diligence, infrastructure and land-development review, pollution prevention, and emerging-contaminant work. PFAS rules create investigation and monitoring needs, but current proposals also demonstrate that deadlines and regulated substances can change, so policy-dependent growth is not guaranteed.

caveats: Public sources do not measure the eligible share of the frozen firm population.
NAICS classification can lag business-model changes and does not reveal repeat revenue or owner dependence.
e.change_evidence: A stratified audit of Census Business Register descriptions or commercial firm profiles for the frozen lower-middle-market population, coding customer type, recurrence, owner dependence, and actual primary service, would replace this judgment.
e.rationale: The six-digit industry is defined around advice sold to businesses and other organizations, so most employer firms in the lower-middle-market are external-service operations within the lens. Deductions cover firms dominated by one-off engagements, owner-dependent boutiques that do not constitute transferable operations, and records whose actual activity belongs in remediation, engineering, or testing classifications.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541620 ($9,743,109,286) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541620 ($22,793,926,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $237,012 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541620); class <5: 5,655 firms, avg revenue/firm $366,869 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $57,415 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,132 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,312,107 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $205,345 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 731 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,656,194 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $415,694 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 613 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,002,701 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,252,423 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 151 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,041,159 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,327,441 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $83,292,287 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $13,035,243 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 764

caveats: The estimate extrapolates from representative regulated workflows rather than measuring every service line.
Low-risk reporting and screening may become more self-service than the base assumes.
State-specific licensing and sign-off rules vary.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and procurement data measuring which environmental scopes have been insourced, eliminated, or purchased as stand-alone software, plus regulator acceptance of machine-generated submissions without professional review, would materially move this estimate.
o.rationale: Most year-five demand should still require an accountable consulting operator because work joins site reconnaissance, interviews, sampling context, professional judgment, client-specific risk decisions, and signed or supervised regulatory conclusions. EPA's All Appropriate Inquiries regime illustrates the durable need for visual inspection and work by or under the responsible charge of a qualified environmental professional. Software can self-serve portions of screening, monitoring, and drafting, but is unlikely to replace the accountable firm for most consequential engagements.

q.bridge: Exponent, a public scientific and engineering consultancy with an Environmental and Health segment, reported 78% of 2025 revenue under time-and-materials arrangements and 22% fixed-price; an older pure environmental consultancy filing also describes revenue as principally the sale of labor hours under a mix of time-and-materials, fixed-price, and cost-plus contracts. These billing mixes are used as proxies for 541620 retention: fixed-price work can retain more labor savings, whereas hourly and cost-plus work passes through much of the benefit. Large-public-firm contract mix may understate or overstate fixed-fee use among smaller firms.
caveats: Exponent is a diversified scientific consultancy, not a pure-play sample of NAICS 541620.
The pure-play environmental filing is older and reflects one public company.
Retention through incremental volume when staff are constrained is plausible but not directly measured.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level billing data from a representative 541620 panel showing revenue by pricing type, renewal cadence, realized write-downs, backlog, and post-automation rate or fee changes would materially narrow this range.
q.rationale: AI can lift margins on fixed-scope deliverables and can convert scarce staff capacity into additional work, but much environmental consulting is explicitly billed by hours or reimbursed cost. Renewals, public procurement, customer negotiation, and competitive repricing should share a substantial fraction of mature savings with clients over five years.

rho.bridge: A 2025 survey of nearly 700 U.S. and Canadian architecture and engineering firms found 53% using AI or machine learning and about two-thirds using generative AI; this adjacent project-based technical-consulting population is used to anchor five-year implementation in environmental consulting. The estimate rises above current adoption because the horizon is five years, then is discounted for verification, data integration, compliance, and legacy-system barriers. It may overstate implementation in small firms and regulated deliverables, but understate catch-up driven by talent shortages.
caveats: Architecture and engineering firms overlap with but are not identical to environmental consulting firms.
Reported tool use does not establish enterprise-wide deployment or realized labor substitution.
Survey respondents may be more digitally engaged than the full small-firm population.
rho.change_evidence: A 541620-specific survey measuring production use by workflow, firm size, percentage of projects touched, review burden, realized hours saved, and failed deployments would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Adoption is already broad enough to make implementation credible, particularly for proposals, document extraction, data analysis, drafting, and knowledge retrieval. Full operational capture should lag tool usage because environmental outputs require traceability, quality control, professional review, secure client data, and integration with project and geospatial systems.

s5.bridge: Gallup's U.S. employer-business survey supplies a 22% five-year sell-or-transfer anchor. The base is adjusted modestly upward because 2025 environmental and sustainability consulting market evidence describes a sellers' market, active acquisition demand down to micro-acquisitions, talent-driven buying, and ownership-transition pressure. The adjustment could overstate qualifying transfers if announced buyer interest does not convert or if activity is concentrated in larger AEC firms.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries rather than environmental consulting specifically.
The industry M&A source is advisory commentary and does not provide a denominator-based transfer rate.
High deal visibility among larger or sponsor-backed firms may not represent all lower-middle-market firms.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census for U.S. 541620 firms, linked to the frozen firm-size universe and separating external control sales from internal recapitalizations, closures, and asset purchases, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Environmental consulting has strategic buyers and private-equity-backed consolidators seeking scarce technical talent, local relationships, and specialized capabilities, which supports transfer likelihood above the all-employer base. Still, many small firms remain independent, transfers take time, internal reorganizations do not qualify, and owner relationships or credentials can impair saleability.

records.principal_driver: Repeatable document-and-data-heavy regulatory workflows can let scarce qualified professionals supervise more output while legally anchored demand persists.
records.principal_weakness: Predominantly time-and-materials billing can pass AI-driven hour reductions to customers before the operator retains them as profit.

caveats: The cited Census page was reachable but did not render the claimed six-digit definition or exclusions, so S1 does not independently substantiate the lens, e, or the industry-definition portion of a and o.

The a and rho bases are judgmental transformations from occupation task content and adjacent A&E adoption, not direct measures of wage-weighted exposure or five-year production implementation in NAICS 541620; the PROXY labels, bridges, and ranges disclose this limitation.

The s5 uplift from 22% to 27% relies on advisory descriptions of a sellers' market rather than a denominator-based rate of qualifying external-control transfers; Gallup's transfer category is broader than the methodology's qualifying-sale concept.

The q value is inferred from billing mix, which does not directly measure retained AI savings, and Exponent's 78%/22% split is company-wide rather than specific to its Environmental and Health segment.

The d5 base is extrapolated from a broader occupation forecast and policy-specific PFAS activity rather than a six-digit constant-price demand forecast; the PFAS pages establish obligations and uncertainty, not an industry-wide 1.03 quantity ratio.

The o estimate extrapolates durable professional involvement from the AAI workflow to a heterogeneous service basket; less consequential screening and reporting work could become more self-service than the base assumes.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census URL reopened, but the rendered page showed only the generic NAICS interface and an empty definition result; it did not expose the 541620 definition or exclusions claimed in the packet. Because a remains supported by S2 and e and o are explicitly estimates with other rationale or evidence, this source-page defect does not change the base tier or action category.

O*NET lists environmental-data collection and analysis, standards and regulation review, permitting, technical communication, inspections, and advisory tasks, supporting the disclosed occupational task-content proxy.
BLS reports 4% employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and says businesses are expected to continue consulting environmental scientists and specialists.
Deltek states that its A&E study covered nearly 700 firms, that 53% use AI or machine learning, and that two-thirds use generative AI for tasks including proposals and data analysis.
ACEC identifies ethical concerns, cultural resistance, lack of standards, and legacy-system integration as adoption barriers and describes the interviews and surveys informing the report.
Exponent's filing reports 78% total company revenue from time-and-materials engagements and 22% from fixed-price engagements in 2025 and separately identifies its Environmental and Health segment.
The filing states that substantially all revenue came from environmental consulting principally through labor hours and reports a mix of time-and-materials, fixed-price, and cost-plus contracts.
Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years in its fall 2024 survey.

EFCG supports a sellers' environment, micro-acquisitions, talent-driven buying, generational transition pressures, and interest in outside capital, but supplies no denominator-based transfer rate and frames several observations across the broader AEC market.

EPA states that AAI requires visual inspection, that many activities must be conducted by or under the responsible charge of a qualified environmental professional, and that the report is signed by such a professional.

EPA confirms the effective CERCLA designation, release reporting, investigation, cleanup, cost-recovery, and enforcement implications for PFOA and PFOS; the packet's property-transfer implication is reasonable but not stated directly on this page.
EPA describes monitoring and compliance requirements and its May 2026 proposals to preserve PFOA and PFOS limits while offering a deadline extension and rescinding standards for several other PFAS.
