lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring or repeat gate and tipping fees, service agreements based on waste type, frequency, weight or volume, municipal or commercial processing contracts, combined collection fees, and secondary revenue from compost, recovered products, environmental attributes, or related management services.
lens.excluded_activities: Hazardous-waste treatment, solid-waste landfills, combustors and incinerators, sewer and sewage-treatment systems, materials recovery facilities primarily sorting recyclables, pure waste collection without treatment, captive internal facilities, public programs without a transferable operating business, and firms outside the lower-middle-market size band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators repeatedly providing external customers with nonhazardous waste treatment or disposal other than landfill, combustion, incineration, or sewer treatment, including commercial composting and other physical, chemical, or biological treatment facilities and combined local collection-and-treatment operations.

a.bridge: O*NET process-machine tasks and BLS treatment-plant operator duties are used as proxies for the mixed 562219 workforce. Instrument review, anomaly detection, dosing and process recommendations, logs, regulatory reports, scheduling, routing, billing, and maintenance planning are treated as AI-exposed; material loading, contamination removal, sampling, cleaning, equipment operation, repairs, and site inspection are treated as less exposed. These occupation proxies may overstate digital exposure for low-tech composting and understate it for sensor-rich industrial treatment. The supplied l value of 1.0835 is a vintage- and denominator-sensitive dataset anomaly and is not treated as a literal feasible labor-cost share.
caveats: No public occupation mix or time-use study exists for NAICS 562219.
The code's treatment technologies vary widely, so exposure can differ materially by facility type.
The frozen l value exceeds one because of its stated wage, receipts, vintage, and harmonization construction and should not be interpreted as a literal controllable compensation share.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted staffing census and task diary across composting, industrial nonhazardous treatment, and combined collection-and-treatment firms, explicitly measuring already automated processes and contractor labor, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: Treatment facilities generate repeat operational, laboratory, customer, and compliance data that AI can structure and analyze, but most frontline work manipulates variable physical waste streams and equipment. Even computerized plants require manual operation during failures, sampling, maintenance, safety response, and licensed or accountable oversight. The estimate therefore assigns a meaningful but minority share of current wage-weighted work to not-yet-automated opportunity.

d5.bridge: EPA reports 66.2 million tons of wasted food from retail, food service, and residential sources in 2019 with only 5% composted, describes mandatory recycling and composting programs that require services, and finds that existing organics facilities process about 28.3 million tons while additional infrastructure is needed. These organics-specific indicators are used as a growth proxy for the broader 562219 service basket. They likely overstate growth for mature or non-organics treatment niches and may understate local growth where diversion mandates and landfill constraints are binding.
caveats: The strongest demand evidence concerns organics and composting rather than the full heterogeneous code.
EPA's tonnage statistics are not a five-year constant-price service forecast and use different reference years and facility populations.
Food-waste prevention and on-site or community composting can reduce outsourced commercial throughput even when diversion rises.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit real-revenue or throughput forecast decomposed into composting, anaerobic or biological processing, physical and chemical treatment, and combined collection-treatment, paired with state mandate implementation, permitted capacity, feedstock contracts, closures, and end-product demand, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Organics diversion, commercial composting mandates, landfill-methane priorities, and insufficient processing infrastructure create a credible growth path. Growth is constrained by contamination, siting opposition, land and transport costs, permitting delays, weak end markets, self-composting, prevention of food waste, and inconsistent enforcement. Because the code also contains treatment activities outside organics, the base is moderate rather than aggressive.

caveats: NAICS classification does not identify private control, recurring revenue, captive status, or normalized EBITDA.
The margin-bridged firm count uses a broad sector margin that may not fit composting and specialized treatment equally.
The boundary with materials recovery, collection, remediation, wastewater, and agricultural composting can be difficult in establishment records.
e.change_evidence: A record-level audit of the frozen 56 firms using Business Register descriptions, state permits, facility websites, customer contracts, ownership records, and normalized financials to code primary activity, external recurrence, control transferability, and EBITDA would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition centers on operating treatment and disposal facilities or combined collection and treatment, which normally creates recurring external-service revenue. Deductions cover public or captive operations, firms whose actual activity belongs in materials recovery or collection, nonrepeat project work, owner-dependent microbusinesses, and records whose margin-bridged size differs from normalized EBITDA. Eligibility is likely a majority of the frozen population but cannot be observed from public aggregates.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 562219 ($1,729,804,382) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 562219 ($1,702,171,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2099 (margins.json[562]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Environmental & Waste Services EBITDA/Sales 20.99%); revenue/employee $341,185 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 562219); class <5: 341 firms, avg revenue/firm $357,824 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $75,107 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 85 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,607,859 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $337,490 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,012,896 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $632,407 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,415,605 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,186,235 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $76,447,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $16,046,351 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,058,231 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,568,623 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 56

caveats: The estimate combines materially different treatment processes with different staffing and self-service potential.
Decentralized or on-site organics processing may displace some external facility demand.
Automation can reduce on-site staffing while leaving legal and operational accountability with the same firm.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement data measuring shifts to on-site treatment, community composting, alternative disposal, or software-only services, plus permit and insurer requirements for qualified on-site operators and evidence of commercially autonomous facilities, would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: Most surviving demand requires an accountable operator to accept and characterize waste, control contamination, run treatment equipment, manage biological or chemical conditions, sample outputs, maintain the site, and comply with permits. Software can self-serve scheduling, documentation, billing, and limited monitoring; generators can also prevent waste, compost on site, or redirect material to alternative pathways. Those substitutions reduce but do not eliminate the operator requirement for centralized commercial treatment.

q.bridge: SusGlobal's organic-composting filing provides a direct service-model example but from a Canadian small public company: revenue comes from tipping fees for delivered waste and compost sales, with service pricing varying by frequency, waste type, weight, volume, and regional market conditions. These per-load or per-ton arrangements can retain labor and process savings until prices reset, while competitive gate pricing, municipal tenders, commodity-linked output economics, and customer negotiation share benefits over time. The example may overstate merchant flexibility or understate fixed-contract protection in U.S. operators.
caveats: One Canadian composting company does not establish the contract mix of U.S. NAICS 562219.
Pricing form does not directly measure the discounted share of AI benefits retained.
Compost, recovered-product, and environmental-credit prices can transmit gains or losses independently of treatment productivity.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and invoice-level data from a representative panel showing revenue by fixed fee, per-ton tip fee, cost-plus, municipal tender, commodity rebate, and product sale, together with renewal cadence and post-productivity repricing, would materially narrow the range.
q.rationale: Treatment is typically priced for an accepted physical quantity or contracted service rather than for disclosed labor hours, so efficiency gains can accrue to the operator in the near term. Retention is moderated by competitive tipping fees, transport alternatives, pass-through clauses, output-price volatility, renewal repricing, and the need to reinvest savings in contamination control and compliance.

rho.bridge: NIST's manufacturing evidence on AI adoption, predictive maintenance, process monitoring, and barriers, plus Census all-business adoption by firm size, is applied to physical waste-treatment operations. The base assumes five-year expansion in sensor analytics, maintenance, documentation, and back-office workflows while discounting for small-firm capital limits, inconsistent input data, outdoor operations, legacy equipment, safety, cybersecurity, and environmental validation. Manufacturing adoption may overstate implementation at low-tech compost sites but understate it at sophisticated continuous treatment plants.
caveats: Manufacturing and all-business surveys do not isolate waste treatment or realized labor substitution.
Reported AI use includes chatbots and business functions that may not touch treatment production.
Implementation feasibility is likely bimodal between simple outdoor sites and instrumented plants.
rho.change_evidence: Structured interviews and system demonstrations from at least fifteen LMM 562219 operators, recording workflow coverage, validated hours saved, avoided downtime, deployment failures, override rates, and total integration cost, would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Many candidate workflows use mature data and document tools rather than autonomous equipment, making partial implementation credible within five years. Broad capture will lag because contaminated and changing feedstocks make model transfer difficult, smaller firms lack data engineering capacity, and operators must preserve manual fallback and regulatory traceability.

s5.bridge: Gallup's 22% five-year sell-or-transfer intention for U.S. employer-business owners is the general anchor. Waste Dive reports sustained high waste and recycling M&A activity, including $10.9 billion of public-company acquisition spending in 2024, numerous tuck-ins, treatment facilities, and active pipelines; this supports an upward adjustment for industry buyer demand. The adjustment may overstate LMM 562219 transfers because the reported spending includes very large collection, landfill, recycling, hazardous, Canadian, and minority transactions, while permitted treatment assets can also be attractive scarce infrastructure.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers.
Waste-sector M&A totals are dominated by large and adjacent business models and provide no 562219 denominator.
Environmental liabilities, permits, land-use approvals, and customer-contract assignment can prevent or delay a sale.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census linked to U.S. 562219 facility permits and the frozen size population, separating qualifying control sales from minority investments, asset purchases, internal reorganizations, public contract awards, and closures, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Strategic waste operators and infrastructure-backed buyers seek permitted facilities, local density, feedstock contracts, and technical capabilities, while independent owners face compliance and capital pressures. Not every planned transfer closes, and some transactions acquire routes or facilities without a separately qualifying control business, so the range remains broad.

records.principal_driver: Mandated organics diversion and repeat physical treatment fees can support data-assisted process and maintenance gains at scarce permitted facilities.
records.principal_weakness: Heterogeneous plant types, physical frontline work, permit and liability risk, and an anomalous compensation anchor make the labor opportunity and target universe unusually uncertain.

caveats: S1 is labeled as a 2022 NAICS source, but the reopened Census archive page is headed 1997 NAICS. The displayed 562219 definition supports the lens, so this is a citation-metadata issue rather than a score-changing defect.

The a and o assessments rely on adjacent process-machine and wastewater-operator duties rather than an observed 562219 staffing or time-use mix. The packet labels and bounds this limitation honestly, but facility heterogeneity remains substantial.

The rho base translates manufacturing and all-business AI adoption into five-year implementation at LMM waste-treatment sites. The sources establish adoption and relevant use cases and barriers, but not realized workflow coverage or labor substitution in 562219.

The s5 uplift above Gallup's broad 22% intention anchor is informed mainly by transactions and pipelines of large public waste companies across adjacent, hazardous, and Canadian activities. It indicates buyer appetite but supplies no LMM 562219 transfer denominator.

S8 establishes per-unit and contract-specific pricing but does not measure the discounted share of AI-enabled benefits retained by U.S. 562219 operators; q therefore remains a broad pricing-form proxy.
The d5 base is a disclosed inference from organics mandates, low composting penetration, and infrastructure need, not a five-year constant-price forecast for the full heterogeneous 562219 code.

sources_audited.note: The archived Census page supports the 562219 definition, exclusions, combined local collection-and-treatment activity, and compost-dump example. Its page header identifies the material as 1997 NAICS, not the packet's stated 2022 title, but this metadata mismatch does not alter the substantive boundary used.

O*NET documents loading, equipment operation, instrument monitoring, control adjustment, sampling, inspection, logging, cleaning, and impurity-removal tasks. It supports the stated task proxy, not a measured occupation mix for 562219.

BLS supports computerized monitoring, sampling, regulatory reporting, maintenance, manual fallback, licensing, emergency duties, and the projected 7% employment decline from 2024 to 2034. Wastewater treatment is adjacent to and excluded from the lens, as the packet discloses through its proxy treatment.

NIST reports 46% current manufacturer AI-tool use and more than 80% expecting increased use within two years, and identifies predictive analytics, maintenance, process applications, data, cost, skills, cybersecurity, and legacy-system barriers.

Census reports December 2025-May 2026 all-business AI usage of 17%-20%, expected six-month usage of 20%-23%, and higher current adoption among larger firms. It also states the question covers AI in any business function, consistent with the packet's caveat.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The measure is an intention for employer businesses generally, not completed qualifying 562219 transfers.

Waste Dive reports $10.9 billion of 2024 acquisitions by five major public waste companies, numerous acquisitions and tuck-ins, treatment-related transactions, and active pipelines. The article is dominated by large, adjacent, Canadian, and hazardous-waste activity rather than LMM 562219 transactions.

The filing supports tipping-fee and compost revenue, contract-specific service pricing by frequency, waste type, weight, volume, and regional factors, plus physical processing, curing, and screening. It demonstrates one Canadian issuer's pricing form but does not measure retained AI benefits.

EPA explains that mandatory recycling and composting rules can require governments or contractors to provide services to all customers and can impose service-provider conditions. It establishes a policy mechanism, not a national throughput forecast.
EPA reports 66.2 million tons of wasted food from the stated sectors in 2019 and 5% composted, and describes facility scale, feedstock, equipment, and contamination considerations.

EPA reports about 28.3 million tons of organic waste processed, states that additional processing infrastructure is needed, and details feedstock contamination, equipment and capital, permitting, land, labor, transport, and market barriers.

caveats: a: BLS occupational shares cover broad NAICS 621400 and are not specific to outpatient mental health and substance abuse centers.
a: O*NET and the Occupational Outlook Handbook describe occupations across employers and do not measure wage-weighted hours or current automation in 621420.
a: Mental health, substance use, intensive outpatient, crisis, and medication-supported programs have different task mixes.
rho: No representative survey measures AI implementation or labor realization in NAICS 621420.
rho: Medicare telehealth policy demonstrates remote delivery feasibility rather than AI adoption or labor substitution.
rho: High-acuity, crisis, and substance use workflows may implement more slowly than routine outpatient psychotherapy.
e: No representative firm-level source measures the combined recurrence, control, transferability, concentration, compliance, and normalized-earnings screens.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad healthcare-facilities margin and may misclassify grant-funded, nonprofit, or program-intensive centers.
e: The code combines mental health and substance use centers with materially different reimbursement, staffing, licensing, and acuity.
s5: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying control sales.
s5: The proxy does not capture nonprofit, public, health-system, or sponsor-backed ownership patterns in 621420.
s5: No representative source reports five-year control-transfer completion for outpatient behavioral-health centers.
q: Medicare and SAMHSA sources establish administered rates, insurance coverage, and patient cost sharing but do not measure the industry's payer mix or retained automation benefit.
q: No representative source reports contract repricing, grant treatment of savings, or post-automation margins for 621420.
q: Retention varies between fee-for-service therapy, intensive programs, CCBHC-style payment, grants, and self-pay services.
d5: BLS projects counselor employment rather than constant-price demand for the current 621420 service basket.
d5: Insurance coverage statements do not measure utilization or demand growth and state Medicaid benefits vary.
d5: Workforce shortages can prevent underlying need from becoming realized center volume.

o: Licensure and task evidence establishes accountable work but does not quantify how much year-five demand will remain with centers rather than independent clinicians, primary care, or digital providers.
o: Telehealth often preserves a licensed operator while bypassing a physical center, so operator retention and site retention are not identical.
o: Digital substitution is more plausible for screening and low-acuity support than for crisis, severe illness, medication, and intensive substance use treatment.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Episode-based and recurring outpatient revenue paid by Medicaid, Medicare, commercial insurers, self-pay patients, grants, and public programs for assessments, individual and group therapy, medication-related services, intensive outpatient care, case management, crisis services, and care coordination.
lens.excluded_activities: Psychiatric hospitals, residential treatment facilities, captive hospital departments, independent practitioner offices classified elsewhere, peer-support groups without accountable clinical operations, shell entities, non-control financing situations, and centers without transferable operations or normalized EBITDA in the target band.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market outpatient centers providing recurring or repeat diagnosis, counseling, psychotherapy, medication-supported care, crisis stabilization, case management, and related treatment for mental health and substance use conditions to external patients.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS occupational employment for broad NAICS 621400, BLS duties and industry employment for behavioral-health counselors, O*NET counselor tasks, and the official 621420 definition. Transformation: mapped records, mandated forms, scheduling, billing, intake, routine follow-up, coordination, and structured measurement to exposed hours, while assigning lower exposure to therapy, diagnosis, risk assessment, crisis intervention, medication decisions, and relationship-intensive work. Error direction: broad outpatient data underweight counselors and social workers relative to 621420 and may bias exposure toward medical workflows, while unmeasured clerical work embedded in clinician time can bias exposure downward.
caveats: BLS occupational shares cover broad NAICS 621400 and are not specific to outpatient mental health and substance abuse centers.
O*NET and the Occupational Outlook Handbook describe occupations across employers and do not measure wage-weighted hours or current automation in 621420.
Mental health, substance use, intensive outpatient, crisis, and medication-supported programs have different task mixes.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task and time study from multi-site outpatient behavioral-health operators, split by clinician type, program intensity, administrative work, documentation, crisis coverage, and pre/post AI deployment, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Intake, scheduling, eligibility, coding, billing, mandated forms, progress-note drafting, treatment-plan updates, referral coordination, reminders, routine follow-up, and measurement-based care are exposed to AI assistance or avoidable hiring. Therapeutic alliance, diagnosis, crisis response, risk assessment, medication decisions, group facilitation, and accountable clinical judgment remain labor-intensive.

d5.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS projects 17% national employment growth for substance abuse, behavioral disorder, and mental health counselors from 2024 to 2034, and SAMHSA documents insurance and public-program coverage for treatment. Transformation: translated the occupational growth signal into a more moderate five-year real-demand increase for outpatient centers, then widened the range for access limits, payer policy, setting shifts, telehealth, prevention, and clinician supply. Error direction: employment growth can overstate service demand if labor intensity rises or shortages persist, and can understate demand if productivity and telehealth expand capacity.
caveats: BLS projects counselor employment rather than constant-price demand for the current 621420 service basket.
Insurance coverage statements do not measure utilization or demand growth and state Medicaid benefits vary.
Workforce shortages can prevent underlying need from becoming realized center volume.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price encounter histories from commercial claims, Medicaid T-MSIS, Medicare, SAMHSA facility reporting, and multi-site operators, separated by mental health, substance use, treatment intensity, telehealth, and site entry, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Behavioral-health treatment demand is supported by persistent mental health and substance use need, expanded coverage, and workforce projections for counselors. Access constraints, payer limits, clinician shortages, prevention, digital self-help, policy changes, and shifts toward other settings temper realized center-level growth.

caveats: No representative firm-level source measures the combined recurrence, control, transferability, concentration, compliance, and normalized-earnings screens.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad healthcare-facilities margin and may misclassify grant-funded, nonprofit, or program-intensive centers.
The code combines mental health and substance use centers with materially different reimbursement, staffing, licensing, and acuity.
e.change_evidence: A center-level dataset covering ownership and tax status, affiliation, payer and grant mix, accreditation, licenses, referral concentration, clinician dependence, service-line margins, normalized EBITDA, and change-of-control requirements would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The industry is intrinsically an outsourced external service and many patients require recurring episodes or longitudinal care. Eligibility is reduced by nonprofit and public ownership, health-system affiliation, grant dependence, weak payer contracts, clinician or referral concentration, licenses and accreditations, owner dependence, poor outcomes or compliance, and economics outside the target band.

o.bridge: Source population/quantity: the official industry definition, O*NET and BLS counselor duties and licensure, CMS behavioral-health telehealth rules, and SAMHSA descriptions of licensed therapy and counseling. Transformation: assigned high operator need to diagnosis, therapeutic engagement, risk assessment, crisis response, treatment planning, medication coordination, mandated records, and escalation, while discounting for telehealth-only delivery, primary-care integration, self-guided digital care, AI support, and independent practitioners. Error direction: current licensing and clinical norms may overstate center dependence as software and remote models improve, while safety, liability, payer, and high-acuity requirements may keep accountable-operator need higher.
caveats: Licensure and task evidence establishes accountable work but does not quantify how much year-five demand will remain with centers rather than independent clinicians, primary care, or digital providers.
Telehealth often preserves a licensed operator while bypassing a physical center, so operator retention and site retention are not identical.
Digital substitution is more plausible for screening and low-acuity support than for crisis, severe illness, medication, and intensive substance use treatment.
o.change_evidence: Patient-cohort and outcomes data comparing staffed centers with digital-only therapy, AI support, self-guided care, primary-care integration, and telehealth-only models, including crisis events, retention, escalation, and payer acceptance, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Telehealth can remove the facility from some visits and software can provide screening, education, reminders, structured exercises, and low-acuity support. Most of the current basket still requires an accountable licensed operator for diagnosis, therapeutic relationships, treatment plans, risk assessment, crisis intervention, medication coordination, mandated records, and escalation.

caveats: Medicare and SAMHSA sources establish administered rates, insurance coverage, and patient cost sharing but do not measure the industry's payer mix or retained automation benefit.
No representative source reports contract repricing, grant treatment of savings, or post-automation margins for 621420.
Retention varies between fee-for-service therapy, intensive programs, CCBHC-style payment, grants, and self-pay services.
q.change_evidence: Matched center-level reimbursement, denial, staffing, utilization, and gross-margin data before and after automation, separated by payer, grant, service intensity, mental health, substance use, telehealth, and self-pay, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Operators can retain some administrative and documentation productivity between contract resets and can use capacity gains to serve more patients. Retention is limited by administered Medicare and Medicaid rates, insurer negotiation, prospective or bundled payment, grants, sliding fees, utilization management, staffing requirements, patient affordability, and pressure to convert gains into access or outcomes.

caveats: No representative survey measures AI implementation or labor realization in NAICS 621420.
Medicare telehealth policy demonstrates remote delivery feasibility rather than AI adoption or labor substitution.
High-acuity, crisis, and substance use workflows may implement more slowly than routine outpatient psychotherapy.
rho.change_evidence: Adoption, error, quality, and labor-hour evidence from multi-state behavioral-health groups, CCBHCs, intensive outpatient programs, and substance use treatment networks using ambient notes, automated intake, coding, and patient-support tools would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years permits diffusion of ambient documentation, intake and triage support, coding assistance, patient communications, outcome tracking, scheduling, and care coordination. Implementation is constrained by privacy, safety, hallucination risk, payer documentation rules, fragmented records, state licensure, crisis liability, clinician acceptance, and small-provider integration capacity.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup's five-year sale-or-transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners. Transformation: used the reported 22% intention rate as an upper anchor and discounted for noncompletion, non-control and family transfers, nonprofit and public ownership, payer and grant approvals, licensure, accreditation, clinician retention, referral concentration, and diligence failures. Error direction: broad intentions overstate completed qualifying sales, while active consolidation among scaled behavioral-health groups could make the lower bound conservative.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying control sales.
The proxy does not capture nonprofit, public, health-system, or sponsor-backed ownership patterns in 621420.
No representative source reports five-year control-transfer completion for outpatient behavioral-health centers.
s5.change_evidence: Closed control-sale counts and eligible-center denominators from behavioral-health transaction databases, healthcare brokers, state licensure and change-of-ownership records, accreditation bodies, and payer enrollment files over a five-year cohort would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year sale or transfer intentions, and recurring behavioral-health operations can be transferable. The qualifying probability is discounted for nonprofit and public ownership, internal or family transfers, noncompletion, licenses, payer and grant approvals, clinician retention, referral concentration, quality, and compliance screens.

records.principal_driver: The principal driver is expanding clinician capacity by automating documentation and coordination around recurring, accountable treatment relationships.
records.principal_weakness: The principal weakness is that core therapeutic and crisis work remains licensed, interpersonal, and liability-intensive while payer and ownership structures limit retained benefit and transferable supply.

caveats: S1's cited Census page is reachable but does not expose the claimed 621420 definition in the reopened content; other cited occupational sources corroborate the core outpatient behavioral-health scope, so this source defect does not require a primitive downgrade that changes the base tier or action.

S7 and S8 were not reopenable because both cited SAMHSA pages returned HTTP 403. Their absence weakens corroboration for coverage, treatment types, and licensed-provider context, but e and q are already honest estimates, while d5 and o retain relevant support from BLS, O*NET, CMS, and the disclosed bridges; the base action remains VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

S5 establishes behavioral-health telehealth feasibility and payment policy, not AI implementation or realized labor savings. The packet explicitly acknowledges this and labels rho ESTIMATE, so it is an evidentiary limitation rather than a material defect.

The d5 bridge converts a national ten-year counselor-employment projection into five-year real demand for outpatient centers. Population, quantity, and error directions are disclosed and the 0.98-1.24 range is appropriately broad, but direct constant-price center-volume evidence is absent.

The a, e, s5, q, and o bases remain substantially model-conditioned or proxy-driven across a heterogeneous mix of mental-health, substance-use, crisis, intensive-outpatient, payer, ownership, and affiliation models. The packet labels those states honestly, provides directional bridges where required, and already restricts the action to validation.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census URL resolves to the official 2022 NAICS search page, but the reopened page does not display the 621420 definition or exclusions that the packet attributes to it.

The May 2023 OEWS page is explicitly for broad NAICS 621400 and reports occupational employment across clinical, counseling, support, management, and administrative groups, including 15.71% office and administrative support employment; the packet discloses the broader-industry population.

O*NET lists counseling, crisis intervention, suicide-risk assessment, mandated forms, progress notes, treatment records, treatment planning, referral, and coordination tasks, supporting the packet's task split while not itself quantifying automation.
BLS supports counselor duties, outpatient-center employment, private-practice licensure, and the stated 17% national employment projection from 2024 to 2034.

The CY 2025 CMS FAQ supports Medicare behavioral-health telehealth payment and remote delivery, including home, no geographic restriction, and audio-only provisions. It does not measure AI adoption or labor realization, a limitation the packet expressly discloses for rho.

The Medicare costs page states 20% beneficiary cost sharing for outpatient mental-health visits and partial-hospitalization professional services after the deductible, supporting the limited administered-payment and cost-sharing claim used for q.
The cited SAMHSA insurance-coverage page returned HTTP 403 on repeated reopening, so its asserted coverage and plan-variation content could not be independently verified.

The cited SAMHSA treatment-types page returned HTTP 403 on repeated reopening, so its asserted licensed-provider, therapy, counseling, telehealth, and setting content could not be independently verified.

Gallup's fall-2024 survey reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years; the take-public component rounds to 0%, and the packet appropriately treats the statistic as a broad intentions upper anchor rather than completed transfers.

caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix was located for NAICS 541910.
a: O*NET covers market research analysts across many industries and does not describe all polling, fieldwork, panel, or agency-support jobs.
a: MRII reports tool use and perceived benefits, not the share of wages technically substitutable.
rho: Vendor capability does not establish reliable deployment across heterogeneous client data and methods.
rho: Global professional survey results may not match smaller U.S. firms.
rho: The range excludes pricing, customer self-service, and demand effects.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with margins unlike the supplied benchmark.
e: Establishment classification does not reveal firm-level repeat revenue or founder dependence.
e: Ipsos is far larger than the lens and is used only to show that project revenue can recur through retained clients.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed qualifying transfers.
s5: The M&A source covers the broader marketing-services sector and does not provide a denominator of eligible firms.
s5: Undisclosed small transactions and family transfers are difficult to observe.
q: Public-company contract structures may not represent small custom-research agencies.
q: No source directly measures five-year sharing of AI-derived gross benefit.
q: Customer self-service and lost volume are reserved for demand primitives rather than included here.
d5: No six-digit constant-price demand forecast was found.
d5: Employment is not service quantity and can diverge as productivity changes.
d5: The forecast concerns a broader industry and a broader occupation population.
o: Synthetic-response expectations are opinions rather than observed replacement.
o: Human oversight can be performed by a client-side team rather than a lens firm.
o: Vendor product descriptions may emphasize capability and speed more than failure rates or applicability limits.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, nonprofit, media, campaign, and government customers buy recurring tracking programs, annual or multi-period subscriptions, retainers, and repeat fixed-fee custom projects. Revenue can combine design, fieldwork or panels, analysis, reporting, and advisory interpretation.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive corporate insights teams, internal support establishments, shells, non-control financing vehicles, pure software vendors, report publishers without a service operation, advertising agencies whose primary activity is not research, and firms without repeat outsourced-service customers.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly supplying external customers with custom market research, brand or customer tracking, survey and polling programs, statistical sampling, qualitative research, data collection, analysis, and research reporting.

a.bridge: O*NET describes the occupation rather than the wage-weighted staffing of NAICS 541910, while MRII surveys global research professionals rather than U.S. lower-middle-market agencies. I mapped the core tasks of data collection and analysis, survey design, literature review, coding, visualization, and report drafting to exposed work, then reduced exposure for respondent recruitment, client discovery, methodological judgment, quality assurance, facilitation, and senior interpretation. This may overstate exposure where small firms are senior-heavy and understate it where survey operations and open-end coding dominate payroll.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix was located for NAICS 541910.
O*NET covers market research analysts across many industries and does not describe all polling, fieldwork, panel, or agency-support jobs.
MRII reports tool use and perceived benefits, not the share of wages technically substitutable.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study of U.S. independent research agencies, segmented by survey operations, qualitative research, analysts, project managers, sales, and principals, with task-level current automation status.
a.rationale: O*NET identifies reporting, data collection and analysis, survey-method design, forecasting, and trend monitoring as core work. MRII finds AI already used in literature reviews and questionnaire development and widely credited with faster data processing, analysis, and report generation. Those activities cover a large share of analyst and production work, but not all judgment, relationship, fieldwork, or quality-control labor.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for NAICS 541900, which combines market research with materially different services, and projects employment for market research analysts across all industries rather than outsourced NAICS 541910 demand. I use the broader industry's 2.4% annual real-output growth and the occupation's 7% decade employment growth as upper and lower directional anchors, then temper them for the current outsourced service basket. The broad 541900 output may overstate demand because veterinary services are a large component, while analyst employment may understate research quantity if AI raises output per worker.
caveats: No six-digit constant-price demand forecast was found.
Employment is not service quantity and can diverge as productivity changes.
The forecast concerns a broader industry and a broader occupation population.
d5.change_evidence: BEA or Census real revenue indexes for six-digit NAICS 541910, combined with agency backlog, client research-budget surveys, project counts, and constant-scope pricing from a representative U.S. panel through 2031.
d5.rationale: BLS projects real output of broader other professional, scientific, and technical services to rise from 200.2 billion chained dollars in 2024 to 253.7 billion in 2034, while market-research analyst employment is projected to grow 7% over the decade because organizations increasingly use data and market research. A five-year base above current demand is defensible, but budget cyclicality and substitution away from traditional studies warrant a low case slightly below one.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with margins unlike the supplied benchmark.
Establishment classification does not reveal firm-level repeat revenue or founder dependence.
Ipsos is far larger than the lens and is used only to show that project revenue can recur through retained clients.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level sample from Data Axle or PitchBook matched to websites and customer contracts, coding ownership, external-service revenue share, recurring or repeat revenue, founder dependence, and software-only business models for firms in the frozen EBITDA band.
e.rationale: The Census industry form identifies market research, public opinion polling, statistical sampling, and survey design as core services, so most employer firms in the EBITDA band should sell external research repeatedly even when individual assignments are project-based. The deduction allows for captive or controlled establishments, software-led businesses, report publishing, non-repeat project shops, and firms whose transferable customer relationships are too dependent on one principal.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541910 ($8,069,771,700) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541910 ($26,698,162,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $318,207 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541910); class <5: 2,509 firms, avg revenue/firm $530,196 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $82,976 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 435 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,835,349 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $287,232 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 296 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,275,365 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $512,595 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 309 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,129,165 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,585,214 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $46,203,783 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,230,892 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 78 firms, avg revenue/firm $191,354,487 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $29,946,977 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 429

o.bridge: Qualtrics surveys market researchers about synthetic responses, SurveyMonkey documents self-service analysis capability, and ESOMAR sets professional oversight expectations; none measures the year-five share of U.S. outsourced research quantity requiring an independent accountable operator. I reduce operator-required quantity for self-service survey design, automated analysis, synthetic sampling, and client-side AI adoption, while preserving quantity requiring method selection, primary-data provenance, privacy and consent controls, bias review, complex qualitative work, validation, and accountable interpretation. Vendor claims may overstate elimination and thus bias the estimate downward; professional standards may preserve oversight without requiring an external agency, biasing it upward.
caveats: Synthetic-response expectations are opinions rather than observed replacement.
Human oversight can be performed by a client-side team rather than a lens firm.
Vendor product descriptions may emphasize capability and speed more than failure rates or applicability limits.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal client procurement survey linking 2026 research spend and study counts to 2031 outcomes, classified as outsourced operator-led, client self-service, software-only, eliminated, or synthetic, with separate results for regulated and complex qualitative studies.
o.rationale: SurveyMonkey can already provide conversational analysis, segmentation, and open-text coding without spreadsheets. Qualtrics reports that 71% of researchers expected synthetic responses to comprise most data collection within three years. Yet ESOMAR guidance expects suppliers to explain AI use, stress-test outputs, maintain data governance, and integrate human oversight, preserving a substantial role for an accountable operator even as routine execution self-serves.

q.bridge: Forrester is a large research and advisory provider outside the exact custom market-research lens, and Fitch describes large global Ipsos. I map their annual subscriptions and fixed-fee project structures to lower-middle-market research contracts: cost savings are initially retained within fixed scopes and prepaid subscriptions, then partially shared through renewals, competitive bids, scope expansion, and price pressure. Large firms may have more pricing power than the lens, biasing retention upward; founder-led specialists with scarce expertise may retain more, while commoditized survey shops may retain less.
caveats: Public-company contract structures may not represent small custom-research agencies.
No source directly measures five-year sharing of AI-derived gross benefit.
Customer self-service and lost volume are reserved for demand primitives rather than included here.
q.change_evidence: Anonymized contracts and renewal cohorts from at least twenty U.S. independent agencies, showing fee basis, gross-margin change after automation, realized price changes, rebids, scope additions, win rates, and customer savings clauses over multiple renewals.
q.rationale: Forrester discloses ratable annual research subscriptions and fixed-fee consulting projects, structures that do not mechanically pass through each labor saving. Ipsos operates in a fragmented, competitive market with limited contractually recurring revenue despite high client return, so discounted retention should reflect renewal repricing and customer bargaining rather than full permanent capture.

rho.bridge: MRII measures current global team adoption and attitudes, SurveyMonkey demonstrates commercially available capabilities, and ESOMAR describes governance expectations; none measures five-year implementation of the remaining exposed wage opportunity in U.S. lower-middle-market firms. I translated high and rising adoption plus available workflow tools into implementation potential, then discounted for data privacy, bias, client restrictions, validation, training, integration, and human-oversight requirements. The estimate may be high for regulated or bespoke qualitative work and low for standardized online-survey production.
caveats: Vendor capability does not establish reliable deployment across heterogeneous client data and methods.
Global professional survey results may not match smaller U.S. firms.
The range excludes pricing, customer self-service, and demand effects.
rho.change_evidence: A cohort study of independent U.S. agencies documenting baseline labor hours, deployed AI workflows, client approval rules, error and rework rates, and realized labor avoidance after at least twelve months.
rho.rationale: MRII reports team AI use of 62%, strong expectations of job improvement, and material gains in routine research tasks. Production tools can already draft and analyze surveys and classify open-ended responses. Implementation is constrained by MRII's reported bias, privacy, ethics, and human-judgment concerns and by ESOMAR's emphasis on transparency, governance, and human oversight.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans for U.S. employer-business owners across industries, while Houlihan Lokey tracks completed and announced marketing-services deals across sizes and subsectors. I use Gallup's 22% employer-owner plan rate as the center and retain a wide range because plans may not close, some transfers are internal or non-controlling, and active marketing-services bolt-ons may lift research-agency transfers above the all-industry rate. This can overstate qualifying outcomes when founder dependence blocks a sale and understate them where consolidators target data and research capabilities.
caveats: Owner intentions are not completed qualifying transfers.
The M&A source covers the broader marketing-services sector and does not provide a denominator of eligible firms.
Undisclosed small transactions and family transfers are difficult to observe.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year U.S. transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, and business brokers for NAICS 541910 firms, separating control sales from minority investments, internal reorganizations, closures, and uncompleted processes, with EBITDA-band matching.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years. Marketing-services M&A remained active in the first half of 2025, with smaller transactions and strategic add-ons dominating, and the report explicitly includes market-research deals.

records.principal_driver: A large share of research production consists of repeatable text, survey, and data workflows that current AI tools can accelerate under human review.
records.principal_weakness: The same tools are available to clients and software platforms, creating material self-service and renewal-pricing leakage.

caveats: The a and rho sources establish exposed task types, current adoption, perceived benefits, available product capabilities, and governance frictions, but do not measure a payroll-weighted automatable share or five-year realized implementation in U.S. lower-middle-market agencies. The PROXY labels and directional bridges are honest, so this does not meet the rejection test.

The e base of 0.84 is an explicit estimate: the Census form establishes industry service categories but not firm-level repeat external revenue, independence, founder dependence, or transferability in the EBITDA band.

The s5 base maps a cross-industry owner-intention rate and broad marketing-services deal activity into qualifying five-year transfers. Neither source supplies a 541910 denominator or distinguishes all control-sale outcomes, but the bridge and range disclose this limitation.

The q range is informed by large public-company contract structures and market conditions, not direct evidence of five-year AI-benefit retention for smaller custom-research agencies. The source facts are accurate, but the numerical retention judgment remains weakly anchored.

The d5 range relies on real-output growth for broader NAICS 541900 and all-industry employment growth for one occupation. These are directionally useful but do not directly forecast six-digit outsourced research quantity.

The o range translates a vendor survey of expectations, self-service product capabilities, and professional governance guidance into an external-operator requirement. None directly measures whether oversight will remain with a lens firm rather than move to clients, a limitation the packet acknowledges.

sources_audited.note: The Census form identifies market research and public opinion polling as 541910 activities and lists the cited research methods, polling, and statistical sampling and survey-design services. It does not itself measure repeat revenue or transferability, a limitation disclosed in the e estimate.

O*NET lists reporting, consumer-data collection and analysis, survey and polling method design, forecasting, trend monitoring, and collaboration as occupation tasks. The cross-industry occupation-to-industry-payroll transformation is explicitly treated as a proxy.

MRII reports 426 global online interviews in February 2025, 62% team use, 53% literature-review use, 50% questionnaire-development use, and the cited perceived benefits and concerns. These are adoption and perception measures, not realized labor substitution, as disclosed.

The ESOMAR guidance directly addresses fitness for purpose, explainability, validation, bias, transparency, human oversight and stress testing, ethical compliance, and data governance. It is governance guidance rather than an implementation-rate measure.

SurveyMonkey documents conversational analysis, data segmentation and explanations, automatic thematic categorization of open text, and AI-assisted survey creation. The source demonstrates commercial capability but not heterogeneous deployment reliability.

Qualtrics reports that 71% of market researchers agreed synthetic responses would make up more than half of data collection within three years and gives the cited motivations. It measures expectations rather than realized substitution, which the packet discloses.

Gallup reports a fall 2024 survey and that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The article also confirms the 1,264-owner analytic sample; intentions are not completed qualifying control transfers.

Houlihan Lokey reports 266 broad marketing-services deals in the first half of 2025, 8% above the first half of 2024, led by smaller transactions and strategic add-ons, and identifies market-research transactions. It supplies no eligible-firm denominator or 541910 transfer rate.

Forrester's 2025 Form 10-K describes annual or multi-year subscription products recognized over the contract term and fixed-fee consulting projects generally completed over short periods. It supports billing structure, not the numerical retention assumption.

Fitch describes Ipsos's competitive fragmented market, recurring contracts slightly above 30% of revenue, project-based visibility limits, repeated revenue from returning large clients, and expected U.S. bolt-ons. Ipsos is much larger than the target lens, as disclosed.

BLS projects 7% employment growth for market research analysts and marketing specialists from 2024 to 2034 and attributes growth to increasing use of data and market research. Occupation employment is not outsourced-service quantity.

BLS Table 2.11 reports 541900 real output of $200.2 billion in 2024 and $253.7 billion in 2034, in chained 2017 dollars, with a 2.4% compound annual growth rate. The cited industry is broader than 541910.

records.title: Marketing Research and Public Opinion Polling

caveats: a: Occupation evidence includes first-party collectors outside the target business population.
a: Task exposure is not equivalent to safe end-to-end autonomy.
rho: Federal rules do not measure adoption or realized margin gains.
rho: State law, client requirements, and data quality can impose tighter constraints.
e: No direct national estimate of transferable EBITDA in the target size band was found.
e: Client consent, licensing, and compliance diligence vary materially by transaction.
s5: Intentions include family transfers and do not equal completed arm's-length sales.
s5: The survey is not specific to collection agencies or the target EBITDA range.
q: A large public debt buyer's vendor arrangements are only an adjacent view of target-company pricing.
q: Contract terms and rebid cadence vary by creditor and debt type.
d5: NAICS 561400 includes answering, telemarketing, and other support services.
d5: Household debt is nominal and excludes commercial collections.
d5: Delinquency and placements are cyclical.
o: Labor automation can improve agency margins without displacing the agency.
o: Observed creditor insourcing rates for the target market were not found.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Creditors and debt owners outsource account placements to agencies, commonly paying a contingent percentage of amounts recovered; some arrangements may use fixed, placement, or servicing fees. Revenue is recurring or repeat through portfolios and creditor relationships but varies with placements and recoveries.
lens.excluded_activities: In-house or captive first-party collection departments; debt buyers whose economics primarily come from owning purchased receivables rather than collecting and remitting client claims; law firms primarily providing legal services; repossession services; credit bureaus; payment processors; and businesses outside the $1 million to $10 million normalized EBITDA range or without transferable going-concern operations.
lens.included_activities: Independent third-party agencies collecting consumer or commercial claims for clients and remitting recoveries, using letters, telephone, email, text, portals, payment processing, skip tracing, dispute handling, and account servicing. Recurring or repeat creditor placements must be supported by transferable client relationships, systems, procedures, licenses, and compliance controls.

a.bridge: Source population: the national Bill and Account Collectors occupation, including collectors employed inside and outside collection agencies. Quantity transformation: map O*NET task, work-activity, context, and software evidence plus BLS's software-productivity statement to the labor-task mix of in-scope agencies, assigning routine contact, documentation, prioritization, and payment processing to the exposed share while reserving judgment-heavy exceptions. Error direction: may overstate exposure if agencies handle more disputes and negotiation, or understate it because scaled agency workflows are more standardized.
caveats: Occupation evidence includes first-party collectors outside the target business population.
Task exposure is not equivalent to safe end-to-end autonomy.
a.change_evidence: BLS projects a 10% decline in collector employment from 2024 to 2034 and explicitly attributes higher productivity to enhanced software and automated calling systems.
a.rationale: Core work repeatedly locates and contacts delinquent accounts, records and verifies information, issues notices, prioritizes files, posts payments, and maintains account histories. These are highly software-mediated language and information-processing tasks. Negotiation, complaints, disputes, hardship, identity questions, and consequential exceptions constrain full task coverage.

d5.bridge: Source population: national household-credit balances and delinquency, the Bill and Account Collectors occupation, and broad NAICS 561400 rather than NAICS 561440 revenue alone. Quantity transformation: combine a stable large delinquent-debt pool with BLS's 2024-2034 real-output path, translate the broad annual projection to five years, and discount for adjacent support activities. Error direction: may be high if broad-industry growth comes from adjacent services or creditors insource, or low if a credit downturn raises placements.
caveats: NAICS 561400 includes answering, telemarketing, and other support services.
Household debt is nominal and excludes commercial collections.
Delinquency and placements are cyclical.
d5.change_evidence: New York Fed reports $18.8 trillion of household debt and 4.8% in some stage of delinquency in 2026Q1; BLS projects 2.2% annual real-output growth for broad NAICS 561400 from 2024 to 2034.
d5.rationale: Collection demand persists because consumer credit balances and delinquent accounts remain large, and BLS projects continued need across student, credit-card, mortgage, and other debts. The broad 561400 industry projection implies real-output growth even as collector employment falls. Cyclicality, lender underwriting, regulation, direct creditor tools, debt relief, mix shifts, and the current 4.8% delinquent share create a wide range around modest real growth.

caveats: No direct national estimate of transferable EBITDA in the target size band was found.
Client consent, licensing, and compliance diligence vary materially by transaction.
e.change_evidence: The Census definition establishes a client-remittance model, while federal rules make documented procedures and compliance controls part of the transferable operating platform.
e.rationale: The code is intrinsically outsourced: agencies collect client claims and remit recoveries. A buyer can usually inherit staff, systems, policies, account placements, and client relationships. Transferability falls with concentrated or terminable client contracts, nontransferable licenses, weak compliance history, contingent revenue volatility, owner-held relationships, litigation, or portfolios whose economics resemble owned debt rather than agency services.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561440 ($6,054,981,468) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561440 ($15,566,000,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $146,595 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561440); class <5: 1,107 firms, avg revenue/firm $394,138 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $61,683 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 456 firms, avg revenue/firm $931,706 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $145,812 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 377 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,031,623 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $317,949 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 418 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,084,873 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $952,283 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 120 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,836,667 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,982,438 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 79 firms, avg revenue/firm $95,886,481 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $15,006,234 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 120

o.bridge: Source population: national occupation projections and federal communication and disclosure rules, not observed client displacement among lower-middle-market agencies. Quantity transformation: interpret expected reductions in collector labor and legally feasible digital channels as a pathway for creditors and platforms to bypass routine agency work, while preserving work requiring entity-level compliance, exceptions, and accountable operations. Error direction: may overstate bypass because automation can improve agency margins, or understate it if creditor platforms and technology-led networks scale rapidly.
caveats: Labor automation can improve agency margins without displacing the agency.
Observed creditor insourcing rates for the target market were not found.
o.change_evidence: BLS expects enhanced software and automated calling to reduce collector labor needs, and Regulation F defines compliant digital-contact routes that creditors and platforms can also deploy.
o.rationale: Enhanced creditor software, automated calling, electronic notices, portals, and self-service payments can let creditors or software vendors absorb routine placements and reduce human handling. Bypass remains incomplete because creditors still need compliant procedures, validation and dispute resolution, data stewardship, monitoring, exception judgment, multi-channel operations, and an accountable regulated operator.

caveats: A large public debt buyer's vendor arrangements are only an adjacent view of target-company pricing.
Contract terms and rebid cadence vary by creditor and debt type.
q.change_evidence: Encore's annual report states that collection agencies receive a contingent fee based on amounts collected, confirming a recovery-linked sharing mechanism.
q.rationale: Contingent fees tied to amounts collected let agencies retain near-term labor and servicing savings and may reward better recovery performance, but clients share upside automatically when recoveries rise. Competitive rebids, commission-rate pressure, client procurement, technology pass-through expectations, data and compliance reinvestment, and vendor costs reduce durable capture.

rho.bridge: Source population: federal debt-collection rules and national occupational projections, not measured AI implementations at lower-middle-market agencies. Quantity transformation: treat legally permitted automated communication and BLS's forecast of software-enabled productivity as a feasible implementation path, then discount exposed tasks for controls, monitoring, exception routing, client integration, and regulatory friction. Error direction: may overstate realization after state-law, compliance, and client-system costs, or understate it where mature agencies already have digital channels and structured data.
caveats: Federal rules do not measure adoption or realized margin gains.
State law, client requirements, and data quality can impose tighter constraints.
rho.change_evidence: Regulation F provides current operational paths for electronic communications and disclosures, while BLS expects enhanced software and automated calling to increase collector productivity during 2024-2034.
rho.rationale: Current rules permit electronic notices and controlled email or text workflows, and BLS expects technology-driven productivity. Realization is limited by contact-frequency rules, consent and opt-out handling, identity and third-party disclosure risks, validation and dispute duties, record quality, state-law variation, model errors, client approvals, and enforcement exposure.

s5.bridge: Source population: Gallup's national survey of U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not collection agencies or the specified EBITDA band. Quantity transformation: use the reported five-year sale-or-transfer intention as the central prior, then widen and modestly lower the base for sector mix, size, financing, succession alternatives, and the gap between intent and completed sale. Error direction: may overstate completed sales because intentions fail or include family transfers, or understate availability if compliance costs and consolidation motivate agency owners to exit.
caveats: Intentions include family transfers and do not equal completed arm's-length sales.
The survey is not specific to collection agencies or the target EBITDA range.
s5.change_evidence: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners plan to sell or transfer their business within five years, supplying a current cross-industry intention benchmark.
s5.rationale: No directly comparable national five-year sale-intent series was found for collection-agency owners. The range centers on the share of employer-business owners planning a sale or transfer in five years, with a lower bound for owners who retain or close and a higher bound for the industry's aging-owner and compliance-investment pressures.

records.principal_driver: The principal driver is the unusually direct fit between repetitive, software-mediated collection work and productivity technology, reinforced by BLS's explicit expectation that enhanced software and automated calling will let collectors handle more accounts.
records.principal_weakness: The principal weakness is that the same digital tools available to an acquirer are available to creditors and scaled platforms, while regulation makes mistakes expensive; operator bypass and compliance costs can absorb a large portion of the apparent labor upside.

caveats: S1's exact Census URL does not display the cited 561440 definition on reopening; it should be replaced with a stable code-specific Census reference even though the business-model premise is corroborated elsewhere.
S5's exact CFPB compilation URL could not be reopened, although the separately cited operative Regulation F sections and FDCPA text support the relevant constraints.
The 0.72 task-exposure base is a judgmental mapping from occupation tasks rather than measured automatable hours at independent collection agencies; the PROXY label and bridge are appropriate.

The rho and o ranges infer realization and operator bypass from legal feasibility and occupational productivity evidence, not observed AI adoption, margin realization, or creditor-insourcing rates in lower-middle-market agencies.

The d5 base extrapolates a broad NAICS 561400 output projection that includes unrelated business-support activities and combines it with current household-debt levels rather than a collection-agency revenue forecast.

The e, s5, and q values remain transaction-relevant estimates or broad proxies without target-band evidence on transferability, completed sales, or durable savings capture; the resulting MODEL_CONDITIONED action correctly limits the conclusion.

sources_audited.note: The cited Census URL resolves to a generic NAICS page, but the reopened page does not surface the 561440 Collection Agencies definition or the stated client-claim collection and remittance language. The business-model premise is independently consistent with S3's description of third-party collection agencies, so this citation defect does not change e's honest ESTIMATE status or the base tier/action.

O*NET identifies the occupation, repeated computer-mediated tasks, automated systems, account records, payment posting, negotiation, complaints, decision making, accuracy requirements, and collection-specific software. This supports the qualitative task-mix basis and limitations used for a.

BLS reports a 10% employment decline for 2024-2034, states that enhanced software and automated calling will increase productivity and allow more accounts per collector, describes continued demand across major debt types, and notes third-party agency employment. It supports the stated directional claims for a, rho, d5, and o, subject to the disclosed occupation-to-industry bridge.

The BLS industry table reports Business support services, NAICS 561400, with real output rising from 87.4 to 108.6 billion chained 2017 dollars and a 2.2% annual output growth rate for 2024-2034. The record correctly labels this as a broad-industry proxy for d5.

The exact CFPB Regulation F compilation URL returned an internal error on repeated reopening, so its stated support could not be directly verified. The specific operative claims used in rho, e, and o are nevertheless supported by the separately reopened S6 and S7 provisions and the FDCPA text in S8; this does not change the base tier or action.

Section 1006.6 substantiates inconvenient-time/place and cease-communication restrictions, procedures for email and text communications, consent and opt-out handling, and third-party-disclosure controls used in rho and o.

Section 1006.42 permits required disclosures in writing or electronically while imposing actual-notice, accessibility, undeliverability, opt-out, and E-SIGN requirements. This supports the electronic-disclosure pathway and constraints used in rho and o.

The FDCPA text substantiates communication and third-party restrictions, cease-contact duties, prohibitions on deceptive or unfair practices, validation notices, dispute rights, and collection pauses pending verification. It supports the compliance constraints used in rho, e, and o.
The New York Fed release reports $18.8 trillion of household debt in 2026Q1 and 4.8% of outstanding debt in some stage of delinquency, exactly supporting the current demand-pool facts used for d5.

Encore's annual report explicitly states that third-party collection agencies receive contingent fees based on amounts collected on its behalf. This supports the recovery-linked pricing mechanism for q, while not directly measuring the 0.60 capture estimate.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and discloses a fall-2024 survey. This supports the cross-industry intention benchmark for s5, with the record correctly disclosing its population and completion-rate limitations.
