caveats: a: OEWS occupation shares do not reveal task hours or current automation penetration.
a: The code combines professions with different hands-on intensity, documentation, devices, and reimbursement.
a: Self-employed practitioners are excluded from OEWS, while the screened population is limited to transferable firms.
rho: APTA guidance shows technology availability and governance needs, not representative adoption or labor removal.
rho: AMA evidence covers physicians and health systems rather than independent rehabilitation practices.
rho: Evidence is strongest for physical-therapy documentation and weaker for the other professions in the code.
e: No source measures eligibility inside the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
e: The supplied count is margin-bridged using a broad healthcare-facilities margin that may misclassify practices.
e: A qualifying revenue base may still depend on one clinician-owner or a concentrated referral source.
s5: Gallup measures intentions across industries rather than completed rehabilitation-practice transfers.
s5: One public consolidator's strategy may not represent the full buyer universe or screened practice sizes.
s5: Transactions with substantial seller rollover or management-service structures may not qualify as straightforward control transfers.
q: The evidence is concentrated in physical therapy and one large public operator.
q: No source directly measures payer or clinician sharing of AI-enabled productivity.
q: Audiology device margins and speech or occupational therapy reimbursement may respond differently from physical-therapy visits.
d5: Occupational employment projections are not direct measures of constant-quality service demand.
d5: National occupation growth includes hospitals, schools, home health, and other settings outside this code.
d5: The code's discipline mix and payer exposure can shift materially over the horizon.
o: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required quantity share.
o: Routine exercise progression and low-acuity speech or hearing support may migrate to self-service faster than expected.
o: Operator necessity does not ensure that today's independent-practice model retains the service rather than hospitals, home health, or integrated networks.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients receive evaluated plans and repeated treatment visits, commonly following referral or medical need; practices earn per-visit or per-service reimbursement from commercial insurers, Medicare, workers' compensation, and patients, with some audiology device revenue and contract services.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospital departments, skilled nursing and home-health providers classified elsewhere, captive employer clinics, software-only exercise products, pure hearing-aid retail, shell entities, and solo practices without transferable operations.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market independent and group outpatient practices providing recurring or repeat physical therapy, occupational therapy, speech-language pathology, audiology, and closely related rehabilitation services to external patients and payors.

a.bridge: BLS measures the exact industry's employment and wages rather than task hours: diagnosing and treating practitioners, therapy assistants and aides, and office-administrative staff dominate the workforce. I map scheduling, eligibility, intake, coding, billing, note drafting, chart review, home-program drafting, routine progress measurement, and communication to high exposure; evaluation and treatment planning to partial exposure; and hands-on examination, manual treatment, guarded exercise, swallowing work, device fitting, patient motivation, and safety accountability to low exposure. The result may overstate exposure if generated records require extensive correction and understate it if computer vision, remote monitoring, and automated exercise progression mature quickly.
caveats: OEWS occupation shares do not reveal task hours or current automation penetration.
The code combines professions with different hands-on intensity, documentation, devices, and reimbursement.
Self-employed practitioners are excluded from OEWS, while the screened population is limited to transferable firms.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study across physical, occupational, speech, and audiology practices separating direct treatment, evaluation, documentation, billing, scheduling, authorization, and home-program work, with audited AI time effects, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The administrative layer and clinician documentation are addressable, but a large wage share sits in licensed therapists and assistants delivering embodied, interactive care that software cannot substitute safely.

d5.bridge: BLS projects national employment growth for physical therapists, occupational therapists, speech-language pathologists, and audiologists over the coming decade. I weight the direction by their exact-industry employment presence and convert it into a moderated five-year constant-quality service-demand ratio, reflecting aging, chronic disease, surgery recovery, developmental needs, and hearing loss while discounting therapist shortages, reimbursement cuts, authorization limits, home-program substitution, and productivity changes. Employment can understate demand when vacancies persist and overstate service quantity when staffing intensity or documentation rises.
caveats: Occupational employment projections are not direct measures of constant-quality service demand.
National occupation growth includes hospitals, schools, home health, and other settings outside this code.
The code's discipline mix and payer exposure can shift materially over the horizon.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry constant-price receipts and visits by discipline, claims-based utilization per patient, referral volumes, wait times, vacancy rates, reimbursement changes, and home-program completion data would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Demographics and clinical need support growth across the principal disciplines, but realized visits remain sensitive to payer economics, referral patterns, licensed workforce availability, and patients' adherence and affordability.

caveats: No source measures eligibility inside the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
The supplied count is margin-bridged using a broad healthcare-facilities margin that may misclassify practices.
A qualifying revenue base may still depend on one clinician-owner or a concentrated referral source.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level Census product-line receipts or a commercial clinic directory matched to discipline mix, ownership, clinician count, payer mix, referral concentration, device sales, repeat visits, and founder dependence for the supplied population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The code consists chiefly of independent private and group rehabilitation practices serving external patients, and repeated treatment episodes fit the recurring-service lens. The discount covers nontransferable founder practices, mixed home-health or institutional operations, audiology businesses dominated by retail devices, captive clinics, misclassification, and practices whose referrals or credentials would not survive a sale.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621340 ($25,528,793,330) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621340 ($45,314,241,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $97,549 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621340); class <5: 19,498 firms, avg revenue/firm $219,208 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,635 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 5,169 firms, avg revenue/firm $664,181 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $104,941 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3,624 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,193,863 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $188,630 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 2,575 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,111,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $491,574 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 460 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,045,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,061,158 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 361 firms, avg revenue/firm $53,374,413 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,433,157 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 821

caveats: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required quantity share.
Routine exercise progression and low-acuity speech or hearing support may migrate to self-service faster than expected.
Operator necessity does not ensure that today's independent-practice model retains the service rather than hospitals, home health, or integrated networks.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal claims and outcomes comparing clinic-led, hybrid, and software-only rehabilitation by diagnosis and discipline, including adherence, adverse events, functional gains, escalation, and payer coverage, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Remote monitoring, guided home exercise, speech applications, hearing-device software, and digital coaching can replace some low-acuity visits, but examination, diagnosis, manual treatment, guarded movement, swallowing and communication assessment, device fitting, progression, motivation, and safety remain accountable clinician functions. Profession-specific ambient-scribe guidance also treats AI as documentation support rather than a substitute for the provider.

q.bridge: U.S. Physical Therapy reports revenue and costs per patient visit, negotiated commercial reimbursement, and a Medicare rate reduction with a direct revenue effect. I map per-visit reimbursement to partial retention of implemented documentation and administrative benefits because rates do not automatically fall with internal cost, then discount for government fee cuts, commercial renegotiation, utilization review, authorization burdens, clinician compensation, audit risk, and the inability to multiply hands-on visits without licensed capacity. The large physical-therapy operator may have greater negotiating leverage and centralized overhead than lower-middle-market practices, while fixed schedules may shield small practices' cost savings in the near term.
caveats: The evidence is concentrated in physical therapy and one large public operator.
No source directly measures payer or clinician sharing of AI-enabled productivity.
Audiology device margins and speech or occupational therapy reimbursement may respond differently from physical-therapy visits.
q.change_evidence: Practice-level contracts and claims linked to net reimbursement, denials, authorization labor, clinician compensation, visit duration, utilization, and margins before and after AI deployment across all four disciplines would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Savings in documentation, revenue cycle, and scheduling can improve cost per visit, but payers control rates and authorization, and the direct-treatment bottleneck limits capacity capture. Durable benefit sharing should therefore be substantial but incomplete.

rho.bridge: APTA documents emerging physical-therapy ambient scribes that capture, transcribe, and summarize encounters into structured notes, while AMA's physician survey and health-system examples show strong relevance and deployment of documentation and administrative AI in adjacent clinical settings. I translate availability and adjacent-provider experience into five-year implementation across exposed rehabilitation workflows, then discount for smaller-practice integration capacity, protected health information, consent, EHR fragmentation, coding and medical-necessity audits, profession-specific templates, clinician review, and limited evidence for occupational therapy, speech therapy, and audiology. Large health-system adoption may overstate resources, while repetitive therapy notes and authorization work may accelerate practice-level uptake.
caveats: APTA guidance shows technology availability and governance needs, not representative adoption or labor removal.
AMA evidence covers physicians and health systems rather than independent rehabilitation practices.
Evidence is strongest for physical-therapy documentation and weaker for the other professions in the code.
rho.change_evidence: A representative survey of independent rehabilitation practices measuring approved AI use by profession and workflow, verified clinician minutes saved, review time, claim denials, documentation errors, privacy incidents, and adoption by practice size would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Documentation and administrative tools fit existing workflows and are already the subject of profession-specific guidance, but broad implementation requires reliable clinical language, system integration, payer acceptance, and accountable review.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports cross-industry five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners. U.S. Physical Therapy reports completed acquisitions of controlling and shared interests in outpatient therapy practices and an ongoing multi-clinic acquisition strategy. I haircut owner intentions for noncompletion, internal transfers, nontransferable referral relationships, clinician departures, and transactions without control, while raising the midpoint relative to an unsupported all-industry translation because exact-sector strategic deal activity is directly observed. The public consolidator may overstate buyer demand and access, while retained seller equity may improve transfer success but complicate qualification as control.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions across industries rather than completed rehabilitation-practice transfers.
One public consolidator's strategy may not represent the full buyer universe or screened practice sizes.
Transactions with substantial seller rollover or management-service structures may not qualify as straightforward control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: Closed U.S. outpatient rehabilitation transactions from a healthcare deal database matched to revenue, EBITDA, discipline, control percentage, seller rollover, referral concentration, and post-close clinician retention, plus broker listing outcomes, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Outpatient rehabilitation has demonstrated strategic buyers and partnership structures, and clinic teams, referrals, payer contracts, and local brands can transfer. Founder retention and shared equity are common, so not every deal is an immediate clean control transfer.

records.principal_driver: Repeated reimbursed visits create scalable clinic operations with addressable documentation and administration around a service that remains clinically necessary.
records.principal_weakness: Hands-on clinician capacity and payer-controlled reimbursement limit both the size and retention of AI-enabled productivity gains.

caveats: The labor-opportunity factor is decision-sensitive: H is 4.10, only slightly above the PRIORITY weakest-link cutoff of 4, while the numerical task-exposure and implementation bases are judgmental mappings rather than measured rehabilitation-practice workflow shares. The PROXY labels, explicit bridges and wide ranges make this uncertainty honest rather than a material defect.

S3 supports ambient-scribe capability and governance concerns but not representative physical-therapy adoption or verified time savings; rho therefore also depends heavily on extrapolating physician evidence from S4 to smaller rehabilitation practices and to occupational therapy, speech therapy and audiology.

The retention primitive q is inferred from one large physical-therapy operator's reimbursement and cost structure. S6 does not directly measure how AI-enabled productivity gains would be divided among practices, clinicians and payers, especially outside physical therapy.

The transferability factor combines cross-industry owner intentions with acquisitions by one public consolidator; neither source supplies a closed-deal denominator for lower-middle-market rehabilitation practices, and the observed transactions include substantial retained seller equity.

The demand and operator-necessity conclusions are directionally supported by occupational duties and outlooks, but the BLS projections cover multiple care and school settings and do not directly measure constant-quality visits or software substitution within independent NAICS 621340 practices.

sources_audited.note: The official 2022 NAICS manual supports the 621340 industry identity and the scope centered on offices of physical, occupational and speech therapists and audiologists. It is classification evidence, not a measure of firm eligibility or automation exposure.

The exact-industry May 2023 OEWS table reports 457,060 jobs and shows the stated mix: healthcare practitioners, therapy assistants and aides, and office-administrative workers are major components. It supports the occupation and wage inputs used in the proxy bridge, not task-hour exposure itself.

APTA defines ambient scribes as AI systems that capture, transcribe and summarize encounters into structured notes and identifies documentation, legal, regulatory, privacy, safety and ethical considerations. The public page announces a member-only advisory and does not establish representative adoption or realized labor savings.

The AMA article reports a survey of nearly 1,200 physicians and supports the cited adjacent use cases, including documentation, care plans, prior authorization, chart summaries and health-system implementation examples. The population is physicians rather than rehabilitation practices, as disclosed.

Gallup reports that 14% of surveyed owners who primarily work for their business planned to sell, go public or transfer ownership within five years. This is cross-industry owner intention rather than completed rehabilitation-practice transactions.

The SEC-hosted U.S. Physical Therapy release reports net rate and operating costs per visit, commercial rate negotiation, Medicare reductions, clinic closures, and acquisitions with 50% and 75% interests and seller retention. It is strong company-specific evidence but not a representative market denominator or direct measure of AI-benefit retention.

BLS documents physical therapists' licensed, hands-on and evaluative duties and projects 11% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, with aging and rehabilitation needs supporting demand. This remains an occupation-wide proxy rather than exact-industry service-volume evidence.

BLS documents occupational therapists' evaluative, treatment, equipment and progress-recording duties and projects 14% employment growth from 2024 to 2034. The page also attributes demand to aging, disability and developmental needs, while covering settings outside NAICS 621340.

BLS documents speech-language pathologists' assessment, individualized treatment, swallowing work and recordkeeping and projects 15% employment growth from 2024 to 2034. The outlook is national and includes schools and other settings outside the screened lens.

BLS documents audiologists' examination, diagnosis, treatment planning, device fitting and progress monitoring and projects 9% employment growth from 2024 to 2034, with aging-related hearing and balance needs. It does not measure the future operator-required share directly.

caveats: a: No source directly reports a wage-weighted exposure share for NAICS 541310.
a: The AIA survey covers architectural professionals and firm leaders rather than a representative payroll-weighted sample of LMM firms.
a: Exposure is limited to current, not-yet-automated tasks; existing CAD/BIM productivity is excluded conceptually but cannot be measured precisely.
rho: Adoption status is not equivalent to implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: The AIA studies include firms outside the frozen EBITDA band and may overweight firms active in the association.
rho: Tool capabilities and professional standards may change substantially during the five-year horizon.
e: The frozen n estimate already bridges firm size into an EBITDA band; e does not re-estimate that input.
e: The 84% architect-of-record statistic is billings-weighted, dated 2019, and not a firm eligibility rate.
e: Repeat-client behavior is not directly quantified in the fetched evidence.
s5: Surveyed likelihood is not observed transaction incidence and includes firms outside the LMM band.
s5: The small-firm transition-plan evidence is from 2020 and planning does not imply a control transfer.
s5: Architecture firms may transition internally over many years, which does not necessarily meet the qualifying-transfer definition.
q: Billing-method shares are from 2019, before the current generative-AI cycle.
q: Professional fee plus reimbursables does not reveal whether the professional component is fixed, hourly, or renegotiable.
q: The estimate does not include demand volume loss, which belongs in d5 and o, or implementation friction, which belongs in rho.
d5: Employment is an input proxy rather than a direct measure of constant-quality service quantity.
d5: The AIA forecast covers nonresidential construction only and extends just through 2027.
d5: Residential work, regional cycles, renovation, interest rates, and construction-cost inflation create substantial dispersion.
o: Licensing rules vary, and many jurisdictions exempt certain small residential, agricultural, or low-risk projects.
o: Title and practice regulation do not guarantee that the current firm model captures all required accountability.
o: The estimate covers year-five quantity after elimination or self-service, not task automation within retained projects.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based outsourced professional services for private and public owners, commonly billed by stipulated sum, professional fee plus reimbursables, hourly rates, or a percentage of construction cost; repeat demand comes from continuing owner relationships and successive projects rather than subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal design units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, design-build contractors classified in construction, landscape architecture, standalone engineering, and firms without transferable operating relationships or accountable architectural-service delivery.
lens.included_activities: US architectural-services firms in the $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that provide building planning, architectural design, documentation, specification, permitting, and construction-phase services to external owners and repeat or recurring clients.

a.bridge: AIA measures present AI use, task-level inefficiency, and common use cases among US architectural professionals, while BLS describes the architect task basket; neither measures wage-weighted task exposure. I map exposed documentation, specification research, takeoffs, visualization, proposals, communications, meeting records, and portions of design iteration onto the wage-bearing workflow, then exclude already-automated CAD/BIM work and retain human review, client judgment, coordination, site work, and sealing. Because survey enthusiasm and experimentation can overstate technically substitutable production work, the proxy is more likely to overstate than understate exposure.
caveats: No source directly reports a wage-weighted exposure share for NAICS 541310.
The AIA survey covers architectural professionals and firm leaders rather than a representative payroll-weighted sample of LMM firms.
Exposure is limited to current, not-yet-automated tasks; existing CAD/BIM productivity is excluded conceptually but cannot be measured precisely.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from AIA, Deltek, or a large architecture-firm panel that reports labor hours and compensation by project phase and task, paired with controlled productivity trials for BIM documentation, specification, code review, and project administration, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Architecture combines a large digital production and information-processing workload with substantial licensed judgment and coordination. AIA identifies cost estimation, takeoffs, product-library updates, and technical specifications as inefficient tasks, while current AI use clusters in chatbots, text analytics, transcription, image generation, and product research. A 40% base reflects material exposure across production and practice operations without treating whole architect roles as substitutable.

d5.bridge: BLS projects architect employment, and AIA forecasts nominal nonresidential construction spending, whereas d5 is constant-price, constant-quality service quantity for the full current architectural-service basket. I use the 4% 2024-34 architect employment projection as a slow-growth five-year anchor and temper it with the 2026-27 construction slowdown and divergent building-sector outlook. Employment can understate quantity when productivity rises, while nominal construction spending can overstate real service demand during cost inflation.
caveats: Employment is an input proxy rather than a direct measure of constant-quality service quantity.
The AIA forecast covers nonresidential construction only and extends just through 2027.
Residential work, regional cycles, renovation, interest rates, and construction-cost inflation create substantial dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year real forecast of architectural firm billings by residential and nonresidential building type, deflated by an architecture-services price index and including renovation, would materially change this range.
d5.rationale: BLS projects architect employment to grow 4% from 2024 to 2034 and says architects will remain needed for construction and renovation, including sustainable design. Near-term conditions are weak: AIA reports nonresidential spending fell 2% in 2025, forecasts a 0.3% decline in 2026 and 3.0% growth in 2027, with sharp sector divergence. The base therefore holds real service quantity approximately flat to slightly higher over five years, with a wide cyclical range.

caveats: The frozen n estimate already bridges firm size into an EBITDA band; e does not re-estimate that input.
The 84% architect-of-record statistic is billings-weighted, dated 2019, and not a firm eligibility rate.
Repeat-client behavior is not directly quantified in the fetched evidence.
e.change_evidence: A representative SUSB-linked survey of $1-10M EBITDA architecture firms reporting external-client revenue, repeat-client billings, principal concentration, architect-of-record status, and transferability after owner exit would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition centers external professional planning and design services, and AIA reports that 84% of 2019 billings were from projects where the firm served as architect of record. Most LMM practices should therefore provide a transferable outsourced service with repeat-client potential, though project-by-project revenue and principal-bound residential or design-boutique relationships disqualify a meaningful minority.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $25,388.4M / sectoral output (T30) $54,890.2M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $263,174 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541310); class <5: 13,996 firms, avg revenue/firm $377,518 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,082 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,719 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,387,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $217,144 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,928 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,115,513 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $487,578 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 1,361 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,632,600 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,664,002 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 228 firms, avg revenue/firm $44,196,899 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,916,815 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $111,625,710 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,469,424 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,589

o.bridge: NCARB observes universal jurisdictional licensing infrastructure and AIA states that the architect of record remains responsible for code compliance and design integrity regardless of AI involvement; neither measures the exact quantity share that still requires a lens-type operator. I translate regulated responsible control, sealing, client coordination, and construction-phase accountability into a high operator-required share, then deduct exempt project types, client self-service, commoditized early design, and software-only tasks. Regulation likely supports the upper side, but using title protection as a proxy may overstate required firm involvement for exempt or unsealed work.
caveats: Licensing rules vary, and many jurisdictions exempt certain small residential, agricultural, or low-risk projects.
Title and practice regulation do not guarantee that the current firm model captures all required accountability.
The estimate covers year-five quantity after elimination or self-service, not task automation within retained projects.
o.change_evidence: A jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction analysis of permit submissions and architecture-practice exemptions, combined with owner surveys on self-service and software-only procurement by project value and type, would materially move o.
o.rationale: All 55 US jurisdictions have architecture boards that issue licenses and regulate practice, and AIA's AI guidance says professional responsibility cannot be delegated and the architect of record remains responsible for code compliance and design integrity. Software can eliminate portions of ideation, drafting, research, and administration, but most permitted-building work should still require a licensed, accountable operator who coordinates and accepts responsibility for the deliverable.

q.bridge: AIA observes billing-method mix and perceived fee pressure, not the discounted five-year retention of an implemented gross benefit. I treat stipulated-sum and other pre-agreed professional-fee work as initially more retainable than hourly work, where fewer hours directly reduce invoices, then discount all methods for renewals, competitive bids, client negotiation, and gradual repricing. Fixed-fee project overrun risk can reduce realized retention, so the billing-method proxy can overstate q; differentiated quality and capacity expansion can offset that direction.
caveats: Billing-method shares are from 2019, before the current generative-AI cycle.
Professional fee plus reimbursables does not reveal whether the professional component is fixed, hourly, or renegotiable.
The estimate does not include demand volume loss, which belongs in d5 and o, or implementation friction, which belongs in rho.
q.change_evidence: Project-level contracts and invoices from a representative LMM firm cohort, linked to AI implementation costs, labor hours, realized margins, renewal pricing, win rates, and client-requested givebacks for five years, would materially change q.
q.rationale: In 2019, stipulated sums represented 37.7% of billings, professional fees plus reimbursables 31.6%, hourly rates 18.1%, and percentage-of-construction-cost work 10.0%. The mix gives operators meaningful near-term capture on fixed or pre-agreed fees, but 46% of firm leaders in 2024 said fee negotiation had become more challenging than four to five years earlier. A 56% discounted retention base reflects partial capture followed by client sharing and competition.

rho.bridge: AIA directly surveys firm-level adoption status, not the share of exposed labor opportunity implemented within five years. I translate 8% already implemented, 20% implementing, and 35% considering adoption into a five-year implementation share, discounting for the markedly lower adoption of smaller firms, workflow integration, data security, accuracy, training, and mandatory professional review. Stated consideration can exceed completed implementation, so the proxy is biased upward unless tool integration accelerates.
caveats: Adoption status is not equivalent to implemented labor substitution or avoided hiring.
The AIA studies include firms outside the frozen EBITDA band and may overweight firms active in the association.
Tool capabilities and professional standards may change substantially during the five-year horizon.
rho.change_evidence: Annual AIA Firm Survey microdata by employee count showing production hours saved, workflow penetration, failed deployments, and sustained headcount avoidance in firms under 50 employees would materially move the range.
rho.rationale: Only 8% of firm leaders reported integrated AI, but another 20% were implementing and 35% considering it, leaving a credible path to meaningful adoption over five years. Implementation should be strongest in low-risk administrative and research workflows and slower in construction documents and design decisions because 90% or more of respondents reported concerns about inaccuracy, unintended consequences, privacy, authenticity, and transparency.

s5.bridge: The AIA survey measures leaders' subjective likelihood of a merger or acquisition over the next 3-5 years, not realized qualifying control transfers among eligible LMM firms. I anchor below the 36% of respondents not classified as unlikely, recognizing that only 5% called a transaction very likely, and exclude non-control combinations, internal reorganizations, failed deals, and nontransferable practices. Expectations may overstate completed transfers, while unplanned retirements may make the proxy understate them.
caveats: Surveyed likelihood is not observed transaction incidence and includes firms outside the LMM band.
The small-firm transition-plan evidence is from 2020 and planning does not imply a control transfer.
Architecture firms may transition internally over many years, which does not necessarily meet the qualifying-transfer definition.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or Axial deal counts matched to NAICS 541310 and firm revenue, plus AIA owner-age and completed succession microdata for 10-49-person practices, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: In the August 2025 AIA survey, 64% of firm leaders said acquisition or merger was not at all likely or not very likely for their firm, while 5% said very likely. Separately, only 15% of small firms had an ownership transition plan in the 2020 AIA Firm Survey, suggesting succession pressure but also execution risk. A 16% five-year probability allows for both third-party control transactions and transferable owner successions while excluding mere closures and internal reorganizations.

records.principal_driver: A labor-intensive digital production workflow creates broad assistive-AI use cases across documentation, specifications, research, visualization, and administration.
records.principal_weakness: Project cyclicality and competitive fee renegotiation can prevent technically achievable labor savings from becoming durable retained cash flow.

caveats: a: OEWS is four-digit Legal Services and May 2023, not six-digit Offices of Lawyers or the LMM lens.
a: Clio reports potential task automation, not observed labor substitution, and its customer/survey population may be more technology-forward than the industry.

a: The frozen l input uses 2024 employee wages over 2022 receipts, is harmonized by a cross-code divisor, and omits proprietor and partner labor; this is material for law firms and may understate economic labor intensity.
rho: Adoption is not implementation depth, and usage surveys disagree sharply because they define AI and samples differently.
rho: Thomson Reuters respondents span multiple professional sectors and countries, and their 12-hour five-year expectation is a forecast, not observed savings.
rho: Implementation varies substantially by practice area, matter sensitivity, court rules, client requirements, and firm technology maturity.
e: No national dataset measures the share of LMM law firms with transferable client relationships or compliant ownership structures.
e: Ethics rules vary by jurisdiction; Arizona, Utah, and D.C. have exceptions or alternative regimes, while multistate practices face overlapping restrictions.

e: The frozen n=7193 is itself an ESTIMATE margin-bridged using a broad Business & Consumer Services EBITDA margin; law-firm margins and partner compensation conventions may move firms into or out of the band.
s5: Lawyer age is not owner age, and retirement is not equivalent to a control transfer.
s5: The public evidence does not provide an industry-wide denominator of eligible firms or verified completed deals.
s5: Sales to nonlawyers are generally constrained, and client choice can impair transferability even when a practice sale is permitted.
q: Clio's firms may not represent all LMM practices, and firm incidence of a fee type does not reveal its revenue share.
q: Benefit retention depends on the ability to refill freed capacity; volume loss is intentionally excluded from q.
q: ABA fee guidance is based on Model Rules and reasonableness; binding state rules and engagement terms vary.
d5: Employment is an imperfect demand proxy and the BLS projection is national, occupational, and ten-year rather than industry-specific and five-year.
d5: The current service basket is heterogeneous and cyclical: litigation, restructuring, transactions, real estate, and consumer practices can move in different directions.
d5: The ratio is intended to be constant-price and constant-quality, but no directly matching public forecast was found.
o: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the current 541110 service basket.
o: Unauthorized-practice and paraprofessional rules vary by state and may liberalize over five years.
o: High-stakes and complex matters are more operator-dependent than routine consumer forms and information queries, so practice mix is decisive.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External individuals, businesses, insurers, and institutions buy matter-based or continuing legal services through hourly, flat, contingent, subscription, retainer, and blended fee arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: In-house legal departments, captive insurance-defense units, nonprofit and government law offices, financing vehicles, dormant practices, and practices whose matters or client relationships are not reasonably transferable.
lens.included_activities: US law firms in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band that provide recurring or repeat legal advice, representation, drafting, transactions, litigation, compliance, and related client service to external individuals or organizations.

a.bridge: May 2023 OEWS measures employment and mean wages for four-digit Legal Services, not six-digit Offices of Lawyers, while Clio estimates automatable task shares rather than wage-weighted, current, not-yet-automated opportunity. I wage-weight the dominant occupational groups implied by OEWS and temper Clio's 57% lawyer, 81% legal-secretary, and up-to-74% billable-task exposure for work already automated and for review, judgment, relationship, and courtroom tasks. Clio's technology-user sample and task methodology likely bias exposure upward; OEWS's inclusion of other legal services and exclusion of proprietors can bias the mix in either direction.
caveats: OEWS is four-digit Legal Services and May 2023, not six-digit Offices of Lawyers or the LMM lens.
Clio reports potential task automation, not observed labor substitution, and its customer/survey population may be more technology-forward than the industry.

The frozen l input uses 2024 employee wages over 2022 receipts, is harmonized by a cross-code divisor, and omits proprietor and partner labor; this is material for law firms and may understate economic labor intensity.
a.change_evidence: Obtain a task-level time study from LMM law firms that maps time and cash compensation by SOC occupation, practice area, and workflow, and separately records tasks already automated; Clio or Thomson Reuters billing-system telemetry linked to task codes would materially tighten the estimate.
a.rationale: Legal Services wages are concentrated in lawyers, with meaningful paralegal and administrative support. Research, document review, drafting, discovery, intake, scheduling, billing, and information gathering are exposed, but persuasion, client counseling, negotiation, fact judgment, and accountable sign-off remain substantially human. The range reflects both a large task ceiling and uncertainty over how much current tool use has already harvested it.

d5.bridge: BLS projects lawyer employment, not constant-price, constant-quality quantity of the current Offices of Lawyers service basket. I convert its 4% 2024-34 lawyer-employment growth to an approximate five-year midpoint ratio of 1.02 and widen the range for productivity, legal-cycle exposure, price competition, outsourcing, and substitution. Employment can understate demand if productivity rises, but it can overstate demand for the current basket if staffing persists while routine work migrates to software or lower-cost providers.
caveats: Employment is an imperfect demand proxy and the BLS projection is national, occupational, and ten-year rather than industry-specific and five-year.
The current service basket is heterogeneous and cyclical: litigation, restructuring, transactions, real estate, and consumer practices can move in different directions.
The ratio is intended to be constant-price and constant-quality, but no directly matching public forecast was found.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain BEA or Census real-output measures specific to 541110, deflate practice-area revenue with matched legal-service price indexes, and combine them with five-year client legal-spend forecasts and matter counts by litigation, transactional, regulatory, and consumer practice.
d5.rationale: BLS expects continued legal-service needs from individuals, businesses, and government and projects lawyer employment up 4% over ten years, while also warning that price competition, automation, and low-cost providers will affect routine work. On a five-year horizon, broadly stable to modestly growing real service quantity is more defensible than either rapid growth or collapse.

caveats: No national dataset measures the share of LMM law firms with transferable client relationships or compliant ownership structures.
Ethics rules vary by jurisdiction; Arizona, Utah, and D.C. have exceptions or alternative regimes, while multistate practices face overlapping restrictions.

The frozen n=7193 is itself an ESTIMATE margin-bridged using a broad Business & Consumer Services EBITDA margin; law-firm margins and partner compensation conventions may move firms into or out of the band.
e.change_evidence: Build a representative broker and state-bar diligence sample of LMM practices showing revenue recurrence, top-client concentration, matter duration, partner-originated revenue, client retention after partner departure, buyer licensing structure, and the fraction that can satisfy each governing jurisdiction's sale and ownership rules.
e.rationale: Most offices of lawyers serve external clients and many generate repeat matters or referrals, but eligibility is reduced by personal-goodwill dependence, client consent and conflicts, nonportable contingent or one-off books, and ownership restrictions. ABA Model Rule 1.17 permits a practice sale only under conditions including sale to lawyers or law firms and client notice; Model Rule 5.4 generally bars nonlawyer ownership, and an ABA review reported the traditional ban in 48 US jurisdictions. The estimate assumes a legally compliant lawyer-controlled buyer or structure rather than unrestricted investor ownership.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541110 ($150,269,970,536) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541110 ($356,242,120,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $330,420 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541110); class <5: 116,668 firms, avg revenue/firm $419,995 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,729 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 22,518 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,416,234 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $221,641 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 10,001 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,187,546 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $498,851 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 6,245 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,168,102 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,591,308 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 948 firms, avg revenue/firm $58,651,846 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,179,014 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 203 firms, avg revenue/firm $612,665,074 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $95,882,084 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 7,193

caveats: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the current 541110 service basket.
Unauthorized-practice and paraprofessional rules vary by state and may liberalize over five years.
High-stakes and complex matters are more operator-dependent than routine consumer forms and information queries, so practice mix is decisive.
o.change_evidence: Measure the share of matters and revenue by practice area that can legally and practically be completed without a lawyer, using state unauthorized-practice rules, court filing data, consumer legal-platform completion rates, client surveys, and longitudinal matter migration from firms to software or licensed paraprofessionals.
o.rationale: A large majority of year-five legal-service quantity should still require a responsible law-firm operator because representation, privileged advice, conflicts, confidentiality, client counseling, negotiation, and court-facing work require licensed judgment and supervision. ABA Formal Opinion 512 says generative AI may assist but cannot replace the judgment and experience needed to advise clients or carry out representations. The deduction recognizes consumer self-service, document automation, alternative legal providers, and software handling routine questions and forms.

q.bridge: Clio measures firm billing modes and reported revenue effects, not the discounted five-year share of a specific implemented gross benefit. I map exclusively hourly billing toward rapid pass-through, flat/contingent/subscription work toward greater initial retention, and mixed billing between them, then reduce retention for renewal repricing, competition, client sharing, and ethical fee limits. Firm-level billing incidence is not revenue-weighted and likely overstates retention where firms offer flat fees on only a small share of matters.
caveats: Clio's firms may not represent all LMM practices, and firm incidence of a fee type does not reveal its revenue share.
Benefit retention depends on the ability to refill freed capacity; volume loss is intentionally excluded from q.
ABA fee guidance is based on Model Rules and reasonableness; binding state rules and engagement terms vary.
q.change_evidence: Obtain firm-level revenue-weighted fee-mix data and matched matter cohorts before and after AI implementation, including hours, realized rates, flat-fee margins, matter volume, client concessions, renewal repricing, write-downs, and competitor pricing over at least three years.
q.rationale: Hourly billing remains a strong leakage channel: lawyers must bill actual time, and faster AI-assisted work can directly reduce invoices unless released capacity is sold. Flat, contingent, and subscription arrangements permit more capture, but even those fees must remain reasonable. Clio reports 41% of firms exclusively hourly and 59% using flat fees either exclusively or alongside hourly fees; among wide AI adopters, pricing responses include increases, reductions, and new fee types, supporting partial rather than full retention.

rho.bridge: ABA adoption surveys and Thomson Reuters expectations measure tool use and predicted hours saved across attorney firm sizes and, for Thomson Reuters, legal, tax, and risk professionals globally; they do not measure the fraction of exposed law-firm labor operationally implemented. I translate current small/midsize adoption, five-year time-saving expectations, and stated blockers into the share of the exposure in a that can survive workflow redesign, verification, confidentiality, supervision, and change management. Respondent optimism may bias upward, while rapid model improvement and falling tool costs may bias downward.
caveats: Adoption is not implementation depth, and usage surveys disagree sharply because they define AI and samples differently.
Thomson Reuters respondents span multiple professional sectors and countries, and their 12-hour five-year expectation is a forecast, not observed savings.
Implementation varies substantially by practice area, matter sensitivity, court rules, client requirements, and firm technology maturity.
rho.change_evidence: Survey the target firms annually for workflow-level deployment, percentage of matters using approved AI, review minutes per AI output, error and rework rates, data-security approvals, and realized FTE or avoided-hire effects; matched pre/post billing and payroll data would be decisive.
rho.rationale: Implementation should advance meaningfully within five years because legal research, drafting, review, discovery, and back-office tools already exist. It remains below the technical ceiling: the ABA found only 24.1% usage in 2-9 lawyer firms and 29.5% in 10-49 lawyer firms in 2024, alongside strong accuracy, reliability, and privacy concerns; professional rules require competent review and secure handling of client information.

s5.bridge: ABA/BLS age data describe all active lawyers, not equity owners of eligible LMM firms or completed control transfers: 13.1% were age 65+ and 17.9% were 55-64 in 2023. I use those cohorts as a succession-pressure proxy, then discount for lawyers who work past 65, firms that close, and internal reorganizations, while allowing for transfers driven by reasons other than retirement. Because owners are likely older than all lawyers, the age proxy tends to understate succession pressure; because many retirements do not create a qualifying transfer, it tends to overstate completed transfer probability.
caveats: Lawyer age is not owner age, and retirement is not equivalent to a control transfer.
The public evidence does not provide an industry-wide denominator of eligible firms or verified completed deals.
Sales to nonlawyers are generally constrained, and client choice can impair transferability even when a practice sale is permitted.
s5.change_evidence: Acquire five years of closed transaction data from specialist law-practice brokers, state-bar succession programs, and legal-sector M&A advisers, with seller age, firm revenue/EBITDA, internal versus external buyer, client retention, and failed or withdrawn processes.
s5.rationale: A meaningful owner cohort is near or beyond conventional retirement age, and law practices can legally be sold, but personal client relationships and regulatory requirements suppress conversion of succession need into qualifying control transfers. The base therefore sits near the observed 65+ lawyer share rather than treating all older lawyers as sellers.

records.principal_driver: High-cost research, drafting, review, discovery, and administrative workflows create meaningful implementable productivity potential.
records.principal_weakness: Hourly billing and lawyer-ownership/client-portability constraints can prevent technical productivity from becoming durable, transferable cash flow.

caveats: The stated no-deviation lens excludes practices whose matters or client relationships are not reasonably transferable even though transfer eligibility is separately estimated in e. This favorable pre-screening should be disclosed as a lens choice, but correcting it would not plausibly reduce the capped F factor enough to change the PRIORITY base tier or the VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The a bridge is directionally plausible but not numerically reproducible from the cited facts: Clio supplies task shares for lawyers and legal secretaries/administrative assistants, while the wage-weighted treatment of paralegals and the remaining occupations and the deduction for already-automated work remain reviewer-visible judgment. Its PROXY label and wide range are honest.

The s5 rationale describes the age evidence as applying to all active lawyers, while S5 is BLS/CPS occupational data and does not identify active-bar status, equity ownership, or eligible practices. The owner and transfer mismatches are otherwise explicitly disclosed, and F remains well above its tier threshold across the registered range.

S8 directly establishes the ownership-rule landscape but only indirectly informs o; no cited source measures the 0.80 operator-required quantity share. The primitive is appropriately labeled ESTIMATE and already keeps the action at VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

The frozen n estimate uses a broad cross-sector EBITDA margin and does not account cleanly for partner-compensation conventions in law firms. This can materially affect the count of firms in the LMM band, but the weakness is disclosed, n is labeled ESTIMATE, and the review contract holds dataset anchors fixed.

The principal driver and weakness are not tightly aligned with the scorecard: H is the weakest base factor, while the ownership-sensitive F factor is capped at 10 and the hourly-billing-sensitive C factor is 8.4. The narrative remains directionally supported, but readers should use the displayed factors rather than treat those labels as a ranking of score contributions.

sources_audited.note: The May 2023 BLS page is for four-digit NAICS 541100 and reports lawyers at 37.49%, paralegals and legal assistants at 23.04%, and office and administrative support at 26.25% of employment, with wage data.

Clio's October 2024 release reports that nearly three-quarters of hourly billable tasks are exposed, including 57% of lawyer tasks and 81% of legal-secretary and administrative-assistant tasks; it is potential automation rather than realized substitution, as disclosed.

The ABA TechReport identifies a late-2024 survey, reports 24.1% use for 2-9 lawyer firms and 29.5% for 10-49 lawyer firms, and quantifies accuracy, reliability, privacy, cost, and learning-time concerns.

The Thomson Reuters article describes a global survey of more than 2,200 legal, tax, and risk/compliance professionals, predicted savings of four hours in one year and 12 hours in five years, and nearly two-thirds emphasizing human oversight.
The ABA demographics page reproduces BLS/CPS occupational data for 2023: 17.9% of lawyers were 55-64, 13.1% were 65+, and median age was 46; it does not identify equity owners or completed transfers.
ABA Model Rule 1.17 permits practice sales subject to seller cessation, sale to lawyers or law firms, client notice and choice, and no fee increase by reason of the sale.
ABA Model Rule 5.4 restricts fee sharing and partnerships with nonlawyers, nonlawyer ownership, and outside direction of professional judgment.

The 2025 ABA magazine article supports the reported D.C., Arizona, and Utah alternatives and states that 48 other U.S. jurisdictions maintain the traditional ban. It is an advocacy article about ownership regimes and does not directly measure eligible-firm share or operator-required service quantity.

Clio's online report states that in 2024, 41% of firms billed exclusively hourly and 59% used flat fees exclusively or alongside hourly billing; it also reports varied pricing responses among wide AI adopters and consumer use of AI for legal problems.

BLS projects lawyer employment growth of 4% from 2024 to 2034, expects continuing legal demand, and identifies price competition plus automation or outsourcing of routine legal work. This is occupational employment evidence, not direct real-output measurement.
The 2026 BLS projections article identifies AI-assisted contract review, discovery, and research for paralegals and legal assistants and reports projected 2024-34 employment change of 0.2%.

ABA Formal Opinion 512 addresses competence, verification, confidentiality, supervision, professional judgment, actual-time hourly billing, and reasonableness of flat and contingent fees when using generative AI.
