records.title: Offices of Lawyers

caveats: a: BLS publishes only a combined 621500 occupation mix, requiring judgment to remove medical-laboratory work and reconstruct the imaging-center mix.
a: Most authorized radiology AI products assist rather than replace accountable clinicians, so device counts do not measure labor substitution.
a: Equipment-related acceleration may increase scan capacity and demand rather than proportionally reduce staffing.
rho: The FDA list establishes authorization, not utilization, workflow coverage, or economic benefit.
rho: RadNet is the largest U.S. freestanding imaging operator and also owns a digital-health segment, unlike a typical LMM center group.
rho: Implementation varies by modality, vendor, PACS and RIS architecture, radiology-group relationship, and state requirements.
e: No source measures default-lens eligibility among the frozen LMM-band firms.

e: The 859-firm count is margin-bridged with a broad hospitals and healthcare-facilities margin that may not fit freestanding imaging centers with different equipment, lease, and professional-fee structures.
e: Imaging-site counts, joint ventures, management companies, and legal firms are not interchangeable denominators.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers.
s5: RadNet's disclosed transactions are one buyer's asset acquisitions and do not provide an industry denominator.
s5: Joint-venture interests, professional radiology practices, management entities, and imaging-center operating companies have different transfer mechanics.
q: Neither source measures five-year retention of implemented AI benefit for LMM diagnostic imaging firms.
q: Technical fees, professional fees, management fees, commercial fee-for-service, government payment, and capitation transmit benefit differently.
q: RadNet's density and scale create procurement, utilization, and payer advantages unavailable to many LMM operators.
d5: Occupational employment is not procedure volume, and the BLS projection spans ten years rather than the five-year horizon.
d5: RadNet's reported growth includes acquisitions, price, and modality mix and is not a constant-price national demand series.
d5: Demand varies sharply across MRI, CT, mammography, ultrasound, X-ray, PET, and nuclear medicine.
o: Operator-required service does not guarantee that the current independent center retains the referral or payer contract.
o: Remote interpretation can centralize professional labor without eliminating the local physical imaging site.
o: The operator-required share differs by modality and could fall faster for standardized screening and image-review workflows.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients are generally referred by physicians, while commercial insurers, managed-care organizations, Medicare, Medicaid, hospitals, physician groups, and patients fund services. Revenue is mainly per procedure under negotiated commercial rates or government fee schedules, with some capitation and management-fee arrangements; repeat referrals and recurring payer or physician relationships support volume.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive hospital and physician-practice imaging departments, equipment manufacturers and repair businesses, software vendors without an operated imaging service, radiation-therapy providers, shells, subscale hobby businesses, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market diagnostic imaging firms repeatedly producing patient images on practitioner referral through MRI, CT, X-ray, mammography, ultrasound, PET, and nuclear-medicine services, including patient scheduling and preparation, image acquisition, technical operations, interpretation coordination, reporting, billing, collections, and referral support.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports employment shares and wages for combined NAICS 621500, including 11.27 percent diagnostic-related technologists, 21.23 percent office and administrative support, management and business staff, as well as medical-laboratory occupations; BLS separately describes hands-on radiologic and MRI duties. FDA and RadNet document extensive radiology AI authorization and uses in image creation, interpretation support, workflow, reimbursement, and collections. Transformation: the parent mix is adjusted toward imaging-center occupations, then wage-weighted exposure is assigned to scheduling, authorization, documentation, coding, billing, collections, worklist triage, image-quality assistance, report drafting, and portions of physician interpretation after excluding physical patient preparation and positioning, contrast administration, scanner operation, safety, maintenance, and final accountable judgment. Direction of error: the mixed parent table may understate imaging technologists and radiologists, public-company AI claims may overstate deployable substitution, and faster image acquisition may create capacity rather than reduce labor; high administrative burden could make exposure larger.
caveats: BLS publishes only a combined 621500 occupation mix, requiring judgment to remove medical-laboratory work and reconstruct the imaging-center mix.
Most authorized radiology AI products assist rather than replace accountable clinicians, so device counts do not measure labor substitution.
Equipment-related acceleration may increase scan capacity and demand rather than proportionally reduce staffing.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit time-and-payroll study across independent MRI, CT, X-ray, mammography, ultrasound, PET, and nuclear-medicine centers separating clinical, reading, authorization, scheduling, billing, and existing-software hours would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Imaging centers have structured digital inputs and meaningful scheduling, prior-authorization, coding, billing, reporting, and workflow labor, while AI can assist reconstruction, protocoling, quality checks, triage, interpretation, and report preparation. Exposure remains limited because patients must be prepared and positioned, equipment operated safely, contrast and radiation managed, images evaluated in clinical context, and final services delivered under professional accountability.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS projects radiologic and MRI technologist employment to grow 5 percent from 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to aging, chronic disease, falls, and cancer; RadNet reports 10.9 percent imaging-center revenue growth in 2025 driven by higher fees and procedure volumes, including organic and acquired volume, and cites growth in PET/CT studies. Transformation: the ten-year occupation forecast and one scaled operator's mixed nominal growth are translated into a five-year constant-price, constant-quality imaging-procedure ratio after excluding acquisition effects, fee changes, mix shifts, and workflow productivity. Direction of error: employment growth may understate procedure growth when scan productivity rises, while RadNet's regional expansion and advanced-modality mix may overstate national LMM demand; payer controls and patient cost sharing could lower utilization.
caveats: Occupational employment is not procedure volume, and the BLS projection spans ten years rather than the five-year horizon.
RadNet's reported growth includes acquisitions, price, and modality mix and is not a constant-price national demand series.
Demand varies sharply across MRI, CT, mammography, ultrasound, X-ray, PET, and nuclear medicine.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price U.S. outpatient imaging procedure counts through 2031 from Medicare, commercial claims, state all-payer databases, and RIS or PACS cohorts, separated by modality, site of service, same-center growth, referral source, and acquisition effects, would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: An older population, chronic disease, cancer screening, falls, and expanding PET, CT, MRI, and preventive applications support modest real procedure growth. Payer prior authorization, high deductibles, reimbursement controls, appropriateness initiatives, and regional capacity constraints temper that growth, and no assumption is made that existing centers capture all demand.

caveats: No source measures default-lens eligibility among the frozen LMM-band firms.

The 859-firm count is margin-bridged with a broad hospitals and healthcare-facilities margin that may not fit freestanding imaging centers with different equipment, lease, and professional-fee structures.
Imaging-site counts, joint ventures, management companies, and legal firms are not interchangeable denominators.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level audit of the frozen 859-firm population using facility websites, ownership, modality mix, payer enrollment, referral concentration, radiology-group and hospital agreements, equipment leases, licenses, normalized EBITDA, joint-venture rights, and change-of-control terms would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition directly describes externally referred diagnostic imaging centers, so most standalone firms should fit the recurring outsourced-service lens. Eligibility is reduced by hospital or physician captive departments, management entities without transferable clinical operations, joint ventures whose governance blocks control transfer, single-contract or single-referrer dependence, equipment-heavy entities outside the EBITDA band, and businesses whose payer participation, professional-service arrangements, licenses, leases, or referral base cannot transfer independently.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $8,581.7M / sectoral output (T30) $23,106.3M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $236,059 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621512); class <5: 2,625 firms, avg revenue/firm $504,206 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $79,665 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 671 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,547,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $244,466 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 496 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,844,788 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $449,477 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 491 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,269,434 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,464,571 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 162 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,685,420 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,006,296 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 206 firms, avg revenue/firm $51,333,189 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,110,644 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 859

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: Census defines the industry as centers producing patient images on practitioner referral; BLS describes patient preparation and positioning, equipment operation, radiation protection, contrast administration, image evaluation, recordkeeping, certification, and state licensure; RadNet states that radiology groups retain control of professional supervision and interpretation while centers provide equipment and technical services, and that current AI aids image creation and interpretation. Transformation: procedures requiring a staffed physical site, regulated equipment, patient handling, safety, technologist work, and accountable professional interpretation are treated as operator-required, while software can automate scheduling, triage, reconstruction, reporting, and some interpretation assistance. Direction of error: physical service requirements make software-only elimination unlikely, but hospital or physician insourcing, mobile providers, remote reading, and AI-enabled throughput could remove more demand from the current LMM operator class than assumed.
caveats: Operator-required service does not guarantee that the current independent center retains the referral or payer contract.
Remote interpretation can centralize professional labor without eliminating the local physical imaging site.
The operator-required share differs by modality and could fall faster for standardized screening and image-review workflows.
o.change_evidence: Claims and facility panels measuring freestanding versus hospital or physician imaging share, mobile substitution, remote reading, autonomous AI completion, repeat scans, site closures, referral migration, and modality-level patient-facing labor through 2031 would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Patients and scanners must be co-located for most modalities, and trained staff must prepare patients, manage contrast and radiation, acquire adequate images, and respond to complications. AI can compress administrative and reading work but generally remains embedded in an accountable imaging operation. Some current centers can still be bypassed through hospital systems, physician-owned facilities, mobile equipment, or consolidated networks.

q.bridge: Source population and quantity: RadNet reports commercial negotiated rates, capitation, Medicare PFS payment, management fees, multi-year contracted reimbursement, competitive managed-care schedules, and the inability to pass through labor inflation without efficiency; CMS confirms that radiology and non-laboratory diagnostic tests are paid under the PFS. Transformation: fixed per-procedure and contracted prices permit initial retention of implemented labor and throughput benefit, then the estimate discounts payer renegotiation, government fee changes, capitation risk, competitive pricing, customer sharing, radiologist compensation, incremental software fees, and utilization controls over five years. Direction of error: RadNet's scale and bargaining leverage may overstate LMM retention, while multi-year rates and local access constraints can delay pass-through; competitive centers with weak payer leverage may retain less.
caveats: Neither source measures five-year retention of implemented AI benefit for LMM diagnostic imaging firms.
Technical fees, professional fees, management fees, commercial fee-for-service, government payment, and capitation transmit benefit differently.
RadNet's density and scale create procurement, utilization, and payer advantages unavailable to many LMM operators.
q.change_evidence: LMM imaging cohorts showing modality-level allowed amounts, payer mix, contract terms and renewals, capitation exposure, professional fees, AI and PACS costs, scan throughput, referral response, contribution margin, and labor cost before and after automation would materially change retained benefit.
q.rationale: Most payments are not cost-plus reimbursements of actual labor, so lower administrative or reading cost and higher scanner throughput can initially improve economics. Retention is constrained by concentrated commercial payers, government fee schedules, capitated risk, competitive contracting, professional-reading arrangements, payer utilization management, AI licensing costs, and the possibility that faster scans are used to expand access rather than remove cost.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: FDA lists AI-enabled medical devices authorized for U.S. marketing and heavily represented in radiology, while RadNet reports more than 700 FDA-cleared radiology AI/ML products by August 2024, current deployment for image creation and interpretation, a 22-solution FDA-cleared portfolio, and operational uses in workflow and collections. Transformation: regulatory availability and scaled-operator deployment are converted into the share of remaining exposed work an LMM imaging platform could implement within five years after PACS and RIS integration, clinical validation, cybersecurity, workflow redesign, training, payer treatment, and radiologist acceptance. Direction of error: authorization does not equal adoption or realized labor savings, and RadNet's scale, proprietary products, capital, and data overstate LMM readiness; continuing vendor integration and cloud distribution may nevertheless make current deployment understate five-year uptake.
caveats: The FDA list establishes authorization, not utilization, workflow coverage, or economic benefit.
RadNet is the largest U.S. freestanding imaging operator and also owns a digital-health segment, unlike a typical LMM center group.
Implementation varies by modality, vendor, PACS and RIS architecture, radiology-group relationship, and state requirements.
rho.change_evidence: Deployment cohorts from independent U.S. imaging centers measuring eligible studies and administrative tasks, active use, scan time, radiologist reading time, overrides, discrepancies, turnaround, denials, throughput, implementation cost, and labor hours before and after AI would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: Radiology has a mature regulated AI supply base, digital source data, and clear use cases in scheduling, authorization, scan acceleration, reconstruction, triage, reading assistance, reporting, coding, and collections. Implementation will be incomplete because products are fragmented by modality and indication, integrations and validation are costly, reimbursement for AI is uncertain, false positives create review work, radiologists retain liability, and smaller centers have limited IT and governance capacity.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years; RadNet discloses eleven imaging-center asset acquisitions totaling about $133.1 million in 2025 and nine totaling about $59.0 million in 2024, alongside joint-venture and digital-health activity. Transformation: the employer intent rate is discounted for plans that do not close, gifts and internal transfers, asset purchases that are not firm-level control deals, nontransferable payer or professional arrangements, and quality or compliance failures, while repeated imaging acquisitions modestly support specialty buyer demand. Direction of error: owner intentions overstate completed transfers and one strategic acquirer's activity is not a market denominator, but capital needs, succession, and active regional consolidation can make qualified centers more transferable than ordinary employers.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers.
RadNet's disclosed transactions are one buyer's asset acquisitions and do not provide an industry denominator.
Joint-venture interests, professional radiology practices, management entities, and imaging-center operating companies have different transfer mechanics.
s5.change_evidence: Completed U.S. control-deal counts and eligible-company denominators from Irving Levin, PitchBook, Capital IQ, imaging-center brokers, lender files, state facility records, and transaction counsel, classified by modality, six-digit activity, EBITDA band, and asset-versus-equity form, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Imaging groups can buy local referral density, payer contracts, modality capacity, equipment, staff, and geographic networks, and the cited acquirer completed repeated center transactions. Qualifying transfers remain constrained by physician and hospital joint ventures, radiology-group contracts, certificate or licensing requirements, payer enrollment, debt and equipment leases, referral concentration, and transaction structures that acquire only assets.

records.principal_driver: Digitized images, high administrative friction, and a large regulated radiology-AI ecosystem support workflow and throughput improvement across repeat procedure volume.
records.principal_weakness: Patient-facing acquisition, expensive equipment, professional accountability, and payer-controlled reimbursement prevent software from removing most operator cost or demand.

caveats: The a range is reconstructed from the mixed NAICS 621500 occupation table and qualitative AI use cases; no six-digit imaging-center time-and-payroll study validates the 0.31 base, although the PROXY label, bridge, error directions, and wide range make the uncertainty honest.

The rho base converts device authorization and deployment by a uniquely scaled operator with its own digital-health segment into LMM five-year implementation. Neither source measures active adoption or realized labor removal at independent LMM centers.

Eligibility e is an explicit firm-level estimate, and the fixed n anchor is itself margin-bridged; the sensitivity interval does not vary that population anchor. These limitations are disclosed and already keep readiness MODEL_CONDITIONED.

The s5 evidence combines cross-industry owner intentions with one buyer's asset acquisitions. Intentions are not completed transfers, and asset transactions are not necessarily firm-level control deals, so the 0.18 base remains a broad transferability proxy.

The q sources establish fee schedules, negotiated rates, capitation, and management-fee mechanics but do not observe five-year retention of AI benefits. The 0.54 base is therefore judgment conditioned on payer bargaining, professional fees, utilization controls, and software costs.

The d5 range bridges a ten-year occupation forecast and RadNet nominal revenue growth that includes price, acquisition, and modality-mix effects into five-year constant-price procedure demand. The packet identifies these mismatches, and the low/high range is appropriately treated as proxy sensitivity rather than a measured forecast.

The lens is coherent, but marking the industry non-heterogeneous understates material differences across MRI, CT, mammography, ultrasound, X-ray, PET, and nuclear medicine in staffing, reimbursement, capital intensity, and AI applicability; the packet partly mitigates this with modality-specific caveats.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS manual defines 621512 as centers primarily producing patient images generally on practitioner referral and lists CT, radiological, X-ray, ultrasound, and MRI examples. It supports the lens and physical-service premise, but not the numeric eligibility or operator-required shares, which are explicitly judgments.

The May 2023 BLS NAICS 621500 table reports the combined Medical and Diagnostic Laboratories population, occupation employment shares and wages, including 11.27% diagnostic-related technologists and technicians and 21.23% office and administrative support. The packet correctly discloses that translating this parent-industry mix into six-digit imaging-center labor exposure is a proxy.

BLS describes patient preparation and positioning, equipment operation, image evaluation, recordkeeping, contrast use, certification and state licensing, and projects 5% employment growth from 2024 to 2034 with aging, chronic disease, falls, and cancer as demand drivers.

The FDA page identifies AI-enabled devices authorized for U.S. marketing, explains applicable premarket review, and contains a large radiology category. The packet properly cautions that authorization does not establish adoption, workflow coverage, or labor savings.

RadNet's 2025 Form 10-K supports the cited modality, AI, workflow, reimbursement, professional-control, growth, and acquisition facts, including 11 listed 2025 imaging-center asset acquisitions totaling $133.109 million and nine in 2024 totaling $59.045 million. It is also correctly disclosed as a scaled public-operator proxy rather than an LMM denominator or outcome study.

CMS states that the Physician Fee Schedule is Medicare's primary payment method for radiology services and diagnostic tests other than clinical laboratory tests, supporting the payment-mechanism claim but not a measured five-year retention rate.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and describes the survey population. The packet correctly treats this as cross-industry intention evidence rather than completed imaging control deals.

records.title: Diagnostic Imaging Centers

caveats: a: The BLS occupation table is May 2023 and covers five-digit NAICS 561620 rather than the exact six-digit population.
a: Occupation shares are employment-weighted; the primitive is wage-weighted and therefore requires wage and task mapping.
a: ADT is a large public operator whose technology and central-monitoring model may overstate the digital task share of LMM firms.
rho: The Census figures are economy-wide and do not isolate security-system services.
rho: The ADT filing describes intended benefits and risks, not independently measured implementation rates.
rho: Cybersecurity, privacy, dispatch regulation, and false-negative risk can slow deployment beyond ordinary office automation.
e: No source measures the eligible share of the supplied LMM firm population.
e: The supplied count uses a margin bridge rather than observed normalized EBITDA.
e: Dealer, subcontractor, monitoring-only, and installation-heavy models may be classified inconsistently in business databases.
s5: Owner age is from data year 2018 and is not industry-specific.
s5: The 57 disclosed acquisitions include adjacent fire-protection businesses and come from the acquirer.
s5: A single strategic buyer's activity does not establish an industry-wide probability or reveal failed transactions.
q: Observed revenue pricing is not a direct measure of retained AI benefit.
q: ADT's residential scale, brand, financing, and contract portfolio differ from independent LMM operators.
q: The range excludes demand loss and operator displacement, which are handled separately.
d5: The BLS series includes locksmiths and measures employment rather than real service quantity.
d5: The projection horizon extends beyond the primitive's five-year horizon.
d5: Product upgrades, bundling, and quality changes make real demand hard to separate from nominal revenue.
o: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share.
o: Residential DIY evidence may overstate displacement for commercial, institutional, and regulated systems.
o: Definitions of accountable operation differ when software vendors, third-party central stations, dealers, and installers share responsibility.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Residential and commercial customers pay installation and equipment charges, time-and-material service fees, and contractual recurring monitoring or maintenance fees. Monitoring can be bundled with installation, maintenance, repair, response notification, and equipment rental.
lens.excluded_activities: Locksmiths; guard services; captive security departments; pure equipment retail or manufacturing; software-only or hardware-only vendors without an accountable service operation; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms that design, install, inspect, test, repair, maintain, or monitor electronic security, intrusion, video-surveillance, access-control, and fire-alarm systems for external customers on recurring contracts or through repeat service relationships.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages in five-digit NAICS 561620, a broader population than six-digit 561621, rather than task exposure. I mapped each major occupation's task mix to AI-susceptible documentation, quoting, design support, event triage, customer service, scheduling, dispatch, billing, sales support, and management work, then bounded the wage-weighted result. Inclusion of locksmith activity likely biases the proxy downward because locksmith work is more physical than electronic security-system work; occupation-level task judgments may bias either direction.
caveats: The BLS occupation table is May 2023 and covers five-digit NAICS 561620 rather than the exact six-digit population.
Occupation shares are employment-weighted; the primitive is wage-weighted and therefore requires wage and task mapping.
ADT is a large public operator whose technology and central-monitoring model may overstate the digital task share of LMM firms.
a.change_evidence: Obtain payroll-weighted task inventories from at least 20 independent electronic-security installers and monitoring operators, including minutes per alarm event, proposal, dispatch, service ticket, and installation workflow before current automation.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is split between a large physical core and a sizable digital or administrative layer. BLS reports installation, maintenance, and repair at 45.18% of employment, office and administrative support at 20.59%, sales at 10.65%, and customer service representatives at 4.56%. AI can reduce labor in alarm and video triage, proposal and system-design preparation, documentation, scheduling, dispatch, billing, customer support, and sales administration. On-site wiring, mounting, commissioning, inspection, troubleshooting, and code-compliance work remain materially human.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment in five-digit NAICS 561620 rather than constant-price, constant-quality demand for six-digit 561621. I convert the reported decade employment growth into an approximate five-year demand anchor and widen it for labor-productivity differences, locksmith inclusion, housing and construction cycles, and new intelligent-security use cases. Employment can understate quantity when productivity rises, while DIY substitution can make it overstate operator demand.
caveats: The BLS series includes locksmiths and measures employment rather than real service quantity.
The projection horizon extends beyond the primitive's five-year horizon.
Product upgrades, bundling, and quality changes make real demand hard to separate from nominal revenue.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain a constant-quality industry demand series separating subscriber counts, monitored premises, active commercial sites, installation units, service calls, and real price changes for exact six-digit NAICS 561621.
d5.rationale: BLS projects industry employment of 157.2 thousand in 2024 and 176.0 thousand in 2034, a 11.9% increase, which implies modest underlying expansion rather than contraction. Security, life-safety, video, access-control, maintenance, and monitoring needs persist, while AI sensing can improve verification and extend use cases. Construction cyclicality, customer budget pressure, and lower-cost DIY systems make a mild decline plausible in the low case.

caveats: No source measures the eligible share of the supplied LMM firm population.
The supplied count uses a margin bridge rather than observed normalized EBITDA.
Dealer, subcontractor, monitoring-only, and installation-heavy models may be classified inconsistently in business databases.
e.change_evidence: Acquire establishment-level revenue-mix data for the supplied LMM firm universe, classifying each firm's monitoring revenue, maintenance agreements, repeat service history, customer concentration, and independent operating status.
e.rationale: The official product definition places installation, maintenance, repair, monitoring, notification, physical response, and bundled equipment rental inside the industry. These activities commonly create recurring monitoring fees, maintenance obligations, or repeat service calls. I exclude firms that merely resell equipment, conduct isolated project work without a repeat relationship, or lack transferable operations. The supplied firm count is margin-bridged, so eligibility cannot be observed directly from it.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561621 ($11,289,977,938) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561621 ($29,671,243,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $235,724 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561621); class <5: 3,202 firms, avg revenue/firm $420,643 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $65,831 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,077 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,265,794 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $198,097 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 701 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,557,264 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $400,212 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 610 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,514,118 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,175,959 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 106 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,085,387 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,334,363 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 65 firms, avg revenue/firm $261,104,292 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $40,862,822 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 716

caveats: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share.
Residential DIY evidence may overstate displacement for commercial, institutional, and regulated systems.
Definitions of accountable operation differ when software vendors, third-party central stations, dealers, and installers share responsibility.
o.change_evidence: Measure customer migration by service type from professionally installed and monitored systems to self-installation, self-monitoring, software-only control, and manufacturer-managed service, separating residential from commercial and regulated fire-alarm work.
o.rationale: A human-accountable operator remains important for commercial design, licensed installation, wiring, commissioning, inspection, repair, emergency-response coordination, code compliance, and support of legacy systems. Monitoring and verification can become more automated, and ADT identifies both expanding DIY offerings and competitors that allow self-monitoring without third-party involvement. I therefore expect meaningful displacement in simpler residential installations and routine monitoring, but not wholesale removal of the operator for the current service basket.

q.bridge: ADT's contractual recurring revenue, pricing outcomes, and competitive disclosures describe a large public residential-security operator rather than LMM firms or retained AI benefits directly. I map contract duration, recurring billing, early-cancellation economics, and demonstrated average-price increases to the share of implemented gross benefits retained after repricing and competition. ADT's brand and scale likely bias retention upward, while LMM commercial relationships and specialized local service can provide stickiness not captured by the residential mix.
caveats: Observed revenue pricing is not a direct measure of retained AI benefit.
ADT's residential scale, brand, financing, and contract portfolio differ from independent LMM operators.
The range excludes demand loss and operator displacement, which are handled separately.
q.change_evidence: Obtain LMM cohort data linking realized automation savings to gross margin, renewal pricing, churn, discounting, customer contract language, and pass-through clauses over at least 36 months.
q.rationale: Recurring monthly contracts and bundled monitoring create a stronger capture channel than open-book labor billing. ADT states that most revenue is recurring and reports 2025 recurring-revenue growth driven by 113 million dollars of average-price increases, demonstrating pricing agency. Retention is not complete: professional installation and monitoring face pricing pressure, DIY competitors offer self-monitoring, renewal and attrition expose savings to customer sharing, and implementation can lower market prices over time.

rho.bridge: The Census working paper measures economy-wide firm AI use, while ADT documents one large operator's AI-enabled sensing, alarm-verification use cases, and integration constraints. I use those observations to bound implementation of the exposed tasks at LMM security-system firms. ADT's scale and capital access likely bias achievable implementation upward; its legacy installed base may bias implementation downward relative to younger firms.
caveats: The Census figures are economy-wide and do not isolate security-system services.
The ADT filing describes intended benefits and risks, not independently measured implementation rates.
Cybersecurity, privacy, dispatch regulation, and false-negative risk can slow deployment beyond ordinary office automation.
rho.change_evidence: Collect five-year technology roadmaps, installed-base compatibility audits, workflow adoption logs, and realized labor-hour changes from at least 20 LMM alarm dealers and central-station operators using AI verification or service automation.
rho.rationale: Implementation is already technically plausible in verification, false-alarm reduction, ambient sensing, customer interaction, knowledge retrieval, and back-office workflows. Census evidence shows adoption is meaningfully higher on an employment-weighted basis and is mainly augmentative. Real constraints are consequential: security and life-safety failures create liability, local rules govern false alarms and dispatch, integrations require testing, and legacy panels may need material hardware investment before modern AI features work.

s5.bridge: Census reports owner age across all responding employer businesses, and Pye-Barker reports acquisition activity across a mixed fire-protection, life-safety, and security-services platform. I translate older-owner prevalence plus demonstrated acquisition of a directly relevant monitored-security firm into a five-year transfer probability for eligible LMM firms. The broad owner-age population and sponsor-led mixed-industry acquisition count likely overstate transferable supply if many firms lack succession readiness; missing transactions by other buyers and undisclosed private sales bias it downward.
caveats: Owner age is from data year 2018 and is not industry-specific.
The 57 disclosed acquisitions include adjacent fire-protection businesses and come from the acquirer.
A single strategic buyer's activity does not establish an industry-wide probability or reveal failed transactions.
s5.change_evidence: Build a five-year transaction census from Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, broker listings, state alarm-license transfers, and lender records, matched to a denominator of independently owned eligible LMM firms and coded for true control change.
s5.rationale: Census reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were at least age 55, creating succession pressure without proving a sale. Deal activity is visibly active: Pye-Barker disclosed 57 acquisitions in 2025 across fire alarm, sprinkler, suppression, and security, and separately disclosed acquiring S.E.M., an operator with monitoring, installation, inspection, testing, and maintenance. A qualifying transfer remains uncertain because internal succession, continued ownership, and operational dependence on the founder can prevent a market sale.

records.principal_driver: Recurring monitoring and maintenance relationships create a channel to retain value from automating high-volume event handling and coordination work.
records.principal_weakness: Nearly half of employment is in installation, maintenance, and repair, while DIY and self-monitoring can displace the operator from the easiest customer segments.

sources.supports: Defines security-system monitoring, response notification, physical response, installation, maintenance, repair, remote video monitoring, and equipment rental bundled with monitoring.
sources.title: NAPCS Product List for NAICS 5616: Investigation and Security Services

sources.supports: Reports 158,450 total employment and detailed occupation shares, including installation and repair, office support, sales, customer service, dispatch, management, business, and computer work.

sources.supports: Documents recurring monitoring revenue, pricing, professional and DIY offerings, self-monitoring competition, AI-enabled verification and false-alarm reduction, legacy-system incompatibility, integration risk, monitoring centers, and skilled installation and service.
sources.title: ADT Inc. 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Projects security systems services employment of 157.2 thousand in 2024 and 176.0 thousand in 2034, an increase of 11.9%.
sources.title: BLS Industry Employment and Output Projections

sources.supports: Reports AI use by 18% of firms and 32% on an employment-weighted basis; 66% of users reported augmentation only and 2% reported employment decreases.
sources.title: The Rapid Adoption of Generative AI

sources.supports: Reports that 51% of responding employer-business owners were at least age 55 in the 2018 data year.

sources.supports: Company announcement reports 57 acquisitions across fire alarm, sprinkler, suppression, and security companies in 2025 and expansion to 9,000 employees across 47 states.

records.title: Security Systems Services (except Locksmiths)

caveats: a: The occupation table is for 2023 NAICS 621600 employers of all sizes, not the exact LMM acquisition lens.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not identify the current automation baseline.
a: Exposure varies materially among skilled nursing, therapy, aide, infusion, hospice, and administrative workflows.
a: The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
rho: Telehealth and remote-monitoring claims are not a direct measure of AI use or back-office automation.
rho: Medicare rules do not describe implementation across Medicaid, commercial, or private-pay business.
rho: Clinical safety, HIPAA, state licensure, and integration constraints vary by service line and agency.
e: The NAICS and Medicare definitions do not measure recurring-revenue quality, transferable payer contracts, or normalized EBITDA.
e: In-home hospice, infusion, therapy, and skilled home health can have different referral, reimbursement, and compliance economics.
e: The injected count of 1,983 relies on a broad healthcare-facilities margin bridge that may not represent agency-level home health margins.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and intentions rather than completed transactions.
s5: No source measures five-year control-transfer probability for eligible LMM home health agencies.
s5: Regulatory, payer, quality, and referral-concentration issues can prevent an intended sale from qualifying.
q: No source directly measures the discounted share of implemented AI benefits retained by LMM agencies.
q: Retention differs across Medicare prospective payment, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, commercial, and private-pay contracts.
q: MedPAC's 20.2% aggregate 2023 FFS Medicare margin for freestanding agencies is not a total-company margin and varies widely across providers.
d5: The BLS forecast covers an occupation employed heavily outside skilled home health agencies.
d5: MedPAC's volume series is limited to fee-for-service Medicare and does not measure total industry demand.
d5: Demand can be capacity-constrained by clinician and aide availability rather than patient need.
o: Medicare's in-person rule does not govern all payer segments or every service in the NAICS code.
o: The source evidence does not quantify the fraction of visits that technology can safely replace.
o: Operator requirement varies by acuity, discipline, state scope-of-practice rules, caregiver availability, and home conditions.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients receive episodic or ongoing home-based clinical care ordered under plans of care; agencies are paid by Medicare, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, commercial insurers, health systems, or private pay through prospective 30-day bundles, per-visit rates, case rates, or contracted fee schedules.
lens.excluded_activities: Nonmedical homemaker and companion services classified outside 621610, independent practitioner offices, equipment-only rental or retail, facility-based care, captive internal programs, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market home health agencies providing recurring or repeat skilled nursing, therapy, home health aide, home infusion, in-home hospice, medical social, and related clinical services in patients' homes to external patients and payers.

a.bridge: BLS's 2023 NAICS 621600 occupation table covers employers of all sizes in the home-health family rather than a wage-weighted task study of lower-middle-market 621610 agencies. It shows 58.14% of employment in home health and personal care aides, 11.21% in registered nurses, 20.44% across healthcare practitioners and technical occupations, and 6.27% in office and administrative support. I map documentation, intake, coding, authorization, scheduling, routing, care-plan summarization, routine patient communication, and portions of monitoring to remaining AI exposure while leaving bathing, lifting, wound care, medication administration, examination, therapy, infusion, and in-home judgment largely unexposed. Employment shares underweight higher-paid clinicians and do not reveal time within occupations, so the mapping could understate exposure in documentation-heavy clinical work or overstate it where digital tools already automate administration.
caveats: The occupation table is for 2023 NAICS 621600 employers of all sizes, not the exact LMM acquisition lens.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not identify the current automation baseline.
Exposure varies materially among skilled nursing, therapy, aide, infusion, hospice, and administrative workflows.
The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study from a representative panel of 621610 agencies separating travel, direct care, assessment, documentation, coding, intake, authorization, scheduling, supervision, communication, and already automated work would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: AI can reduce clerical coordination and the documentation burden carried by clinicians and supervisors, but most direct-care hours require travel, touch, observation, skilled intervention, and accountable judgment in an uncontrolled home environment. The base therefore treats about one-quarter of current wage-weighted work as exposed rather than treating the large aide workforce as broadly substitutable.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 17% growth from 2024 to 2034 for home health and personal care aides across several industries and cites population aging and movement from institutions to home and community settings, while MedPAC reports declining FFS Medicare home health users and periods through 2023 amid migration to Medicare Advantage and fewer hospitalizations. I translate those offsetting occupation-wide and payer-specific indicators into modest five-year growth in constant-price demand for the current 621610 service basket. The BLS series includes nonmedical services outside 621610 and may overstate skilled-home-health growth; FFS data omit Medicare Advantage and may understate total demand.
caveats: The BLS forecast covers an occupation employed heavily outside skilled home health agencies.
MedPAC's volume series is limited to fee-for-service Medicare and does not measure total industry demand.
Demand can be capacity-constrained by clinician and aide availability rather than patient need.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-price 621610 revenue, unique patients, completed visits by discipline, referral acceptance, Medicare Advantage and Medicaid utilization, private-pay hours, and hospital-to-home discharge rates would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: Aging, chronic disease, patient preference, and care migration support home-based demand, but payer authorization, workforce supply, reduced hospitalization, benefit design, and substitution toward less-skilled home and community services constrain realized volume. The base assumes growth, not the full occupation forecast.

caveats: The NAICS and Medicare definitions do not measure recurring-revenue quality, transferable payer contracts, or normalized EBITDA.
In-home hospice, infusion, therapy, and skilled home health can have different referral, reimbursement, and compliance economics.
The injected count of 1,983 relies on a broad healthcare-facilities margin bridge that may not represent agency-level home health margins.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 621610 roster matched to Medicare enrollment, state licenses, tax records, payer mix, visit recurrence, referral concentration, ownership independence, normalized EBITDA, quality sanctions, and change-of-ownership history would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is centered on agencies delivering skilled and related care in the home, which generally creates repeat visits, payer relationships, referral channels, and transferable operating infrastructure. Ineligibility remains for owner-dependent clinical practices, weakly documented agencies, predominantly one-off infusion or equipment relationships, nontransferable licenses or payer enrollments, highly concentrated referral sources, and firms lacking normalized LMM earnings.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621610 ($71,424,773,339) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621610 ($113,350,417,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: BLS describes hands-on aide work and in-home monitoring, medication support, recordkeeping, and physical assistance, while CMS allows telecommunications in the plan of care only when it does not substitute for an ordered in-person visit. I map those occupational and Medicare rules to the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable home health agency and its clinicians or aides. Medicare may overstate in-person requirements relative to other payers, while patient acuity, liability, and the uncontrolled home setting may preserve even more operator involvement.
caveats: Medicare's in-person rule does not govern all payer segments or every service in the NAICS code.
The source evidence does not quantify the fraction of visits that technology can safely replace.
Operator requirement varies by acuity, discipline, state scope-of-practice rules, caregiver availability, and home conditions.
o.change_evidence: Payer rules, plan-of-care audits, patient-level visit substitutions, adverse-event data, and controlled before-and-after studies showing which in-person nursing, therapy, aide, and monitoring services can be safely eliminated or self-served would materially change this share.
o.rationale: AI can support monitoring, education, triage, and documentation, but it cannot generally bathe or transfer a patient, assess a wound in context, administer an infusion, perform therapy, reconcile a complex home situation, or carry clinical accountability. Some check-ins and low-acuity monitoring can be removed or self-served, leaving a large agency-operated core.

caveats: No source directly measures the discounted share of implemented AI benefits retained by LMM agencies.
Retention differs across Medicare prospective payment, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, commercial, and private-pay contracts.
MedPAC's 20.2% aggregate 2023 FFS Medicare margin for freestanding agencies is not a total-company margin and varies widely across providers.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and claims dataset showing payer mix, unit of payment, rate-update clauses, denial rates, visit thresholds, contract renewal outcomes, technology costs, and gross-margin changes before and after workflow automation would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Medicare's case-mix-adjusted 30-day payment creates room to retain productivity gains within a payment period, especially from documentation, scheduling, authorization, and supervision rather than fewer required visits. Retention is reduced by annual rate updates and behavioral adjustments, Medicare Advantage and Medicaid contracting, competitive referral markets, quality requirements, wage pressure, software and integration expense, and payer audit or repricing responses. MedPAC's high aggregate Medicare margin for freestanding agencies indicates economic room in that payer segment but does not measure AI-derived retention or total-company profitability.

rho.bridge: CMS payment and coverage materials describe structured OASIS assessment, plan-of-care, billing, and telecommunications rules, while MedPAC reports that only 1.2% of 2023 Medicare 30-day periods included telehealth or remote monitoring. I translate the presence of digital clinical and claims workflows into five-year implementation of AI-assisted documentation, intake, coding, scheduling, quality review, and monitoring, then haircut for privacy, clinical validation, interoperability, payer audit, consent, workforce training, and liability constraints. Medicare's low reported telehealth or monitoring use may understate back-office AI adoption but signals that patient-facing substitution could diffuse more slowly than administrative assistance.
caveats: Telehealth and remote-monitoring claims are not a direct measure of AI use or back-office automation.
Medicare rules do not describe implementation across Medicaid, commercial, or private-pay business.
Clinical safety, HIPAA, state licensure, and integration constraints vary by service line and agency.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of independent home health agencies reporting workflow-level AI deployment, clinician adoption, audit findings, documentation time, denied claims, visit productivity, patient outcomes, implementation cost, and tool retirement rates would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Structured records, recurring assessments, high documentation load, centralized scheduling, and prospective-payment workflows make assistance tools implementable. Implementation will be uneven because agencies must integrate multiple payer and referral systems, protect health information, validate clinical outputs, and keep licensed clinicians accountable.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among US employer-business owners across industries, reporting 22%, rather than completed external control transactions for home health agencies. I haircut that intention rate for plans that do not close, family or internal transfers, payer-enrollment and license transfer requirements, quality liabilities, referral dependence, and owner clinical involvement. The all-industry proxy may overstate qualifying deals in compliance-troubled agencies but may understate activity in a fragmented healthcare segment with established strategic buyers.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and intentions rather than completed transactions.
No source measures five-year control-transfer probability for eligible LMM home health agencies.
Regulatory, payer, quality, and referral-concentration issues can prevent an intended sale from qualifying.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of 621610 control deals from CMS change-of-ownership files, state license records, PitchBook, Capital IQ, Irving Levin, and healthcare brokers, matched to the eligible LMM firm roster and excluding internal reorganizations, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Home health agencies can be transferable operating platforms with clinicians, payer enrollments, referral relationships, and recurring census, but a qualifying control transfer must survive diligence on billing, quality, staffing, licenses, and ownership-change approvals. Completed external transactions should be materially below broad owner intentions.

records.principal_driver: AI can remove meaningful documentation and coordination burden from a large recurring home-based care workforce under partly bundled reimbursement.
records.principal_weakness: Hands-on care, travel, clinical accountability, payer oversight, and workforce scarcity limit substitutable labor and complicate implementation.

sources.supports: Defines 621610 around skilled nursing in the home with personal care, therapy, medical social, medication, equipment, counseling, 24-hour, nutrition, speech, audiology, infusion, hospice, and related services, and distinguishes nonmedical home care and equipment-only businesses.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual: 621610

sources.supports: Describes hands-on daily living, housekeeping, medication, vital-sign, exercise, recordkeeping, and reporting duties; projects 17% occupation growth from 2024 to 2034 and cites aging and movement toward home and community care.

records.title: Home Health Care Services

caveats: a: Occupation shares measure staffing rather than exposed tasks.
a: The BLS table covers employers of all sizes and predates the run date.
rho: Acute-care hospital adoption is not residential behavioral-health adoption.
rho: Reported tool use does not establish net labor substitution.
e: SAMHSA's survey includes outpatient and hospital facilities in addition to residential operators.
e: Facility counts do not map directly to firms or the frozen earnings band.
s5: Broad behavioral-health deal tracking is not specific to this NAICS code.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed transactions.
q: Public operators include hospitals and outpatient programs outside the lens.
q: Payer and program mix varies sharply among mental-health, substance-use, adolescent, and adult facilities.
d5: Employment growth is not a direct measure of constant-quality demand.
d5: SAMHSA prevalence and treatment receipt combine every level of behavioral-health care.
o: The estimate concerns continued operator requirement, not whether software reduces operator staffing.
o: Payer level-of-care policies may shift volume outside the lens even when human treatment remains necessary.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients are service recipients, while commercial insurers, Medicaid, Medicare and other public programs, and self-pay clients fund care. Revenue is generally episode, day, service, or negotiated-rate based and reduced by payer contractual adjustments, discounts, and collection concessions.
lens.excluded_activities: Exclusively outpatient behavioral-health centers, licensed psychiatric hospitals emphasizing medical treatment and monitoring, residential intellectual-disability facilities, temporary shelters, correctional facilities, shell entities, captive units, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: United States lower-middle-market operators providing recurring or episode-based residential care and treatment for mental-health and substance-use conditions, including nonhospital rehabilitation facilities, mental-health halfway houses, psychiatric convalescent settings, and residential group homes, with room, board, supervision, counseling, rehabilitation, and support services.

a.bridge: The source quantity is the BLS industry occupation and wage mix, with large counselor, social-service, healthcare-support, clinical, management, and administrative groups. I transform it into wage-weighted task exposure by assigning high exposure to documentation, coding, utilization-review preparation, scheduling, intake, billing, care-plan drafting, and routine education; moderate exposure to structured counseling support and clinical synthesis; and low exposure to therapeutic alliance, crisis judgment, medication administration, and residential supervision. The proxy may overstate substitution because occupation titles conceal safety-critical interpersonal tasks, or understate it because higher-paid clinicians and managers carry substantial documentation load.
caveats: Occupation shares measure staffing rather than exposed tasks.
The BLS table covers employers of all sizes and predates the run date.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study for this industry that separates therapy, observation, documentation, payer administration, coordination, and facility operations would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: Behavioral-health facilities employ a larger share of information-intensive counselors, social workers, managers, nurses, and office staff than many residential-care industries. AI can reduce clerical and synthesis time and support staff decisions, but direct counseling, safeguarding, assessment, and continuous supervision remain human responsibilities.

d5.bridge: The source quantities are BLS projected employment growth for this exact industry, SAMHSA prevalence and treatment-gap measures, and public-company behavioral-health patient-day trends. I transform employment and utilization into a five-year constant-price, constant-quality residential service-demand index, then widen the range for insurance access, Medicaid policy, bed supply, stigma, clinical incidence, outpatient and telehealth substitution, and shifts in length of stay. The proxy may overstate quantity if projected employment reflects lower productivity, or understate demand if unmet need and parity enforcement convert rapidly into residential admissions.
caveats: Employment growth is not a direct measure of constant-quality demand.
SAMHSA prevalence and treatment receipt combine every level of behavioral-health care.
d5.change_evidence: A national industry series for inflation-adjusted residential treatment days, admissions, occupancy, average stay, authorization denials, bed supply, and level-of-care transitions would materially change the demand index.
d5.rationale: Mental-health and substance-use need remains large, treatment gaps persist, and BLS expects this industry to grow. Growth should be moderate rather than explosive because most treatment occurs outside residential settings and payers increasingly direct appropriate patients toward outpatient, medication, telehealth, and shorter-stay care.

e.bridge: The source population is SAMHSA's national inventory of substance-use, mental-health, and combined treatment facilities, where private nonprofit operation exceeds private for-profit operation and government facilities form a smaller share. I map operation type to the eligible lower-middle-market residential-firm universe by excluding public providers, heavily discounting nonprofits and mixed outpatient operations, and retaining transferable private residential operators. The proxy may understate eligibility if nonprofit assets or subsidiaries can transfer control, or overstate it because the survey includes facilities outside this NAICS and does not test earnings size or transfer restrictions.
caveats: SAMHSA's survey includes outpatient and hospital facilities in addition to residential operators.
Facility counts do not map directly to firms or the frozen earnings band.
e.change_evidence: A national facility-to-firm crosswalk with NAICS, level of care, ownership and tax status, normalized earnings, licenses, payer enrollment, property control, and change-of-control restrictions would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Residential treatment is an external, repeat service and thus fits the operating lens, but the broader facility population has substantial nonprofit ownership. Mixed levels of care, state licensure, real-estate structures, payer enrollment, and single-site dependence further reduce the portion of dataset-estimated firms that are coherent and transferable.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 623220 ($13,481,974,541) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 623220 ($22,002,475,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: The estimate concerns continued operator requirement, not whether software reduces operator staffing.
Payer level-of-care policies may shift volume outside the lens even when human treatment remains necessary.
o.change_evidence: Comparative longitudinal evidence on residential admissions avoided or shortened by digital, outpatient, and home-based alternatives, stratified by diagnosis and acuity with safety and relapse outcomes, would materially change this value.
o.rationale: Patients appropriate for residential care need an accountable licensed operator for housing, continuous supervision, counseling, medication coordination, crisis response, safeguarding, meals, documentation, and transition planning. Digital therapy, monitoring, outpatient care, and telehealth can prevent or shorten some stays, but software alone cannot safely provide the current residential service basket for most admitted patients.

q.bridge: The source quantities are public-company disclosures that behavioral-health revenue comes from government programs, commercial insurers, and patients and is recorded after contractual adjustments and concessions, together with SAMHSA evidence that facilities accept diverse payment types. I transform payer structure into retained implemented benefit by allowing savings under fixed or negotiated episode and day rates, then discounting for commercial renewal repricing, public-rate updates, utilization review, network competition, patient discounts, compliance spend, and quality reinvestment. The proxy may overstate retention at Medicaid-heavy facilities and understate it at differentiated self-pay residential programs.
caveats: Public operators include hospitals and outpatient programs outside the lens.
Payer and program mix varies sharply among mental-health, substance-use, adolescent, and adult facilities.
q.change_evidence: Facility-level contracts and cohorts linking AI deployment to allowed revenue, denials, labor cost, length of stay, renewal rates, price changes, and contribution margin would materially change retention.
q.rationale: An operator can retain some administrative and staffing productivity when payment is fixed per day, episode, or contract period. Capture varies widely: self-pay and differentiated programs can hold price, while government and managed-care payers can reprice, deny days, tighten authorization, or demand lower-cost outpatient alternatives.

rho.bridge: The source population is nonfederal acute-care hospitals reporting predictive AI integrated with electronic records, including rapid growth in billing and scheduling uses. I translate this adjacent healthcare adoption into a five-year implementation share for exposed residential behavioral-health tasks, discounting for smaller independent operators, fragmented EHRs, substance-use privacy protections, clinical bias, crisis risk, consent, and required human review. The proxy likely overstates smaller-facility readiness because hospitals have greater technology resources, but it may understate generative documentation adoption not captured by predictive-AI questions.
caveats: Acute-care hospital adoption is not residential behavioral-health adoption.
Reported tool use does not establish net labor substitution.
rho.change_evidence: Residential behavioral-health provider surveys or audited deployment studies showing AI use, note time saved, claim performance, clinical error rates, staff acceptance, and avoided hiring would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Administrative use cases have clear precedents and should spread through EHR and revenue-cycle vendors. Clinical and patient-facing uses will move more slowly because behavioral-health errors can cause serious harm, patient narratives are sensitive, and direct crisis response and treatment decisions demand professional accountability.

s5.bridge: The source populations combine employer-business owners in Gallup's succession survey, private-equity behavioral-health transactions tracked by PESP, and a public behavioral-health operator's disclosed acquisition of a residential treatment provider. I use owner transfer intention as a broad prior and adjust it upward for active platform acquisition, then discount for deals outside residential care, minority growth investments, nonprofit ownership, licensing, investigations, and transactions that do not qualify as control transfers. This may overstate probability because broad behavioral-health deal counts include outpatient assets, or understate it because small private add-ons are incompletely disclosed.
caveats: Broad behavioral-health deal tracking is not specific to this NAICS code.
Owner intentions are not completed transactions.
s5.change_evidence: A verified denominator of eligible residential operators and closed qualifying control deals by provider size, level of care, ownership, payer mix, and state would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Behavioral health has sustained sponsor and strategic interest, and residential treatment assets appear in disclosed acquisitions. A fragmented provider base and owner succession support supply, but care-quality scrutiny, state transaction review, reimbursement exposure, and nonprofit ownership reduce executable qualifying transfers.

records.principal_driver: A large counseling and administrative workforce, persistent treatment need, and active healthcare technology adoption support practical workflow automation.
records.principal_weakness: Residential treatment is clinically sensitive and payer-managed, so automation must remain supervised while much of the benefit can be repriced or diverted to lower-cost care settings.

caveats: a: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for six-digit NAICS 541850.
a: The occupation profile reports headcount shares rather than compensation shares and does not fully expose installation or maintenance occupations.
a: Advertising-industry AI evidence includes brands, agencies, publishers, and digital media rather than only display operators.
rho: General advertising adoption may run ahead of traditional local billboard operators.
rho: Declared use or intent does not establish sustained workflow integration or labor avoidance.
rho: Digital and static display operators may implement at very different rates.
e: Public sources do not measure eligibility in the frozen lower-middle-market population.
e: The industry combines display creation and placement with varying degrees of asset ownership, which affects transferability but not necessarily service eligibility.
s5: Gallup covers all employer industries rather than display advertising specifically.
s5: One large consolidator's acquisition count may not represent the probability for each eligible firm.
s5: Asset purchases do not always constitute qualifying firm control transfers.
q: Clear Channel and Lamar are much larger and more asset-intensive than many lower-middle-market firms.
q: The industry includes creative and operational services whose prices may track labor more closely.
q: Retention could be lower if self-service buying shifts bargaining power to advertisers or platforms.
d5: Industry revenue is not constant-price, constant-quality demand quantity.
d5: The 4.5% forecast was published in a 2024 industry report and ends in 2029 rather than 2031.
d5: Trade-association measures may not capture weaker segments or small-operator dispersion.
o: The estimate weights the asset-operating core more heavily than design-only services within the NAICS code.
o: Software could disintermediate local selling faster than expected even while a physical asset operator remains.
o: Human content approval is an industry best practice rather than a universal legal mandate.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Advertisers and agencies buy or lease access to location-specific billboard, transit, airport, street-furniture, retail, and other display inventory, commonly in campaign periods or network packages. Rates are driven primarily by location, audience, demand, competition, format, and occupancy; operators may also charge for design, printing, installation, and measurement.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house advertising units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo nonemployers, general advertising agencies classified elsewhere, media-buying agencies, sign manufacturers whose primary activity is fabrication, and firms outside the lower-middle-market band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat public-display advertising services to external customers, including campaign design, inventory sales, billboard and place-based display placement, digital-display management, printing, installation, and related measurement and account service.

a.bridge: A 2026 industry profile reports that management, business and financial, arts/design/media, sales, and office-support occupations together represent 89% of the workforce in Indoor and Outdoor Display Advertising. Advertising-industry AI use cases and buyer surveys are used to assign greater exposure to creative generation, prospecting, media planning, inventory matching, proposals, campaign setup, optimization, measurement, and administration, and lower exposure to relationship negotiation, physical installation, inspection, maintenance, and field operations. The source provides employment rather than wage weights and may overstate exposure where senior sales and management wages dominate.
caveats: No source directly measures wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure for six-digit NAICS 541850.
The occupation profile reports headcount shares rather than compensation shares and does not fully expose installation or maintenance occupations.
Advertising-industry AI evidence includes brands, agencies, publishers, and digital media rather than only display operators.
a.change_evidence: A representative wage-weighted timesheet study of 541850 firms, segmented by sales, creative, campaign operations, administration, installation, maintenance, and management and adjusted for software already in production, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is concentrated in cognitive, creative, commercial, and administrative work that AI can assist or partially substitute. Clear Channel is already piloting AI-enabled sales tools, while industry evidence shows AI use across planning, audience selection, forecasting, creative, and performance analysis. The opportunity is capped by high-value local selling, direct negotiations, brand judgment, and hands-on display operations.

d5.bridge: OAAA reported record U.S. OOH revenue of $9.46 billion in 2025, up 3.6%, with digital OOH up 10.5% and 36.3% of total revenue. Its 2024 facts report cited a 4.5% annual OOH ad-sales forecast for 2025 through 2029. These nominal revenue measures are converted into a lower five-year constant-price, constant-quality service-quantity estimate by discounting for media-rate inflation, digital mix shift, and the older forecast endpoint. This may still overstate real volume if pricing drives reported growth, or understate it if digital screens create materially more sellable campaign slots.
caveats: Industry revenue is not constant-price, constant-quality demand quantity.
The 4.5% forecast was published in a 2024 industry report and ends in 2029 rather than 2031.
Trade-association measures may not capture weaker segments or small-operator dispersion.
d5.change_evidence: A current U.S. five-year OOH forecast separating real impressions, occupancy, number of campaigns, rate inflation, static versus digital inventory, and local versus national spend would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: The medium has recorded sustained recent growth, with digital conversion, programmatic access, better measurement, and a broad local advertiser base expanding use. Demand is cyclical and correlated with GDP, and display operators compete with fast-growing digital channels, so a recession or weak attribution could produce a flat or declining five-year outcome despite the positive base case.

caveats: Public sources do not measure eligibility in the frozen lower-middle-market population.
The industry combines display creation and placement with varying degrees of asset ownership, which affects transferability but not necessarily service eligibility.
e.change_evidence: A stratified audit of the frozen firm universe using Business Register descriptions or commercial profiles, coding external customers, repeat revenue, asset operations, owner dependence, and actual primary activity, would replace this judgment.
e.rationale: NAICS 541850 is defined around creating, designing, or placing public display advertising for customers, so most employer firms in the lower-middle-market are external-service businesses within the lens. Deductions cover misclassified sign fabrication, owner-dependent operations without transferable systems, entities holding isolated sites without an operating service, and businesses without repeat customers or campaigns.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
