caveats: a: BLS occupation data are from May 2023 and omit the self-employed.
a: AAPOR focuses on survey research, which is important but not coextensive with social-science and humanities R&D.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a vintage mismatch and therefore may not represent the same operating year as the occupation evidence.
rho: Gallup covers the full U.S. employee population rather than this industry or firm-size band.
rho: The five-year estimate assumes continued model improvement and workable data-governance controls without assuming that experimental use becomes production use automatically.
e: No source measures eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm population.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged from receipts and may misstate the denominator for nonprofit, grant-funded, or unusually labor-intensive firms.
e: Firm-level NAICS assignment can blur program evaluation, management consulting, market research, and social-science R&D.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and includes employer businesses outside the lower-middle market.
s5: Intentions may not result in transactions, and the survey measure includes transfers that may not satisfy the control-transfer definition.
q: FAR Part 35 governs federal R&D contracting and does not measure the industry's customer or contract mix.
q: Grant-funded work and reimbursable contracts may permit little direct margin capture, while fixed-fee private work may permit substantially more.
q: This range excludes demand loss and implementation friction, which are represented elsewhere.
d5: NCSES business R&D expenditures cover only R&D-performing companies and are not total industry receipts.
d5: The NCSES comparison uses 2021 and 2023 nominal dollars and may reflect survey revision, sampling error, or classification changes.
d5: BLS occupation projections span the whole economy, and GSA's subgroup sits under a neighboring consulting category.
d5: Government funding decisions can change faster than the historical evidence indicates.
o: AAPOR's guidance is survey-specific and reflects current technology rather than proven year-five capability.
o: Operator requirement varies sharply between simple desk research and regulated, longitudinal, sensitive, or field-based studies.
o: This primitive concerns service displacement and self-service, not internal labor efficiency.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include government agencies, foundations, nonprofits, and businesses. Revenue commonly comes from task orders, multiyear studies, cost-reimbursement contracts, time-based work, fixed-fee deliverables, and recurring evaluation or evidence-building programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Academic departments, captive research units, grant-only or self-directed institutes without a repeat outsourced-service model, advocacy or publishing operations, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing external social-science or humanities research, survey research, behavioral and economic research, program and policy evaluation, qualitative research, and related study design, data collection, analysis, and reporting.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS measures employment shares and mean wages by occupation within NAICS 541720, while AAPOR identifies AI-susceptible and human-critical tasks in survey research. Transformation: occupation employment and wage data are used to approximate wage weights, then tasks such as literature synthesis, questionnaire drafting, coding, analysis, and report production are mapped to exposed work. Direction of error: survey-research guidance may overstate exposure for field-heavy humanities work, while occupation titles conceal automatable subtasks and may understate exposure.
caveats: BLS occupation data are from May 2023 and omit the self-employed.
AAPOR focuses on survey research, which is important but not coextensive with social-science and humanities R&D.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a vintage mismatch and therefore may not represent the same operating year as the occupation evidence.
a.change_evidence: A task-level time study from a representative set of NAICS 541720 firms, linked to payroll by role and separating already-automated work, would materially refine the wage-weighted exposed share.
a.rationale: The industry is unusually knowledge-work intensive. BLS reports large shares in social-science, business, computer, research-assistant, and administrative occupations, and AAPOR documents credible AI use in instrument drafting, literature synthesis, coding, theme identification, and exploratory analysis. Research design, primary fieldwork, causal judgment, ethics, and client accountability remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: NCSES measures externally funded domestic business R&D in NAICS 541720, BLS projects employment for constituent social-science occupations, and GSA documents a federal channel for program-evaluation contractors. Transformation: sponsored R&D spending, occupation outlooks, and procurement infrastructure are combined into a constant-price year-five quantity ratio for the current service basket. Direction of error: nominal R&D spending overstates real quantity when prices rise, occupation growth can understate output when productivity improves, and the adjacent GSA evaluation category may overstate demand attributable to this NAICS code.
caveats: NCSES business R&D expenditures cover only R&D-performing companies and are not total industry receipts.
The NCSES comparison uses 2021 and 2023 nominal dollars and may reflect survey revision, sampling error, or classification changes.
BLS occupation projections span the whole economy, and GSA's subgroup sits under a neighboring consulting category.
Government funding decisions can change faster than the historical evidence indicates.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar time series of NAICS 541720 receipts and awarded backlog, segmented by federal, state, foundation, nonprofit, and commercial customer, plus recompete and cancellation rates, would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: NCSES reports only modest nominal growth in externally funded domestic industry R&D between 2021 and 2023, implying weak real growth. BLS outlooks are mixed across relevant occupations, while GSA's evaluation subgroup supports continuing evidence-building demand. Public-budget exposure and project timing justify a wide downside despite durable uses in health, education, policy, and business research.

caveats: No source measures eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm population.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged from receipts and may misstate the denominator for nonprofit, grant-funded, or unusually labor-intensive firms.
Firm-level NAICS assignment can blur program evaluation, management consulting, market research, and social-science R&D.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census from business databases, federal award records, Form 990 filings, and company websites that tags revenue model, customer concentration, repeat work, independence, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is centered on research and analysis, and NCSES shows meaningful externally funded business R&D, supporting a sizable outsourced-service population. Eligibility is reduced by grant-only institutes, self-directed research organizations, captive-like operations, and firms whose work is too episodic or principal-dependent to constitute a repeat transferable service.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: AAPOR assesses whether AI can perform survey-research tasks and identifies human-oversight requirements, while the BLS occupation mix shows the breadth of expert and research-assistant labor in NAICS 541720. Transformation: task-level evidence is translated into the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable research operator. Direction of error: survey research may be more standardized and therefore more self-serviceable than field-heavy social research, but synthetic data and automated analysis may improve faster than current guidance anticipates and make this estimate too high.
caveats: AAPOR's guidance is survey-specific and reflects current technology rather than proven year-five capability.
Operator requirement varies sharply between simple desk research and regulated, longitudinal, sensitive, or field-based studies.
This primitive concerns service displacement and self-service, not internal labor efficiency.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and win-loss data showing which studies were canceled, internalized, replaced by software, or awarded to accountable external operators, paired with validation results for synthetic respondents and autonomous interviewing, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Clients can increasingly self-serve literature synthesis, preliminary analysis, simple instruments, and report drafting. The operator remains important for study design, sampling, field execution, sensitive interviewing, causal interpretation, privacy, disclosure, validation, and defensible conclusions. AAPOR specifically warns that synthetic respondents are not a reliable substitute for human data and that autonomous interviewing creates material oversight risks.

q.bridge: Source population and quantity: FAR Part 35 describes federal R&D contract form, stating that cost-reimbursement is usually appropriate when specifications and costs are uncertain, while fixed-price work can be used for defined minor projects. Transformation: this contract-form guidance is translated into a discounted retention share for a mixed customer book, with low retention on reimbursed work and higher retention on fixed-fee deliverables and growth capacity. Direction of error: the federal rule may understate retention where commercial and foundation customers buy fixed deliverables, but may overstate it if competitive renewals rapidly reprice labor savings across the broader market.
caveats: FAR Part 35 governs federal R&D contracting and does not measure the industry's customer or contract mix.
Grant-funded work and reimbursable contracts may permit little direct margin capture, while fixed-fee private work may permit substantially more.
This range excludes demand loss and implementation friction, which are represented elsewhere.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level diligence on billing form, fee structure, indirect-cost treatment, renewal cadence, price competition, change orders, and realized margin after automation at a representative set of firms would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Cost-reimbursement and time-based arrangements pass labor savings to customers directly or through renewal pricing. Fixed-fee studies can retain savings during a contract term, and faster delivery can create capacity, but competitive procurement and transparent labor categories limit durable capture.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup measures current AI use and workflow effects across employed U.S. adults, and AAPOR describes implementation controls for survey research. Transformation: current knowledge-worker adoption and research-specific governance constraints are translated into the share of exposed opportunity that a lower-middle-market research operator could implement within five years. Direction of error: economy-wide adoption may understate uptake in digitally capable research firms, but self-reported productivity and tool use may overstate production-grade implementation.
caveats: Gallup covers the full U.S. employee population rather than this industry or firm-size band.
The five-year estimate assumes continued model improvement and workable data-governance controls without assuming that experimental use becomes production use automatically.
rho.change_evidence: A structured survey of operating leaders at NAICS 541720 firms covering production deployment, review time, error rates, client restrictions, and realized labor hours by workflow would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Adoption is already broad enough to support substantial implementation, especially in drafting, synthesis, coding, and analysis. Gallup nevertheless finds that workplace transformation remains limited, while AAPOR requires human review, validation, disclosure, privacy controls, and auditability. These constraints keep the base well below full implementation.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years. Transformation: that intention rate is discounted for plans that do not complete, family gifts and internal transfers that are not qualifying control events, and research firms lacking transferable operations. Direction of error: the broad small-business sample may overstate qualifying events by including nonmarket transfers, but may understate them because lower-middle-market firms are generally more sale-ready than very small employers.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes employer businesses outside the lower-middle market.
Intentions may not result in transactions, and the survey measure includes transfers that may not satisfy the control-transfer definition.
s5.change_evidence: Completed control-deal counts and denominators for social-research, survey-research, and program-evaluation firms from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, and specialist broker interviews would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A meaningful pool of employer owners expects an ownership event, but stated plans are not completed deals and the qualifying definition is narrower. Founder reputation, principal-investigator relationships, and dependence on key technical staff further reduce the share of eligible research firms that can complete a transferable control transaction.

records.principal_driver: A wage-heavy mix of research, analytical, writing, and administrative tasks offers substantial implementable workflow automation.
records.principal_weakness: Cost-reimbursement pricing and public-budget exposure can prevent productivity gains from becoming durable operator cash flow.

caveats: S1's exact Census URL is reachable but does not display the cited 541720 definition in the returned page. Because e is already an ESTIMATE and other evidence supports only its direction, removing S1 would not change the PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The a and o bases extrapolate survey-research task evidence to the broader social-science and humanities R&D code, and the packet does not show a reproducible occupation-to-task wage-weight calculation; their PROXY labels and wide ranges are therefore essential.
The rho base is translated from economy-wide, self-reported employee adoption and productivity evidence rather than production deployment at lower-middle-market research firms.

No cited source measures the share of the frozen firm population that satisfies the repeat outsourced-service and transferable-operator lens, so e=0.68 remains an explicitly model-conditioned estimate.
The s5 evidence measures five-year owner intentions across employer businesses, not completed qualifying control transactions in this industry or size band.
FAR Part 35 supports federal R&D contract-form logic but supplies no empirical industry contract mix or retained-margin rate, leaving q=0.32 highly dependent on the stated proxy transformation.

The d5 base combines a narrow nominal BERD series, whole-economy occupation projections, and an adjacent-code GSA channel rather than a constant-dollar series of total 541720 service demand or backlog.

sources_audited.note: The exact Census URL loads the general NAICS search page, but the returned page contains neither code 541720 nor its definition; reachability alone therefore does not substantiate the claimed definition. This does not pass the materiality test because e is already an ESTIMATE supported only directionally, and removing this citation does not change the base tier or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

The May 2023 industry-specific OEWS page covers NAICS 541720 employers and reports employment shares and wages, including business and financial operations, computer and mathematical, social-science research assistants, and office and administrative support occupations.

The 2026 AAPOR report documents AI use across survey workflow tasks including instrument work, transcription, coding, thematic analysis, modeling, reporting, and interviewing, while also requiring validation, human oversight, disclosure, privacy controls, and careful comparison of synthetic with human responses.
The BLS 2024-2034 projection table shows mixed changes among relevant occupations, including -5.2% for survey researchers, +1.2% for economists, and +4.4% for social-science research assistants.

The 2023 BERD tables report $549 million of domestic R&D paid for by others and performed by NAICS 541720 companies, with source-of-funds and cost detail; the source is explicitly limited to business R&D performers rather than total industry receipts.
The 2021 BERD table reports $525 million of domestic R&D paid for by others and performed by NAICS 541720 companies, supporting the stated same-industry nominal baseline.

FAR Part 35 states that cost-reimbursement contracts are usually appropriate when R&D specifications and costs are uncertain, while short-duration or minor well-defined projects may use fixed prices; it also distinguishes contracts from grants. The packet correctly labels the extension to industry retention as a proxy and discloses that FAR does not measure customer or contract mix.

GSA documents a program-evaluation subgroup of qualified, pre-vetted contractors through which agencies can issue evaluation RFPs and awards. It is under adjacent SIN 541611, a limitation the packet explicitly discloses.

Gallup's February 2026 survey of 23,717 U.S. employees reports 50% using AI at least a few times yearly, 28% at least a few times weekly, productivity gains among adopters, and limited evidence of fundamental workflow change. The bridge accurately identifies the broad employee population.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 9% of nonemployers, and describes the 2024 survey population. The packet appropriately discounts intentions and nonqualifying transfers.

caveats: a: The BLS matrix covers the full heterogeneous industry rather than the narrowed payer model.
a: O*NET tasks are occupation-wide and do not measure current AI substitution.
a: The wage adjustment is judgmental because the matrix reports employment rather than payroll by occupation.
rho: Economy-wide adoption does not establish care-provider implementation or labor avoidance.
rho: State privacy, licensing, documentation, and incident-reporting requirements vary.
rho: Savings from documentation assistance may be redeployed to resident care rather than reduce hiring.
e: No source measures eligibility under the narrowed ownership and earnings lens.
e: Legal entities may operate facilities across several neighboring residential-care codes.
e: The injected firm-count estimate uses a broad healthcare-facility margin bridge that may not represent publicly reimbursed group homes.
s5: Gallup measures intentions across industries rather than completed care-provider deals.
s5: Nonprofit affiliations and asset transfers may not qualify as conventional control transfers.
s5: Public transaction databases undercount small private and nonprofit combinations.
q: The CMS direct-care spending rule applies to specified Medicaid services and has flexibilities, not every activity or payer in the narrowed lens.
q: State reimbursement and contract structures vary materially.
q: No source directly measures retention of AI-enabled benefits.
d5: Employment is a proxy for real service demand and can move with staffing rules or labor availability.
d5: The BLS line covers excluded orphanage, camp, and atypical residential models.
d5: National Medicaid HCBS trends do not identify this six-digit industry or distinguish qualifying group-home settings.
o: No source directly measures the operator-required share of future service quantity.
o: Remote monitoring can change staffing models without eliminating accountable-provider demand.
o: Policy-driven reclassification or movement to home-based services can displace lens operators while resident need persists.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring occupancy- or service-linked payments, commonly per-diem reimbursement, public-agency placement contracts, Medicaid or managed-care reimbursement, and block or capacity contracts, with residents receiving continuous supervision and personal care.
lens.deviation_from_default: Narrowed for coherence to staffed group-home operators supported mainly by public or managed-care reimbursement. The full code also contains donor-funded orphanages, disciplinary camps, and atypical private-pay homes whose payer, ownership, transferability, and demand economics are too different for one screen; the narrowing is based on business-model comparability, not attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Donation-dependent orphanages, boot or disciplinary camps, private-pay atypical residences, temporary shelters, nursing facilities, assisted living, residential mental health or substance-abuse facilities, intellectual and developmental disability facilities classified elsewhere, correctional institutions, captive government operations, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market operators of staffed residential group homes that provide recurring supervision, personal care, habilitation, case support, and daily living assistance to children, disabled people without nursing care, or justice-involved residents under government, Medicaid, managed-care, or agency placement arrangements.

a.bridge: BLS measures the employment mix of Other Residential Care Facilities but not wage-weighted AI exposure, while O*NET describes occupation tasks across settings. I map high exposure to records, reports, case-plan drafting, scheduling, billing, compliance preparation, and routine management; moderate exposure to information synthesis in social-service roles; and low exposure to direct personal care, observation, crisis response, counseling, transport, and safety supervision. Employment shares are adjusted conceptually for the higher pay of managers and professional social-service staff. The all-industry task descriptions may overstate standardizable documentation in small group homes, while employment rather than payroll weighting biases the unadjusted mix downward where exposed professional work is better paid.
caveats: The BLS matrix covers the full heterogeneous industry rather than the narrowed payer model.
O*NET tasks are occupation-wide and do not measure current AI substitution.
The wage adjustment is judgmental because the matrix reports employment rather than payroll by occupation.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll-by-occupation extract plus time-and-motion studies separating direct presence, documentation, scheduling, case-plan preparation, billing, and compliance hours at LMM group-home operators would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: BLS shows that healthcare support, community and social service, and personal-care roles dominate employment. O*NET confirms that most aide and residential-advisor work requires in-person care, observation, relationship-building, safety rounds, first aid, and judgment, while meaningful documentation, case-plan, report, coordination, and administrative tasks remain exposed. The opportunity is therefore material but well below a majority of wage-weighted work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for the full Other Residential Care Facilities industry rather than constant-price demand for the narrowed group-home lens. I translate the modest industry employment increase into roughly flat five-year real service demand, then widen the range for state placement policy, Medicaid funding, disability and youth-service need, reimbursement adequacy, and movement toward less institutional settings. Employment may understate demand if labor shortages constrain staffed capacity and overstate it if staffing intensity rises because of regulation or acuity.
caveats: Employment is a proxy for real service demand and can move with staffing rules or labor availability.
The BLS line covers excluded orphanage, camp, and atypical residential models.
National Medicaid HCBS trends do not identify this six-digit industry or distinguish qualifying group-home settings.
d5.change_evidence: State-by-state five-year forecasts of licensed beds, funded placements, waitlists, occupancy, per-diem rates, closures, and transitions to family-based or self-directed care for the included populations would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS projects only modest growth for the industry, while Medicaid data show that policy increasingly favors home- and community-based services over isolated institutional settings. Small community group homes can qualify as community-based, but youth congregate-care reduction and individualized home supports can still reduce placements. Demand should be durable for high-acuity residents yet highly dependent on state policy and funded capacity.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the narrowed ownership and earnings lens.
Legal entities may operate facilities across several neighboring residential-care codes.
The injected firm-count estimate uses a broad healthcare-facility margin bridge that may not represent publicly reimbursed group homes.
e.change_evidence: A stratified commercial-database review linking legal form, ownership, site count, payer mix, licenses, revenue recurrence, normalized EBITDA, and primary NAICS for sampled firms would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: A substantial part of the code consists of recurring residential supervision and personal-care services, but the eligible share is reduced by mission-driven or nonprofit organizations without conventional transferable equity, small single-site homes, government-controlled programs, donor-supported models, disciplinary camps, entities outside the target earnings band, and operators whose primary activity belongs in another residential-care code. The narrowed population retains multi-home or scaled group-home operators with external public or managed-care reimbursement and standalone control rights.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 623990 ($7,804,668,938) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 623990 ($10,206,165,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $82,656 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 623990); class <5: 1,165 firms, avg revenue/firm $223,537 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $35,319 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 589 firms, avg revenue/firm $510,915 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $80,725 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 547 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,045,366 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $165,168 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 761 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,970,929 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $469,407 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 364 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,747,181 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,540,055 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 147 firms, avg revenue/firm $22,205,095 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,508,405 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 511

caveats: No source directly measures the operator-required share of future service quantity.
Remote monitoring can change staffing models without eliminating accountable-provider demand.
Policy-driven reclassification or movement to home-based services can displace lens operators while resident need persists.
o.change_evidence: Placement-flow data tracking transitions from licensed group homes to family care, self-directed services, independent housing with remote support, government provision, and technology-only alternatives would materially change this estimate.
o.rationale: Residential supervision, personal care, safeguarding, crisis response, medication support, transport, and accountable case coordination require a licensed human service operator. Software can assist records, scheduling, communication, and monitoring but cannot independently house, supervise, or protect vulnerable residents. Operator displacement is mainly from family placements, self-directed supports, government insourcing, or migration to providers classified in neighboring care industries rather than from software-only supply.

caveats: The CMS direct-care spending rule applies to specified Medicaid services and has flexibilities, not every activity or payer in the narrowed lens.
State reimbursement and contract structures vary materially.
No source directly measures retention of AI-enabled benefits.
q.change_evidence: Contract and rate-file cohorts showing reimbursement methodology, cost-report treatment, direct-care spending tests, renewal resets, quality penalties, and realized margin after documented workflow savings would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Per-diem and public-program reimbursement can let an operator retain administrative savings until the next rate review, but capture is limited by state rate setting, cost reporting, contract renewal, quality obligations, and chronic pressure to improve direct-care compensation and staffing. CMS is increasing transparency and requires a large minimum share of certain Medicaid personal-care payments to support direct-care compensation beginning within the horizon, reducing the scope to convert care-labor savings into durable profit. Back-office savings outside covered service payments may be retained more fully.

rho.bridge: The Census survey measures any AI use across nonfarm businesses, not implementation of exposed labor opportunity in residential care. I start from recent economy-wide adoption near one-fifth, allow for five-year diffusion of documentation and back-office tools, and discount for fragmented provider systems, sensitive resident data, state-specific reporting, safeguarding, human review, and limited technical capacity. General business adoption likely overstates current readiness among small care providers but may understate future uptake through vertical care-management software.
caveats: Economy-wide adoption does not establish care-provider implementation or labor avoidance.
State privacy, licensing, documentation, and incident-reporting requirements vary.
Savings from documentation assistance may be redeployed to resident care rather than reduce hiring.
rho.change_evidence: A provider-level survey of deployed AI features, sustained user adoption, error and incident rates, saved labor hours, payer acceptance, and implementation cost across state-funded residential programs would materially change the value.
rho.rationale: Operators can implement assisted case notes, shift summaries, incident-report drafting, schedule support, authorization checks, billing review, policy retrieval, recruiting administration, and management reporting. Implementation should remain partial because records affect vulnerable residents, benefits, courts, placements, and safety decisions; reliable human review and workflow integration are mandatory.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures intended sale or ownership transfer among employer-business owners across industries, not completed control transfers of licensed residential-care providers. I discount the broad intention rate for plans that do not close, nonprofit or mission-controlled ownership, licensure and payer approvals, and buyer sensitivity to compliance history. The broad employer survey likely overstates transferable equity availability in this field, though owner succession among qualifying for-profit operators may offset part of the discount.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions across industries rather than completed care-provider deals.
Nonprofit affiliations and asset transfers may not qualify as conventional control transfers.
Public transaction databases undercount small private and nonprofit combinations.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year database of control transactions involving NAICS 623990 group-home operators, matched to the eligible firm stock and supplemented by owner-age, legal-form, licensure-transfer, and failed-deal data, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Qualifying transfers can occur when founder-led multi-home operators face succession, but conventional deal flow is constrained by nonprofit prevalence, state approvals, Medicaid enrollment, property and license structures, resident continuity, and serious diligence on incidents and compliance. Completed control deals should be materially below broad employer-owner intentions.

records.principal_driver: Recurring publicly funded occupancy, persistent resident need, and heavy documentation and coordination burden create a focused opportunity for workflow assistance across multi-home operators.
records.principal_weakness: Direct human care and safeguarding dominate the service, while administered reimbursement, nonprofit ownership, and regulatory transfer constraints limit both benefit capture and the conventional acquisition pool.

caveats: The cited Census NAICS page was reachable but did not render the specific 623990 definition or examples, limiting direct verification of the detailed lens-boundary claims.

The exact BLS matrix URL was not reachable during review, leaving the occupation mix and employment projection unverified; because a and d5 are already labeled PROXY and the action is already VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS, this does not by itself change the base tier or action.
The numeric bases for a and rho are judgmental transformations rather than direct source measurements, but their PROXY labels, bridges, caveats, and ranges disclose that limitation.

The 86.2% HCBS statistic is a level across all Medicaid LTSS users, not evidence of growth for this six-digit industry or narrowed lens; the packet acknowledges that limitation in the d5 bridge and caveats.

Eligibility, completed transfer probability, benefit retention, and operator-required demand remain sector-specific estimates not directly measured by the cited sources; the ESTIMATE/PROXY states and MODEL_CONDITIONED action treat them honestly.

sources_audited.note: The official Census NAICS page reopened, but its rendered content did not expose the queried 623990 definition or examples, so the detailed industry-definition claims could not be fully verified from the cited page.
The exact BLS National Employment Matrix URL could not be reopened, so the stated occupation mix and 165.8-to-170.0-thousand employment projection were not independently verified.
O*NET supports the cited mix of direct personal care, health monitoring, household help, transport, case review, and recordkeeping tasks for Personal Care Aides.
O*NET supports the cited mix of observation, safety rounds, first aid, mediation, rule enforcement, resident-plan, counseling, reporting, and administrative tasks for Residential Advisors.
O*NET supports the cited case-record, report, interview, service-plan, legal, counseling, referral, coordination, and placement-related tasks for Child, Family, and School Social Workers.
The Census article reports a nationally representative biweekly survey, overall AI use of 17%-20%, higher use at larger firms, and measurement across 15 business functions.

Medicaid.gov supports the 86.2% 2021 HCBS-use statistic and the policy distinction between community and institutional settings, but it does not establish demand growth for NAICS 623990 or the narrowed group-home lens.

The CMS slides support the generally applicable 80% direct-care compensation minimum beginning July 9, 2030 for specified Medicaid homemaker, home-health-aide, and personal-care services, with flexibilities and exceptions.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale, transfer, or public offering within five years; the packet appropriately treats this as a broad intention proxy rather than completed sector transactions.
