caveats: a: No public source provides a wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure measure for NAICS 541191.
a: Qualia is a software vendor and its respondent and case-study populations may overrepresent technology-forward firms.

a: The injected labor ratio is MED quality and combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts before IPS harmonization; that vintage mismatch affects the labor pool to which a is applied, not this task-share estimate.
rho: Reported AI use can include personal tools or light assistance and is not equivalent to implemented labor substitution.
rho: A single high-performing agency case does not establish typical rollout speed or durable savings.
rho: The injected labor ratio's 2024-wage and 2022-receipt vintage mismatch is separate from implementation feasibility.
e: Public industry counts mix independent agencies, affiliated offices, and some vertically integrated operations.
e: The injected n of 102 is itself an ESTIMATE bridged from SUSB size classes using a broader-sector EBITDA margin; e is applied to that uncertain count and does not re-estimate it.
e: State licensing and underwriter appointment transferability can make nominally operating firms ineligible.
s5: Gallup's population is all US employer businesses and does not isolate title agencies or the EBITDA band.
s5: Plans are not completed transfers, and some transfers are gifts or internal successions rather than arm's-length control sales.
s5: ALTA's statement that activity is accelerating is directional and supplies no transaction denominator.
q: Fee components and title-insurance rate regulation vary substantially by state.
q: Consumers often pay while lenders or real-estate professionals influence provider selection, making pass-through behavior indirect.
q: No public study traces an implemented AI cost saving into title-agency prices over five years.
d5: Title-insurance premium growth is nominal and affected by property values, mix, and pricing, so it is not a direct constant-price quantity measure.
d5: Occupational employment projections incorporate productivity and cover workers outside NAICS 541191, so they are not demand forecasts.
d5: The estimate does not assume that a cyclical rebound repeats at the recent annual rate.
o: The FinCEN deed sample concerned a specific non-financed transfer population and was not nationally representative.
o: An accountable attorney, insurer, creditor, or escrow provider does not necessarily belong to NAICS 541191.
o: The FHFA pilot is limited to selected low-risk refinances and does not establish broad elimination of settlement operations.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is predominantly transaction-based: fixed or scheduled fees for search, examination, escrow, document preparation, recording, and closing, plus commissions retained when acting as an agent for a title insurer. Demand is repeatedly referred by lenders, brokers, builders, and attorneys even though the end borrower or property party often pays.
lens.excluded_activities: Offices of lawyers, direct title insurance carriers acting only as underwriters, captive internal service units, shell or ghost agencies, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent and affiliated US title abstract, title search, title production, escrow, and real-estate settlement offices serving external lenders, real-estate professionals, buyers, and sellers, restricted to transferable firms in the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: O*NET measures tasks and work context for title examiners rather than wage-weighted task time across NAICS 541191, while Qualia surveys title and escrow professionals about AI use rather than substitution potential. I map document intake, search, extraction, comparison, data entry, drafting, and routine coordination to exposed work; retain exception curing, legal interpretation, funds control, stakeholder resolution, and final accountability as less exposed; and qualitatively weight the mix across title production, escrow, administrative, sales, and management work. Vendor sponsorship and early-adopter respondents may bias exposure upward, while O*NET's focus on examiners may omit exposed clerical work and bias it downward.
caveats: No public source provides a wage-weighted, not-yet-automated task exposure measure for NAICS 541191.
Qualia is a software vendor and its respondent and case-study populations may overrepresent technology-forward firms.

The injected labor ratio is MED quality and combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts before IPS harmonization; that vintage mismatch affects the labor pool to which a is applied, not this task-share estimate.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from title-production systems such as Qualia, SoftPro, Resware, or RamQuest that allocates payroll by occupation, task, current automation status, and file complexity across a representative sample of independent agencies would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The industry's core work contains unusually dense document and data workflows. O*NET lists searching records, examining and summarizing legal documents, preparing reports and policies, entering records, and checking closing files; Qualia reports current AI use in order opening, preliminary exams, verification, drafting, and workflow automation. The estimate excludes opportunity already automated at the measurement date and leaves a substantial human share for curative judgment, communication, security controls, and sign-off.

caveats: Title-insurance premium growth is nominal and affected by property values, mix, and pricing, so it is not a direct constant-price quantity measure.
Occupational employment projections incorporate productivity and cover workers outside NAICS 541191, so they are not demand forecasts.
The estimate does not assume that a cyclical rebound repeats at the recent annual rate.
d5.change_evidence: A transaction-weighted forecast through 2031 combining MBA or GSE purchase and refinance originations, Census new-home sales, NAR existing-home transactions, MSCI Real Assets commercial transfers, and agency files by service type would materially narrow this range.
d5.rationale: Current-basket demand is primarily driven by property purchases, mortgage originations and refinancings, plus commercial and non-sale transfer work. The 2025 premium rebound shows the category can recover sharply from weak transaction conditions, while BLS projects only slow growth for the core title-examiner occupation over the longer horizon. The base assumes modest real transaction growth from the current cycle rather than extrapolating nominal premiums; the wide bounds reflect mortgage-rate, affordability, inventory, refinancing, and commercial-real-estate sensitivity.

caveats: Public industry counts mix independent agencies, affiliated offices, and some vertically integrated operations.
The injected n of 102 is itself an ESTIMATE bridged from SUSB size classes using a broader-sector EBITDA margin; e is applied to that uncertain count and does not re-estimate it.
State licensing and underwriter appointment transferability can make nominally operating firms ineligible.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level list for the 102 estimated band firms, enriched with ultimate ownership, underwriter appointments, customer concentration, license status, EBITDA quality, owner dependence, and external revenue share, would directly identify eligibility.
e.rationale: Most firms that clear the EBITDA-band screen should provide external, repeat transaction services and possess transferable referral, underwriter, employee, and production-system relationships. The industry is fragmented, and its service mix is operationally coherent. The discount removes insurer-owned or lender-affiliated captive operations, owner-dependent practices, shells, and firms whose revenue or licenses would not survive a control change.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541191 ($4,182,567,257) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541191 ($12,511,998,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $168,353 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541191); class <5: 4,072 firms, avg revenue/firm $292,418 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $45,763 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,314 firms, avg revenue/firm $898,276 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $140,580 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 724 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,931,006 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $302,202 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 527 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,806,233 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $908,676 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 102 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,974,725 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,343,545 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $64,930,969 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,161,697 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 102

o.bridge: FinCEN's deed analysis measures whether a settlement agent, title insurer, or attorney appeared in a limited sample of potentially reportable non-financed transfers, not whether year-five service quantity requires a NAICS 541191 operator. I start from the near-universal presence of an accountable professional, subtract work performed by attorneys, insurers, creditors, and escrow firms outside the lens, and allow for automated title review and lender acceptance pathways. The narrow county sample and its search design may bias professional involvement upward; omission of financed-purchase compliance and funds handling may bias it downward.
caveats: The FinCEN deed sample concerned a specific non-financed transfer population and was not nationally representative.
An accountable attorney, insurer, creditor, or escrow provider does not necessarily belong to NAICS 541191.
The FHFA pilot is limited to selected low-risk refinances and does not establish broad elimination of settlement operations.
o.change_evidence: GSE pilot results and loan-level closing data showing the share of purchases and refinances completed without a title or settlement firm, combined with state-by-state attorney-closing rules and automated-title acceptance rates, would directly move o.
o.rationale: Most mortgage closings still require title verification, standardized closing disclosures, funds movement, lien resolution, recording, and a named accountable contact. CFPB rules formalize settlement-agent responsibilities, while FHFA's limited low-risk refinance pilot demonstrates that some lender-title work can be waived after automated review. Software should reduce labor and bypass a minority of files, but fraud exposure, state variation, curative work, licensing, and liability keep an accountable operator necessary for most future quantity.

caveats: Fee components and title-insurance rate regulation vary substantially by state.
Consumers often pay while lenders or real-estate professionals influence provider selection, making pass-through behavior indirect.
No public study traces an implemented AI cost saving into title-agency prices over five years.
q.change_evidence: Agency-level cohorts showing realized price per file, insurer commission split, labor cost per file, referral-source concentration, renewal or master-service terms, and win rates before and after automation would identify the retained discounted benefit directly.
q.rationale: Per-file fees and title-insurance commissions are not cost-plus reimbursements, so an operator can retain much of an early productivity gain without an automatic contractual pass-through. Retention erodes over time because many title and settlement services are shoppable, customers can request quotes, referral partners compare responsiveness and total closing cost, and each new transaction can be repriced. Regulated or scheduled insurance pricing and service-based competition slow pass-through, while lender and underwriter bargaining plus software-enabled entrants accelerate it.

rho.bridge: Qualia's survey of 631 title and escrow professionals measures tool use, attitudes, and selected workflows, and its AEGIS example measures one early adopter's throughput rather than the share of exposed opportunity implemented industrywide within five years. I translate widespread current use, purpose-built workflow availability, and demonstrated file-level productivity into an implementation fraction, then discount for small-firm integration capacity, data heterogeneity, accuracy, fraud, privacy, state compliance, and change management. Vendor and early-adopter evidence likely biases the estimate upward; rapid product diffusion could make the discount too conservative.
caveats: Reported AI use can include personal tools or light assistance and is not equivalent to implemented labor substitution.
A single high-performing agency case does not establish typical rollout speed or durable savings.
The injected labor ratio's 2024-wage and 2022-receipt vintage mismatch is separate from implementation feasibility.
rho.change_evidence: Matched production-system telemetry from a multi-vendor panel showing labor hours per completed file before and after AI deployment, segmented by state, transaction type, title complexity, agency size, and months since deployment, would materially move rho.
rho.rationale: Implementation is already practical in order intake, document extraction, preliminary examination, communication, and data verification. It should spread further through existing production platforms, but O*NET reports exactness as extremely important for all respondents and identifies compliance evaluation, decision-making, and external communication as core work. Human review, audit trails, cybersecurity, county-record variation, and underwriter acceptance therefore prevent full realization of exposed capacity.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or ownership-transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers among LMM title agencies. I use the employer-business intention rate as an anchor, increase confidence because ALTA reports accelerating title-agency M&A, and discount intentions for failed processes, family gifts without a purchasable control event, internal reorganizations, and firms that close. The all-industry population likely understates title-industry deal interest, while intention-to-completion slippage biases it upward.
caveats: Gallup's population is all US employer businesses and does not isolate title agencies or the EBITDA band.
Plans are not completed transfers, and some transfers are gifts or internal successions rather than arm's-length control sales.
ALTA's statement that activity is accelerating is directional and supplies no transaction denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of closed title-agency control transactions from state insurance change-of-control filings, underwriter appointment records, PitchBook or S&P Capital IQ, and specialist title-industry brokers, matched to the estimated EBITDA-band denominator, would replace the proxy.
s5.rationale: Gallup found that employer-business owners are materially more likely than nonemployers to plan a transfer, and older employer-owners report still higher intentions. ALTA separately describes accelerating agency M&A and active interest from strategic buyers, underwriters, and private equity. A completed qualifying transfer remains less likely than an expressed plan, so the base stays below the strongest intention subgroup.

records.principal_driver: Document-intensive title production and closing coordination create a sizable implementable labor opportunity while regulation and liability preserve demand for accountable operators.
records.principal_weakness: Cyclical transaction volume and the absence of direct industry measurements for remaining task exposure, completed control transfers, and benefit retention make the range unusually sensitive to operating diligence.

sources.supports: Defines the core occupation and lists document examination, title search, reporting, data entry, policy preparation, compliance evaluation, and communication tasks. It reports that exactness is extremely important for all respondents, that 48% describe the occupation as highly automated, and that projected employment growth is 1% to 2% for 2024 through 2034.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Title Examiners, Abstractors, and Searchers

sources.supports: Reports a nationwide survey of 631 title and escrow professionals, nearly 90% using at least one AI tool, one third using tools daily, another 30% weekly, and 63% concerned about AI risks. It documents live use cases in order opening, preliminary exams, verification, drafting, and workflow automation, plus an early-adopter example that doubled examiner commitments per day.
sources.title: How AI Is Transforming Title & Escrow: Seven Key Takeaways From Qualia's 2025 Report

sources.supports: States that lender's title insurance is usually required for a mortgage, identifies title search, lender policy premium, issuance services, and in most states the closing fee as title-service fees, and explains that some listed title services can be shopped separately.
sources.title: Consumer Financial Protection Bureau: What are title service fees?

sources.supports: Reports a weighted US owner survey conducted in 2024. It finds 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older, 22% of employer owners plan a sale or ownership transfer within five years, and the corresponding figure is 30% for employer owners age 55 or older.
sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: States that title-industry mergers and acquisitions activity continues to accelerate as owners consider succession, technology, regulation, and fraud, and that strategic buyers, underwriters, and private equity investors are seeking agencies.
sources.title: ALTA: Sell or Scale? A Strategic Workshop for Title Agency Owners

sources.supports: Describes about 6,800 US companies, an average single location with 11 workers and $1.8 million annual revenue, top-50 firms at 39% of industry sales, and revenue from title search, escrow, filing, recording, documentation, delivery, and title-insurer agency commissions.
sources.title: Vertical IQ: Title Abstract and Settlement Offices

sources.supports: Reports $18.5 billion of title-insurance premiums in 2025, up 13.8% from 2024, and describes an industry of more than 17,000 title-insurance companies with over 90% classified as small businesses.
sources.title: ALTA Reports 2025 Market Share and Title Insurance Premium Volume

sources.supports: Explains that lenders normally provide independent clear-title verification through title insurance or a legal opinion and that a controlled pilot waives that requirement only for certain low-risk refinances while preserving lender warranties and oversight.
sources.title: FHFA: Director Sandra Thompson's Statement on Title Acceptance Pilot

sources.supports: Specifies closing-cost and transaction disclosures and settlement-agent contact and licensing information, illustrating the named, accountable role and regulated information flow in mortgage closings.
sources.title: CFPB Regulation Z Section 1026.38: Closing Disclosure

sources.supports: Its regulatory analysis identifies NAICS 541191 in the closing-professional population, reports 6,023 firms below 20 employees and 6,571 below 500 employees in 2021, notes that title and settlement agents often perform both functions, and finds only three of one hundred sampled potentially reportable transfers lacked a settlement agent, title insurer, or attorney.
sources.title: FinCEN Proposed Rule: Anti-Money Laundering Regulations for Residential Real Estate Transfers

records.title: Title Abstract and Settlement Offices

caveats: a: BLS occupation pages describe workers across industries, not the wage mix inside NAICS 541370.
a: RICS evidence is qualitative, UK-based, and spans geomatics activities beyond the frozen US firm population.
a: The boundary between mature automation and new AI is especially uncertain for photogrammetry, point clouds, and feature extraction.
rho: UAS and GPS are adjacent mature technologies, not direct measures of generative or predictive AI implementation.
rho: State transportation agencies have procurement scale and technical resources unlike many privately held firms.
rho: POB respondent selection and firm-size composition are not reported well enough to establish representativeness.
e: No source measures the eligible share of the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
e: Project-based revenue can be repeat without being contractually recurring.
e: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged from older Census size data, so the firms represented may not align perfectly with current normalized EBITDA.
s5: The best available transaction evidence covers broader AEC firms and is not a probability sample of NAICS 541370.
s5: Surveyed AEC firms skew larger than the full small-firm population, although the frozen EBITDA band overlaps part of the sample.
s5: Retirement triggers and buyer intentions may not result in completed qualifying control transfers.
q: The billing source is guidance from a Massachusetts trade association, not a national revenue-share survey.
q: Federal highway procurement rules apply only to covered public contracts.
q: Retention may be lower where firms sell staff hours and higher where scarce licensure, capacity, or trusted local records drive selection.
d5: Occupational employment is not service quantity and includes workers outside NAICS 541370.
d5: USGS program benefits are estimates and next-generation collection depends on funding.
d5: The supplied labor ratio combines more recent payroll with older receipts and therefore may not reflect the same demand vintage.
o: Licensure applies most directly to land surveying and legal certification, not every cartographic or GIS deliverable.
o: Public LiDAR and imagery can complement firms but can also let customers self-serve some mapping work.
o: Future software liability regimes and customer acceptance are uncertain.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Professional project and task-order services for public agencies, engineering and construction firms, utilities, real-estate owners, and resource clients. Engagements are commonly billed hourly or per diem, as cost plus a fixed fee, as a lump sum for defined scope, or by a fee related to construction cost; repeat business comes from continuing capital programs, land transactions, compliance, asset records, and map updates.
lens.excluded_activities: Geophysical surveying, map and atlas publishing, captive government or corporate survey units, software-only products, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without repeat outsourced-service relationships.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market providers of cadastral, boundary, topographic, geodetic, construction, hydrographic, aerial, photogrammetric, LiDAR, GIS, and cartographic surveying or mapping services supplied to external customers on repeat project, task-order, or continuing-service relationships.

a.bridge: The sources describe tasks for US surveyors, surveying and mapping technicians, and cartographers, plus UK geomatics examples of AI use, rather than measuring a wage-weighted task share in NAICS 541370. I mapped data entry, image and point-cloud processing, feature extraction, CAD and GIS drafting, document research, report generation, scheduling, and first-pass quality checks into exposure, then excluded already-automated GPS, robotic-instrument, and drone gains and discounted physical fieldwork, monument recovery, client interaction, and licensed judgment. Occupational pages omit some administrative labor, which tends to understate exposure, while vendor-like professional examples of AI capability tend to overstate immediately substitutable work.
caveats: BLS occupation pages describe workers across industries, not the wage mix inside NAICS 541370.
RICS evidence is qualitative, UK-based, and spans geomatics activities beyond the frozen US firm population.
The boundary between mature automation and new AI is especially uncertain for photogrammetry, point clouds, and feature extraction.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from a representative sample of NAICS 541370 firms, joined to payroll by role and identifying which workflows already use automated capture or processing, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The core occupations combine substantial digital processing with irreducible field and professional work. Technicians process imagery, enter data, calculate measurements, combine GIS and field notes, and use CAD; cartographers integrate survey, LiDAR, GIS, and imagery data; surveyors research legal records, verify data, prepare maps and reports, and certify boundaries. RICS identifies feature extraction, digitization, report generation, document conversion, spatial-data analysis, and quality checks as suitable AI tasks, but also emphasizes human setup, interpretation, relationships, and judgment. The range therefore represents a moderate current, not-yet-automated wage-weighted opportunity rather than automation of the whole job. [S2, S3, S4, S5]

d5.bridge: BLS measures projected employment growth for US surveyors, surveying and mapping technicians, and cartographers, while USGS documents coverage and planned recollection of national elevation data. I translate those occupation-level employment paths and repeat mapping requirements into a five-year constant-price quantity ratio for the current service basket, allowing for technology-driven output per worker and construction cyclicality. Employment growth can understate service demand when productivity rises, while planned public mapping benefits and collections can overstate demand if appropriations or project schedules slip.
caveats: Occupational employment is not service quantity and includes workers outside NAICS 541370.
USGS program benefits are estimates and next-generation collection depends on funding.
The supplied labor ratio combines more recent payroll with older receipts and therefore may not reflect the same demand vintage.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census service revenue for NAICS 541370, combined with five-year public infrastructure and geospatial contract backlogs and private construction forecasts, would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS projects positive employment growth for all three core occupations and states that boundary certification, construction-site review, map information, planning, and disaster mapping will continue to require work. USGS reports near-complete baseline elevation coverage but proposes recurring higher-quality recollection to meet documented needs. This supports modest quantity growth, balanced by housing and construction cycles, public-budget risk, mature baseline coverage, and ongoing productivity gains. [S2, S3, S4, S9]

caveats: No source measures the eligible share of the frozen lower-middle-market firm count.
Project-based revenue can be repeat without being contractually recurring.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged from older Census size data, so the firms represented may not align perfectly with current normalized EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level review of the supplied lower-middle-market population using revenue by customer, repeat-client share, service classification, license-holder depth, and control-transfer feasibility would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: NAICS 541370 is already defined around establishments selling surveying and mapping services, so most firms in the supplied lower-middle-market count should be external service providers. Boundary, construction, infrastructure, utility, and mapping work also commonly produces repeat client or program relationships even when individual scopes are project-based. I exclude firms dominated by isolated assignments, software or publishing economics, captive operations, or owner-specific licenses and relationships that cannot support a transferable controlled operation. [S1, S2, S13]

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541370 ($4,275,045,767) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541370 ($8,875,752,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $161,963 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541370); class <5: 4,125 firms, avg revenue/firm $264,017 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $41,319 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,456 firms, avg revenue/firm $810,381 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $126,825 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 758 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,761,821 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $275,725 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 469 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,603,267 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $876,911 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 76 firms, avg revenue/firm $16,153,224 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,527,980 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 43 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,923,953 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,152,599 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 119

o.bridge: BLS measures occupation requirements and describes regulation, not the share of year-five service quantity requiring a lower-middle-market surveying or mapping operator. I map licensed boundary certification, site verification, field control, standards, accuracy review, and client accountability to operator-required quantity, while discounting commodity map production, public-data reuse, client self-service, and software-generated outputs. Occupational descriptions centered on professional roles likely overstate the operator-required share of the broader mapping basket, while legal accountability makes them more representative of cadastral and construction surveying.
caveats: Licensure applies most directly to land surveying and legal certification, not every cartographic or GIS deliverable.
Public LiDAR and imagery can complement firms but can also let customers self-serve some mapping work.
Future software liability regimes and customer acceptance are uncertain.
o.change_evidence: A revenue-weighted service-line study separating legally certified, field-verified, accountable deliverables from self-service, software-only, and commodity mapping outputs would materially change this share.
o.rationale: All states and the District of Columbia require surveyor licensure before public practice or certification of legal documents, and BLS expects surveyors to remain necessary for boundary certification and construction-site review. BLS also notes that technicians must review and interpret technology outputs for accuracy and completeness. AI and public geospatial data can reduce operator demand for routine mapping, but legal boundaries, physical evidence, specified accuracy, site conditions, and accountable delivery preserve a large human-operated service core. [S2, S3, S4, S9]

q.bridge: ACEC describes available billing methods for engineering and land-surveying firms, and federal highway rules prescribe qualifications-based selection for covered design-related consulting, but neither source measures revenue shares or retained AI benefits in NAICS 541370. I assign higher retention to lump-sum and differentiated qualifications-based work, lower retention to hourly, per-diem, and cost-based work, and discount all methods for rebids, renewal repricing, customer negotiation, and competitive imitation. Public qualifications-based selection may overstate insulation because fees are still negotiated and many private jobs remain price competitive; the absence of contract-mix weights makes the estimate directionally uncertain.
caveats: The billing source is guidance from a Massachusetts trade association, not a national revenue-share survey.
Federal highway procurement rules apply only to covered public contracts.
Retention may be lower where firms sell staff hours and higher where scarce licensure, capacity, or trusted local records drive selection.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract audit showing NAICS 541370 revenue by billing type, renewal cadence, utilization treatment, realized price changes after productivity improvements, and customer concentration would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Defined-scope lump-sum jobs can preserve labor savings through a contract term, while hourly and cost-based engagements expose fewer billable hours and invite pass-through. Qualifications-based public procurement can protect differentiation because price is excluded from initial ranking, but scopes are competed and fees are negotiated. Over five years, renewals, customer sharing, and rival adoption should dissipate a meaningful portion of gross benefits. [S13, S14]

rho.bridge: FHWA reports UAS adoption and time or cost savings among state transportation agencies; NIST reports historical GPS adoption among licensed surveyors; and POB reports scanner and drone use among surveying respondents. I use those adjacent-technology adoption quantities to infer implementation capacity, then discount for the narrower AI-exposed opportunity, fragmented small-firm systems, accuracy validation, professional liability, training, and workflow redesign. Mature GPS and UAS adoption at public agencies and self-selected survey respondents probably overstates five-year AI implementation in lower-middle-market firms, although rapid improvement in packaged software could make the discount too severe.
caveats: UAS and GPS are adjacent mature technologies, not direct measures of generative or predictive AI implementation.
State transportation agencies have procurement scale and technical resources unlike many privately held firms.
POB respondent selection and firm-size composition are not reported well enough to establish representativeness.
rho.change_evidence: A current NAICS 541370 survey measuring production AI deployment, task-level usage, realized labor hours, error rates, and deployment by firm size would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: The industry has repeatedly integrated precision instruments, GPS, laser scanning, drones, and digital workflows. FHWA reports that most state transportation agencies had used UAS and cites substantial surveying time savings; NIST reports GPS use by almost all surveyed licensed surveyors; POB reported meaningful scanner and drone penetration. This installed digital base favors implementation, but exposed tasks still require reliable geospatial data, tolerances, review, integration with CAD and GIS, and accountable sign-off. [S5, S6, S7, S8]

s5.bridge: The source population is broader architecture, engineering, and environmental consulting, with survey respondents concentrated above very-small-firm size; it reports recent transactions, buyer intentions, and retirement-triggered transaction expectations rather than a five-year control-transfer probability for eligible NAICS 541370 firms. I translate those signals into a cumulative probability and discount transactions that are internal successions, recapitalizations without qualifying control, acquisitions outside the frozen size band, or plans that do not close. The broader AEC transaction evidence is likely upward-biased because active buyers and larger firms are more visible, while omission of private unannounced sales may bias observed deal counts downward.
caveats: The best available transaction evidence covers broader AEC firms and is not a probability sample of NAICS 541370.
Surveyed AEC firms skew larger than the full small-firm population, although the frozen EBITDA band overlaps part of the sample.
Retirement triggers and buyer intentions may not result in completed qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A NAICS 541370 transaction census linking five years of announced and private control changes to the eligible firm denominator, with deal type, seller EBITDA, and internal versus external succession, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: AEC transaction activity is active, founder-led sellers are common, and surveying and geospatial capabilities are named acquisition themes. The outlook survey also reports widespread acquisition intent and retirement as an expected transaction trigger. Against that, formal succession planning is weaker at smaller firms, intentions are not completed transfers, and many transitions will be internal or otherwise nonqualifying. The resulting probability is deliberately well below the reported retirement and acquisition-intent shares. [S10, S11, S12]

records.principal_driver: A large digital production layer sits beside durable field verification and licensed accountability, creating room to expand capacity while preserving an operator role.
records.principal_weakness: No industry-specific evidence directly measures five-year control transfers, AI implementation, or retained productivity benefits for lower-middle-market firms.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as surveying and mapping of the earth's surface, including the sea floor, and lists cadastral, cartographic, topographic, and geodetic services while excluding geophysical surveying and map publishing.
sources.title: NAICS 541370 Surveying and Mapping (except Geophysical) Services

sources.supports: Describes legal-record research, field measurement, verification, mapping and reporting duties; reports 56,100 US jobs in 2024, projected employment growth of 4 percent through 2034, and licensure requirements in every state and the District of Columbia.
sources.title: Surveyors

sources.supports: Describes field measurement, data entry, imagery processing, GIS and CAD work, calculations, and technology-output review; reports 59,400 US jobs in 2024 and projected employment growth of 5 percent through 2034.
sources.title: Surveying and Mapping Technicians

sources.supports: Describes collecting and integrating surveys, LiDAR, imagery, GIS, and CAD data and updating maps; reports 13,400 US jobs in 2024 and projected employment growth of 6 percent through 2034.
sources.title: Cartographers and Photogrammetrists

sources.supports: Identifies data analysis, feature extraction, digitization, report generation, quality assurance, and document conversion as AI applications in geomatics while stressing field operation, human judgment, relationships, and accountability.
sources.title: AI will support rather than replace surveyors

sources.supports: Reports broad state transportation agency use of unmanned aircraft and cited surveying time reductions, including a Utah example in which surveying time fell by as much as half.
sources.title: States Expand UAS Use to Save Time and Money

sources.supports: Reports survey evidence that licensed-surveyor GPS adoption rose from about one fifth in 1990 to about seven tenths in 2000 and that almost all surveyed surveyors now use GPS in some capacity.
sources.title: The Economic Benefits of the Global Positioning System

sources.supports: Reports that 42 percent of respondent firms used laser scanners and about one third used drones in 2020, with productivity and efficiency the leading purchase trigger.
sources.title: Equipment Report Reveals Newer Tech Taking Hold for Surveyors

sources.supports: Reports that 98.3 percent of US baseline elevation coverage was available or in progress in fiscal 2024 and proposes repeated, higher-quality recollection to meet documented user needs.
sources.title: The Next Generation 3D Elevation Program

sources.supports: Reports more than 2,700 AEC transactions since 2021, describes many sellers as small or mid-sized and founder-led, and identifies steady surveying deal activity tied to geospatial, resilience, and infrastructure demand.
sources.title: AEC M&A Outlook 2025

sources.supports: Reports that roughly two thirds of surveyed buyers expected acquisitions during the next five years and more than 55 percent of surveyed firms expected senior-principal retirement to trigger a transaction.
sources.title: AEC M&A Outlook 2025: Buyer Intentions and Retirement

sources.supports: Reports an ownership-transition survey of 186 engineering firm owners, including that 62 percent had an ownership plan and only 51 percent of firms with fewer than 50 employees had a formal plan.
sources.title: ACEC-FMI Ownership Transition Study Finds Gap Between Knowledge and Intent

sources.supports: Describes hourly or per-diem, salary-cost multiplier, cost-plus-fixed-fee, lump-sum, and construction-cost-linked billing methods used by engineering and land-surveying firms.
sources.title: How an Engineering Firm or Land Surveying Firm Charges for Its Services

sources.supports: Requires qualifications-based selection for covered federal-aid highway engineering and design-related consulting, bars price from initial ranking, and provides for fee negotiation after selection.
sources.title: 23 CFR 172.7 Procurement Methods and Procedures

records.title: Surveying and Mapping (except Geophysical) Services

caveats: a: The occupation source is a commercial compilation that states it uses US government financial data; the displayed mix is headcount, not wages or time, and its broad occupation labels do not isolate licensed landscape architects.
a: The ASLA AI survey covered members across landscape architecture firms, multidisciplinary firms, government, academia, and other settings rather than only the frozen LMM firm lens.
a: The estimate excludes tasks already automated by existing CADD, BIM, GIS, templates, and conventional software, but respondents may differ in what they considered AI versus established automation.
rho: Current adoption is not the same as operational implementation or avoided hiring.
rho: Survey respondents were self-selected technology-interested practitioners, so usage and expectations may be upward biased.
rho: The five-year forecast is especially sensitive to reliable CAD/BIM automation and acceptable professional-liability controls, neither of which the sources quantify.
e: The supplied firm count is an ESTIMATE derived from size-class receipts and a margin bridge, not a named census of firms; eligibility is applied to that estimated population.
e: Winning projects through repeat clients is not identical to the share of firms having any repeat work, and the published ASLA summary does not disclose the full response distribution.
e: NAICS assignment reflects primary establishment activity and cannot reveal owner dependence or customer recurrence.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and reports owner intentions, not completed deals or this industry.
s5: The AIA evidence is for architecture firms, not landscape architecture firms, and includes mergers and acquisitions that may not transfer control of an eligible standalone firm.
s5: Multi-owner professional firms may transfer minority stakes gradually; those events are excluded unless control changes.
q: The AIA billing survey is an architecture-firm proxy and reports percentage of firms using each method, not the percentage of revenue under each method.
q: The ASLA standard agreement demonstrates available terms, not adoption frequency.
q: The estimate depends on competitive intensity, backlog, utilization, and contract renewal timing that public sources do not quantify for the LMM lens.
d5: The BLS projection is occupational and national, not an industry service-volume forecast, and assumes a technology and productivity path that may differ from this screen.
d5: The revenue history is nominal, ends in 2022, and cannot establish constant-quality quantity growth.
d5: Demand is cyclical and geographically uneven, especially for private development and discretionary residential projects.
o: Licensure rules vary in scope and exemptions even though the title and profession are regulated nationwide; not every deliverable or project requires a stamp.
o: The operator-required share is judgmental because no source measures revenue-weighted substitution between firms, customer self-service, and software.
o: Small residential and garden-planning work may be more substitutable than the public and commercial project mix represented in professional surveys.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Project-based professional services sold to developers, public agencies, institutions, architects and engineers, businesses, and property owners. Revenue is commonly earned through fixed or stipulated project fees, hourly fees, supplemental-service fees, and sometimes fees linked to construction cost; repeat clients are an important source of new projects.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal design units, non-control financing vehicles, shells, firms outside the approximate $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, and landscape installation or maintenance businesses classified outside NAICS 541320.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market landscape architecture firms providing outsourced land planning, site design, landscape design, documentation, permitting support, and construction-phase observation for commercial, institutional, public, infrastructure, recreational, and residential clients, with recurring relationships or repeat project work.

a.bridge: The industry occupation-mix source reports headcount rather than wage-weighted task time: architecture and engineering occupations are 50% of workers, office and administrative support 11%, management 9%, business and financial operations 6%, computer occupations 5%, and life/physical/social science occupations 5%. I mapped those groups to the documented landscape-architect workflow and current AI use cases, then applied broad relative-pay judgment to approximate wage weights. Drafting, documentation, data processing, reports, RFPs, early concepts, estimating, and code checks are treated as exposed; client discovery, original design judgment, site inspection, multidisciplinary coordination, and accountable review are treated as substantially less exposed. This likely overstates exposure if the higher-paid principal and licensed-professional time is more relationship-, site-, and judgment-heavy than the group averages.
caveats: The occupation source is a commercial compilation that states it uses US government financial data; the displayed mix is headcount, not wages or time, and its broad occupation labels do not isolate licensed landscape architects.
The ASLA AI survey covered members across landscape architecture firms, multidisciplinary firms, government, academia, and other settings rather than only the frozen LMM firm lens.
The estimate excludes tasks already automated by existing CADD, BIM, GIS, templates, and conventional software, but respondents may differ in what they considered AI versus established automation.
a.change_evidence: Obtain an anonymized six-month time-and-billing export from at least 20 independent US landscape architecture firms, mapped by role and project phase to a task taxonomy, then have licensed practitioners classify each task for current automation, partial assistance, and required review. Payroll by role would permit a true wage-weighted estimate.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is heavily professional and digital, and the core role includes CADD production, specifications, cost estimates, environmental analysis, information processing, and documentation. In an industry survey, AI was already used for reports, conceptual design, and RFP responses, while respondents expected future help with repetitive CAD and plant-list work and with grading or code checks. Those are material task pools, but the same occupation also centers on creative problem solving, client communication, site-specific judgment, coordination, compliance, and field inspection. The range therefore represents a meaningful but clearly sub-majority wage-weighted opportunity rather than wholesale substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment of landscape architects, not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 541320 services. Translating the 3% ten-year employment projection into five years gives roughly 1.015 under a constant-growth, stable-productivity assumption, rounded to a 1.02 base. Historical Census Service Annual Survey revenue rose from $4.574 billion in 2019 to $5.778 billion in 2022, but that nominal series is used only as directional context because it includes price changes and a pandemic-era mix shift. Employment may understate service demand if productivity rises, while nominal revenue overstates real quantity growth under inflation.
caveats: The BLS projection is occupational and national, not an industry service-volume forecast, and assumes a technology and productivity path that may differ from this screen.
The revenue history is nominal, ends in 2022, and cannot establish constant-quality quantity growth.
Demand is cyclical and geographically uneven, especially for private development and discretionary residential projects.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain inflation-adjusted quarterly billings and backlog by landscape-architecture project type from ASLA or a purpose-built panel of firms, paired with Census QSS/SAS price deflators and Dodge or ConstructConnect project starts. A five-year forecast by public, residential, commercial, institutional, and resilience work would materially narrow the range.
d5.rationale: The central case is broadly flat to modestly growing real service quantity. BLS expects landscape architects to remain needed for commercial, industrial, and residential development, while water conservation and pollution prevention may add demand. Parks, public space, campuses, mixed-use development, housing, and urban design diversify end markets, but exposure to construction and real-estate cycles warrants a downside below current demand. Climate adaptation and infrastructure investment support the upside.

caveats: The supplied firm count is an ESTIMATE derived from size-class receipts and a margin bridge, not a named census of firms; eligibility is applied to that estimated population.
Winning projects through repeat clients is not identical to the share of firms having any repeat work, and the published ASLA summary does not disclose the full response distribution.
NAICS assignment reflects primary establishment activity and cannot reveal owner dependence or customer recurrence.
e.change_evidence: Commission a firm-level survey of the 46 dataset-identified LMM-band firms, asking revenue share from external customers, repeat-client revenue share, client concentration, project recurrence, owner billable dependence, and whether installation or maintenance is bundled. Confirm classifications against customer invoices and CRM histories.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is intrinsically an outsourced professional-design category and excludes integrated landscape installation and maintenance. The ASLA industry survey reports that 61% of firms win new projects through repeat clients, directly supporting repeat-work relevance. The base exceeds 61% because eligibility requires some recurring or repeat outsourced service, not that repeat clients be the primary source of all wins. A minority is excluded for predominantly one-off residential or bespoke commissions, owner-personal practices that are not operationally transferable, captive-like arrangements, or mixed operations that do not fit the service lens even though they carry the code.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541320 ($2,653,798,071) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541320 ($5,788,521,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $206,548 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541320); class <5: 3,549 firms, avg revenue/firm $379,051 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $59,321 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 800 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,193,496 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $186,782 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 390 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,621,638 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $410,286 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 256 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,121,246 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $957,975 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 33 firms, avg revenue/firm $23,646,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,700,651 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 13 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,128,077 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,428,544 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 46

caveats: Licensure rules vary in scope and exemptions even though the title and profession are regulated nationwide; not every deliverable or project requires a stamp.
The operator-required share is judgmental because no source measures revenue-weighted substitution between firms, customer self-service, and software.
Small residential and garden-planning work may be more substitutable than the public and commercial project mix represented in professional surveys.
o.change_evidence: Survey procurement officers, developers, architects, public agencies, insurers, and state boards on which landscape-architecture deliverables will still require a contracted firm and professional stamp in five years. Match this with project-level revenue by complexity and jurisdiction, plus observed substitution rates for AI garden-design and automated site-planning products.
o.rationale: Most year-five demand should still require an accountable operating firm because the service integrates site conditions, client requirements, public safety, permitting, multidisciplinary coordination, design judgment, construction documents, and field observation. Licensure is required to practice and use the landscape architect title in all 50 states, licensed professionals can stamp drawings and lead practices, and only 7% of ASLA AI-survey respondents said their firm permits AI-generated content in stamped plans. Self-service and software can eliminate some low-complexity residential concept work and automate pieces of analysis, visualization, and documentation, but they are less likely to replace the accountable operator on public, institutional, commercial, infrastructure, and complex residential projects.

q.bridge: The sources observe contract and billing structures, not the discounted share of AI benefit retained. The adjacent AIA survey reports fixed-fee and hourly methods are both widespread, with stipulated sums used regularly by 70% of responding firms and hourly rates regularly by 42%; ASLA's standard agreement permits a negotiated core fee, hourly supplemental services, progress billing, and compensation renegotiation. I map fixed-fee work to high initial retention that decays as competitive bids and renewals reprice, and hourly work to faster pass-through through fewer billable hours, partly offset by backlog and capacity redeployment. Because method adoption overlaps and is not revenue share, the translation is deliberately wide. Using architecture billing may overstate pricing similarity for small residential landscape practices but should be closer for commercial and public design work.
caveats: The AIA billing survey is an architecture-firm proxy and reports percentage of firms using each method, not the percentage of revenue under each method.
The ASLA standard agreement demonstrates available terms, not adoption frequency.
The estimate depends on competitive intensity, backlog, utilization, and contract renewal timing that public sources do not quantify for the LMM lens.
q.change_evidence: Collect five years of proposals, contracts, invoices, write-offs, and renewals from at least 20 target firms. Measure revenue by fixed fee, hourly, percentage-of-construction, and cost-plus terms; then compare realized price, hours, and margin before and after specific AI workflow deployments at the same project phase and client.
q.rationale: Project fees give operators a window to retain productivity gains when scope and price are agreed before delivery, while hourly and cost-linked work transmit savings more quickly to clients. Over five years, repeated competitive proposals and a repeat-client market should share gains through lower fees, expanded scope, or slower fee inflation. Professional accountability, design quality, and the ability to redeploy scarce staff into backlog preserve some value, but sustained full retention is unlikely.

rho.bridge: The ASLA survey measures current use, reported time savings, experimentation constraints, deliverable policy, and practitioner expectations, not the five-year implemented share of the exposed labor opportunity. I translate 55% current use but only 27% reporting time saved, 15% allowing billable experimentation, and 7% permitting AI-generated content in stamped plans into a moderate implementation base. The 68% expecting help on repetitive work and 63% expecting technical assistance support upside. The bridge may overstate LMM implementation because survey respondents include large firms and non-firm settings, but may understate five-year implementation because it captures an early adoption stage.
caveats: Current adoption is not the same as operational implementation or avoided hiring.
Survey respondents were self-selected technology-interested practitioners, so usage and expectations may be upward biased.
The five-year forecast is especially sensitive to reliable CAD/BIM automation and acceptable professional-liability controls, neither of which the sources quantify.
rho.change_evidence: Repeat the ASLA Digital Technology PPN survey annually with a panel restricted to independent US firms, capturing production deployment by task, percentage of labor hours saved, error and rework rates, client restrictions, and whether savings reduced staffing or merely increased output. Validate with software telemetry from CAD/BIM and project-management vendors.
rho.rationale: Implementation should advance as AI functions enter the CAD, BIM, image, GIS, writing, and project-management tools firms already use. However, current realized savings lag usage, informal trial-and-error learning dominates, few firms allow billable experimentation, and stamped plans remain tightly controlled. Data quality, professional liability, client confidentiality, interoperability, change management, and licensed review constrain how much exposed work becomes dependable labor substitution within five years.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures plans among US business owners rather than completed qualifying transfers among eligible landscape architecture firms: 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years, and 52.3% of employer businesses were owned by people age 55 or older. An AIA survey of adjacent architecture firms found 5% had completed a merger or acquisition in the prior year, but that event measure includes transactions outside this lens and cannot simply be compounded. I center the estimate near Gallup's 22% employer plan rate, reduce it for plans that do not close or do not constitute a transferable control transaction, and retain an upside case for the older professional-services owner base and observed architecture deal activity. The architecture comparison may overstate landscape-firm liquidity if buyers prefer larger architecture platforms.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and reports owner intentions, not completed deals or this industry.
The AIA evidence is for architecture firms, not landscape architecture firms, and includes mergers and acquisitions that may not transfer control of an eligible standalone firm.
Multi-owner professional firms may transfer minority stakes gradually; those events are excluded unless control changes.
s5.change_evidence: Build a five-year transaction cohort from DealStats, GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, and state professional-license records for US NAICS 541320 firms in the target EBITDA band. Supplement with confidential interviews of 30 firm principals aged 55 or older about timing, successor, ownership percentage to be transferred, and buyer readiness.
s5.rationale: Employer firms are materially more transferable than nonemployer practices, and broad US owner demographics create a real five-year succession pipeline. Adjacent architecture firms show actual M&A activity, while many leaders also say a merger or acquisition is unlikely over the next three to five years. The resulting probability is meaningful but not dominant because intentions fail, internal reorganizations may not qualify, personal-client dependence can impair transferability, and some owners close instead of selling.

records.principal_driver: Digitally produced plans, documents, analysis, and proposals create a material assistance opportunity inside a high-compensation professional workflow.
records.principal_weakness: Client relationships, site-specific judgment, licensure, stamped-plan liability, and mixed hourly billing limit both implementation speed and durable retention of savings.

sources.supports: Survey of more than 370 respondents documents the digital production base: AutoCAD was extremely important to 73%, roughly two-thirds used Revit or Vectorworks with some consistency, AI and BIM were each cited by around 40%, and 45% of respondents worked at firms with fewer than 10 people.

a: Ambient-scribe results come from physicians and advanced-practice practitioners rather than optometrists.
a: Automated instruments and retinal interpretation are already present in some practices, so gross technical capability overstates the not-yet-automated share.
rho: The AMA adoption population is physicians rather than optometrists.
rho: The ambient-scribe study was voluntary and short-duration, and its health-system support may not transfer to independent groups.
rho: CMS code 92229 covers a narrow retinal-imaging use case rather than broad autonomous eye care.
e: No public source measures the share of frozen LMM firms satisfying every lens criterion.
e: BLS occupation shares do not map cleanly to enterprise ownership or eligibility.
e: The dataset's LMM count uses a broad healthcare-provider margin rather than observed optometry EBITDA.
s5: BizBuySell provides medical-practice benchmarks but no optometry-only sale probability or eligible-firm denominator.
s5: The platform's median sold practice is smaller than the target EBITDA band.
s5: A transfer can fail economically if patients, payer panels, or optical sales follow the departing owner.
q: Public evidence does not quantify the lens's clinical-versus-optical revenue or gross-benefit mix.
q: The FTC rule documents prescription portability and competition but not the magnitude of AI savings passed through to patients.
q: Freed clinician or technician capacity has little value when appointment demand is the binding constraint.
d5: BLS projects an occupation rather than real output or visit quantity for offices of optometrists.
d5: The occupation includes work settings outside NAICS 621320.
d5: Product sales, clinical services, and screening can follow different demand paths.
o: The estimate distinguishes continued need for an accountable practice from headcount per encounter.
o: State scope-of-practice rules differ even though licensure is universal.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patients receive the service and may pay directly, while medical or vision insurers fund covered examinations and procedures. Practices may also earn product revenue from eyeglasses, contact lenses, and related optical goods. Clinical revenue is generally visit- or procedure-based, whereas optical revenue combines product margin and fitting service; repeat examinations, disease monitoring, and prescription renewals create repeat demand without guaranteed contracts.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive hospital, HMO, or government units; ophthalmology practices; pure optical-goods retailers without optometric practice; online eyewear or software businesses; shell or non-operating entities; non-control financing positions; and solo or owner-dependent practices that cannot support a transferable operating company or fall below the normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent group and multisite optometry practices in the lower-middle-market band that repeatedly provide eye examinations, refraction, disease detection and management, corrective-lens prescribing and fitting, contact-lens care, vision therapy, pre- and postoperative care, and integrated optical dispensing to external patients and payers.

a.bridge: O*NET's national Optometrists task map, a multicenter ambient-scribe study of physicians and advanced-practice practitioners, and CMS recognition of AI-interpreted retinal imaging are translated into the wage-weighted exposed share of a scaled optometry office. The estimate includes documentation, intake, scheduling, billing support, patient messaging, image pre-analysis, test summarization, inventory and ordering, recall, and portions of optical sales support; it reserves patient examination, complex interpretation, treatment choice, prescribing, fitting, procedures, counseling, exceptions, and accountability for people. The clinician-only O*NET map may understate support and retail labor, while physician health-system documentation results may overstate EHR burden and integration readiness; CMS's existing retinal-imaging code demonstrates that some diagnostic automation is already deployed and therefore must not be counted as wholly new opportunity.
caveats: O*NET measures optometrist duties, not the full occupation and wage mix of NAICS 621320 offices.
Ambient-scribe results come from physicians and advanced-practice practitioners rather than optometrists.
Automated instruments and retinal interpretation are already present in some practices, so gross technical capability overstates the not-yet-automated share.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion and payroll study of independent LMM optometry groups separating optometrists, technicians, opticians, billing, front desk, call center, inventory, and management work, with before/after AI deployment results and current automation baselines, would materially move this range.
a.rationale: Optometry combines digitally captured test data and standardized administrative and retail workflows with high-contact licensed care. O*NET shows core work in instrument-based examination, test analysis, prescribing, treatment planning, counseling, and referral, plus daily face-to-face interaction and high accuracy requirements. AI can assist the information-processing perimeter, but the wage-heavy clinician core and hands-on examination and fitting place a ceiling on substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects optometrist employment to grow 8% from 2024 to 2034. Taking the square root of 1.08 yields an approximately 1.04 five-year directional demand multiplier. Occupational employment is not constant-price, constant-quality demand for NAICS 621320 and includes optometrists working in physician offices and outpatient centers; it may overstate office demand if productivity or non-office employment grows faster, or understate service quantity if automated testing increases visits per clinician.
caveats: BLS projects an occupation rather than real output or visit quantity for offices of optometrists.
The occupation includes work settings outside NAICS 621320.
Product sales, clinical services, and screening can follow different demand paths.
d5.change_evidence: A 621320-specific constant-dollar revenue and encounter forecast segmented by clinical exams, medical eye care, contact lenses, eyewear, payer, age, disease state, online substitution, and automated screening would materially move this estimate.
d5.rationale: BLS identifies aging, refractive errors, digital eye strain, and diabetes-related retinal disease as continuing sources of eye-care demand. Downside comes from retail and online substitution, autonomous screening, payer limits, price competition, and greater productivity from diagnostic equipment. The range therefore allows modest contraction as well as growth around the occupational projection.

caveats: No public source measures the share of frozen LMM firms satisfying every lens criterion.
BLS occupation shares do not map cleanly to enterprise ownership or eligibility.
The dataset's LMM count uses a broad healthcare-provider margin rather than observed optometry EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of 621320 companies in the normalized EBITDA band showing ownership, locations, optometrist and optician staffing, clinical versus optical revenue, payer mix, owner production, repeat-patient behavior, and institutional affiliation would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: NAICS 621320 is defined around independent optometric practice and explicitly includes clinical care plus prescription eyewear and contact-lens provision, so most LMM firms serve external patients repeatedly. Eligibility is reduced for captive offices in hospitals or HMOs, government units, optical-dominant operations with weak clinical identity, non-control situations, and practices whose patient or referral goodwill and licensed production cannot transfer beyond the selling owner. The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms around the EBITDA band.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
