caveats: The estimate distinguishes continued need for an accountable practice from headcount per encounter.
State scope-of-practice rules differ even though licensure is universal.
The automated retinal-imaging precedent covers one disease-screening task and still routes positive or inconclusive cases to eye-care professionals.
o.change_evidence: Evidence on autonomous screening and refraction penetration, comprehensive-exam substitution, state-law changes, false-negative and referral rates, patient willingness to use remote or retail channels, optical e-commerce share, and human escalation frequency would materially change this range.
o.rationale: Comprehensive eye examinations, prescribing, disease management, treatment planning, procedures, counseling, referral, and fitting continue to require an accountable licensed optometrist or trained staff under supervision, and every state requires optometrist licensure. However, CMS already recognizes AI interpretation for a narrow retinal-imaging service, while federal prescription-release rules allow patients to separate the examination from eyewear purchase. Screening, refraction support, routine monitoring, and optical purchasing can therefore migrate toward automated or self-service channels even though complex care remains operator-led.

caveats: Public evidence does not quantify the lens's clinical-versus-optical revenue or gross-benefit mix.
The FTC rule documents prescription portability and competition but not the magnitude of AI savings passed through to patients.
Freed clinician or technician capacity has little value when appointment demand is the binding constraint.
q.change_evidence: Practice-level contracts and ledgers showing clinical reimbursement, optical gross margin, managed-vision discounts, prescription capture, repricing, staff cost, remakes, inventory turns, and contribution margin before and after AI deployment would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Visit- and procedure-based clinical reimbursement can let a practice retain administrative and throughput gains without repricing each labor hour. Optical products add a different mechanism: automation can reduce ordering, inventory, sales-support, and remakes, but prescription portability lets patients shop elsewhere and exposes eyewear margins to online and retail competition. Managed vision plans, payer fee schedules, procurement, vendor fees, and customer expectations can claim part of the benefit.

rho.bridge: The AMA's 2026 physician survey supplies a U.S. clinical-adoption proxy, the six-system ambulatory study demonstrates implemented documentation assistance, and CMS's automated retinal-imaging code supplies an optometry-adjacent production foothold. The AMA reports 72% of physicians incorporating at least one surveyed AI use case in 2026, while CMS describes point-of-care AI interpretation of a retinal examination without requiring ophthalmologist interpretation as a diagnostic service. Physicians and health systems have greater integration resources than many independent optometry groups, tending to overstate adoption, but optometry's structured imaging, testing, EHR, scheduling, and inventory systems may speed packaged deployment over five years.
caveats: The AMA adoption population is physicians rather than optometrists.
The ambient-scribe study was voluntary and short-duration, and its health-system support may not transfer to independent groups.
CMS code 92229 covers a narrow retinal-imaging use case rather than broad autonomous eye care.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of independent U.S. optometry groups by size measuring paid AI deployment, diagnostic-device penetration, EHR integration, override and referral rates, documentation edits, staff hours, optical conversion, and realized payroll changes would materially tighten this estimate.
rho.rationale: Implementation can be meaningful because several exposed workflows are already digital and diagnostic AI has a reimbursable precedent. Realization remains constrained by device and EHR integration, patient privacy, vendor cost, state scope rules, diagnostic error, cybersecurity, clinician review, workflow redesign, staff training, and the need to distinguish automated screening from comprehensive eye care.

caveats: BizBuySell provides medical-practice benchmarks but no optometry-only sale probability or eligible-firm denominator.
The platform's median sold practice is smaller than the target EBITDA band.
A transfer can fail economically if patients, payer panels, or optical sales follow the departing owner.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort from optometry practice brokers, lenders, state boards, vision-plan networks, or transaction databases identifying eligible LMM practices, completed control transfers, failed listings, recapitalizations, seller transitions, and post-close patient and clinician retention would materially move this estimate.
s5.rationale: BizBuySell explicitly includes optometry in its sold medical-practice benchmark population, establishing a transfer channel. Five-year qualifying availability still depends on owner timing, licensed buyer or employed-clinician capacity, payer credentialing, lease and equipment obligations, patient retention, optical inventory, and the separation of goodwill from the selling doctor. The frozen LMM population is larger than the chiropractic counterpart but remains estimate-based, and public transaction data provide no denominator.

records.principal_driver: Structured diagnostic, administrative, and optical workflows offer multiple AI-enabled efficiency levers around a durable recurring need for licensed eye care.
records.principal_weakness: Prescription portability, retail and online competition, and partial automation can separate optical economics and routine screening from the practice while licensed-clinician continuity still constrains transfers.

sources.supports: Defines 621320 as independent O.D. practices examining, diagnosing, treating, and managing visual-system disease and providing corrective lenses, vision therapy, and optician-like fitting and sales.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 621320 Offices of Optometrists

sources.supports: Provides the optometrist task and work-context map, including instrument-based eye examination, test analysis, treatment planning, prescribing, fitting, counseling, referral, daily face-to-face discussion, frequent contact, and high accuracy requirements.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Optometrists (29-1041.00)

sources.supports: Reports 47,800 optometrist jobs in 2024, work-setting shares including 54% in optometrist offices and 11% self-employed, universal state licensure, 8% projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034, and aging, refractive errors, digital eye strain, and diabetes as demand drivers.
sources.title: U.S. BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook: Optometrists

sources.supports: Reports U.S. physician AI use, including 72% incorporating at least one surveyed use case in 2026, and identifies privacy, safety, efficacy, liability, training, and oversight as adoption considerations.
sources.title: American Medical Association: 2026 Physician AI Survey

sources.supports: A 2024 pre/post quality-improvement study of 263 ambulatory clinicians across six U.S. health systems found lower documentation burden and an average-equivalent 10.8 minutes saved per workday after 30 days of ambient-scribe use, with voluntary participation and no optometry-specific sample.
sources.title: JAMA Network Open: Use of Ambient AI Scribes to Reduce Administrative Burden and Professional Burnout

sources.supports: Describes CPT 92229 for point-of-care automated retinal-image analysis using AI to interpret the eye examination without requiring ophthalmologist interpretation as a diagnostic service, demonstrating a narrow operational automation precedent.
sources.title: CMS MLN Matters MM12071: Remote Retinal Imaging CPT 92229

sources.supports: States that optometrists and ophthalmologists must provide a free prescription copy immediately after a refractive exam and before offering eyeglasses, enabling consumers to shop elsewhere and supporting optical-retail competition and compliance burden.
sources.title: Federal Trade Commission: Final Eyeglass Rule Updates

sources.supports: Reports transaction and valuation benchmarks for sold medical practices from 2021 through 2025 and explicitly identifies optometry among included specialties, demonstrating a transfer channel while not providing an optometry-specific probability or eligible-firm denominator.
sources.title: BizBuySell: Medical Practice Business Valuation Benchmarks

records.title: Offices of Optometrists

caveats: a: The BLS industry occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541400 and includes graphic, industrial, and other specialized design services.
a: O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, technical feasibility, or current automation.
a: The estimate concerns current not-yet-automated work; the available adoption survey does not quantify how much work is already automated.
rho: Regular tool use is not equivalent to production-grade implementation or realized labor avoidance.
rho: The interior-design survey is small, global, and associated with a 3D-design platform.
rho: Autodesk's AECO population is much broader than independent US LMM interior design firms and overrepresents Autodesk users.
e: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
e: The injected firm-count bridge uses a 15.65% broad-sector margin and may place some procurement-heavy or owner-compensation-heavy firms in the wrong EBITDA band.
e: NAICS 541410 includes aesthetic-only decorating consultants, increasing business-model variation within the code.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than interior design or the LMM band.
s5: Survey intentions combine sales with other transfers and do not establish completed qualifying control transactions.
s5: Founder dependence may reduce sale completion or require long earn-outs.
q: The pricing survey is from 2019, UK-based, and mostly residential, not US LMM commercial practices.
q: Pricing-model shares do not directly measure five-year benefit retention.
q: Procurement markups and pass-through purchases may make receipts an imperfect measure of service economics.
d5: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541400, not 541410.
d5: Occupation employment is not service quantity and includes interior designers working outside specialized design firms.
d5: The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant and therefore excludes new AI-enabled offerings.
o: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of interior design demand.
o: Aesthetic-only residential and decorating work is substantially more exposed to self-service than regulated commercial, healthcare, or institutional work.
o: Rapid improvement in spatially accurate generative design, automated code checking, and direct-to-consumer procurement could push the value below the range.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External property owners, developers, operators, institutions, and homeowners buy project-based services. Revenue may be fixed fee, hourly, a percentage of project cost, procurement markup, or a hybrid; repeatability is strongest with commercial, hospitality, institutional, developer, and multi-site clients.
lens.excluded_activities: Architectural and engineering firms outside NAICS 541410; furniture retailers and manufacturers; construction and installation contractors; captive corporate design teams; shells, non-control financing vehicles, solo practitioners below the LMM band, and firms limited to nonrepeat one-off engagements.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market interior design and interior decorating firms providing repeat outsourced planning, design, documentation, specification, procurement coordination, and project-administration services for commercial, hospitality, healthcare, institutional, and residential clients.

a.bridge: BLS measures the occupation mix for four-digit NAICS 541400, not six-digit 541410, and O*NET describes tasks for interior designers rather than all firm employees. I mapped higher exposure to CAD drafting, early rendering, materials research, estimating, routine documentation, and office support, and lower exposure to client discovery, code responsibility, field inspection, purchasing, and multidisciplinary coordination. The broader industry includes graphic and industrial design, so its mix may understate 541410's interior-designer concentration but overstate purely digital-production exposure.
caveats: The BLS industry occupation table is four-digit NAICS 541400 and includes graphic, industrial, and other specialized design services.
O*NET task importance does not measure time, wage weight, technical feasibility, or current automation.
The estimate concerns current not-yet-automated work; the available adoption survey does not quantify how much work is already automated.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 541410 staffing table linked to time-and-motion data by workflow, or payroll-level job and wage data from a representative sample of US interior design firms, would materially change the wage-weighted estimate.
a.rationale: Specialized design services employ a large design workforce plus meaningful office support, and interior-design work includes several document- and image-intensive tasks that generative and drafting tools can accelerate. Much of the workflow nonetheless depends on physical-site facts, safety and accessibility rules, client judgment, vendor execution, and accountable coordination, keeping the exposed share below a majority at base.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-dollar output for four-digit specialized design services to grow 2.0% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 1.10 over five years, while interior-designer employment grows 3% over the decade and BLS cites renovation, code, and accessibility demand. I center below the broad output implication to account for the slower occupation outlook and cyclical exposure of renovation and fit-out work. The broader industry may grow differently from interior design, and output growth may include quality or service-mix changes, creating two-sided error.
caveats: The BLS output forecast is for NAICS 541400, not 541410.
Occupation employment is not service quantity and includes interior designers working outside specialized design firms.
The estimate holds quality and the current service basket constant and therefore excludes new AI-enabled offerings.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit BLS or Census real-output forecast for 541410, supplemented by inflation-adjusted ASID project billings and backlog by end market, would materially change the year-five ratio.
d5.rationale: Renovation needs and continuing code and accessibility requirements support modest real demand growth, while commercial fit-out, housing, hospitality, and discretionary residential projects remain sensitive to construction cycles, interest rates, and client capital budgets. A roughly flat downside and modest-growth base are therefore defensible for the current service basket.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the frozen recurring-or-repeat outsourced LMM lens.
The injected firm-count bridge uses a 15.65% broad-sector margin and may place some procurement-heavy or owner-compensation-heavy firms in the wrong EBITDA band.
NAICS 541410 includes aesthetic-only decorating consultants, increasing business-model variation within the code.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment census or transaction database segmented by revenue source, customer type, repeat-client share, independence, and normalized EBITDA would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently an external professional service, and firms large enough to reach the LMM EBITDA band are more likely than sole decorators to serve repeat commercial, hospitality, institutional, developer, or affluent-residential referral channels. Ineligible firms remain where revenue is mostly one-off residential decoration, product resale, construction, captive work, or owner-personalized engagements that are not repeat outsourced services.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541410 ($4,053,951,989) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541410 ($18,324,956,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No source directly measures the future operator-required share of interior design demand.
Aesthetic-only residential and decorating work is substantially more exposed to self-service than regulated commercial, healthcare, or institutional work.
Rapid improvement in spatially accurate generative design, automated code checking, and direct-to-consumer procurement could push the value below the range.
o.change_evidence: Customer cohort data from major design platforms and US interior design firms showing five-year substitution, project unbundling, and client migration from full-service firms to self-service AI by project type would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Self-service software and AI can eliminate or unbundle low-complexity mood boards, decorative concepts, product discovery, and early visualization. Most LMM-firm demand should still require an accountable operator because the current service includes client discovery, site-specific measurement, construction documents, code and accessibility judgment, procurement, multidisciplinary coordination, installation oversight, and post-completion verification.

q.bridge: The British Institute of Interior Design measured UK interior designers' charging methods, not US LMM benefit retention: 51% used combinations, 21% fixed fees, 18% hourly rates, and 6% project-cost percentages. I map fixed and percentage-based fees to higher initial retention, hourly billing to lower retention because fewer billable hours can reduce revenue, and hybrid arrangements to the middle, then discount all models for competitive repricing and customer sharing over five years. UK residential-heavy respondents may understate fixed-fee prevalence in US commercial work and make the direction of bias uncertain.
caveats: The pricing survey is from 2019, UK-based, and mostly residential, not US LMM commercial practices.
Pricing-model shares do not directly measure five-year benefit retention.
Procurement markups and pass-through purchases may make receipts an imperfect measure of service economics.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of US 541410 contracts and project P&Ls showing fee model, procurement markup, renewal repricing, realized hours, and gross margin before and after AI deployment would materially move retention.
q.rationale: A mixed pricing market permits meaningful capture where firms deliver a defined scope for a fixed fee or earn procurement and project-cost-linked revenue. Capture is weaker in hourly phases because productivity can shrink billable hours, and renewals and competitive bids should share part of the benefit with customers. The result is moderate retention rather than either full pass-through or full capture.

rho.bridge: A 2025 Mattoboard survey reported regular AI use among 328 interior-design professionals in more than 70 countries, while Autodesk surveyed the broader AECO sector. I translate tool use and sector sentiment into the share of the remaining exposed opportunity operationalized within five years, discounting for experimentation, workflow integration, quality control, client acceptance, and the survey's non-US and vendor-selected population. The 82% user rate likely overstates mature implementation, while current use concentrated in early ideation likely leaves room for deeper workflow capture.
caveats: Regular tool use is not equivalent to production-grade implementation or realized labor avoidance.
The interior-design survey is small, global, and associated with a 3D-design platform.
Autodesk's AECO population is much broader than independent US LMM interior design firms and overrepresents Autodesk users.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal US ASID or firm-software survey reporting production hours, rework rates, and headcount avoided by workflow before and after AI deployment would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: AI is already used for concept sketches, mood boards, layouts, early renderings, sourcing, bookkeeping, and deadline management, which lowers behavioral adoption barriers. Five-year implementation should spread across these bounded workflows, but construction documents, code compliance, material accuracy, intellectual-property concerns, and integration with CAD/BIM and project controls constrain conversion of exposure into dependable labor avoidance.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I use that as an upper anchor and discount gifts, internal transfers, unexecuted intentions, and firms whose client relationships or creative identity are too owner-dependent for a qualifying control sale. LMM scale and employees improve transferability relative to nonemployers, but the creative-services dependence on principals may make the industry less transferable than the all-employer average.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses rather than interior design or the LMM band.
Survey intentions combine sales with other transfers and do not establish completed qualifying control transactions.
Founder dependence may reduce sale completion or require long earn-outs.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, BizBuySell, or broker data isolating US interior-design control transactions and failed sale processes by eligible firm count over five years would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Employer firms have materially more transferable operations than nonemployers, and owner succession creates a plausible five-year supply of control opportunities. However, a stated plan is not a completed transaction, and interior design practices can depend heavily on founder taste, reputation, and client relationships, so the base qualifying-transfer probability is below the survey's 22% plan rate.

records.principal_driver: Document-, image-, research-, and coordination-heavy workflows offer substantial bounded AI leverage while regulated, site-specific delivery preserves the operator role.
records.principal_weakness: Founder dependence and mixed hourly or pass-through economics can limit both firm transferability and retained productivity benefit.

sources.supports: Defines 541410 as planning, designing, and administering interior-space projects across commercial, hospitality, healthcare, institutional, residential, and aesthetic-only decorating contexts.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual: NAICS 541410 Interior Design Services

sources.supports: Provides the four-digit occupation mix, including 28,420 interior designers and 15,730 office and administrative support workers, used as an occupation-mix proxy for AI exposure.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Specialized Design Services

sources.supports: Documents interior-designer tasks including CAD construction documents, rendering, research and estimating, client advising, code compliance, professional coordination, purchasing, and on-site inspection.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Interior Designers (27-1025.00)

sources.supports: Reports a 2025 global survey in which 82% of 328 interior-design professionals used AI regularly and describes use in ideation, visualization, sourcing, bookkeeping, and deadline management, with important quality and human-judgment limits.
sources.title: Concepts, 'tedious tasks' and mood boards: Here's how interior designers are using AI

sources.supports: Provides broader AECO evidence on AI sentiment, skilled-talent constraints, cost pressure, and planned investment relevant to five-year implementation.
sources.title: Autodesk 2025 State of Design & Make: Industry Insights

sources.supports: Finds 22% of US employer-business owners planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years and describes transferability differences between employer and nonemployer businesses.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports mixed, fixed-fee, hourly, and project-cost-percentage charging methods and explains their economics, used as a proxy for benefit retention under mixed pricing.

sources.supports: Projects NAICS 541400 output from $57.5 billion to $70.3 billion in chained 2017 dollars, a 2.0% annual increase, used as a broad demand-growth proxy.
sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024-2034

sources.supports: Projects 3% interior-designer employment growth from 2024 to 2034, cites renovation, code, and accessibility demand, and describes operator-required duties and industry settings.

caveats: a: The occupation evidence spans captive and outsourced centers and does not provide an industry wage-weighted task distribution.
a: Inbound customer service and outbound telemarketing have materially different task complexity and regulatory constraints.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input combines different vintages and does not isolate controllable agent labor.
rho: Gallup is economy-wide and does not measure production deployment in outsourced contact centers.
rho: FTC requirements apply to covered telemarketing, while inbound service and many business-to-business contacts differ.
rho: The estimate assumes continued model and telephony improvement without assuming autonomous use is accepted for every client or contact.
e: No cited source measures outsourced-service eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm population.
e: The NAICS definition explicitly includes centers serving other establishments of the same enterprise.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with offshore labor, subcontractors, performance marketing economics, or atypical owner compensation.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and includes employer businesses outside the lower-middle market.
s5: The survey measures sale or transfer intentions rather than completed qualifying control events.
s5: No cited source measures NAICS 561422 deal frequency.
q: No public source measures the industry's contract mix or retained share of automation benefits.
q: Retention differs sharply between seat-based commodity service, outcome-priced sales, and complex regulated support.
q: This range excludes implementation friction, demand change, and self-service displacement, which are represented elsewhere.
d5: Employment and occupation projections are not constant-price measures of outsourced interaction volume.
d5: The occupation data include captive contact centers outside the screened lens.
d5: Outbound telemarketing and inbound customer service have different demand trajectories that a single range can only approximate.
d5: Regulatory changes, consumer behavior, offshoring, and client-channel strategy can move faster than historical employment.
o: The evidence describes occupations and broad customer-service demand rather than direct buyer substitution in outsourced NAICS 561422 firms.
o: Operator requirement varies sharply by inbound versus outbound work, sector, complexity, contact value, and regulation.
o: This primitive concerns service displacement and self-service rather than internal labor efficiency.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers outsource campaigns or ongoing service queues and commonly pay per agent hour, productive hour, contact, seat, transaction, sale, qualified lead, outcome, dedicated team, or fixed monthly capacity, often under service-level and quality commitments.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house contact centers, telephone answering and message-only services classified in 561421, software-only contact-center platforms, market-research polling, owned-product retailers or sellers, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly operating outsourced inbound or outbound contact centers for external customers, including customer information and assistance, order taking, complaint handling, retention, lead qualification, product promotion, telesales, and contribution solicitation across telephone, email, chat, and related communication modes.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET describes U.S. telemarketer and customer-service-representative tasks, and BLS provides employment projections for both occupations. Transformation: scripted pitches, routine product information, order entry, interaction records, follow-up, account changes, complaint triage, and standard resolution are mapped to the wage-bearing agent and support work of NAICS 561422 firms. Direction of error: the occupations span captive centers and may overstate exposure for complex sales, regulated service, or emotionally difficult calls, but may understate it because quality monitoring, summarization, coaching, scheduling, and back-office follow-up also contain automatable tasks.
caveats: The occupation evidence spans captive and outsourced centers and does not provide an industry wage-weighted task distribution.
Inbound customer service and outbound telemarketing have materially different task complexity and regulatory constraints.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input combines different vintages and does not isolate controllable agent labor.
a.change_evidence: A wage-linked time study from representative NAICS 561422 firms separating outbound sales, inbound service, order entry, complaints, retention, quality assurance, training, workforce management, back-office work, and already-automated tasks would materially refine the exposed share.
a.rationale: Contact-center work is heavily digital, recorded, scripted, and repetitive, giving language and speech systems broad opportunity across agent assistance and partial automation. Negotiation, persuasion, unusual complaints, complex eligibility or billing, vulnerable customers, escalation, and client accountability remain harder to substitute.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports recent payroll employment for NAICS 561422, projects a 22.1 percent ten-year decline for telemarketers and a 5.5 percent decline for customer-service representatives, and attributes lower customer-service demand partly to automation and self-service. Transformation: the mixed occupation outlook and recent industry employment contraction are translated into a year-five constant-price demand ratio for the current contact-center basket, with labor declines moderated because productivity can reduce employment faster than handled contacts. Direction of error: employment may understate service quantity where automation increases contacts per worker, but may overstate outsourced demand where digital channels, regulation, or customer self-service eliminate contacts or campaigns.
caveats: Employment and occupation projections are not constant-price measures of outsourced interaction volume.
The occupation data include captive contact centers outside the screened lens.
Outbound telemarketing and inbound customer service have different demand trajectories that a single range can only approximate.
Regulatory changes, consumer behavior, offshoring, and client-channel strategy can move faster than historical employment.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar series of NAICS 561422 receipts, billable interactions, client programs, renewal rates, churn, bookings, and backlog segmented by outbound sales, inbound service, sector, channel, onshore/offshore delivery, and pricing model would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: Customer assistance, order handling, retention, and complex issue resolution remain durable business functions, while outbound consumer telemarketing faces regulatory and acceptance pressure. Self-service, apps, web channels, and automation reduce simple contacts; recent employment and mixed occupational outlooks support contraction in the current service basket.

caveats: No cited source measures outsourced-service eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm population.
The NAICS definition explicitly includes centers serving other establishments of the same enterprise.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with offshore labor, subcontractors, performance marketing economics, or atypical owner compensation.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census using business databases, client contracts, websites, regulatory registrations, and management interviews to tag external revenue, captive status, recurring programs, campaign duration, customer concentration, owner dependence, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The official code includes both outsourced centers serving clients and captive centers serving other establishments of the same enterprise. Most independent firms of sufficient scale should fit the recurring outsourced-service lens, but captive operations, campaign-only businesses, extreme customer concentration, nontransferable client approvals, and firms functioning primarily as software or staffing intermediaries reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561422 ($17,861,129,852) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561422 ($32,736,522,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $84,840 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561422); class <5: 926 firms, avg revenue/firm $563,971 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $88,261 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 247 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,328,636 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $207,931 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 210 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,113,986 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $487,339 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 400 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,157,090 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $807,085 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 201 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,411,512 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,820,402 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 315 firms, avg revenue/firm $77,024,206 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $12,054,288 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 201

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET describes scripted sales, routine product information, order entry, records, complaints, and escalation; BLS states that self-service and automation reduce simple customer-service interactions while complex inquiries continue to use representatives. Transformation: routine contacts are treated as self-serviceable or software-supplied, while persuasion, negotiation, emotionally difficult conversations, complex billing or eligibility, regulated disclosures, exceptions, and accountable resolution retain an operator requirement. Direction of error: current occupation evidence may overstate human need as models improve, but may understate it where customers value empathy, brand representation, compliance evidence, conversion skill, and reliable escalation.
caveats: The evidence describes occupations and broad customer-service demand rather than direct buyer substitution in outsourced NAICS 561422 firms.
Operator requirement varies sharply by inbound versus outbound work, sector, complexity, contact value, and regulation.
This primitive concerns service displacement and self-service rather than internal labor efficiency.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and queue-level win-loss data showing automated containment, channel shift, insourcing, canceled campaigns, software replacement, conversion, repeat contacts, escalation, and which interactions remain outsourced to human-backed operators would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Buyers can increasingly self-serve routine status, FAQs, basic orders, simple changes, and scripted outreach through apps, chat, email, and voice automation. An accountable operator remains valuable for difficult complaints, complex transactions, retention, persuasion, regulated interactions, vulnerable customers, and multistep resolution across client systems.

caveats: No public source measures the industry's contract mix or retained share of automation benefits.
Retention differs sharply between seat-based commodity service, outcome-priced sales, and complex regulated support.
This range excludes implementation friction, demand change, and self-service displacement, which are represented elsewhere.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level diligence on billing units, minimum volumes, outcome fees, gainsharing, staffing commitments, service-level penalties, technology pass-through, renewal cadence, repricing, offshoring, and realized gross margin after automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Outcome, transaction, fixed-capacity, and service-level pricing can retain part of productivity gains, and automation can add scalable capacity. Per-agent and per-hour billing directly monetizes labor, transparent handle-time metrics invite sharing, clients reprice or insource at renewal, and software-first competitors compress unit prices.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup measures U.S. employee AI use and reported workflow effects, O*NET documents digitally mediated contact-center tasks, and the FTC specifies compliance duties for covered telemarketing campaigns. Transformation: broad adoption is adjusted upward for mature call recording, CRM, scripting, knowledge-base, routing, and quality systems but downward for integration, latency, model error, consent, disclosure, Do Not Call controls, client restrictions, and human escalation. Direction of error: economy-wide adoption may understate implementation in large digital workflows, while self-reported use and task automation may overstate reliable end-to-end deployment after compliance and quality review.
caveats: Gallup is economy-wide and does not measure production deployment in outsourced contact centers.
FTC requirements apply to covered telemarketing, while inbound service and many business-to-business contacts differ.
The estimate assumes continued model and telephony improvement without assuming autonomous use is accepted for every client or contact.
rho.change_evidence: Client- and queue-level deployment data from contact-center operators covering agent-assist use, automated containment, transfer accuracy, handle time, after-call work, quality scores, conversion, complaints, compliance exceptions, client opt-outs, and realized staffing would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Agent-assist tools for retrieval, drafting, summarization, coaching, and after-call work can be implemented incrementally, while automated interactions can absorb routine contacts. Production implementation remains constrained by fragmented client systems, data security, regulatory obligations, varied accents and emotions, and the need to transfer difficult contacts without service failure.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Transformation: that intention rate is discounted for plans that do not close, non-control gifts or internal transfers, and contact centers lacking transferable contracts, client consents, management, or compliant operating systems. Direction of error: the broad employer sample may overstate qualifying events, while it may understate sale readiness among scaled recurring-revenue centers with distributed teams and documented processes.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes employer businesses outside the lower-middle market.
The survey measures sale or transfer intentions rather than completed qualifying control events.
No cited source measures NAICS 561422 deal frequency.
s5.change_evidence: Completed control-deal counts and eligible-firm denominators for outsourced contact centers from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, Capstone Partners, lender records, and specialist M&A advisers would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Contact centers can be transferable when client contracts, workforce management, technology, compliance, and middle management are institutionalized. Stated owner plans still exceed completed deals, and concentration, performance volatility, regulatory exposure, offshoring, or client change-of-control rights can block transactions.

records.principal_driver: Recorded, scripted, repetitive, and measurable contact workflows offer a large implementable AI labor opportunity across agents and support functions.
records.principal_weakness: Automation and self-service directly reduce billable contacts and seats while transparent labor-based contracts and renewal pricing constrain durable retention.

caveats: The numeric bases for a, rho, d5, and o are synthesized from occupation task lists, broad adoption evidence, employment trends, and regulatory context rather than direct measurements from outsourced NAICS 561422 operators; the PROXY labels, bridges, and wide ranges disclose this limitation.

Automation and self-service appear in both d5's demand-ratio rationale and o's operator-required-share rationale, creating some overlap within D. Removing that overlap would raise D and would not change the PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action because F remains below 6 and estimates remain material.
The recent NAICS payroll contraction in S6 measures employment rather than constant-price service quantity and is extrapolated into a five-year demand proxy; this limitation is expressly disclosed.
The s5 anchor is a cross-industry owner-intention survey rather than completed qualifying contact-center transactions; the record discounts the 22% intention rate and labels the result PROXY.

Eligibility e, retained benefit q, and the injected firm count n remain estimate-driven, so the record appropriately stays MODEL_CONDITIONED and directs users to validate assumptions rather than advance directly.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS Manual defines 561422 as contact centers initiating or receiving communications by telephone, facsimile, email, or other modes for promotion, orders, contributions, information, or assistance, on behalf of clients or related establishments.
O*NET lists telemarketer tasks including solicitation, scripted pitches, customer questions, computerized order entry, contact records, follow-up, and script adjustment.

O*NET lists customer-service tasks including information and order handling, account changes, complaint and billing resolution, interaction records, follow-up, and escalation of unresolved grievances.
The BLS 2024-2034 table reports projected employment changes of -22.1% for telemarketers and -5.5% for customer service representatives.
The BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook attributes reduced demand for simple customer-service work to automation and self-service while noting continuing use of representatives for complex inquiries.
BLS Table B-1a reports seasonally adjusted NAICS 561422 employment of 294.0 thousand in June 2025 and 276.2 thousand in May 2026.

FTC guidance documents prompt disclosures, Do Not Call procedures, calling-hour restrictions, prerecorded-message and consent requirements, recordkeeping, monitoring, and exemptions for covered telemarketing.
Gallup's February 2026 survey reports U.S. employee and organizational AI adoption, task-level productivity gains, staffing changes, and limited organization-wide workflow transformation.
Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public their business within five years and provides the 2024 survey population and methods.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include homebuyers and sellers, property owners and managers, lenders, insurers, developers, and public or institutional clients. Residential work is generally sold as a fixed inspection fee adjusted for property size and add-on tests; larger commercial and programmatic work may use project fees, unit-price schedules, or recurring service contracts. Repeat business often comes through referral channels and portfolio or construction relationships even when each property produces a separate report.
lens.excluded_activities: Government code-enforcement departments, captive internal inspection units, pest and termite inspection, hazardous-material consulting, real-estate appraisal, engineering-led design services, testing laboratories, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Private lower-middle-market firms whose primary service is outsourced inspection of residential or commercial buildings, including home inspections, property-condition inspections, and repeat inspection programs for external customers.

a.bridge: O*NET and BLS describe the broader construction-and-building-inspector occupation rather than the wage-weighted task mix of private NAICS 541350 firms. I mapped plan review, code research, report drafting, photograph organization, scheduling, records, and routine customer communication to current AI exposure, while treating site access, measurement, equipment use, and physical defect judgment as largely unexposed. Task importance is not time or wage share, so it can overstate highly important desk tasks; omission of dispatch, sales, and administrative occupations can understate firmwide exposure.
caveats: The occupational sources include government and construction-sector inspectors outside the private-industry lens.
Task importance ratings do not measure hours or compensation weights.
Current inspection software adoption is not measured, so the not-yet-automated condition is judged rather than observed.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from multi-inspector home and commercial inspection firms, split by field inspection, travel, report production, sales, scheduling, and administration, combined with payroll by role and observed AI-assisted cycle-time changes.
a.rationale: The work contains a substantial document and information layer that AI can assist or partially substitute, but BLS says inspectors spend most of their time examining worksites and O*NET assigns core importance to physical inspection, monitoring, measuring, and test-equipment use. The base therefore places exposed labor below half of current wage-weighted work.

caveats: Occupational employment is not service demand and includes government inspectors outside the lens.
Monthly housing indicators are noisy and cover only part of the industry's demand basket.
The base does not assume that a cyclical housing recovery will occur on a specific schedule.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast that combines existing-home transactions, housing starts and completions, commercial construction, renovation permits, insurer inspection mandates, and actual inspection counts from major scheduling platforms, separated by residential and commercial service lines.
d5.rationale: Inspection demand is tied to property transactions, construction, renovation, lender and insurer requirements, and ongoing safety concerns. BLS projects nearly flat inspector employment while explicitly noting that safety and construction quality support demand and remote inspection restrains staffing. Current Census housing data are volatile, with weaker new-home sales alongside a recent rebound in starts, so the base assumes roughly stable constant-quality service volume and the range preserves substantial cycle risk.

caveats: The supplied firm count is an estimate bridged from establishment-size data and an industry margin, not a verified buyer universe.
The NAICS definition excludes government code-enforcement establishments, but firm-level miscoding and mixed operations remain possible.
Eligibility of owner-led inspection practices depends on whether licenses, referral relationships, and quality controls survive a change of control.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the supplied 40 LMM candidates using websites, service mix, customer concentration, ownership, license portability, recurring-contract share, and normalized EBITDA quality.
e.rationale: The Census definition is centered on external building-condition inspection and reporting, which fits the default outsourced-service lens. Most private firms in the supplied LMM count should therefore qualify. The discount covers mixed-service firms, customer concentration, captive-like arrangements, weak recurring or referral channels, and firms whose owner dependence or licensing structure makes control ownership impractical.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541350 ($1,864,147,476) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541350 ($3,995,784,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $146,226 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541350); class <5: 6,523 firms, avg revenue/firm $193,991 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $30,360 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 612 firms, avg revenue/firm $827,992 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $129,581 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 305 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,798,961 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $281,537 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 143 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,816,294 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $910,250 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,547,304 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,902,653 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 17 firms, avg revenue/firm $24,508,765 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,835,622 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 40

o.bridge: BLS and O*NET measure occupational duties, work setting, and licensing rather than the year-five share of industry quantity requiring a lens operator. I treat physical site examination, access, measurement, equipment use, signed judgment, and regulated accountability as operator-required; I treat remote triage, automated plan checks, and limited self-service as potential quantity removal. Because occupational task evidence does not reveal customer willingness to self-serve, the bridge may overstate operator need if computer vision and remote capture mature quickly, but government-focused remote-inspection evidence may also overstate elimination for private condition reports.
caveats: Requirements differ by state, customer, and inspection specialty.
Remote inspection can preserve an accountable operator while changing travel and field labor, so it should not automatically be counted as service elimination.
Rapid improvement in sensor integration or accepted self-capture would lower operator need.
o.change_evidence: State-by-state regulatory analysis plus insurer, lender, real-estate buyer, and municipal acceptance data for unattended computer-vision inspections, together with the share of paid inspections replaced by self-capture or software-only reports.
o.rationale: Most service quantity should still require an accountable inspection firm because the product depends on physical access, measurements, trade knowledge, defect judgment, and often licensure or certification. AI can reduce labor per report and automate narrow checks, but current tools emphasize assistance, source verification, and faster review rather than autonomous acceptance of property condition and liability.

caveats: No representative billing-mix or pass-through study for NAICS 541350 was found.
Residential one-off work and negotiated portfolio contracts may retain benefits differently.
The estimate excludes volume effects, which belong in demand and operator-need assumptions.
q.change_evidence: Contract and invoice samples from residential, commercial, lender, insurer, and public-program customers showing fixed-fee, hourly, cost-plus, and unit-price revenue shares, repricing cadence, discount history, and margin changes after workflow automation.
q.rationale: A building inspection produces a defined condition report, and residential customers typically pay the inspection cost, with price varying by property size and add-on tests. Output-based fees let an operator retain much of a labor saving during a contract period. Local price comparison, realtor referral competition, renewal negotiation on portfolio or public work, and pressure to spend savings on faster turnaround or stronger quality should share part of the benefit over five years.

rho.bridge: ICC product evidence and an AI plan-checking pilot in 11 cities show technical feasibility in code research and compliance review, not five-year implementation by private building-inspection firms. I translate demonstrated tools into the share of exposed workflows likely to reach routine production, then discount for integration, local-code variation, verification, training, liability, and change management. Vendor and partner claims likely bias the proxy upward, while their focus on permitting may omit easier private-firm reporting and scheduling uses and bias it downward.
caveats: The strongest implementation examples concern code officials and permitting rather than buyer-paid private inspections.
The ICC sources are product and partnership materials, not independent adoption studies.
Legal responsibility and insurer acceptance may vary materially by state and inspection type.
rho.change_evidence: Production data from inspection-management vendors or a cohort of private firms showing active usage, error and rework rates, report cycle time, labor hours per inspection, and retained staffing changes after at least one year.
rho.rationale: Purpose-built code tools already provide source-linked answers, and the ICC collaboration demonstrates AI-assisted plan checking in real jurisdictions. Implementation should be meaningful in research, drafting, and quality control, but mandatory verification, local code variation, fragmented systems, and professional liability prevent full conversion of exposed work.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sell, transfer, or public-offering plans among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers in building inspection. I use the 22 percent employer intention rate as an upper anchor, reduce it for gifts, internal transfers, abandoned plans, and businesses that are too owner-dependent to transfer, then allow some upward offset because LMM firms are larger and more operationally transferable than the typical surveyed employer. The intention-to-close gap makes the proxy more likely to overstate completed market transactions than understate them.
caveats: The Gallup sample spans all employer industries and is not restricted to the LMM band.
Survey intentions may not be realized and include transfers that would not qualify as an outside control event.
No direct industry deal-rate series was found.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed NAICS 541350 transaction records from Axial, GF Data, PitchBook, BizBuySell, SBA change-of-ownership loans, and industry brokers, with a verified denominator of eligible private firms and separation of internal succession from outside control sales.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners are more transfer-oriented than nonemployers, and owner aging supplies a credible succession catalyst. Still, a stated plan is not a completed third-party control transaction, and personal reputation, licensure, and referral dependence can make inspection firms harder to transfer.

records.principal_driver: A repeatable report-and-code workflow sits beside indispensable field inspection, creating room for AI-enabled throughput without requiring customers to abandon the trusted operator.
records.principal_weakness: The available-firm denominator and five-year control-transfer rate are modeled from broad proxies, while housing cyclicality and owner-dependent licenses or referral relationships can sharply reduce realizable opportunity.

sources.supports: BLS describes field and office duties, licensing, physical requirements, work settings, the 2024 employment base, nearly flat projected employment, replacement openings, remote-inspection pressure, and safety-supported demand.

sources.supports: The 2026 occupation profile details the importance of plan review, approval, physical inspection, site monitoring, measurement, recordkeeping, customer communication, and test-equipment tasks.

sources.supports: The official NAICS definition centers 541350 on building-condition evaluation and reporting for transaction participants, includes home inspection and inspection bureaus, and identifies excluded pest, hazardous-material, appraisal, and government-enforcement activities.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual

sources.supports: ICC describes an expert-trained AI tool for code research and plan-review questions, direct links for source verification, state and model-code coverage, and the continuing need to check the official code text.
sources.title: Digital Codes Premium AI Navigator

sources.supports: The July 2025 release describes AI-powered compliance checking, a pilot with 11 U.S. cities, and reported improvements in plan-review speed and consistency, while remaining partnership evidence rather than an independent controlled study.
sources.title: International Code Council Collaborates with Archistar to Modernize Permitting and Accelerate Housing Development

sources.supports: Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned a sale, transfer, or public offering within five years and documents older ownership, transfer intentions, and survey methods from fall 2024.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: NAR describes the building systems examined, buyer responsibility for inspection cost, price variation with property size and extra tests, and the role of the inspector's report in a home transaction.
sources.title: Consumer Guide: Home Inspections

sources.supports: The June 2026 Census and HUD release reports weaker new single-family home sales than the prior month and prior year, illustrating current transaction cyclicality and statistical uncertainty.

caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix or task-level payroll study was located.
a: O*NET describes one occupation and does not capture every administrative, management, or sales role in an agency.
a: Reported AI use does not measure hours avoided, and existing online booking and reservation systems have already automated part of the task base.
rho: The Travel Weekly sample is self-selected, not statistically weighted, and includes many home-based advisors outside the target firm band.
rho: Enterprise travel-management technology may not be affordable or transferable to lower-middle-market agencies.
rho: Use of an AI platform can be limited to marketing and does not establish end-to-end workflow implementation.
e: Industry classification establishes agency activity but not repeat revenue, independence from an owner, or transferability.
e: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and agency accounting can distinguish poorly between commissions, fees, and gross travel value.
e: Home-based contractor networks and host agencies complicate the boundary between a firm, an establishment, and an advisor book.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed qualifying control transfers.
s5: Travel Weekly's advisor age and role profile is self-selected and not a firm-level ownership census.
s5: Publicly reported acquisitions skew toward larger agencies and do not provide an eligible-firm denominator.
q: No source directly measures the five-year share of AI-derived gross benefit retained by lower-middle-market agencies.
q: Amex GBT and Booking Holdings are far larger and more technology-intensive than the lens.
q: Commission economics vary materially across air, hotel, cruise, package, corporate, and custom-itinerary work.
d5: No six-digit constant-price demand forecast was located.
d5: The 561500 output forecast includes tour operators and reservation services with different digital economics.
d5: Employment is not constant-quality service quantity, and travel demand is unusually exposed to recessions, health events, and geopolitics.
o: Large-platform risk disclosures do not measure actual displacement of independent agencies.
o: Human support may be supplied by an online platform, carrier, or client travel team rather than a lens firm.
o: The operator-required share differs sharply between simple point-to-point travel and complex or disrupted itineraries.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Leisure travelers and business accounts buy advice, itinerary planning, booking, ticketing, changes, disruption support, and travel-program services. Agencies receive supplier commissions and volume-linked payments, client transaction or planning fees, management fees, retainers, subscriptions, and fixed fees for selected professional services.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal travel desks, shells, non-control financing vehicles, tour operators classified in 561520, standalone reservation services classified elsewhere, pure software vendors without an accountable agency service, and agencies without recurring or repeat customer relationships.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market travel agencies repeatedly arranging and selling business or leisure transportation, accommodation, cruises, excursions, and related travel services for external customers, including agencies serving repeat leisure clients and contracted corporate accounts.

a.bridge: O*NET and BLS describe the travel-agent occupation rather than the wage-weighted staffing of NAICS 561510, and Travel Weekly surveys individual advisors rather than payroll. I mapped fare and destination research, cost comparison, itinerary drafting, routine booking and changes, record maintenance, payment processing, customer messages, and marketing production to exposed work, then reduced exposure for needs discovery, persuasion, supplier negotiation, exception handling, duty of care, and firsthand destination judgment. This may overstate exposure where lower-middle-market firms concentrate payroll in senior relationship advisors and understate it where coordinators and back-office staff dominate.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-wage matrix or task-level payroll study was located.
O*NET describes one occupation and does not capture every administrative, management, or sales role in an agency.
Reported AI use does not measure hours avoided, and existing online booking and reservation systems have already automated part of the task base.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study of independent U.S. travel agencies, split by leisure advisors, corporate agents, reservation and ticketing staff, support staff, sales, and owners, with each task coded for current automation and demonstrated AI substitution.
a.rationale: Core travel-agent work contains substantial computer-mediated information retrieval, calculation, itinerary construction, reservation, transaction, and recordkeeping activity. Current industry use of AI for content and itinerary support and large-agency deployment of natural-language assistants and recommendations confirm technical exposure. Client conversation, sales, exactness, complex-trip judgment, and disruption resolution preserve a large human component, while existing reservation systems mean some routine automation is already embedded rather than still available.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broader NAICS 561500, which includes travel agencies, tour operators, and other reservation services, and employment for travel agents across employer and self-employed settings. I use the broader industry's 2.5% annual real-output growth as an upper directional anchor and the occupation's 2% decade employment growth as a lower signal, then temper the five-year center for cyclical travel budgets and substitution within the current agency service basket. The broader output series may overstate 561510 demand if online reservation services grow faster, while employment may understate service quantity when productivity rises.
caveats: No six-digit constant-price demand forecast was located.
The 561500 output forecast includes tour operators and reservation services with different digital economics.
Employment is not constant-quality service quantity, and travel demand is unusually exposed to recessions, health events, and geopolitics.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit constant-price revenue and transaction indexes for 561510 from Census or BEA, combined with ARC ticket counts, agency booking volumes, corporate travel budgets, leisure sales, cancellations, and constant-scope client fees through 2031.
d5.rationale: BLS projects broader travel-arrangement and reservation-services real output from 83.7 billion chained 2017 dollars in 2024 to 107.2 billion in 2034, while travel-agent employment is projected to grow 2% over the decade. BLS expects gradual growth for personalized expertise but says online self-booking limits demand. A modestly higher year-five service quantity is defensible, with a low case allowing cyclical contraction and substitution.

caveats: Industry classification establishes agency activity but not repeat revenue, independence from an owner, or transferability.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and agency accounting can distinguish poorly between commissions, fees, and gross travel value.
Home-based contractor networks and host agencies complicate the boundary between a firm, an establishment, and an advisor book.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level sample from Data Axle or PitchBook matched to agency websites, ARC or IATA accreditation, customer contracts, and revenue records, coding external-service revenue, repeat-client share, owner dependence, pass-through booking value, and software-only models for firms in the frozen EBITDA band.
e.rationale: The Census definition centers on agencies acting for the general public and commercial clients, so most employer firms in the supplied EBITDA band should provide an external service. Eligibility is reduced for businesses dominated by one-off transactions, owner-only books that do not transfer independently, technology-led operations without accountable advisory service, captive relationships, and firms whose apparent scale reflects pass-through travel value rather than durable service revenue. Repeat leisure customers and contracted business accounts support a high but explicitly estimated eligible share.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $7,729.2M / sectoral output (T30) $36,821.3M, year 2022

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $458,147 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561510); class <5: 6,236 firms, avg revenue/firm $469,518 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $73,480 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 689 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,063,269 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $322,902 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 253 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,174,640 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $653,331 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 202 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,264,381 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,293,376 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $34,659,729 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,424,248 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $558,919,417 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $87,470,889 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 250

o.bridge: BLS describes persistent demand for personalized advice and disruption handling alongside online self-booking pressure, Amex GBT demonstrates a hybrid software-plus-human service model, and Booking Holdings discusses AI agents as a disintermediation threat to online travel platforms. None measures the year-five share of U.S. agency quantity requiring an independent accountable operator. I reduce operator-required quantity for automated research, simple itineraries, routine booking, and client self-service, while preserving complex, group, luxury, corporate-policy, cross-border, and disrupted travel requiring responsibility and intervention. Platform disclosures may overstate disintermediation risk for relationship-led niches and understate it for commodity trips.
caveats: Large-platform risk disclosures do not measure actual displacement of independent agencies.
Human support may be supplied by an online platform, carrier, or client travel team rather than a lens firm.
The operator-required share differs sharply between simple point-to-point travel and complex or disrupted itineraries.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal U.S. client procurement and traveler survey linking 2026 agency-managed trips to 2031 outcomes, classified as operator-led, hybrid self-service, software-only, supplier-direct, or eliminated, with separate results for corporate, complex leisure, group, luxury, and commodity travel.
o.rationale: Simple travel search and booking is already self-served, and AI assistants can extend self-service into comparison, itinerary creation, personalization, and transaction execution. The surviving agency market is more concentrated in advice, negotiated content, complex itineraries, group or corporate requirements, and support when plans change. BLS still expects gradual demand for expertise, and Amex GBT pairs AI efficiencies with a global support team, indicating that software can reduce routine work without removing accountable service altogether.

q.bridge: BLS and WTAAA describe industry commission and professional-fee models, while Amex GBT reports enterprise transaction, supplier, management, subscription, and fixed-fee revenues and Booking Holdings describes price and commission pressure in online travel. I map these structures to lower-middle-market agencies: labor savings are initially retained when supplier commissions, transaction charges, retainers, or fixed fees do not mechanically reimburse cost, then partially shared through fee competition, client renewals, expanded service, and supplier commission pressure. Large-platform scale may overstate retention, while specialized agencies with scarce expertise may retain more than commodity booking shops.
caveats: No source directly measures the five-year share of AI-derived gross benefit retained by lower-middle-market agencies.
Amex GBT and Booking Holdings are far larger and more technology-intensive than the lens.
Commission economics vary materially across air, hotel, cruise, package, corporate, and custom-itinerary work.
q.change_evidence: Anonymized contracts and renewal cohorts from independent leisure and corporate agencies showing commission schedules, fee basis, service-level commitments, automation-driven gross-margin changes, renewal pricing, rebates, win rates, and client sharing over multiple contract cycles.
q.rationale: Agency revenue is usually linked to bookings, supplier commissions, professional fees, or contracted services rather than reimbursed labor cost, so automation does not automatically pass through. Fee adoption gives agencies a direct way to monetize advice, and subscriptions or retainers can preserve savings within a term. Retention is discounted because travelers can compare direct and online prices, corporate clients can renegotiate transaction and management fees, and AI-enabled channels may pressure both customer fees and supplier commissions.

rho.bridge: Travel Weekly measures current AI-tool use among a nonweighted U.S. advisor sample, while Amex GBT demonstrates deployment at a global enterprise far larger than the lens. I translate broad current use, advisor interest in itinerary tools, and proven travel-platform capabilities into five-year implementation of the remaining exposed work, then discount for fragmented systems, supplier-content complexity, accuracy, privacy, training, integration, and human review. The estimate may be high for small agencies with weak technology infrastructure and low for standardized corporate and reservation workflows.
caveats: The Travel Weekly sample is self-selected, not statistically weighted, and includes many home-based advisors outside the target firm band.
Enterprise travel-management technology may not be affordable or transferable to lower-middle-market agencies.
Use of an AI platform can be limited to marketing and does not establish end-to-end workflow implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of U.S. agencies documenting baseline labor hours, deployed AI workflows, system integration costs, booking and servicing error rates, rework, client consent rules, and realized avoided hiring after at least twelve months of production use.
rho.rationale: In the 2025 Travel Weekly survey, 59% of respondents had used AI tools and 42% of agency owners and managers reported using AI for marketing or website content. Amex GBT reports AI-powered recommendations, natural-language assistants, rate optimization, and operational-efficiency initiatives. Adoption has a meaningful base, but the survey also reports accuracy and personal-touch concerns, and travel servicing requires reliable handling of changing fares, rules, documents, cancellations, and supplier systems.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans for U.S. employer-business owners across industries, Travel Weekly surveys a nonrepresentative advisor population, and its M&A review reports selected transactions rather than a denominator-based transfer rate. I center near Gallup's 22% intention rate and modestly increase it for the advisor sample's older ownership profile and visible agency consolidation, while allowing for failed plans, internal or non-control transfers, and owner-dependent client books. This may overstate qualifying transfers when relationships cannot survive the founder and understate them where host agencies and strategic buyers actively consolidate books.
caveats: Owner intentions are not completed qualifying control transfers.
Travel Weekly's advisor age and role profile is self-selected and not a firm-level ownership census.
Publicly reported acquisitions skew toward larger agencies and do not provide an eligible-firm denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year U.S. transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, travel-industry brokers, ARC, and host-agency records for 561510 firms, separating control sales from book purchases, internal reorganizations, minority investments, closures, and failed processes and matching the frozen EBITDA band.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. The 2025 Travel Weekly survey reports that 56% of respondents were older than 55 and 71% identified as owners or managers, although the sample is not representative. Travel Weekly also documents both large and smaller travel-agency acquisitions in 2025, supporting the existence of strategic transfer channels without establishing their frequency.

records.principal_driver: A substantial share of agency production is digital information, itinerary, booking, transaction, and communication work that AI can accelerate within commission and fee-based revenue models.
records.principal_weakness: AI agents, online platforms, and suppliers can offer the same planning and booking convenience directly, shrinking operator-required quantity and pressuring fees and commissions.
