lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers submit text, recordings, templates, or document files and pay by page, word, audio minute, project, hour, service bundle, or recurring volume commitment. Revenue ranges from one-off assignments to repeat workflow outsourcing and master-service arrangements.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal document teams, one-person or purely episodic consumer resume practices without repeat outsourced revenue, court reporting, translation, printing and copying shops, data-processing firms, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced document editing, proofreading, typing, word processing, desktop publishing, transcription, formatting, conversion, and related secretarial document services to external business, professional, nonprofit, and government customers.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET describes the tasks of U.S. word processors and typists, while BLS projects employment for word processors, typists, desktop publishers, proofreaders, and related office occupations. Transformation: the high-frequency tasks of typing from drafts or recordings, spelling and grammar checks, document merging, reformatting, layout adjustment, and file handling are mapped to the wage-bearing production work of NAICS 561410 firms. Direction of error: the occupation proxy may overstate exposure where firms specialize in complex, confidential, or technically exact documents, but it may understate exposure because current occupation labels do not isolate automatable coordination and quality-control subtasks.
caveats: O*NET measures an occupation rather than the industry's full wage-weighted task mix.
BLS occupation projections are economy-wide and reflect employment rather than current task exposure.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input combines different vintages and does not identify controllable labor by workflow.
a.change_evidence: A task-level time and payroll study from a representative sample of NAICS 561410 firms, separating transcription, editing, formatting, desktop publishing, intake, quality review, and already-automated work, would materially refine the wage-weighted exposed share.
a.rationale: The industry's core output is already digital and consists heavily of transcription, text manipulation, proofreading, formatting, and layout work that current language, speech, and document models can accelerate. Client intake, ambiguous-source resolution, technical formatting, exception handling, and final accountability remain less substitutable.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports payroll employment for the 56141 document-preparation industry and projects steep declines for word processors and typists and a smaller decline for desktop publishers. Transformation: recent industry employment contraction and occupation outlooks are translated into a year-five constant-price demand ratio for the current document-preparation service basket, with quantity decline moderated because labor productivity and subcontracting can reduce employment faster than customer document volume. Direction of error: employment may understate service quantity when technology raises output per worker, but it may overstate durable outsourced demand if self-service and software eliminate purchases rather than only labor.
caveats: BLS industry employment is published at five-digit 56141 and is not a constant-price output measure.
Occupation projections span the whole economy and may reflect insourcing, productivity, or reclassification rather than demand for outsourced firms.
The current payroll series shows a sharp recent decline that may include sampling or classification volatility.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-dollar series of NAICS 561410 receipts, project volumes, active customers, renewal rates, and backlog segmented by transcription, editing, formatting, desktop publishing, and document conversion would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: Businesses will continue to produce reports, forms, presentations, transcripts, and other documents, but routine typing, basic proofreading, and simple layout face secular insourcing and software substitution. Recent payroll employment is lower and BLS projects continued contraction in key occupations, supporting a declining base with a wide range.

caveats: No source measures repeat outsourced-service eligibility within the frozen lower-middle-market firm population.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify firms with unusually high subcontractor expense, offshore labor, or owner compensation.
Industry boundaries can blur document preparation with translation, printing, court reporting, virtual assistance, and data processing.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census using business databases, customer websites, contract records, and management interviews to tag customer mix, repeat revenue, owner dependence, service line, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is itself centered on fee-based document preparation, so most firms of sufficient scale should provide an external outsourced service. Eligibility is reduced by businesses dominated by episodic consumer resumes, owner-specific relationships, nonrepeat projects, captive-like arrangements, or operations that cannot transfer without the principal.

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET identifies the core digital manipulation, proofreading, formatting, and clerical tasks of word processors and typists, while BLS projects declining employment and attributes desktop-publisher contraction partly to task redistribution and electronic publishing. Transformation: automatable or self-serviceable routine work is removed from year-five quantity, while complex formatting, confidential handling, client-specific standards, exception resolution, and accountable quality control are retained as operator-required work. Direction of error: the occupation evidence may overstate operator need because software can absorb more end-to-end workflow, but it may understate it where buyers require security, technical precision, rapid exception handling, or contractual accountability.
caveats: The evidence describes occupations and employment, not buyer substitution decisions in NAICS 561410.
Operator requirement varies widely between commodity text conversion and regulated, technical, or highly formatted documents.
This primitive concerns service displacement and self-service rather than internal labor efficiency.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and win-loss data identifying projects canceled, internalized, shifted to software, or retained with outsourced operators, paired with error and rework data by document complexity, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Simple typing, transcription drafts, proofreading, and layout are increasingly self-serviceable. An external operator remains useful where source material is poor, formats are complex, volumes fluctuate, deadlines are tight, or customers need secure handling and defensible final quality.

caveats: No public source measures the industry's billing-method mix or retained share of automation benefits.
Retention likely differs sharply between commodity transcription and high-complexity or confidential document work.
This range excludes implementation friction and volume loss, which are represented in rho, d5, and o.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level diligence across a representative firm sample covering billing units, fixed-fee share, minimum volumes, renewal cadence, price concessions, subcontractor pass-through, change orders, and gross margin before and after automation would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Per-project, per-page, per-word, and per-audio-minute pricing can preserve savings during a contract term, while faster turnaround can create capacity. Hourly work, transparent unit pricing, customer insourcing, competitive bidding, and rapid repricing of standardized services pass much of the benefit back to buyers over five years.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup measures AI use and reported workflow effects among U.S. employees, and O*NET documents that core word-processing work is performed with digital files and software. Transformation: broad adoption is adjusted upward for the technical tractability of digital document workflows but downward for client confidentiality, integration, accuracy review, legacy formats, and change management at lower-middle-market operators. Direction of error: economy-wide adoption may understate implementation in a digitally native service, while self-reported use and productivity can overstate governed production deployment and net labor savings after review.
caveats: Gallup covers all employed U.S. adults rather than document-preparation firms or the lower-middle-market band.
Office and administrative workers in Gallup report less benefit than technical and professional workers, so broad adoption does not guarantee workflow redesign.
The estimate assumes usable speech, language, and document models continue to improve over five years without eliminating review obligations.
rho.change_evidence: Operator interviews and system logs from NAICS 561410 firms showing production AI use, exception rates, review minutes, confidentiality restrictions, error claims, and realized labor hours before and after deployment would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Document intake, transcription drafts, proofreading, reformatting, and first-pass layout can be inserted into existing software workflows with limited physical-process change. Implementation should nevertheless remain below full exposure because sensitive documents, inconsistent source files, customer-specific templates, and error liability require controls and human review.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Transformation: the intention rate is discounted for plans that do not close, gifts or internal transfers that are not qualifying control events, and document-preparation firms whose customer relationships or production knowledge are not transferable. Direction of error: the broad employer sample may overstate qualifying transactions, but it may understate transfer readiness for larger process-driven firms with recurring accounts.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and includes employer businesses outside the lower-middle market.
The survey measures intentions, including transfers that may not satisfy the qualifying-control definition.
No cited source measures completed transactions in NAICS 561410.
s5.change_evidence: Completed control-deal counts and eligible-firm denominators for document preparation, transcription, and desktop-publishing businesses from PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, broker interviews, and lender records would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Employer-owner intentions indicate a meaningful succession pool, but intentions are not completed control deals. Transferability improves when templates, quality procedures, staff, and customer contracts are institutionalized, and deteriorates when the owner personally performs or sells the work.

records.principal_driver: Core production consists of digital transcription, editing, proofreading, formatting, and layout tasks that AI can materially compress.
records.principal_weakness: Customer self-service and rapid repricing can shrink outsourced volume and prevent labor savings from becoming durable operator cash flow.

a: Exposure varies sharply between static camera-monitoring posts, mobile patrols, armed protection, access control, and event security.
a: The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, creating a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.

rho: Customer ownership of cameras, access systems, and networks can slow contractor-led implementation.
rho: GAO's federal contract-guard findings are a public-sector case and may not generalize to commercial sites.

s5: No industry-specific owner-age or completed-control-deal series was found.
s5: Customer change-of-control rights, licensing, insurance, wage claims, and contract concentration can prevent a planned sale from qualifying.

d5: GAO examines one federal program and does not represent commercial contract demand.
d5: Guard demand varies with office occupancy, retail conditions, events, crime concerns, public budgets, and customer technology adoption.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Commercial property owners, residential communities, healthcare and educational institutions, industrial sites, retailers, event operators, government agencies, and other organizations buy guard hours, staffed posts, patrol routes, screening, or protection under recurring contracts, repeat work orders, or scheduled events, commonly priced per labor hour, post, route, or fixed contract period.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house guards, public police, installation or remote monitoring of electronic security systems classified in NAICS 56162, armored-car services, investigation services, one-off engagements without a repeat service relationship, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced staffed security posts, mobile patrols, access control, visitor screening, parking security, event security, bodyguard, guard-dog, alarm-response, and related on-site protective services to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe the security-guard occupation across outsourced and captive employers rather than a wage-weighted task study of 561612 firms. I map camera and alarm observation, routine access-log review, dispatch assistance, scheduling, patrol documentation, incident-report drafting, and administrative communication to remaining AI exposure, while leaving visible deterrence, physical patrol, identity and bag screening, confrontation, escort, first aid, emergency response, and site-specific judgment largely unexposed. Because 59% of guards are in investigation, guard, and armored-car services, the occupation is directionally close; inclusion of captive guards may bias the mapping either way, and already installed video and access-control systems may overstate not-yet-automated opportunity.
caveats: The occupation includes guards employed directly by customers and workers in adjacent investigation and armored-car activities.
No source measures the current automation baseline or wage-weighted task mix of lower-middle-market contract-guard firms.
Exposure varies sharply between static camera-monitoring posts, mobile patrols, armed protection, access control, and event security.
The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages and 2022 receipts, creating a stated vintage mismatch outside this primitive.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study across contract-guard firms that separates already automated monitoring from patrol, access control, screening, incident response, supervision, scheduling, dispatch, reporting, and administration would materially change this value.
a.rationale: AI video analytics, alarm triage, scheduling, dispatch support, and report generation can reduce monitoring and administrative hours or allow each supervisor and remote operator to cover more sites. Most paid guard time still purchases physical presence, deterrence, patrol, access intervention, emergency response, and accountable action, keeping the exposed share below one-third at base.

d5.bridge: BLS projects essentially flat security-guard employment from 2024 to 2034, says guards remain needed because of crime and vandalism concerns, and says AI-enabled remote monitoring may limit employment. GAO documents continuing reliance on 13,000 contract guards at federal facilities and operational harm from shortages. I translate these occupation and federal-site indicators into roughly stable five-year constant-price demand for the current outsourced service basket, allowing modest growth from safety concerns and outsourcing and downside from technology and customer budget pressure. Employment can understate service quantity when productivity rises and the federal case may overstate public-sector persistence, so the net direction is uncertain.
caveats: BLS forecasts occupation employment across outsourced and captive employers, not purchased 561612 service quantity.
GAO examines one federal program and does not represent commercial contract demand.
Guard demand varies with office occupancy, retail conditions, events, crime concerns, public budgets, and customer technology adoption.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-price US contract-guard revenue, staffed-post counts, billable hours, patrol stops, customer security budgets, contract awards, and insourcing or technology-substitution losses, segmented by end market, would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: Organizations continue to need protection, access control, screening, patrol, and emergency response across occupied facilities. Demand is mature rather than rapidly growing, and remote monitoring can reduce staffed hours at low-risk posts, but replacement needs, guard shortages, and the physical-response requirement support a stable core.

caveats: The Census definition does not measure recurring revenue, contract transferability, or the eligible share of LMM firms.
Special-event and bodyguard work may be repeat business but can be materially less recurring than staffed-site contracts.
The injected count of 625 uses a broad Business and Consumer Services margin bridge that may not reflect thin-margin, wage-intensive guard firms.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 561612 roster matched to tax records and coded for recurring revenue, external-customer mix, contract duration and assignment, ownership independence, normalized EBITDA, licensing, insurance, and customer concentration would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition is already centered on outsourced guard and patrol services, and recurring staffed posts, patrol routes, access control, and scheduled-event relationships fit the default lens. Ineligibility remains for predominantly one-off assignments, firms dependent on a nontransferable principal, captive units misclassified into the code, weakly documented cash businesses, or operations without transferable customer contracts.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: BLS describes physical patrol, access control, deterrence, screening, emergency response, and reporting while explicitly stating that AI-enabled remote monitoring may limit some guard employment. O*NET shows high importance for locking entrances, patrol, alarm response, circulation among people, access authorization, first aid, and emergency calls, and GAO shows federal facilities closing when contract guards were unavailable. I translate those tasks and operating evidence into the share of year-five service quantity still requiring an accountable guard or patrol operator. Public and federal sites may overstate physical-presence requirements, while high-liability commercial sites may similarly require response and accountability.
caveats: Occupational duties do not measure the fraction of contracted service quantity that customers will continue purchasing.
Remote monitoring and automated access control can fully replace some low-risk static posts.
Federal facility evidence may overstate operator requirements relative to commercial sites, while armed and high-risk assignments may require more.
o.change_evidence: Customer post orders, insurer and regulator requirements, incident data, contract renewals, and site-level before-and-after studies showing which staffed posts can be removed or self-served without degrading response, deterrence, or liability coverage would materially change this share.
o.rationale: AI can watch more feeds, identify anomalies, automate routine entry, and improve dispatch, but cameras and software cannot generally confront intruders, inspect bags, escort people, administer first aid, manage crowds, secure a failed door, or take responsibility at an incident. Low-risk static observation posts and routine access points face the most elimination or customer self-service.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits for US lower-middle-market guard contractors.
Retention differs between fixed-price outcomes, per-post contracts, and cost-plus or per-hour staffing.
Customer security budgets may convert labor savings into expanded coverage rather than provider margin.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and bid dataset showing pricing unit, wage-escalation and technology clauses, renewal cadence, customer concentration, rebid outcomes, guard-hours by post, overtime, and gross-margin changes after AI or remote-monitoring deployment would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed-price and per-post contracts can retain savings from lower monitoring, scheduling, supervision, overtime, and reporting effort until renewal. Retention is limited because many contracts are transparently priced per guard-hour, wage and benefit costs are passed through, public and enterprise customers rebid frequently, competitors can buy similar technology, and customers may demand that savings fund broader coverage or lower rates. Technology, integration, training, insurance, cyber, and privacy costs absorb additional benefit.

rho.bridge: BLS says AI-integrated remote monitoring may limit some guard employment, and SIA describes AI video systems that recognize people, vehicles, objects, and events and generate alarms while reducing surveillance fatigue. I translate demonstrated technical availability into five-year implementation of the exposed guard workflow, then haircut for installed-camera coverage, integration with customer systems, false alarms, privacy and cyber controls, labor agreements, insurance, customer acceptance, capital, and the need for human response. Technology-industry guidance likely overstates smooth deployment at smaller guard companies, while standardized monitoring and reporting may diffuse faster than assumed.
caveats: BLS provides an occupational outlook, not a measured adoption rate for 561612 firms.
SIA is a security-technology industry association and describes capabilities and safeguards rather than representative realized savings.
Customer ownership of cameras, access systems, and networks can slow contractor-led implementation.
GAO's federal contract-guard findings are a public-sector case and may not generalize to commercial sites.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of independent contract-guard firms reporting site-level AI and remote-monitoring deployment, customer approvals, capital and integration cost, false-alarm rates, guard-hours displaced or avoided, response outcomes, and contract renewal effects would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Video analytics, digital post orders, workforce scheduling, mobile patrol verification, automated logs, and report drafting are operational technologies rather than distant concepts. Implementation remains uneven because guard contractors often operate on customer-owned sites and systems, cannot control camera quality or network access, and must prove reliability in safety-critical, privacy-sensitive settings.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed control acquisitions of security-guard firms. I haircut the reported 22% intention rate for plans that do not close, family or internal transfers, license and insurance requirements, customer consent, contract rebids, labor liabilities, and owner dependence. The broad survey may overstate qualifying external transfers, though guard businesses with management depth and recurring site contracts may be more transferable than personal-service firms.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed transactions.
No industry-specific owner-age or completed-control-deal series was found.
Customer change-of-control rights, licensing, insurance, wage claims, and contract concentration can prevent a planned sale from qualifying.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, security-business brokers, state licensing records, and customer award notices, matched to the eligible firm roster and distinguishing external control deals from internal transfers, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A large employer base and recurring customer contracts create plausible transaction supply, but qualifying control transfers must preserve licenses, bonding, insurance, supervisors, recruiting capability, and customer relationships. Planned transfers materially exceed completed external acquisitions.

records.principal_driver: AI-assisted monitoring, scheduling, dispatch, and reporting can improve coverage per labor hour across a very wage-intensive recurring service.
records.principal_weakness: Most guard value is embodied physical presence sold by the hour, limiting both substitutable task share and the provider's ability to retain savings.

caveats: The base values for a, rho, d5, and o are broad but disclosed proxy transformations from occupation duties, technology capabilities, an employment forecast, and one federal program; none of the sources measures the numeric primitive for lower-middle-market contract-guard firms.
The q value is an honest estimate, but its central billing-mix, rebid, and savings-retention assumptions are not directly evidenced by S2 or S4.

Remote-monitoring substitution appears in both d5 and o, creating some risk of double-counting downward pressure in D; correcting it would raise D, but D is not the weakest factor and would not change the PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
The 0.86 eligible-share estimate is supported only by the industry definition, not firm-level evidence on recurring revenue, transferability, customer concentration, or normalized economics.
The 15% five-year transfer probability is a disclosed haircut from a cross-industry intention survey rather than observed completed external transactions.

The fixed firm-count anchor of 625 depends on a broad sector margin bridge, and the compensation anchor combines different vintages; both limitations are disclosed but remain outside the research-input sensitivity envelope.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS manual defines 561612 as guard and patrol services, gives bodyguard, guard-dog, and parking-security examples, and separately classifies armored-car, security-system, and public-police activities.

BLS supports the cited duties, the 59% employer-industry share, flat 2024-2034 employment projection, continued protection need, and possible employment limitation from AI-enabled remote monitoring. It does not directly measure the numeric exposure, implementation, retention, demand, or operator-share primitives.

O*NET supports the stated mix and importance ordering of physical patrol, access control, alarm response, first aid, reporting, enforcement, emergency calls, screening, inspection, and escort tasks, but provides no wage-weighted task shares for contract-guard firms.

SIA supports AI-video recognition of people, vehicles, objects, and events, alarm generation, reduced surveillance-fatigue error, and the need for privacy and security controls. It is guidance, not evidence of representative adoption, realized savings, or provider retention.

GAO supports the roughly 13,000-guard federal program, physical screening and emergency-response role, failed digitized post oversight, and office closures associated with guard shortages. The evidence is a federal case and does not establish commercial-sector adoption or demand rates.

Gallup supports the fall-2024 finding that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or transfer within five years. It does not measure completed external control transactions or security-guard firms specifically.

caveats: a: No cited source measures the occupation or wage mix within NAICS 621399 firms.
a: Dietitian and acupuncturist tasks do not represent every included modality.
a: The AMA physician survey is a broad adoption proxy and may not represent nonphysician independent practices.
rho: AMA adoption is self-reported and does not verify reliable deployment or labor release.
rho: Physician workflows and resources differ from the nonphysician practitioner mix in this code.
rho: Implementation will vary materially between regulated clinical care and cash-pay wellness practices.
e: The Census definition demonstrates extreme modality heterogeneity but does not measure LMM eligibility.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad provider margin and may misclassify cash-pay, product-heavy, labor-intensive, or owner-compensated practices.
e: Independent legal status does not ensure transferable operations or recurring revenue.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and not restricted to healthcare or the lower-middle market.
s5: The survey measures intended sale or transfer rather than completed qualifying control events.
s5: No cited source measures transaction frequency for NAICS 621399 or its component modalities.
q: No public source measures contract mix or retained automation benefit for this heterogeneous code.
q: Retention may be much higher in differentiated cash-pay practices than in payer-dependent nursing or clinical services.
q: Implementation difficulty and service displacement are excluded here and represented in rho, d5, and o.
d5: Occupation projections are not direct measures of constant-price demand for independent 621399 firms.
d5: Dietitians and nurse midwives represent only part of the code and their shares are unknown.
d5: Discretionary wellness demand, payer coverage, state scope rules, and substitution can differ sharply across modalities.
o: The evidence covers selected occupations rather than the full revenue-weighted 621399 basket.
o: Operator requirement differs substantially between childbirth, respiratory care, dental hygiene, acupuncture, nutrition counseling, and wellness services.
o: Some demand may persist but shift to a different provider type, integrated setting, or software-enabled channel.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Practices receive patient cash, commercial insurance, Medicare or Medicaid where covered, employer or institutional contract, per-visit, per-procedure, package, membership, and care-management payments; economics vary sharply between reimbursed licensed care and cash-pay wellness-oriented services.
lens.excluded_activities: Practices separately classified as physicians, dentists, chiropractors, optometrists, mental health specialists, physical or occupational therapists, speech therapists, audiologists, or podiatrists; hospital-owned or other captive departments; product-only retailers; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market independent practices providing recurring or repeat health services to external patients under NAICS 621399, including offices of acupuncturists, dental hygienists, denturists, dietitians, homeopaths, hypnotherapists, inhalation or respiratory therapists, midwives, naturopaths, and registered or licensed practical nurses.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET describes tasks for dietitians and acupuncturists, two included occupations, while the AMA surveys physicians across specialties about AI use cases; none provides a wage-weighted task distribution for NAICS 621399. Transformation: histories, records, care-plan drafts, education materials, research synthesis, patient messaging, scheduling, coding, billing, and portions of routine counseling are mapped to exposed work, while physical assessment, procedures, childbirth care, individualized judgment, and accountable treatment remain less exposed. Direction of error: these example occupations may overstate exposure for highly procedural nursing, midwifery, dental-hygiene, or respiratory work, but may understate it for nutrition, education, intake, and administrative-heavy practices.
caveats: No cited source measures the occupation or wage mix within NAICS 621399 firms.
Dietitian and acupuncturist tasks do not represent every included modality.
The AMA physician survey is a broad adoption proxy and may not represent nonphysician independent practices.
a.change_evidence: A wage-linked time study across the actual 621399 revenue and employment mix, separating each practitioner type, clinical and administrative occupations, counseling, physical treatment, documentation, billing, and already-automated work, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: The code mixes hands-on treatment with information-rich counseling and extensive records. AI has meaningful reach into preparation, documentation, education, and administration, but the broad practitioner mix prevents a high-confidence wage-weighted estimate.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS projects dietitian and nutritionist employment to grow 6 percent and nurse-midwife employment to grow 11 percent from 2024 to 2034, and attributes demand to preventive care, aging, chronic disease, and healthcare-service needs. Transformation: these included but non-exhaustive occupations are converted into a five-year constant-price demand ratio for the current 621399 basket, with a wide range for the unknown modality weights and for reimbursement, birth, chronic-disease, wellness, and substitution trends. Direction of error: the selected occupations may overstate growth if the code is weighted toward slower cash-pay or marginal modalities, but may understate it if advanced nursing and preventive nutrition account for more scaled-firm activity.
caveats: Occupation projections are not direct measures of constant-price demand for independent 621399 firms.
Dietitians and nurse midwives represent only part of the code and their shares are unknown.
Discretionary wellness demand, payer coverage, state scope rules, and substitution can differ sharply across modalities.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price receipts, visits, procedures, active patients, bookings, referral volumes, payer-authorized utilization, and churn for NAICS 621399 segmented by practitioner type and payer model would materially change this range.
d5.rationale: Representative included occupations have positive outlooks, and aging, chronic disease, preventive care, and expanded nonphysician practice support growth. The code's unknown revenue mix and exposure to discretionary cash-pay spending justify a range spanning contraction through strong expansion.

caveats: The Census definition demonstrates extreme modality heterogeneity but does not measure LMM eligibility.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad provider margin and may misclassify cash-pay, product-heavy, labor-intensive, or owner-compensated practices.
Independent legal status does not ensure transferable operations or recurring revenue.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census using business databases, licensing records, payer enrollment, websites, clinician rosters, revenue mix, recurring-patient metrics, owner production, external customer share, and normalized EBITDA by practitioner modality would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The official code covers independent practitioner offices serving external patients, so many scaled firms meet the service lens. Eligibility is reduced by single-practitioner owner dependence, modality-specific licensing and payer constraints, practices dominated by retail products or nonrecurring packages, facility-controlled arrangements, weak clinical management, and the wide gap between reimbursed medical care and cash-pay wellness models.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621399 ($9,673,437,477) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621399 ($19,100,045,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $152,113 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621399); class <5: 24,879 firms, avg revenue/firm $248,362 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $39,241 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,071 firms, avg revenue/firm $831,844 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $131,431 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,436 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,672,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $264,193 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 784 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,640,608 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $575,216 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 160 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,748,212 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,540,218 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 124 firms, avg revenue/firm $28,639,952 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,525,112 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 284

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET shows that dietitians assess needs, interpret laboratory tests, counsel, personalize plans, and keep histories, while acupuncturists collect histories, diagnose, educate, maintain care records, and perform hands-on treatments; BLS describes nurse midwives performing examinations, prenatal care, delivery, emergency management, and patient education. Transformation: physical assessment, procedures, childbirth, regulated decisions, personalized counseling, and accountable treatment are treated as operator-required, while generic education, plan drafting, monitoring, records, scheduling, and some routine follow-up can be self-served or software-supplied. Direction of error: current task descriptions may overstate human need as remote and AI tools improve, but may understate it where licensure, physical presence, patient trust, adherence coaching, and safety preserve practitioner involvement.
caveats: The evidence covers selected occupations rather than the full revenue-weighted 621399 basket.
Operator requirement differs substantially between childbirth, respiratory care, dental hygiene, acupuncture, nutrition counseling, and wellness services.
Some demand may persist but shift to a different provider type, integrated setting, or software-enabled channel.
o.change_evidence: Buyer and patient studies plus episode-level data by practitioner type showing self-service, software-only completion, channel shift, repeat use, adverse events, payer acceptance, and substitution to other providers would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Most included modalities retain a physical, relational, licensed, or accountable clinical element, but nutrition education and other information-heavy services face meaningful self-service and software substitution. The mixed code requires a wider range than a single clinical specialty.

caveats: No public source measures contract mix or retained automation benefit for this heterogeneous code.
Retention may be much higher in differentiated cash-pay practices than in payer-dependent nursing or clinical services.
Implementation difficulty and service displacement are excluded here and represented in rho, d5, and o.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level diligence segmented by practitioner type on cash versus payer revenue, billing units, fee schedules, capitation, packages and memberships, renewal cadence, technology costs, staffing changes, utilization, price changes, and realized gross margin would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Cash-pay, package, and membership practices may keep a substantial portion of administrative productivity until competition reprices services, while coded reimbursement can also leave short-run savings with the operator. Retention falls where payers set rates, institutional contracts demand sharing, low barriers intensify price competition, software fees absorb savings, or saved clinician time becomes added capacity rather than removable cost.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: the AMA's 2026 U.S. physician survey measures self-reported AI incorporation or awareness and identifies documentation, summarization, privacy, safety, training, and liability considerations. Transformation: adoption momentum is applied to exposed 621399 work over five years, discounted for small-practice software fragmentation, variable EHR use, protected health information, clinical validation, state scope rules, cash-pay workflow differences, staff training, and licensed review. Direction of error: physician results may overstate implementation resources available to small miscellaneous practitioners, while lightweight consumer, scheduling, documentation, and practice-management tools may spread faster in cash-pay practices than in physician organizations.
caveats: AMA adoption is self-reported and does not verify reliable deployment or labor release.
Physician workflows and resources differ from the nonphysician practitioner mix in this code.
Implementation will vary materially between regulated clinical care and cash-pay wellness practices.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor telemetry and practice surveys segmented by 621399 practitioner type, practice size, payer model, EHR use, deployed use case, error and override rate, staff time saved, and realized headcount or capacity effects would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Documentation, education, intake, communication, and office workflows can adopt assistive tools incrementally. The diversity of practice systems and regulation, plus safety and privacy requirements, makes uniform implementation unlikely.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Transformation: this intention rate is discounted for plans that do not close, internal or family transfers without qualifying control, and practitioner firms whose licenses, personal reputation, patient relationships, payer status, or owner production cannot transfer. Direction of error: the broad employer sample may overstate completed qualifying events, but an aging owner base and consolidation of scaled clinical practices could make some modalities more transaction-prone.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and not restricted to healthcare or the lower-middle market.
The survey measures intended sale or transfer rather than completed qualifying control events.
No cited source measures transaction frequency for NAICS 621399 or its component modalities.
s5.change_evidence: Completed control-deal counts and eligible-firm denominators by included practitioner type from PitchBook, Irving Levin Associates, Axial, healthcare brokers, lender records, licensing boards, payer-change-of-ownership filings, and management interviews would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A multi-practitioner platform with institutionalized referrals, records, billing, and management can transfer, but many miscellaneous health practices are inseparable from a licensed or personally branded owner. Intentions therefore provide only a loose upper anchor.

records.principal_driver: Information-heavy counseling and administration coexist with repeat patient relationships across a potentially broad pool of scaled practitioner firms.
records.principal_weakness: The code's extreme modality heterogeneity makes average automation, demand, retention, and transfer assumptions fragile without firm-level mix data.

caveats: S1 is mislabeled as a 2022 NAICS definition even though the cited archive page is headed 1997 NAICS. The page still supports the operative 621399 definition and examples, so this metadata and source-vintage defect does not change the base tier or action.

The a, rho, and o bases extrapolate from two selected practitioner task profiles and a physician AI survey to a code spanning many materially different modalities. The bridges and error directions disclose this limitation, and the ranges are wide enough that no correction is shown to change the base PRIORITY tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The d5 base converts employment projections for dietitians and nurse midwives into a constant-price demand ratio for the full industry. That transformation is a broad but explicitly disclosed proxy rather than direct demand evidence.

Firm availability remains highly model-conditioned: e is an estimate, n is margin-bridged, and s5 discounts a cross-industry ownership-intention survey rather than completed qualifying transactions. These weaknesses are disclosed and appropriately restrict the action to VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS.

The q base is an uncited estimate of retained automation benefit across mixed cash-pay, payer, package, membership, and institutional models. It is labeled ESTIMATE and bounded broadly, so the uncertainty is honestly represented rather than presented as observed evidence.

sources_audited.note: The Census archive page defines 621399 as independent miscellaneous health-practitioner offices, lists the cited included examples, and supplies the relevant cross-references. The packet title calls it a 2022 definition, while the reopened page is headed 1997 NAICS.
O*NET lists dietitian tasks including needs assessment, laboratory-test evaluation, individualized care planning, counseling, education, coordination, and recording patient and family histories.
O*NET lists acupuncture treatment, individualized planning, history collection, record maintenance, education, diagnosis, and outcome evaluation, supporting the cited task mix.
BLS projects 6 percent dietitian and nutritionist employment growth from 2024 to 2034 and attributes demand to preventive care, disease management, and population aging.

BLS reports nurse-midwife employment rising from 8,600 in 2024 to 9,500 in 2034, an 11 percent increase, and describes examinations, prenatal care, delivery, emergency management, and patient education.

The AMA's 2026 survey of U.S. physicians documents AI use and expected adoption in research summarization, care-plan and note creation, billing and chart documentation, chart summaries, and patient messaging, alongside privacy, safety, efficacy, liability, and training concerns.

Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned within five years to sell, transfer ownership, or take the business public; the packet reasonably uses this as a broad intention anchor before discounting it.
