caveats: a: BLS publishes the occupation mix for parent NAICS 561500, which includes travel agencies, tour operators, and convention bureaus in addition to this code.
a: The residual industry combines highly digital reservation services with membership and physical-assistance models, so one wage-weighted task mix is necessarily approximate.
a: Online booking, rules engines, chatbots, and workflow automation are already mature, making the remaining rather than pre-automation exposure difficult to observe.
rho: HBX surveys its global client base of travel agents, tour operators, and wholesalers rather than a representative U.S. NAICS 561599 sample.
rho: Roland Berger's cross-industry panel included about 90 transportation managers and emphasizes service centers, not the full operating model.
rho: Smaller operators may lack clean inventory, CRM, payment, and policy data needed for reliable automation.
e: No source measures default-lens eligibility among the 198 frozen LMM-band firms.

e: The all-other code is intrinsically heterogeneous and may contain establishments whose principal economics are roadside assistance, membership, payments, advertising, or software rather than reservation fees.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged with a broad business-and-consumer-services margin that may not fit merchant, agency, subscription, and membership models equally.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers.
s5: Carlsquare's 400-plus travel-tech transactions since 2020 are global, include growth investments, and do not provide an eligible-company denominator.
s5: Automobile-club and time-share exchange entities may have very different transfer patterns from ticketing and reservation platforms.
q: Neither source measures five-year retained gross benefit for U.S. lower-middle-market NAICS 561599 firms.
q: Expedia is a global public platform whose scale, brand, marketing costs, and supplier leverage differ sharply from LMM operators.
q: Merchant, agency, referral, ticket-fee, subscription, membership, and exchange models have materially different pass-through dynamics.
d5: Travel spending is not the same quantity as reservation, ticketing, exchange, or automobile-club service demand.
d5: The published forecast ends in 2030, one year short of a full five-year horizon from 2026.
d5: The code's subsegments are exposed differently to air travel, lodging, restaurants, entertainment, time-share use, and drive travel.
o: No source quantifies the year-five operator-required share for the full heterogeneous code.
o: Consumer surveys are global and often vendor-sponsored; stated willingness to use AI is not completed autonomous booking behavior.
o: Self-service pressure is much greater in commodity air or hotel reservations than in scarce-event ticketing, time-share exchange, complex disruptions, or roadside membership services.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include travelers, members, travel suppliers, lodging and restaurant operators, venues, rights holders, and distribution partners. Revenue varies across supplier or traveler commissions, ticketing and service fees, merchant spreads, subscriptions and memberships, referral or advertising revenue, payment fees, and time-share exchange charges; transactions and renewals provide repeat volume, while some automobile-club services include physical roadside fulfillment.
lens.excluded_activities: Travel agencies, tour operators, convention and visitors bureaus, captive supplier booking functions, software sold without an operated reservation or exchange service, shells, subscale hobby businesses, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing external ticketing, airline, bus, cruise, lodging, car-rental, restaurant, amusement, sports, and theatrical reservation services, condominium time-share exchange services, and road and travel services through automobile clubs, including transaction processing, customer support, supplier connectivity, changes, refunds, and exception handling.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports employment and wage shares for parent NAICS 561500 and separately describes travel-agent and reservation work, while industry surveys document current AI use in travel distribution and customer service. Transformation: the parent mix is used to approximate wage weights, then remaining tasks in search, itinerary and offer preparation, customer inquiries, booking support, changes, documentation, marketing, sales administration, fraud triage, and reporting are mapped to AI exposure after discounting mature online booking and rules automation already deployed. Direction of error: the parent's large travel-agency share may not represent this residual code, current digital operators may have less remaining manual work than assumed, and automobile-club roadside operations may reduce exposure; high-touch exceptions and fragmented legacy systems may instead leave more avoidable labor.
caveats: BLS publishes the occupation mix for parent NAICS 561500, which includes travel agencies, tour operators, and convention bureaus in addition to this code.
The residual industry combines highly digital reservation services with membership and physical-assistance models, so one wage-weighted task mix is necessarily approximate.
Online booking, rules engines, chatbots, and workflow automation are already mature, making the remaining rather than pre-automation exposure difficult to observe.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit time-and-payroll study segmented among ticketing, reservation platforms, time-share exchanges, and automobile-club travel services, with current automation and exception rates separated, would materially change the wage-weighted exposed share.
a.rationale: The industry's core information flows are structured, digital, repetitive, and language-heavy, making customer support, booking assistance, content, sales preparation, reconciliation, and routine exception handling exposed. BLS explicitly expects online booking and AI to reduce or reshape travel-agent work. Exposure remains below the full workforce because supplier contracting, complex disruptions, fraud decisions, relationship management, regulated payments, platform engineering, and physical roadside delivery need accountable staff.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: U.S. Travel Association forecasts inflation-adjusted spending through 2030 for total, leisure, business, domestic, group, and international travel, and BLS projects gradual demand for travel expertise. Transformation: real travel spending growth is used as an activity anchor for the underlying current service basket, with a discount for international volatility, shorter and cheaper trips, ticket and reservation mix, and the fact that spending is not transaction count; elimination or self-service of intermediaries is reserved for o. Direction of error: spending growth may overstate transaction volume when prices or trip complexity rise, while increasing reservation frequency, experiences, and ancillary products could make service demand grow faster.
caveats: Travel spending is not the same quantity as reservation, ticketing, exchange, or automobile-club service demand.
The published forecast ends in 2030, one year short of a full five-year horizon from 2026.
The code's subsegments are exposed differently to air travel, lodging, restaurants, entertainment, time-share use, and drive travel.
d5.change_evidence: Current constant-dollar revenue and transaction cohorts for U.S. ticketing, reservation, time-share exchange, and automobile-club travel services, including bookings, changes, cancellations, memberships, and product mix through 2031, would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: U.S. Travel forecasts continued real expansion through 2030 across leisure, business, domestic, and group travel, supporting the underlying need to search, reserve, ticket, exchange, and service trips. International inbound remains volatile and below its prior peak, and consumers are shifting toward shorter or lower-cost trips. The estimate therefore reflects modest real growth in the service basket without assuming that existing intermediaries capture it.

caveats: No source measures default-lens eligibility among the 198 frozen LMM-band firms.

The all-other code is intrinsically heterogeneous and may contain establishments whose principal economics are roadside assistance, membership, payments, advertising, or software rather than reservation fees.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged with a broad business-and-consumer-services margin that may not fit merchant, agency, subscription, and membership models equally.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level audit of the frozen population using websites, customer and supplier contracts, GDS and ticketing accreditations, revenue mix, membership obligations, ownership, captive status, change-of-control terms, recurring-customer cohorts, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: Most listed activities are repeat external services with commissions, fees, memberships, subscriptions, or exchange charges, so a substantial share of LMM firms should meet the default lens. Eligibility is reduced by captive supplier offices, entities misclassified from travel agencies or tour operators, automobile-club operations whose value is inseparable from a broader membership or insurance organization, time-share exchanges with concentrated sponsor relationships, and firms whose technology, inventory access, licenses, or customer accounts cannot transfer independently.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561599 ($8,262,697,660) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561599 ($23,493,772,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $388,654 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561599); class <5: 1,052 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,624,727 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $254,270 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 168 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,514,351 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $706,496 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 111 firms, avg revenue/firm $10,837,486 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,696,067 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 87 firms, avg revenue/firm $26,780,069 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $4,191,081 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $68,013,712 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,644,146 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 62 firms, avg revenue/firm $225,106,581 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $35,229,180 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 198

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS describes online-booking substitution, IATA measures passenger preference for direct and mobile airline booking, Booking.com measures willingness and trust regarding AI travel planning, and Expedia identifies direct suppliers and AI agents as competitors. Transformation: commodity search, comparison, itinerary, reservation, ticketing, and first-line support volumes are treated as increasingly direct, self-served, or agent-automated, while complex exceptions, supplier aggregation, payments, fraud, servicing, proprietary inventory, time-share exchange, member assistance, and physical roadside coordination remain operator-required. Direction of error: platform and AI-agent advances may disintermediate intermediaries faster than assumed, while limited trust, fragmented inventory, disruption risk, and regulated transaction accountability may preserve more operator demand.
caveats: No source quantifies the year-five operator-required share for the full heterogeneous code.
Consumer surveys are global and often vendor-sponsored; stated willingness to use AI is not completed autonomous booking behavior.
Self-service pressure is much greater in commodity air or hotel reservations than in scarce-event ticketing, time-share exchange, complex disruptions, or roadside membership services.
o.change_evidence: Consumer and supplier transaction panels measuring direct versus intermediary booking, AI-agent completion, human escalation, post-booking service, refunds, fraud, repeat membership, exchange fulfillment, platform traffic, and supplier contract retention by subsegment would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Direct supplier apps and websites, online self-service, and AI planning are already normal and are expected to strengthen. Booking.com's survey found high future interest in AI travel planning but low full trust and limited comfort with autonomous decisions, leaving room for accountable service. Operators remain useful where they aggregate fragmented supply, control scarce tickets or exchange rights, handle payment and fraud, recover disrupted trips, support members, or coordinate physical assistance.

q.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS describes commissions and service fees in travel intermediation, while Expedia discloses merchant spreads, agency commissions or ticketing fees, advertising revenue, competitive supplier terms, promotions, direct-supplier channels, and AI-agent competition at global platform scale. Transformation: transaction-, commission-, membership-, and subscription-based revenue is treated as allowing initial internal retention of implemented labor benefit, then discounted for supplier renegotiation, customer incentives, search and platform acquisition costs, direct booking, contract renewal, and fee competition over five years. Direction of error: Expedia's scale and concentrated online model may overstate competitive repricing and technology costs for defensible niches, while transparent prices and low switching costs may make smaller intermediaries retain less.
caveats: Neither source measures five-year retained gross benefit for U.S. lower-middle-market NAICS 561599 firms.
Expedia is a global public platform whose scale, brand, marketing costs, and supplier leverage differ sharply from LMM operators.
Merchant, agency, referral, ticket-fee, subscription, membership, and exchange models have materially different pass-through dynamics.
q.change_evidence: Firm cohorts showing commission rates, merchant take rates, service and membership fees, supplier concessions, customer incentives, acquisition costs, renewals, contribution margin, and labor cost before and after automation would materially change retained benefit.
q.rationale: Most revenue is not billed as labor hours, so automation can initially improve unit economics or fund additional volume and service. Retention is constrained by transparent comparison, supplier bargaining, commissions and ticket fees that can reset, promotional spending, direct airline and hotel channels, dominant platforms, and AI agents. Membership and proprietary exchange networks can retain more than commodity ticketing or reservations.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: HBX Group reports AI use among its global B2B travel-distribution clients, and Roland Berger surveys global customer-service managers including transportation respondents. Transformation: demonstrated content, customer-interaction, pricing, itinerary, recommendation, and routine-service uses are translated into the share of remaining exposed work a scaled U.S. operator could implement within five years after systems integration, training, trust, process redesign, cybersecurity, payments, and regulatory constraints. Direction of error: vendor-client and large service-center samples may overstate lower-middle-market readiness, while five years of product maturation and strong digital process fit may make current early-stage use understate eventual implementation.
caveats: HBX surveys its global client base of travel agents, tour operators, and wholesalers rather than a representative U.S. NAICS 561599 sample.
Roland Berger's cross-industry panel included about 90 transportation managers and emphasizes service centers, not the full operating model.
Smaller operators may lack clean inventory, CRM, payment, and policy data needed for reliable automation.
rho.change_evidence: Deployment cohorts from U.S. reservation and ticketing operators measuring eligible contacts, automated resolution, booking accuracy, rework, chargebacks, fraud loss, escalation, integration cost, active usage, and labor hours before and after AI would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: HBX reports that 65 percent of surveyed distributors already use AI but often only in targeted early-stage applications, while Roland Berger reports broad service-center implementation and travel use cases. Transaction data and digital interaction channels support scaling. Legacy reservation systems, fragmented supplier data, payment and identity risk, hallucination costs, training gaps, and the need for human recovery during disruptions prevent full implementation.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports five-year sale or ownership-transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners, while Carlsquare reports global travel-tech M&A activity that includes acquisitions and growth investments across a wider company set. Transformation: the 22 percent employer intention rate is discounted for plans that do not close, gifts and internal changes that are not qualifying control transfers, platform dependence, supplier-approval risk, and businesses that cannot transfer; observed travel-tech consolidation modestly offsets that discount for scalable technology and distribution assets. Direction of error: intent data overstate completed deals and global travel-tech activity is skewed to venture-backed platforms, but active strategic consolidation may make qualified digital operators more liquid than ordinary small employers.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying transfers.
Carlsquare's 400-plus travel-tech transactions since 2020 are global, include growth investments, and do not provide an eligible-company denominator.
Automobile-club and time-share exchange entities may have very different transfer patterns from ticketing and reservation platforms.
s5.change_evidence: Completed U.S. control-deal counts and an eligible-firm denominator from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, travel-tech advisers, ticketing and reservation brokers, and lender records, classified by NAICS activity and excluding minority growth investments, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Employer succession evidence supports a recurring transfer pool, and travel technology has active strategic and financial buyers seeking distribution, inventory, payments, and AI capabilities. The eligible LMM subset is narrower than the global travel-tech market, and supplier contracts, ticketing credentials, founder relationships, membership structures, or platform concentration can prevent a qualifying transfer.

records.principal_driver: Digital booking, support, content, reconciliation, and sales workflows are highly compatible with AI and already show broad industry adoption.
records.principal_weakness: The same self-service and AI tools can bypass commodity intermediaries, intensifying direct-supplier and platform competition.

caveats: The labor-exposure and implementation ranges are not measured for NAICS 561599: they extrapolate from a parent-industry occupation table and global vendor or cross-industry customer-service surveys. The bridges and error directions disclose this limitation, and the resulting ranges are broad enough to show it.

Firm eligibility and five-year transfer probability remain weakly anchored. The 198-firm denominator is margin-bridged, eligibility is an explicit estimate, Gallup measures cross-industry owner intentions rather than completed deals, and Carlsquare covers global travel-tech activity without an eligible-company denominator.

Retention and operator-required demand rely heavily on Expedia-scale economics and global consumer evidence. Commodity booking, ticketing, time-share exchange, and automobile-club services may have sharply different pass-through and disintermediation paths within this heterogeneous residual code.

The demand anchor is inflation-adjusted travel spending through 2030, not transaction volume through the full 2031 horizon. The packet discloses both mismatches, so this weakens precision rather than constituting a hidden construct error.

S8 demonstrates global mobile reliance but describes direct-airline booking preference specifically for European respondents; treating that preference as broadly representative should remain a minor attribution caveat.

sources_audited.note: The 2022 NAICS manual defines the parent residual category, lists the cited reservation, ticketing, time-share exchange, and automobile-club activities, and provides the stated cross-references.

The BLS table reports employment shares and wages for NAICS 561500, including travel agents, customer service, sales, management, technical, and administrative occupations; the packet correctly identifies it as a parent-industry proxy.

BLS explicitly attributes reduced travel-agent utilization to online booking, greater administrative and marketing efficiency to AI, and ticket-counter task substitution to mobile applications and kiosks.
BLS describes research, itinerary, reservation, documentation, disruption, commission, service-fee, and sales work, as well as gradual demand growth and online self-booking pressure.

HBX reports 65% AI use in its global travel-distributor client survey, targeted content and customer-interaction use, positive reported impact, and barriers involving trust, training, usability, and workflow embedding.

Roland Berger identifies a 550-manager global service panel with about 90 transportation respondents, broad routine-task AI adoption, travel use cases, and the stated process, skills, regulation, data, and integration constraints.
Booking.com reports more than 37,000 consumers across 33 markets and the cited 89% future travel-planning interest, 6% full trust, and 12% comfort with autonomous decisions.

IATA supports rising global smartphone use across booking and the journey, digital payments, and cybersecurity concerns; the direct-airline booking preference shown on the cited page is specifically described for European respondents rather than as a global rate.
The U.S. Travel summary presents inflation-adjusted growth through 2030, domestic support, international volatility, and a shift toward shorter and lower-cost trips.
The Spring 2026 PDF provides the stated 2019-2030 inflation-adjusted spending tables across total, leisure, business, domestic, transient, group, and international segments.

Expedia's 2025 Form 10-K describes merchant, agency, commission, ticket-fee, advertising, and GDS economics and competition from direct suppliers, search, generative AI, AI agents, promotions, and changing terms.
Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and clearly identifies the intent-based U.S. survey population.

Carlsquare reports 400-plus global travel-tech transactions since 2020 with aggregate value above $27 billion, identifies strategic and AI-led consolidation, and states that the count includes PE growth investments.

a: No source supplies wage-weighted task shares or a measured not-yet-automated fraction.
a: The AI review discusses potential and reported applications but does not measure displacement in US lower-middle-market providers.

rho: AI used to improve service quality or expand caseload capacity may not reduce current payroll.
rho: State-by-state authorization, privacy, accessibility, and procurement differences widen the range.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Providers are commonly authorized or contracted by government vocational-rehabilitation programs and paid through fixed fee schedules, time-based service units, documented deliverables, or placement and successful-closure milestones. Other revenue can come from workers' compensation, employer, nonprofit, grant, and participant-program sources.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines outsourced counseling and supported-employment providers with sheltered workshops and enterprises that employ participants in production, retail, custodial, or logistics work. The lens is narrowed to externally paid vocational-rehabilitation service providers because workshop operating labor, revenue, automation, and transfer economics would make a combined screen incoherent.
lens.excluded_activities: State agencies performing services internally, captive programs, shells, non-control financing vehicles, schools classified elsewhere, general career counseling that is not rehabilitative, and sheltered-workshop or social-enterprise operations whose primary business is employing participants to produce or sell goods rather than supplying vocational rehabilitation services to an external payer.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market providers of recurring or repeat vocational assessment, rehabilitation counseling, case management, job development, placement, coaching, supported-employment, and related training services delivered for external state agencies, federal programs, workers' compensation payers, employers, or other institutional customers.

a.bridge: BLS measures occupations across the full vocational-rehabilitation industry, including sheltered-workshop production and material-moving roles excluded from the narrowed lens. It shows substantial counselor, human-service, administrative, and management employment; task descriptions and a vocational-rehabilitation AI review identify documentation, assessment support, planning, job matching, monitoring, and communication as AI-relevant. I map those task components to the corresponding wage-bearing roles, exclude already digitized work and the irreducibly interpersonal share of counseling and coaching, and remove excluded workshop labor. The full-industry occupational mix likely understates the counseling and administrative concentration of the narrowed provider lens, while the literature's emphasis on potential applications may overstate the share that is substitutable rather than merely augmented.
caveats: The BLS occupation table covers the full NAICS category rather than the narrowed lens.
No source supplies wage-weighted task shares or a measured not-yet-automated fraction.
The AI review discusses potential and reported applications but does not measure displacement in US lower-middle-market providers.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study of eligible private providers that separates documentation, assessment, matching, counseling, coaching, employer outreach, travel, supervision, and workshop activity, while recording current software use, would materially change this value.
a.rationale: Current exposed work is concentrated in case documentation, progress summaries, records review, service-plan drafting, labor-market research, job-lead screening, routine communication, scheduling, and structured monitoring. Client assessment, counseling judgment, employer negotiation, accommodation planning, advocacy, crisis response, in-person coaching, and supervised work remain substantially human. The high labor ratio makes task boundaries especially consequential, but it does not itself establish exposure.

d5.bridge: BLS projects only modest growth for rehabilitation-counselor employment, not constant-quality demand for eligible outsourced providers. Federal budget and reallotment sources show a large, continuing state VR program, inflation-linked authorization, substantial funding demand in some agencies, and simultaneous relinquishment or unused-fund problems in others. Disability employment statistics show a persistent employment gap but do not measure service purchasing. I combine these signals into a near-flat five-year service-demand ratio, allowing upside from unmet need and aging and downside from budget execution, policy changes, and insourcing. Counselor employment may understate demand if technology raises caseloads, while appropriations and unmet need may overstate demand that becomes contracted volume.
caveats: The BLS forecast is occupational and cross-industry rather than demand for NAICS 624310 services.
Government appropriations, reallotment requests, and disability employment gaps are not direct measures of purchased service quantity.
Policy, agency capacity, and state budget variation can move vendor demand independently of underlying client need.
d5.change_evidence: State-by-state forecasts of referrals, authorizations, vendor service units, waitlists, case closures, real fee rates, federal and state funding, and the outsourced share of spending would materially change the year-five ratio.
d5.rationale: Need remains durable because people with disabilities continue to face a large employment gap and state-federal programs have ongoing statutory support. The most relevant occupational forecast indicates limited growth, and federal evidence shows that agencies can struggle both to secure sufficient funds and to deploy available funds. Real outsourced volume therefore looks more stable than rapidly expanding.

caveats: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using an estimated industry margin and may be materially distorted by grants, donations, participant wages, and nonprofit accounting.
Public fee schedules establish vendor use but do not identify how many vendors fall inside the earnings band.
The narrowed service-provider lens cannot be isolated in published six-digit establishment data.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census matched to service mix, payer contracts, tax status, ownership, normalized EBITDA, related-party status, and a separable sale perimeter would materially change this estimate.
e.rationale: The eligible share is reduced by the code's sheltered-workshop and participant-employment segment, nonprofit and public-service structures that do not support a conventional control acquisition, providers below the earnings band, diversified disability-service organizations without a separable vocational-rehabilitation perimeter, and firms serving consumers directly rather than through repeat external contracts. State fee schedules and federal procurement rules confirm that an external contracted-provider segment exists, but they do not size it.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No source directly measures substitution of an accountable vocational-rehabilitation provider.
Digital access and usefulness vary materially by disability and accommodation requirement.
The estimate applies to the narrowed outsourced-service basket, not excluded sheltered-workshop production.
o.change_evidence: Payer data comparing vendor-authorized cases with agency-insourced and digital self-service cases by acuity, disability type, service, completion, employment outcome, accommodation need, and cost would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Most year-five demand should still require an accountable provider because rehabilitation plans, individualized counseling, disability-sensitive assessment, employer engagement, accommodation support, verified placement, progress monitoring, and ongoing coaching cannot be reduced to self-service software for many clients. Lower-acuity resume, job-search, matching, information, and routine follow-up needs can migrate to accessible digital tools or be performed directly by state systems, employers, or clients.

caveats: The cited fee rules cover selected public programs and not workers' compensation, employer, or philanthropic contracts.
No source measures the discounted retained share of implemented benefit.
Provider savings may be absorbed by uncompensated documentation, client complexity, vacancies, or required service expansion.
q.change_evidence: A representative payer-contract sample showing billing units, fixed-fee and milestone shares, cost-reporting duties, rate-review cadence, rebid timing, staffing requirements, disallowances, and post-automation margin changes would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Fixed per-service and milestone payments can let a provider retain workflow savings until a fee study, schedule revision, rebid, or contract renewal. Retention is weaker for time-unit billing, reimbursed cost, and payment rules that require the same documented human activity. Outcome milestones also require verified placement and continued service, limiting the ability to monetize speed alone. Public payers can reset fees or demand greater service capacity when productivity improves.

rho.bridge: The vocational-rehabilitation literature supports technical use of AI for documentation, assessment support, planning, matching, and outcome monitoring, while federal guidance documents disability-discrimination and accommodation risks in algorithmic hiring. Neither source measures implementation by eligible providers over five years. I translate feasible workflow uses into an implementation share and discount for government procurement, fragmented state systems, privacy and security controls, accessibility, bias testing, counselor oversight, payer authorization, staff training, and limited provider technology budgets. Because the application literature emphasizes potential benefits more than failed deployments, the proxy is more likely to overstate implementation.
caveats: The sources do not report implementation rates for the screened firm population.
AI used to improve service quality or expand caseload capacity may not reduce current payroll.
State-by-state authorization, privacy, accessibility, and procurement differences widen the range.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of contracted community rehabilitation providers reporting production use, eligible workflow coverage, counselor override rates, accessibility audits, adverse-event reviews, client outcomes, implementation cost, and realized labor hours would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Documentation and workflow assistance can be introduced without delegating final eligibility, rehabilitation-plan, or placement decisions, making partial implementation credible. Implementation is constrained by sensitive disability data, uneven interoperability, contractual reporting rules, accessible-design requirements, and the need to prevent automated screening from reproducing disability bias. Smaller providers may adopt general tools before they can integrate them safely into case systems.

s5.bridge: Two provider announcements show that disability-service and vocational-program organizations can combine through acquisitions or nonprofit mergers, but they are selected examples with no eligible-firm denominator and include broader service portfolios. I use them only to establish a feasible transfer mechanism, then apply a low five-year probability because much of the field is nonprofit, mission-governed, public, grant-dependent, or difficult to separate from other programs. Publicized successful combinations create upward selection bias, while unannounced small transactions would create some downward bias.
caveats: The examples are not limited to NAICS 624310 or the lower-middle-market band.
Neither source supplies a population denominator or a systematic transfer rate.
A nonprofit governance change may not create the economics or control rights expected in a conventional acquisition.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction census of US community rehabilitation providers that records nonprofit mergers, asset and program transfers, for-profit control sales, closures, seller governance, enterprise size, and continued payer authorization would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Qualifying transfers appear more likely to take the form of nonprofit affiliation, merger, program transfer, or acquisition by a larger human-services provider than a conventional founder sale. Governance approvals, restricted funding, payer credentialing, continuity obligations, and mixed program portfolios make transfers possible but relatively infrequent.

records.principal_driver: Case documentation, planning support, monitoring, and job-development workflows offer implementable AI assistance inside recurring payer-authorized services.
records.principal_weakness: The code's nonprofit, public, and sheltered-workshop composition makes the eligible transferable-firm population unusually uncertain and limits conventional control transactions.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Advertisers and agencies engage media buyers through recurring retainers, labor-based fees, fixed or output-based fees, commissions or percentage-of-spend arrangements, and occasional performance incentives; client media spend may flow through agency receipts.
lens.excluded_activities: Media sales representatives, publishers and media owners, creative-only advertising agencies, software-only adtech vendors, captive in-house buying teams, shell firms, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market agencies providing repeat outsourced media planning and buying, price and placement negotiation, campaign trafficking, performance monitoring, research, verification, and post-campaign analysis for advertisers or other agencies.

a.bridge: Source population: BLS May 2023 employees in broader NAICS 541800 Advertising, Public Relations, and Related Services, combined with IAB evidence about AI functions across agencies, brands, and publishers. Transformation: emphasize the wage-bearing media-buyer work in management, market research, project management, computer/data, and administration and assign high exposure to audience analysis, plan generation, partner selection, forecasting, trafficking, optimization, reporting, and verification, but lower exposure to client strategy, negotiation, brand-safety judgment, and relationship ownership. Direction of error: broad 541800 includes creative and advertising-sales jobs that are not representative of 541830, while IAB use cases may overstate substitutability by describing technical capability rather than current task quality.
caveats: OEWS is available only for NAICS 541800 and includes media representatives, creative agencies, public relations, and other businesses unlike media buyers.
IAB evidence describes organizations across the buy and sell sides, not wage-weighted tasks in LMM media buying agencies.

The frozen labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may be materially distorted because receipts can include resold or pass-through media spend; it is also harmonized by a cross-industry median adjustment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary from 541830 LMM agencies separating already-programmatic execution from still-manual planning, trafficking, optimization, analytics, reporting, negotiation, client service, and governance would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: In broader 541800, management is 16.77% of employment, business and financial operations 16.65%, computer and mathematical work 5.18%, arts/design/media 24.48%, sales 18.41%, and office support 11.22%; market research analysts alone are 8.15%. IAB says AI can build media plans, generate audience segments, select partners, forecast performance, and support attribution, directly covering a large portion of media-buying workflow. Existing programmatic automation means already-automated auction execution is excluded from the remaining opportunity.

d5.bridge: Source population: US digital advertising revenue and surveyed media-buyer spending plans, plus national employment projections for advertising and marketing managers, rather than constant-price demand for 541830's current service basket. Transformation: translate strong nominal digital and programmatic growth into a lower five-year real service-demand ratio after removing price effects, cyclical event effects, and channel mix, while recognizing growing cross-platform complexity. Direction of error: rapid digital, creator, commerce, and video expansion could raise buying-service demand more than assumed, while direct platform buying, consolidation, recessions, and movement away from traditional channels could lower demand for the current basket.
caveats: Digital ad revenue is publisher/platform revenue, not agency service quantity.
One-year nominal growth and buyer plans are cyclical and do not directly measure five-year constant-price demand.
Political and sports events, recession, privacy rules, and platform changes can materially alter spending.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted 541830 net service revenue, agency managed-spend histories, client retention and pitch volumes, and channel-level five-year forecasts from IAB, Magna, and WARC would directly test demand for the current basket.
d5.rationale: IAB reports US digital ad revenue rose 13.9% to $294.6 billion in 2025 and programmatic revenue rose 20.5% to $162.4 billion. Buyers projected 9.5% ad-spend growth for 2026, or 7.1%–7.8% excluding major events. BLS projects overall advertising, promotions, and marketing managers to grow 6% over 2024–34, but advertising and promotions managers alone to decline 2%, supporting growth in media activity with meaningful structural displacement risk.

caveats: No source measures the share of 541830 firms satisfying every LMM lens condition.

The frozen firm count uses a generic 15.65% margin bridge; media pass-through accounting and commission structures can make receipts a poor proxy for agency economic revenue and therefore distort size-band membership.
e.change_evidence: A stratified review of 541830 LMM firms covering net revenue versus gross billings, repeat-client share, contract duration and assignability, customer concentration, founder billability, platform dependence, and second-layer management would directly measure eligibility.
e.rationale: Recurring outsourced client service is central to media buying, and the Census product definition includes planning, placement negotiation, trafficking, monitoring, and post-campaign analysis. Eligibility is reduced for agencies that primarily resell media with limited service differentiation, rely on a founder's client relationships, have severe customer concentration, lack assignable contracts, perform mostly one-off campaigns, or function as software wrappers.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 541830 ($2,332,376,359) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 541830 ($9,013,654,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[54]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $435,127 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 541830); class <5: 406 firms, avg revenue/firm $751,810 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $117,658 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 90 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,826,767 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $598,889 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 65 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,081,677 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,264,782 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 56 firms, avg revenue/firm $21,907,357 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,428,501 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 28 firms, avg revenue/firm $94,779,679 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $14,833,020 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $219,892,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $34,413,115 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 121

o.bridge: Source population: IAB agencies, brands, publishers, and media buyers across company sizes, plus Census descriptions of current media-buying services, not a measured year-five operator-required share for LMM 541830. Transformation: classify routine plan assembly, audience segmentation, bidding, trafficking, monitoring, optimization, and reporting as increasingly platform- or agent-supplied, while retaining external operator need for objectives, cross-platform allocation, negotiation, measurement design, brand safety, privacy, exception handling, and accountable client representation. Direction of error: interoperable agentic systems and brand insourcing could eliminate more agency quantity, while platform fragmentation, opaque measurement, governance, and bargaining complexity could preserve more.
caveats: Current AI adoption and stated buyer priorities do not directly measure eliminated agency service quantity.
The operator/software boundary is fluid because agencies can embed proprietary technology and sell managed outcomes.
Large platforms may both automate execution and increase demand for independent verification and cross-platform accountability.
o.change_evidence: Buyer interviews and campaign-level loss data identifying work moved in-house or to self-service platforms, together with audited benchmarks of autonomous agents on cross-channel planning, spend control, brand safety, incrementality, and exception handling, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Programmatic advertising already automates a large volume of buying, and IAB describes agentic systems moving planning, activation, and optimization toward autonomous execution. It also reports that half of agencies fear brands bringing AI capabilities in-house. Yet Census defines the service to include price negotiation, traffic management, performance monitoring, verification, and post-campaign analysis, while IAB identifies data quality, protection, fragmentation, transparency, and measurement as unresolved constraints that preserve accountable operator work.

q.bridge: Source population: 101 ANA client-side marketers representing 336 agency relationships across advertising services, not specifically LMM 541830 clients. Transformation: infer lower retention where labor-based fees rebase with staffing and hours, higher retention where fixed/output, commission, retainer, or performance structures decouple fees from effort, and discount all models for renewal competition, client procurement, transparency demands, and insourcing. Direction of error: media-buying commissions and percent-of-spend fees may retain more benefit than the broad agency sample, while platform price transparency and measurable automation may force faster pass-through.
caveats: ANA's 2022 sample is small, includes large marketers, and is not media-buying-only.
Compensation method incidence does not reveal revenue shares, contract duration, or realized pass-through.
The five-year discounted retention rate depends on future client procurement and competitor adoption that are not observed.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level 541830 data on net agency revenue by labor fee, fixed fee, retainer, commission, percentage of spend, and performance fee—linked to renewal repricing, staffing changes, and client churn after AI deployment—would materially move this estimate.
q.rationale: ANA found labor-based fees remained dominant, 82% of marketers used fee-based compensation in at least one agreement, and smaller advertisers used labor-based agreements more often at 76%. Fixed/output fees are growing and are negotiated without regard to labor time, allowing better initial retention, but 51% planned to change full-service compensation agreements. IAB also found half of brands worry about agency AI transparency and half of agencies worry brands will bring AI capabilities in-house, intensifying sharing and repricing pressure.

rho.bridge: Source population: more than 500 advertising experts from agencies, brands, and publishers in IAB's 2025 study, plus 205 media buyers in its 2026 outlook, rather than 541830 LMM agencies. Transformation: use observed integration and near-term adoption intent to estimate the five-year implemented share of exposed agency labor, then discount for data quality, privacy, fragmented tools, client approvals, model errors, governance, and workflow redesign. Direction of error: media buying is more digital and automation-ready than many agency services, raising implementation, while small agencies may have weaker proprietary data and integration resources than the surveyed leaders, lowering it.
caveats: Full campaign-lifecycle integration is not the same as implementing a given share of labor substitution.
IAB respondents skew toward digitally engaged industry participants and may be more advanced than LMM agencies.
Ad-platform automation can shift work rather than eliminate it by increasing campaign variants and measurement demands.
rho.change_evidence: A 541830-specific panel measuring AI-enabled hours saved, avoided hiring, campaigns run per employee, error and override rates, data integration, and client approval constraints by workflow would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: IAB found only 30% of agencies, brands, and publishers had fully integrated AI across the campaign lifecycle in 2025, but half of those not fully integrated expected to do so by 2026. Nearly two-thirds cited data quality, data protection, and tool fragmentation as leading barriers. By 2026, five of buyers' top six focus areas involved AI and two-thirds focused on agentic buying, supporting substantial five-year implementation without assuming complete autonomous operation.

s5.bridge: Source population: US employer-business owners across industries in Gallup's fall 2024 survey and middle-market marketing-services transactions tracked by Capstone, not eligible 541830 firms. Transformation: begin with the 22% of employer owners planning a five-year sale or transfer, discount for nonexecution, internal gifts, owner-dependent practices, and deals outside the lens, then allow some upward pressure from active marketing-services consolidation. Direction of error: technology-enabled recurring media agencies may attract strategic and sponsor buyers more readily than typical small businesses, raising transfers, but client concentration and platform dependence may prevent contemplated deals, lowering them.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions across all employer industries, not completed media-agency transfers.
Capstone covers broad marketing services and middle-to-large transactions without an at-risk firm denominator.
Agency client relationships can be nontransferable even when the legal entity is sold.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year 541830 transaction cohort from PitchBook, Capital IQ, COMvergence, Axial, broker records, and state filings—matched to the eligible LMM denominator and separating add-ons, internal successions, and closures—would materially improve this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Capstone reports marketing-services M&A volume increased each year since 2023, averaging 4.7% annual growth through 2025, with 2026 year-to-date dealmaking up 7.5%; acquirers prioritized recurring revenue, low concentration, and technology-enabled offerings. Qualifying completed control transfers remain a subset of intentions and broad sector transactions.

records.principal_driver: Remaining manual campaign planning, optimization, analytics, and reporting are directly exposed to AI in a large and growing digital advertising market.
records.principal_weakness: The same AI and platform capabilities that create labor efficiency also let clients self-serve and reprice agency work, while accounting for media pass-through obscures true firm size and labor intensity.

caveats: a: The BLS occupation table is for NAICS 561400 and is heavily shaped by call centers and collection agencies rather than credit bureaus.
a: O*NET credit-authorizer tasks include lending and collection work and do not measure time, wages, feasibility, or current automation inside bureaus.
a: TransUnion's technology disclosures describe a large incumbent whose automation and technical occupation mix may exceed those of LMM specialists.
a: The sharp growth in complaints includes automated, duplicate, and abusive submissions and is not a clean measure of legitimate bureau labor demand.

a: The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may understate controllable labor where bureau receipts monetize proprietary data or include large nonlabor technology and data costs.
rho: Public-company platform migration demonstrates capability, not audited labor avoidance or representative LMM adoption.
rho: Current high automation means the marginal workflows left to implement may be unusually exception-heavy and difficult.
rho: Accuracy, privacy, discrimination, cybersecurity, and FCRA reinvestigation requirements can slow deployment or require human review.
rho: Complaint-volume growth can create an implementation incentive while also overwhelming quality-control systems.
e: No source measures eligibility under the frozen LMM recurring-or-repeat outsourced-service lens.
e: NAICS 561450 includes consumer and commercial bureaus, credit rating, and investigation services with different degrees of recurring revenue.
e: The injected firm count uses a broad 15.65% Business & Consumer Services margin; data-rich bureaus can have materially different margins and therefore different implied EBITDA-band membership.
e: The three nationwide bureaus dominate general-purpose consumer credit reporting but are outside the LMM target band; the relevant population is the smaller specialist tail.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and includes transfers or gifts rather than only completed control sales.
s5: TransUnion's disclosed acquisitions are not a representative count of US LMM 561450 deals and include international or adjacent businesses.
s5: No source supplies an eligible-firm denominator, failed-process rate, owner-age distribution, or LMM transaction completion rate.
s5: Consumer data rights, cybersecurity history, regulatory exposure, and portability of data-supply contracts can impair transferability.
q: The cited contract disclosures come from global public incumbents, not LMM specialty bureaus.
q: Neither source measures the share of AI benefit retained after renewal or competition.
q: High implementation and compliance spending may consume productivity without appearing as customer pass-through.
q: Retention can differ sharply between proprietary recurring datasets, commodity report resellers, consulting, and one-off investigations.
d5: The BLS forecast is for NAICS 561400, which includes call centers, collection agencies, document preparation, and other support services.
d5: Experian's growth is nominal, global-company evidence and includes adjacent fraud, analytics, health, automotive, marketing, and consumer services.
d5: Demand is cyclical with mortgage, consumer credit, hiring, rental, and insurance activity.
d5: The estimate holds the current service basket and quality constant; new analytics products are not treated as guaranteed demand.
o: No source directly measures the future share of credit-reporting quantity requiring a bureau operator.
o: FCRA obligations support accountable operation but do not guarantee that today's specialist firm, delivery channel, or data product remains necessary.
o: Direct score licensing, consumer-permissioned data, open banking, lender consortiums, and inexpensive public data can bypass parts of the bureau value chain.
o: Large incumbents' scale, proprietary files, and workflow embedding may overstate the operator-required share for small report resellers or narrow specialists.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Financial institutions, landlords, employers, insurers, utilities, retailers, governments, and other permissible users purchase bureau data through per-report or other transaction fees, fixed-term data contracts, recurring licenses and subscriptions, batch services, monitoring, implementation, support, and occasional analytics or governance consulting. Bureau products are commonly delivered in real time or embedded in customer decision workflows.
lens.excluded_activities: Debt collection, credit repair and counseling, lending, captive bank credit departments, general background investigation without a credit-reporting function, generic data-broker or software products not operated as a bureau, the nationwide bureaus themselves when outside the LMM band, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and one-off investigations without a recurring or repeat reporting service.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market credit bureaus and specialty consumer or commercial reporting agencies that repeatedly compile credit, employment, payment, identity, or related histories and sell reports, scores, monitoring, batch data, decision support, or credit-investigation services to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for four-digit NAICS 561400, which is dominated by call centers and also includes collection agencies and other support services, while O*NET describes credit-authorizer work rather than the full six-digit bureau workforce. I map information intake, record matching, document extraction, dispute triage, correspondence, report preparation, routine analysis, coding, testing, monitoring, and first-line customer support to higher AI exposure; I map data governance, security, model validation, regulatory judgment, complex reinvestigation, sales, client integration, and final accuracy accountability to lower exposure. The broad BLS mix likely overstates low-wage call-center work and understates the software, data-science, security, and compliance share of credit bureaus, biasing an unadjusted exposure estimate upward.
caveats: The BLS occupation table is for NAICS 561400 and is heavily shaped by call centers and collection agencies rather than credit bureaus.
O*NET credit-authorizer tasks include lending and collection work and do not measure time, wages, feasibility, or current automation inside bureaus.
TransUnion's technology disclosures describe a large incumbent whose automation and technical occupation mix may exceed those of LMM specialists.
The sharp growth in complaints includes automated, duplicate, and abusive submissions and is not a clean measure of legitimate bureau labor demand.
The injected labor ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and may understate controllable labor where bureau receipts monetize proprietary data or include large nonlabor technology and data costs.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit 561450 payroll occupation table combined with workflow-level time, wage, current-automation, rework, and error data from specialty bureaus and nationwide-bureau business units would materially move the estimate.
a.rationale: Credit bureaus already automate data ingestion, matching, scoring, fulfillment, and many customer decisions, so the primitive measures only the exposed work that remains. Generative and predictive AI can still compress document-heavy disputes, anomaly review, correspondence, data quality investigation, analytics development, software work, and support. The high legal cost of inaccurate files, cyber risk, source-data ambiguity, and the need to defend models and adverse outcomes keep the base below half of current wage-weighted work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for four-digit NAICS 561400 to grow 2.2% annually from 2024 to 2034, implying about 1.11 over five years. Experian reported 10% North American and 8% B2B organic revenue growth in FY2026 and describes broad credit, fraud, identity, and verification use cases, but those figures are nominal company growth across businesses and geographies. I center on the BLS five-year implication, use incumbent growth and expanding decision uses as directional support, and widen downward for the broad-code mismatch, credit cycles, customer concentration, regulation, and direct or alternative data competition. The public-company results likely overstate typical LMM growth.
caveats: The BLS forecast is for NAICS 561400, which includes call centers, collection agencies, document preparation, and other support services.
Experian's growth is nominal, global-company evidence and includes adjacent fraud, analytics, health, automotive, marketing, and consumer services.
Demand is cyclical with mortgage, consumer credit, hiring, rental, and insurance activity.
The estimate holds the current service basket and quality constant; new analytics products are not treated as guaranteed demand.
d5.change_evidence: A six-digit BLS or Census real-output series for 561450, supported by inflation-adjusted US specialty-bureau report volumes, contract values, renewals, inquiry volumes, and customer cohorts by end market, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Credit, employment, rental, insurance, utility, fraud, identity, and verification decisions create repeat demand, while more digital transactions and alternative data expand the service basket around reporting. Mortgage and lending cycles, regulatory restrictions, free consumer access, customer consolidation, direct score licensing, and open-banking or first-party data create downside. The range therefore spans flat real demand to moderate growth for the current service basket.

caveats: No source measures eligibility under the frozen LMM recurring-or-repeat outsourced-service lens.
NAICS 561450 includes consumer and commercial bureaus, credit rating, and investigation services with different degrees of recurring revenue.
The injected firm count uses a broad 15.65% Business & Consumer Services margin; data-rich bureaus can have materially different margins and therefore different implied EBITDA-band membership.
The three nationwide bureaus dominate general-purpose consumer credit reporting but are outside the LMM target band; the relevant population is the smaller specialist tail.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit establishment census or commercial company database segmented by independence, normalized EBITDA, principal service, recurring revenue, contract duration, and repeat-customer share would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is intrinsically an outsourced information service sold to financial institutions, retailers, and other evaluators, and CFPB documents both nationwide and specialty consumer reporting companies serving repeat decision use cases. Most LMM employer firms should therefore satisfy the external recurring-or-repeat lens. Ineligibility remains for project-only credit investigations, adjacent data or software businesses, captive units, misclassified firms, and companies whose normalized EBITDA is outside the band despite the dataset bridge.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: No source directly measures the future share of credit-reporting quantity requiring a bureau operator.
FCRA obligations support accountable operation but do not guarantee that today's specialist firm, delivery channel, or data product remains necessary.
Direct score licensing, consumer-permissioned data, open banking, lender consortiums, and inexpensive public data can bypass parts of the bureau value chain.
Large incumbents' scale, proprietary files, and workflow embedding may overstate the operator-required share for small report resellers or narrow specialists.
o.change_evidence: Customer-level cohort data showing five-year bureau-spend retention, direct-data substitution, open-banking adoption, score-licensing bypass, software self-service, insourcing, and vendor consolidation by credit, tenant, employment, insurance, and commercial-reporting use case would materially move this share.
o.rationale: Many report-generation, monitoring, identity-matching, and decision-support steps can be automated or accessed through software, and customers may use first-party, open-banking, direct-score, or alternative data. Most current quantity should nevertheless require a bureau operator because the service depends on continuously aggregated multi-source histories, permissible-purpose controls, consumer access, dispute intake, reasonable investigation, correction, auditability, security, and contractual accountability. Embedded bureau data and feedback loops are difficult for an individual customer to recreate, especially in regulated adverse decisions.

q.bridge: Equifax describes transactional, tier-priced, annual subscription, and multi-year subscription contracts, while Experian describes transactional and fixed-term data contracts, recurring licenses, batch services, and recurring support. I map fixed subscriptions, licenses, and support to higher initial retention of an implemented labor benefit, and transaction or volume contracts to neutral-to-moderate retention because prices are based on reports or access rather than labor hours. I then discount renewal repricing, concentrated-customer procurement, competitive data products, regulatory service obligations, and price pressure. Large-bureau contract structures may be more embedded and defensible than those of LMM specialists, biasing retention upward.
caveats: The cited contract disclosures come from global public incumbents, not LMM specialty bureaus.
Neither source measures the share of AI benefit retained after renewal or competition.
High implementation and compliance spending may consume productivity without appearing as customer pass-through.
Retention can differ sharply between proprietary recurring datasets, commodity report resellers, consulting, and one-off investigations.
q.change_evidence: A representative set of current US specialty-bureau contracts and account P&Ls showing pricing basis, term, renewal uplift, volume tiers, service levels, dispute obligations, gross margin, and customer concessions before and after automation would materially move retention.
q.rationale: Customers generally buy data access, reports, scores, reliability, and compliant decisions rather than staff hours, so labor productivity does not mechanically reduce billing. Recurring licenses and multi-year contracts can preserve near-term gains as margin or capacity. Over five years, renewals, volume tiers, buyer concentration, alternative data, direct score licensing, and competition should share some benefit, while regulatory and data-quality spending absorbs another portion.

rho.bridge: TransUnion reports internal and product use of AI, a centralized AI-enabled data and analytics platform, and migration of its US credit business, while Experian reports largely completed North American cloud migration. These are large, sophisticated incumbents rather than LMM bureaus, so I use them to establish technical feasibility and industry direction, then discount implementation for smaller-firm capital, data engineering, governance, security, model-risk, and change constraints. Incumbent evidence may overstate LMM implementation capacity, while smaller platforms with less legacy technology may move faster.
caveats: Public-company platform migration demonstrates capability, not audited labor avoidance or representative LMM adoption.
Current high automation means the marginal workflows left to implement may be unusually exception-heavy and difficult.
Accuracy, privacy, discrimination, cybersecurity, and FCRA reinvestigation requirements can slow deployment or require human review.
Complaint-volume growth can create an implementation incentive while also overwhelming quality-control systems.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after operating data from US specialty bureaus showing AI coverage by workflow, straight-through processing, analyst and dispute hours, false-match and correction rates, compliance exceptions, model approvals, and headcount avoided would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: The industry's products are already digital, structured, high-volume, and embedded in automated decisions, which makes workflow integration more feasible than in analog services. However, the remaining opportunity is concentrated in exceptions and controlled processes: FCRA reinvestigation, accuracy, privacy, cybersecurity, explainability, bias, and client validation require auditable deployment and human escalation. These constraints support meaningful but incomplete implementation within five years.

s5.bridge: Gallup found that 22% of US employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while TransUnion discloses a strategy and record of acquiring complementary data, credit, and decisioning businesses. I use the owner-plan rate as a broad upper anchor and the incumbent acquisition record as evidence that strategic transfers occur, then discount gifts, internal transfers, unexecuted plans, non-US or above-LMM transactions, regulatory and data-license consent risk, security diligence, and specialist firms too dependent on founders or unique data access. Gallup is broader than the industry and the public-company examples bias toward larger strategic assets, so either can overstate qualifying LMM control-sale probability.
caveats: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and includes transfers or gifts rather than only completed control sales.
TransUnion's disclosed acquisitions are not a representative count of US LMM 561450 deals and include international or adjacent businesses.
No source supplies an eligible-firm denominator, failed-process rate, owner-age distribution, or LMM transaction completion rate.
Consumer data rights, cybersecurity history, regulatory exposure, and portability of data-supply contracts can impair transferability.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, or an industry adviser dataset isolating completed and failed US control-sale processes for LMM consumer and commercial reporting bureaus by eligible-firm denominator over five years would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Proprietary datasets, bureau memberships, permissible-purpose workflows, recurring contracts, and embedded integrations can attract strategic buyers, and large incumbents actively acquire data and decisioning capabilities. The addressable LMM population is small, highly specialized, and exposed to regulatory, cyber, data-rights, and integration diligence; some owner transitions will not produce a qualifying control sale. A five-year probability below the broad employer-owner plan rate is therefore appropriate.

records.principal_driver: Recurring, workflow-embedded data products can retain AI-enabled savings from dispute, data-quality, support, and analytics work without billing by labor hour.
records.principal_weakness: The industry is already heavily automated, and the small LMM specialist population faces severe data, regulatory, cyber, and incumbent-dependence risks.

caveats: a: The occupation distribution is published at NAICS 54186 and is not isolated to the narrowed integrated-provider lens.
a: O*NET describes occupations across industries and does not measure AI substitution or wage-weighted exposure.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input combines nonmatching QCEW and SUSB vintages and is harmonized through a cross-industry adjustment.
rho: The IAB population spans advertising rather than direct mail alone.
rho: The direct-mail survey represents marketers and operational-mail leaders, not a payroll-weighted panel of providers.
rho: Vendor-sponsored research may select technology-forward respondents and emphasize automation benefits.
e: No public source measures the share of LMM firms matching the narrowed integrated-provider lens.
e: Industry classification can include adjacent print, fulfillment, software, and omnichannel activities in varying proportions.
e: The frozen LMM firm count uses size classes and a broader-sector margin bridge, so firms near the band boundaries may be misclassified.
s5: Broad marketing-services activity is led by digital capabilities and does not measure the direct-mail transfer rate.
s5: Public announcements omit many private transactions and provide no eligible-firm denominator.
s5: One cited transaction was an asset sale and may not represent a whole-company control transfer.
q: No public source reports the billing mix or post-automation benefit retention of the narrowed population.
q: Postage and physical production costs can dominate campaign economics but vary sharply by format, volume, and presort qualification.
q: Price competition may convert productivity into richer personalization or faster turnaround rather than reported margin.
d5: Postal piece volume is not constant-quality demand for integrated direct-mail services.
d5: The 2021 comparison was influenced by pandemic recovery and election mail.
d5: Buyer and consumer surveys were sponsored by a direct-mail automation vendor and may not represent total US advertising demand.
o: No source directly measures future operator-required quantity for the narrowed lens.
o: Software vendors may themselves perform the accountable operator role, making the boundary between self-service and outsourced service unstable.
o: Physical production needs can persist even when campaign strategy and execution are brought in-house.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business, nonprofit, and government customers buy managed campaigns through project, campaign, per-piece, and recurring program arrangements. Provider revenue can include creative and data fees, production charges, fulfillment fees, and marked-up or passed-through print and postage costs.
lens.deviation_from_default: The industry definition permits campaign creation and data work, physical mailing preparation, or both. The lens is narrowed to integrated managed-service providers because creative-only and letter-shop-only firms have materially different labor, capital, contract, and displacement economics; the narrowing is for analytical coherence rather than attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: Standalone creative agencies, pure list brokers, commodity printers, fulfillment-only warehouses, delivery-only businesses, one-off campaign vendors without repeat customer relationships, captive units, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market integrated direct-mail providers that repeatedly manage customer campaigns and data while also preparing, producing, or orchestrating physical mail delivery. Included work covers campaign design, list preparation, personalization, print coordination, letter-shop operations, postal preparation, tracking, and related fulfillment when sold as one managed service.

a.bridge: The reported occupation mix for NAICS 54186 is combined with O*NET task descriptions for its prominent creative, market-research, sales, and physical-mail occupations, then translated into a wage-weighted share of currently manual tasks for integrated providers. The mapping likely overstates exposure where the reported mix represents creative-only firms and understates exposure where production planning and administrative work are hidden in broad occupational groups.
caveats: The occupation distribution is published at NAICS 54186 and is not isolated to the narrowed integrated-provider lens.
O*NET describes occupations across industries and does not measure AI substitution or wage-weighted exposure.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input combines nonmatching QCEW and SUSB vintages and is harmonized through a cross-industry adjustment.
a.change_evidence: Obtain six-digit OEWS employment and wage weights, then conduct task-level time studies at integrated providers that separate already automated workflows from remaining manual work across creative, data, sales, production, and administration.
a.rationale: Creative layout, copy development, list cleaning, segmentation, audience research, campaign analysis, proposals, estimates, proofs, and account paperwork are exposed to generative and analytical AI. Client discovery, relationship selling, final creative judgment, production supervision, equipment operation, material handling, defect inspection, and postal handoff are less substitutable. The frozen labor share is modest because print, postage, materials, and equipment also matter economically.

d5.bridge: USPS Marketing Mail piece volume is used as a physical-demand proxy and is tempered by direct-mail buyer and consumer surveys. Marketing Mail includes pieces not produced by the narrowed providers, while provider demand also includes creative, data, and fulfillment value per piece; therefore volume decline may overstate service-demand contraction, whereas vendor-sponsored budget intentions may overstate growth.
caveats: Postal piece volume is not constant-quality demand for integrated direct-mail services.
The 2021 comparison was influenced by pandemic recovery and election mail.
Buyer and consumer surveys were sponsored by a direct-mail automation vendor and may not represent total US advertising demand.
d5.change_evidence: Acquire Census Service Annual Survey receipts for NAICS 541860, deflate service fees separately from postage and print inputs, and pair them with USPS Marketing Mail volume by commercial category, client retention cohorts, campaign frequency, and independent advertiser budget forecasts.
d5.rationale: USPS Marketing Mail volume fell from its 2021 level through 2025 and declined again in the latest annual comparison, while postage increases and digital alternatives pressure physical quantity. Countervailing evidence shows strong buyer intent, perceived cross-channel value, consumer attention, and improved targeting. The base case assumes continuing piece-volume erosion partly offset by higher-value personalization and integrated campaign services.

caveats: No public source measures the share of LMM firms matching the narrowed integrated-provider lens.
Industry classification can include adjacent print, fulfillment, software, and omnichannel activities in varying proportions.
The frozen LMM firm count uses size classes and a broader-sector margin bridge, so firms near the band boundaries may be misclassified.
e.change_evidence: Build a firm-level sample from Census Business Register or commercial databases and verify each candidate's service integration, repeat-customer revenue, recurring program revenue, customer concentration, owner dependence, and normalized EBITDA through websites, interviews, and quality-of-earnings materials.
e.rationale: Full-service direct mail is an identified industry offering, and repeat campaigns can produce durable client relationships. The narrowed lens excludes creative-only shops, commodity printers, letter shops without campaign management, fulfillment-only operations, and businesses dependent on one-off jobs. Public product data do not reveal how firms or revenue split across these models.
