caveats: No source directly measures future operator-required quantity for the narrowed lens.
Software vendors may themselves perform the accountable operator role, making the boundary between self-service and outsourced service unstable.
Physical production needs can persist even when campaign strategy and execution are brought in-house.
o.change_evidence: Interview buyers that renewed, reduced, or ended outsourced direct-mail relationships and measure spend shifted to self-service software or in-house teams; compare those findings with provider churn, scope compression, software attach rates, and fully automated campaign volumes.
o.rationale: Brands can increasingly self-serve targeting, creative variants, triggers, and campaign measurement through software. An integrated operator remains useful for data hygiene, postal preparation, physical production, material handling, quality control, regulatory and brand approvals, complex personalization, exception resolution, and accountable coordination across channels and vendors. Routine campaigns face the greatest self-service risk.

caveats: No public source reports the billing mix or post-automation benefit retention of the narrowed population.
Postage and physical production costs can dominate campaign economics but vary sharply by format, volume, and presort qualification.
Price competition may convert productivity into richer personalization or faster turnaround rather than reported margin.
q.change_evidence: Review customer contracts, bids, and invoice histories from integrated providers to quantify revenue by fee type, pass-through treatment, labor-hour billing, renewal repricing, contribution margin, automation-linked capacity gains, and customer givebacks.
q.rationale: Per-piece and fixed campaign pricing can preserve near-term savings from faster creative, targeting, proofing, and job setup. Competitive rebids, transparent postage and print inputs, volume commitments, and customer expectations for more personalization will share gains over renewals. Benefits in pass-through postage and materials do not belong to the operator, while internal overhead and throughput gains are more retainable.

rho.bridge: AI-use evidence from US advertising executives and automation expectations from direct-mail buyer surveys are translated into the share of exposed labor opportunity that integrated US providers can implement within five years. Buyer adoption may overstate provider-side labor realization, while the advertising-wide sample may overstate creative adoption relative to production-heavy firms; persistent manual workflow evidence offsets those upward biases.
caveats: The IAB population spans advertising rather than direct mail alone.
The direct-mail survey represents marketers and operational-mail leaders, not a payroll-weighted panel of providers.
Vendor-sponsored research may select technology-forward respondents and emphasize automation benefits.
rho.change_evidence: Survey integrated NAICS 541860 operators by workflow and measure production deployment, model-review time, exception rates, customer approval requirements, realized hours avoided, implementation failures, and the share of gains attributable to prior non-AI automation.
rho.rationale: Advertising firms already use AI for creative content, audience targeting, support, and predictive analytics, and direct-mail leaders expect greater automation. Integrated providers can embed models in segmentation, versioning, proofing, job setup, and measurement. Data quality, customer approvals, privacy, brand safety, legacy production systems, postal rules, and physical exception handling constrain full implementation.

s5.bridge: Marketing-services transaction trends and three recent acquisitions involving direct-mail, mailing, or integrated print-and-mail providers are used to infer a five-year control-transfer probability for eligible firms. The broad market data favor digital targets, while the exact examples are selected announcements without a denominator; the first effect may understate direct-mail-specific succession supply and the second may overstate market frequency.
caveats: Broad marketing-services activity is led by digital capabilities and does not measure the direct-mail transfer rate.
Public announcements omit many private transactions and provide no eligible-firm denominator.
One cited transaction was an asset sale and may not represent a whole-company control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: Use PitchBook, GF Data, CapIQ, and Graphic Arts Advisors records to identify US control transfers matching the exact code and narrowed lens, classify platform versus add-on deals, and divide them by a verified eligible-firm panel; supplement with owner-age and succession interviews.
s5.rationale: Marketing-services deal activity is active, sponsor participation is rising, and buyers have recently acquired integrated direct-mail and mailing capabilities. Strategic interest favors technology, recurring revenue, low customer concentration, secure production, and omnichannel integration. Commodity capacity, secular volume pressure, and customer concentration reduce transferability for weaker operators.

records.principal_driver: AI can compress the creative and data-intensive front end while an integrated provider continues to coordinate consequential physical execution.
records.principal_weakness: Declining postal volume and customer self-service can erode the service basket before productivity gains become durable owner economics.

caveats: a: The BLS occupation table covers all NAICS 541800 advertising-related services and is dominated by business and creative models unlike physical distribution.
a: Commercial vendor pages demonstrate available workflows but do not measure adoption or saved wage hours across the industry.

a: Field distributors and brand ambassadors may be independent contractors; their labor is omitted from the injected payroll-based compensation ratio, potentially understating the economically relevant labor base.
rho: The sources describe available software and one large staffing operator, not representative realized savings.
rho: Implementation may improve quality and verification more than it reduces payroll.
rho: This range excludes customer repricing and demand substitution, which are captured in q, d5, and o.
e: No public source measures repeat-customer eligibility among lower-middle-market 541870 firms.
e: Classification boundaries with direct mail, printing, experiential marketing, merchandise demonstration, and full-service agencies can be porous.

e: The injected firm count uses a generic service-sector margin bridge; contractor-heavy networks, bundled printing, and campaign seasonality can materially misclassify firms into the target EBITDA band.
s5: The owner-age evidence is broad and based on 2018 data, not this industry.
s5: The M&A evidence covers marketing services broadly and is tilted toward digital and technology-enabled targets.
s5: Asset sales, team lift-outs, and customer-book purchases may not preserve a transferable operating company.
q: The pricing evidence comes from individual vendors and may not represent negotiated lower-middle-market contracts.
q: The relative mix of flyer delivery and live product sampling within 541870 is unknown.
q: The range concerns commercial sharing of implemented benefit and does not duplicate implementation or volume risk.
d5: BLS publishes output only for NAICS 541800, whose growth includes digital and agency services.
d5: Capstone measures broad marketing trends and transactions rather than physical distribution volume.
d5: Political cycles, retail foot traffic, event schedules, paper costs, local solicitation rules, and economic conditions can cause large short-run swings.
o: No public evidence directly measures self-service substitution for NAICS 541870.
o: Operator need is higher for regulated product sampling and national rollouts than for simple local flyer drops.
o: This estimate does not count demand eliminated by digital advertising a second time; that volume effect is in d5.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers are local businesses, franchises, consumer brands, agencies, political campaigns, and event operators. Revenue is usually campaign-based and quoted by pieces or locations delivered, through bundled print-and-distribution packages, or by field-staff hours plus coordination and management fees. Repeat revenue comes from recurring local promotions, launches, sampling programs, seasonal campaigns, and multi-market rollouts.
lens.excluded_activities: Direct mail advertising; advertising campaign creation and media placement; merchandise demonstrations beyond sample handout; distribution of advertising specialties; publishers and digital media; printing-only businesses; captive internal field teams; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US firms providing repeat outsourced physical distribution of advertising materials or samples for external customers, including door-to-door circular, flyer, coupon, and door-hanger delivery; hand-to-hand street distribution; and sample handout programs in retail or public locations, together with directly supporting routing, staffing, verification, and reporting.

a.bridge: BLS publishes the occupation mix for four-digit advertising, public relations, and related services, not six-digit advertising-material distribution. I mapped partial exposure in management, marketing, sales, administrative, project, dispatch, and reporting work to quoting, route design, staff matching, scheduling, customer communication, creative adaptation, proof review, and campaign reporting, then sharply reduced the result because 541870's defining work is physical delivery or sample handout. The four-digit mix contains far more creative and professional staff than this industry, so it likely biases exposure upward; widespread contractor field labor omitted from payroll can also make the wage-weighted construct unrepresentative.
caveats: The BLS occupation table covers all NAICS 541800 advertising-related services and is dominated by business and creative models unlike physical distribution.
Commercial vendor pages demonstrate available workflows but do not measure adoption or saved wage hours across the industry.

Field distributors and brand ambassadors may be independent contractors; their labor is omitted from the injected payroll-based compensation ratio, potentially understating the economically relevant labor base.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-linked time studies from 20 to 30 independent 541870 firms, separating field employees, contractors, dispatch, account management, creative, and administration and identifying tasks already automated, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: AI can reduce information work around campaigns, while route and proof-of-delivery software already automate parts of dispatch, tracking, and reporting. It cannot presently replace the walking, driving, material handling, or face-to-face sample handout that defines the service. [S1, S2, S7, S8]

d5.bridge: BLS projects four-digit advertising, public relations, and related services real output to grow 2.7 percent annually from 2024 to 2034, but Capstone says sector participants and spending are migrating from traditional media toward embedded and interactive digital channels. I use broad advertising growth only as an upper anchor, then apply a substantial channel-shift discount to the current physical distribution basket. Broad output likely overstates 541870 demand because digital drives growth; Capstone's deal-oriented framing may understate resilient local flyer, political, and product-sampling uses.
caveats: BLS publishes output only for NAICS 541800, whose growth includes digital and agency services.
Capstone measures broad marketing trends and transactions rather than physical distribution volume.
Political cycles, retail foot traffic, event schedules, paper costs, local solicitation rules, and economic conditions can cause large short-run swings.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-dollar six-digit receipts or a representative operator panel reporting pieces delivered, sampling shifts, repeat orders, customer vertical, and lost budgets by destination channel would materially change the demand ratio.
d5.rationale: Physical delivery retains niche advantages for local services, launches, political campaigns, events, and product trial, but advertiser demand for measurable short paths to purchase continues to favor digital channels. The base therefore assumes moderate real contraction, with wide bounds for cyclical ad budgets and a potentially stronger experiential-sampling mix. [S3, S4, S7, S9]

caveats: No public source measures repeat-customer eligibility among lower-middle-market 541870 firms.
Classification boundaries with direct mail, printing, experiential marketing, merchandise demonstration, and full-service agencies can be porous.

The injected firm count uses a generic service-sector margin bridge; contractor-heavy networks, bundled printing, and campaign seasonality can materially misclassify firms into the target EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: A current establishment-to-firm roster with revenue by physical distribution activity, customer concentration, repeat-customer share, independence, contractor model, and normalized EBITDA would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is already an outsourced delivery service, and vendor examples show campaigns for many external customers, so most true lower-middle-market firms should fit the recurring-or-repeat service lens. The haircut removes captive units, operators dependent on a single temporary campaign, misclassified printing or agency businesses, firms without transferable field operations, and establishments whose economics do not support normalized target-band EBITDA. [S1, S7, S9]

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: The Census definition and current vendor workflows show that the service quantity consists of placing physical materials or samples, while software handles route assignment, GPS proof, staffing communication, and reporting. No source measures future operator requirement. I infer that most surviving year-five demand still needs an accountable operator to recruit and supervise field labor, manage materials and permissions, verify completion, handle exceptions, and stand behind campaign results. Platform marketplaces could let customers self-serve more work and bias this estimate upward; compliance and accountability needs could make the low case too pessimistic.
caveats: No public evidence directly measures self-service substitution for NAICS 541870.
Operator need is higher for regulated product sampling and national rollouts than for simple local flyer drops.
This estimate does not count demand eliminated by digital advertising a second time; that volume effect is in d5.
o.change_evidence: Customer procurement surveys and operator cohorts separating work lost to direct gig-platform booking, customer insourcing, software-only self-service, and competing physical operators would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Digital substitution reduces the quantity demanded in d5, but the physical campaigns that remain cannot be supplied entirely by software. Some brands can hire gig workers directly through marketplaces, yet multi-location execution, insurance, no-show coverage, training, inventory control, and proof of performance generally preserve an operator role. [S1, S7, S8, S9]

q.bridge: Two current vendor pages show different adjacent pricing constructs: flyer delivery quoted per piece with volume pricing and optional bundled printing, and product-sampling staff priced by labor hour plus management and coordination. I infer that per-piece and bundled campaigns initially retain routing, scheduling, and administrative savings, while hourly staffing and transparent coordination charges pass more benefit to clients. Vendor list prices do not reveal realized discounts, gross margins, renewal behavior, or the contract mix; advertised pricing may therefore overstate retention if competition is intense.
caveats: The pricing evidence comes from individual vendors and may not represent negotiated lower-middle-market contracts.
The relative mix of flyer delivery and live product sampling within 541870 is unknown.
The range concerns commercial sharing of implemented benefit and does not duplicate implementation or volume risk.
q.change_evidence: A sample of 150 recent contracts and renewals from 20 firms, coded by per-piece, fixed campaign, bundled, hourly, cost-plus, management-fee, discount, and rebid terms and matched to realized margin, would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Value capture is moderate to low. Fixed campaign and per-piece prices can hold during a statement of work, but buyers can benchmark piece rates and staffing hours, rebid frequently, demand better reporting at the same price, or move budget to digital channels. Renewals should therefore share a substantial portion of implemented benefit. [S6, S9]

rho.bridge: Current vendor systems show GPS check-in, route logging, dispatcher synchronization, lead capture, live reporting, and automated data handoff in flyer and sampling operations, but do not measure five-year AI implementation in lower-middle-market 541870 firms. I treat these digital foundations as making workflow AI relatively implementable, while discounting for fragmented systems, thin management teams, inconsistent field data, worker adoption, privacy, client-specific processes, and the need for human exception handling. Technology-forward vendors may overstate industry readiness; inexpensive mobile tools may make the low case too conservative.
caveats: The sources describe available software and one large staffing operator, not representative realized savings.
Implementation may improve quality and verification more than it reduces payroll.
This range excludes customer repricing and demand substitution, which are captured in q, d5, and o.
rho.change_evidence: A 2028 survey of 541870 operators reporting production use, deployment cost, field adoption, error rates, and measured hours saved for quoting, routing, scheduling, proof review, and reporting would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: The exposed work is mostly bounded coordination and information processing that can be added to existing mobile field systems without automating physical delivery. Implementation should therefore be faster than exposure itself, though contractor churn, data quality, and operational exceptions prevent full realization. [S7, S8]

s5.bridge: Census reports broad US employer-owner aging, while Capstone reports active middle-market marketing-services M&A. I translate succession pressure plus adjacent buyer activity into a five-year control-transfer probability for eligible physical-distribution firms, then discount because current acquirers favor digital, attribution, advanced technology, recurring revenue, and low customer concentration rather than labor-intensive flyer delivery. Broad owner age may overstate succession for institutionally owned firms, and broad marketing deal flow likely overstates liquidity for 541870 specialists.
caveats: The owner-age evidence is broad and based on 2018 data, not this industry.
The M&A evidence covers marketing services broadly and is tilted toward digital and technology-enabled targets.
Asset sales, team lift-outs, and customer-book purchases may not preserve a transferable operating company.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, Capital IQ, BizBuySell, and GF Data transactions matched to a verified roster of 541870 firms, plus owner-age and succession interviews, would materially change the transfer probability.
s5.rationale: Owner succession and marketing-services consolidation create some availability, but small physical-distribution operators can be founder-dependent, contractor-heavy, locally concentrated, and lightly differentiated. Those features reduce qualifying transfers even when owners wish to exit. [S4, S5]

records.principal_driver: AI can cheaply improve routing, staffing coordination, verification, and reporting while surviving physical campaigns still require accountable field execution.
records.principal_weakness: The current service basket faces persistent digital substitution and price transparency, limiting both five-year demand and retained efficiency gains.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as direct physical distribution or delivery of advertisements or samples, including door-to-door, windshield, and retail-store handout methods, and identifies adjacent excluded activities.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual: 541870 Advertising Material Distribution Services

sources.supports: Provides the nearest published occupation and wage mix for advertising, public relations, and related services used as a broad task-exposure proxy.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 541800

sources.supports: Projects advertising, public relations, and related services employment from 497,700 to 509,600 and real output from $197.3 billion to $257.3 billion, a 2.7 percent annual output increase.
sources.title: BLS Industry Employment and Output Projections, 2024-2034

sources.supports: Reports migration from traditional media toward interactive digital channels and active marketing-services M&A, with buyer preference for recurring revenue, low customer concentration, attribution, and technology-enabled offerings.
sources.title: Marketing Services Market Update - April 2026

sources.supports: Provides broad US employer-business owner-age evidence from the 2019 Annual Business Survey for use only as a succession proxy.

sources.supports: Shows adjacent product-sampling and flyer-distribution field staffing sold by labor hour, with management and coordination added to the staffing budget.

records.title: Advertising Material Distribution Services

caveats: a: The occupational mix is an arithmetic residual for NAICS 624110 plus 624190, not a direct six-digit observation.
a: Occupations and duties are translated into wage-weighted task exposure rather than directly measured as such.
rho: The Census source covers all industries and firm sizes, not social services.
rho: Crisis-service technology demonstrates digital workflow adoption but not autonomous AI implementation.
e: No direct denominator of firms satisfying the narrowed recurrence, control, independence, and size criteria was found.
e: The frozen LMM firm count is based on an estimated 8% margin in a heterogeneous, often grant-funded social-assistance industry.
s5: Gallup measures intentions for all employer businesses, not completed transfers in this industry.
s5: BizBuySell's broad service category is commercial and owner-operated, unlike much of NAICS 624190.
q: The source covers the entire charitable nonprofit sector, not NAICS 624190 or the eligible subset.
q: Indirect-cost reimbursement rules describe cost recovery, not direct measurement of AI-benefit retention.
d5: Occupation projections span multiple industries and are not a measure of NAICS 624190 real output.
d5: 988 is only one crisis-service segment and its volume is not representative of all included activities.
o: Occupation duties cover social workers and assistants across industries, not only the narrowed lens.
o: The industry spans low-risk information referral and high-risk crisis or counseling work, so operator requirement varies substantially.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue can come from cost-reimbursement or fixed-price government contracts, grants with service obligations, institutional or employer agreements, philanthropy, and client fees. Delivery ranges from recurring case-management and counseling caseloads to continuously staffed crisis lines and multi-program service centers.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines materially different models, including fee-paid counseling, government-contracted case management, crisis contact centers, community-action agencies, self-help organizations, and multi-purpose welfare centers. The screen is narrowed to organizations with an ongoing paid or contract-backed duty to external clients or funders; purely charitable, advocacy, volunteer, or one-time assistance models are excluded because their transferability and value-capture economics are not coherent with accountable recurring service providers.
lens.excluded_activities: Programs specifically classified as child and youth services, elderly or disability services, residential care, outpatient mental health practices, emergency food or housing relief, government-operated offices, captive internal programs, non-control financing vehicles, and activities consisting chiefly of one-time charitable aid, volunteer self-help, advocacy, or information publication without an accountable recurring service relationship.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market organizations that remain accountable for recurring or repeat case management, family or marriage counseling outside mental-health-practitioner offices, crisis and hotline coverage, personal and social-problem referral, offender rehabilitation, parenting support, or multi-program family services supplied under ongoing contracts, service plans, memberships, or repeat engagements.

a.bridge: BLS does not publish a six-digit occupational table for NAICS 624190. Subtracting same-vintage NAICS 624120 employment from broader NAICS 624100 leaves a residual that combines NAICS 624110 and 624190: 707,480 total jobs, including about 43.5% community and social service, 12.1% office support, 10.6% management, 5.9% business and financial operations, and 5.8% healthcare support. I map research, referral, paperwork, recordkeeping, drafting, scheduling, reporting, information retrieval, and routine case coordination within this knowledge-heavy residual to current AI exposure, with wage weighting increasing the influence of managerial and professional work. Including Child and Youth Services may overstate interpersonal safeguarding work and understate hotline or referral-center information work relative to 624190; direction is therefore uncertain.
caveats: The occupational mix is an arithmetic residual for NAICS 624110 plus 624190, not a direct six-digit observation.
Occupations and duties are translated into wage-weighted task exposure rather than directly measured as such.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit occupational table plus a time-and-motion study separating client conversation, crisis judgment, travel, research, documentation, referrals, applications, supervision, fundraising, compliance, and administration would materially change the range.
a.rationale: A large share of labor involves text, records, eligibility and resource research, treatment or service plans, referrals, reporting, follow-up, and administrative coordination, all of which have meaningful current AI exposure. Exposure is not equivalent to replacement: counseling, crisis assessment, trust building, safeguarding, advocacy, complex judgment, and accountable decisions remain human-intensive.

d5.bridge: BLS projects both social workers and social and human service assistants across all industries to grow 6% from 2024 to 2034, while noting demand from social-service needs, substance-use treatment, and aging but constraints from state and local budgets. SAMHSA reports more than 8 million 988 contacts in 2025, showing large current crisis-service demand. I translate the occupational ten-year growth into modest five-year real service growth and use crisis volume as evidence of a durable need rather than a growth rate. Employment may understate unmet need when budgets or labor constrain delivery, but may overstate constant-quality demand if documentation or staffing intensity rises.
caveats: Occupation projections span multiple industries and are not a measure of NAICS 624190 real output.
988 is only one crisis-service segment and its volume is not representative of all included activities.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price revenue, caseload, contact, waitlist, referral, and contracted-capacity data for NAICS 624190, separated by service model and payer, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Family stress, housing and benefit navigation, mental-health and substance-use crises, reentry, immigration support, and complex public-program eligibility sustain demand. Realized service growth is limited by appropriations, grants, reimbursement, charitable funding, and institutional procurement, making demand more policy-sensitive than underlying need.

caveats: No direct denominator of firms satisfying the narrowed recurrence, control, independence, and size criteria was found.
The frozen LMM firm count is based on an estimated 8% margin in a heterogeneous, often grant-funded social-assistance industry.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census linking six-digit classification, ownership and tax status, revenue by contract or client, recurring caseload, normalized EBITDA or unrestricted surplus, customer concentration, and independence would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: Some LMM organizations have recurring contract-backed case-management, counseling, hotline, referral, or institutional service economics that fit the narrowed lens. Many establishments are nonprofits, grant-dependent community programs, volunteer self-help groups, advocacy-oriented agencies, government affiliates, or multi-purpose centers without transferable commercial economics; others may lack normalized EBITDA in the target band even when receipts are substantial.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 624190 ($26,228,520,417) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 624190 ($53,725,819,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.0800 (margins.json[624]: ESTIMATE: social assistance runs thin, largely grant/Medicaid-funded; 8% placeholder chosen before deriving results); revenue/employee $119,934 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 624190); class <5: 16,734 firms, avg revenue/firm $213,331 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,067 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 3,487 firms, avg revenue/firm $733,061 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $58,645 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 2,509 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,486,026 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $118,882 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 3,131 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,421,919 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $353,754 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 1,356 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,045,131 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $963,610 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 421 firms, avg revenue/firm $32,525,131 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,602,010 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 421

o.bridge: BLS describes social and human service work as assessing needs, researching benefits and services, coordinating care, completing paperwork, checking outcomes, counseling, crisis response, monitoring, and recordkeeping. SAMHSA's 988 model already delivers services through call, text, chat, automated routing prompts, and a network of more than 200 local crisis centers, but connects users to trained counselors. I map routine information, referral, form, navigation, and first-line triage tasks to potential self-service or software supply while retaining accountable operators for counseling, safeguarding, eligibility judgments, crisis assessment, mandated reporting, complex advocacy, and service continuity. The occupational sources may overstate operator need for information-only cases; the crisis example may overstate safety-critical human requirements for routine referral work.
caveats: Occupation duties cover social workers and assistants across industries, not only the narrowed lens.
The industry spans low-risk information referral and high-risk crisis or counseling work, so operator requirement varies substantially.
o.change_evidence: Service-line data comparing human-handled, digitally self-served, AI-assisted, abandoned, escalated, and successfully resolved cases, with safety and equity outcomes, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: A meaningful minority of current demand can migrate to self-guided benefit tools, resource directories, automated intake, digital forms, and AI-assisted navigation. Most formal demand still requires an accountable organization because clients have complex or sensitive circumstances, funders require documentation and supervision, and crisis, counseling, safeguarding, and multi-agency coordination create liability and judgment that software alone cannot assume within five years.

q.bridge: The National Council of Nonprofits reports that the nonprofit sector earns about one-third of revenue under written government agreements and that federally funded government arrangements must reimburse reasonable indirect costs. I use this broad evidence to model a substantial cost-reimbursement, grant-budget, and administered-contract share in individual and family services, where documented savings may be reflected in future budgets, allowable costs, renewal pricing, or service-volume expectations rather than retained as operator profit. The nonprofit-wide revenue share may understate government dependence in public social services but overstate it for fee-paid counseling and commercial employee-assistance providers.
caveats: The source covers the entire charitable nonprofit sector, not NAICS 624190 or the eligible subset.
Indirect-cost reimbursement rules describe cost recovery, not direct measurement of AI-benefit retention.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level data identifying cost-reimbursement, fixed-price, outcome-based, grant, donation, and client-fee revenue, followed by renewal pricing and unrestricted-margin outcomes after measured productivity gains, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Operators can retain value from higher case capacity, faster response, cleaner reports, lower administrative burden, improved contract compliance, fundraising productivity, and reduced avoidable hiring. Retention is constrained when grants or cost-reimbursement contracts define allowable costs, funders rebase budgets, nonprofits reinvest surplus into mission, and clients or institutional buyers expect lower fees or more service. Fixed-price contracts and differentiated fee-paid services offer greater capture.

rho.bridge: Census's nationally representative 2026 AI supplement reports 18% firm adoption, 32% employment-weighted adoption, limited use in three or fewer functions for 57% of adopters, and worker-task use at 23% of firms. I project those cross-industry adoption and depth measures over five years for exposed casework, contact-center, counseling-support, and administrative workflows. Broad adoption likely overstates readiness among thinly funded nonprofits and small social-service providers, while the text-heavy workflow and embedding of AI into case-management, contact-center, grants, and office software may make it understate eventual implementation.
caveats: The Census source covers all industries and firm sizes, not social services.
Crisis-service technology demonstrates digital workflow adoption but not autonomous AI implementation.
rho.change_evidence: A provider survey or case-management and crisis-platform telemetry showing production AI use, human review, error and escalation rates, funder acceptance, and labor hours saved by workflow would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Implementation should spread in intake summarization, resource search, referral matching, application support, case-note drafting, quality checks, translation, contract reporting, grants, scheduling, and supervised digital triage. Privacy, bias, client consent, record retention, licensing, funder rules, crisis safety, fragmented data, weak budgets, and the need for accountable human review prevent full implementation.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. BizBuySell reports 5,839 completed service-business sales analyzed over 2021-2025 and a median 170 days on market, demonstrating broad service-business liquidity. I discount the owner-intention rate for eligible NAICS 624190 organizations because nonprofit governance changes, internal affiliations, grant novations, license or contract approvals, founder dependence, and charitable asset restrictions often do not produce a qualifying control transfer. Gallup intentions overstate completed deals, while BizBuySell's owner-operator marketplace underrepresents nonprofit combinations and privately negotiated provider transactions.
caveats: Gallup measures intentions for all employer businesses, not completed transfers in this industry.
BizBuySell's broad service category is commercial and owner-operated, unlike much of NAICS 624190.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year cohort of eligible NAICS 624190 organizations recording owner or board changes, asset sales, mergers, contract novations, licenses, buyer type, and failed transactions would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Transfer activity is plausible for for-profit counseling, case-management, employee or family-assistance, and outsourced contact-center providers with transferable contracts and management depth. Availability is lower than in ordinary services because much of the code is nonprofit or funder-dependent and client trust, professional staff, government contracts, and restricted assets can be difficult to transfer.

records.principal_driver: The principal driver is a text-heavy, repeat casework and coordination workflow in which AI can expand capacity while an accountable provider preserves trust, compliance, and escalation.
records.principal_weakness: The principal weakness is the code's mixture of nonprofit, grant-funded, volunteer, crisis, counseling, and government-contracted models, many of which lack transferable control economics or durable benefit retention.

caveats: The labor-exposure primitive is built from an accurately calculated but non-six-digit residual combining NAICS 624110 and 624190, then maps occupations and duties to a wage-weighted AI-exposure range without direct task-time evidence.

The five-year implementation value projects national, cross-industry AI adoption into thinly funded and privacy-sensitive social-service workflows; S10 demonstrates multichannel digital delivery and human counseling, not production AI adoption.

Eligibility and the frozen firm-count anchor remain especially uncertain because no firm-level denominator supports the narrowed recurring, independent, transferable subset and n relies on an estimated 8% margin.

The transfer probability is a judgmental discount from broad employer-owner intentions and small owner-operated service-business transactions, not observed control transfers among eligible NAICS 624190 organizations.

The retention value is only indirectly informed by nonprofit-wide government-contract revenue and indirect-cost rules; neither source measures AI benefit retention or the payer mix of the narrowed subset.

The demand and operator-required-share values use broad occupational projections and one crisis-service segment; they do not directly measure five-year real output or software substitution across the heterogeneous industry.

S10 could not be fetched directly during review, and its indexed official text supports the main volume, channel, network, and counselor claims but not the bridge's reference to automated routing prompts.

sources_audited.note: The May 2023 BLS NAICS 624100 table reports 2,983,600 total jobs and the cited major-group employment counts used in the occupational residual.
The same-vintage BLS NAICS 624120 table reports 2,276,120 total jobs and the cited major-group counts; subtraction from S2 reproduces the 707,480-job residual and stated shares to rounding.
BLS describes needs assessment, service research, coordination, paperwork, and follow-up duties and projects 6% employment growth for social and human service assistants from 2024 to 2034.

BLS describes assessment, referral, crisis response, monitoring, recordkeeping, counseling, and licensure and projects 6% overall social-worker employment growth from 2024 to 2034, with budget constraints noted for child and family social work.

The Census working-paper page identifies nationally representative 2026 BTOS supplement data and reports 18% firm adoption, 32% employment-weighted adoption, limited functional use among 57% of adopters, and worker-task use at 23% of firms.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership in the next five years; the source is broad succession-intention evidence rather than completed industry transactions.
BizBuySell reports 5,839 sold service-business listings analyzed, a 170-day median time on market, and transaction benchmarks for service businesses over 2021-2025.

The National Council of Nonprofits states that the nonprofit sector earns about one-third of revenue under written government agreements and that reasonable indirect costs must be reimbursed where federal dollars are in the funding stream.

Direct reopening returned HTTP 403, but the indexed official SAMHSA page confirms more than 8 million 2025 contacts by call, text, chat, and ASL videophone, a network of more than 200 local centers, and access to skilled counselors. It does not substantiate the bridge's added reference to automated routing prompts.

records.title: Other Individual and Family Services

caveats: a: No six-digit 541840 task or occupation table was found; NAICS 541800 includes advertising agencies, public relations, and other business models.
a: Salesforce respondents span countries, industries, and firm sizes and may use a broader definition of AI than labor substitution.
a: The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and therefore has a vintage mismatch outside this primitive estimate.
rho: Experimentation is not equivalent to operational implementation or realized labor avoidance.
rho: The evidence does not isolate lower-middle-market independent media representatives.
e: The sources define the industry but do not measure the eligible share within the lower-middle-market band.
e: The injected count of 50 is an estimate from a SUSB size and margin bridge rather than an observed roster of qualifying firms.
e: A commission stream can recur while still being fragile if tied to one media owner or one rainmaker.
s5: Gallup reports owner intentions across all employer industries, not realized 541840 control transfers.
s5: Capstone's marketing-services category is broader than media representatives and can include more scalable digital agencies.
s5: The small estimated LMM population makes realized five-year counts volatile.
q: No source directly measures five-year sharing of AI-derived savings in this industry.
q: The range is highly sensitive to whether savings expand selling capacity or merely reduce service effort visible to media-owner principals.
q: Customer and principal concentration could accelerate benefit pass-through at renewal.
d5: Advertising spend is not the same as demand for independent representation and includes inventory sold directly or through platforms.
d5: The channel mix of lower-middle-market 541840 firms is unobserved.
d5: One-year nominal growth rates and event-year forecasts are weak anchors for a five-year constant-price ratio.
o: BLS occupational employment is not service quantity and includes advertising sales agents outside independent representative firms.
o: Programmatic growth can complement representatives on complex campaigns rather than fully displace them.
o: Agentic buying and selling could change the operator boundary faster than historical employment projections imply.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The media owner is the represented customer or principal; the representative arranges sales to advertisers and agencies and earns a fee or commission, often with performance-linked sales compensation inside the firm.
lens.excluded_activities: Media buying agencies, advertising agencies, captive sales offices owned by media companies, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms without recurring or repeat outsourced representation relationships.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent representatives that repeatedly sell advertising time or space for media owners across print, television, radio, internet, and other media on a fee or commission basis.

a.bridge: BLS measures tasks for advertising sales agents and the occupation mix of broader NAICS 541800, while Salesforce measures cross-industry sales teams. I translate the clerical, research, proposal, reporting, and account-administration content into a wage-weighted share for 541840, then reduce it because programmatic placement is already automated. Broader advertising-services data likely understate 541840's sales concentration but Salesforce likely overstates applicability to smaller firms, making the net error direction uncertain.
caveats: No six-digit 541840 task or occupation table was found; NAICS 541800 includes advertising agencies, public relations, and other business models.
Salesforce respondents span countries, industries, and firm sizes and may use a broader definition of AI than labor substitution.
The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 QCEW wages with 2022 SUSB receipts and therefore has a vintage mismatch outside this primitive estimate.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-motion study from a representative sample of independent media-representation firms, split by digital versus legacy media and identifying workflows already handled by programmatic systems, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Advertising sales work includes prospect research, estimates, correspondence, proposals, media kits, contracts, account reporting, and competitive monitoring, all materially assistable by AI. The relationship, persuasion, negotiation, and judgment core is less substitutable, and IAB reports that automated programmatic buying is already at scale, so the estimate is for the remaining current task base rather than gross technical exposure.

d5.bridge: IAB measures and forecasts US advertising spending by channel, not constant-price demand for independent representation. I convert strong digital and overall nominal growth into modest real growth for the current cross-media service basket, haircutting for inflation, event-driven cyclicality, and the steep decline in linear and other traditional channels. If 541840 is more legacy-media weighted than assumed, this proxy overstates demand; if it is concentrated in CTV, social, commerce, or creator media, it understates demand.
caveats: Advertising spend is not the same as demand for independent representation and includes inventory sold directly or through platforms.
The channel mix of lower-middle-market 541840 firms is unobserved.
One-year nominal growth rates and event-year forecasts are weak anchors for a five-year constant-price ratio.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of 541840 revenue by represented medium, deflated with channel-appropriate price indices and paired with forward publisher inventory and contract pipelines, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Advertising demand is expanding overall and digital revenue reached 294.6 billion dollars in 2025, but channel dispersion is extreme: IAB expected continued double-digit growth in several digital channels while projecting a 14.4% 2025 decline in linear television. A diversified current basket therefore supports only modest real five-year growth with a wide downside range.

caveats: The sources define the industry but do not measure the eligible share within the lower-middle-market band.
The injected count of 50 is an estimate from a SUSB size and margin bridge rather than an observed roster of qualifying firms.
A commission stream can recur while still being fragile if tied to one media owner or one rainmaker.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level review of the 50 estimated LMM firms using contracts, customer concentration, revenue recurrence, ownership independence, and normalized EBITDA would directly establish eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition itself requires independent representation for external media owners, and the product list describes fee- or commission-based sales-agent services across media. Most firms in the injected LMM band should therefore meet the external-service criterion; the discount allows for one-off brokerage, economically captive arrangements, key-person practices, and firms whose relationships are not genuinely recurring or transferable.

o.bridge: BLS projects advertising-sales-agent employment and describes the limiting effect of automated digital placement, while IAB measures the scale of programmatic buying; neither directly measures the share of year-five media-representation quantity requiring an independent accountable operator. I preserve the relationship-intensive, negotiated, cross-channel, and problem-resolution work identified by BLS while treating standardized placement as increasingly self-served. Employment decline may overstate operator displacement because productivity and occupational reclassification also affect headcount, but platform consolidation may make this estimate too high.
caveats: BLS occupational employment is not service quantity and includes advertising sales agents outside independent representative firms.
Programmatic growth can complement representatives on complex campaigns rather than fully displace them.
Agentic buying and selling could change the operator boundary faster than historical employment projections imply.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with media owners, agencies, and advertisers plus transaction-level data separating direct, programmatic, and representative-mediated sales by channel would materially change the year-five operator-required share.
o.rationale: Programmatic buying reached 162.4 billion dollars in 2025 and automated buying continues to scale. BLS nevertheless describes ongoing work in client discovery, persuasion, tailored recommendations, negotiation, account maintenance, and issue resolution, which supports a continuing operator role for complex and fragmented inventory even as standardized placements move to platforms.

caveats: No source directly measures five-year sharing of AI-derived savings in this industry.
The range is highly sensitive to whether savings expand selling capacity or merely reduce service effort visible to media-owner principals.
Customer and principal concentration could accelerate benefit pass-through at renewal.
q.change_evidence: A contract sample covering commission schedules, renewal and termination clauses, performance rebates, producer compensation, and five-year repricing history across digital and traditional representative firms would materially change the retained-share estimate.
q.rationale: Fee- and commission-based contracts do not mechanically pass labor savings through like cost-plus billing, so an operator can initially retain productivity gains through lower support cost, greater account capacity, or avoided hiring. Over five years, media-owner renewals, commission renegotiation, price competition, producer compensation, and direct platform alternatives should share away a material portion of that benefit.

rho.bridge: Salesforce's cross-industry sales-team adoption and implementation barriers, together with advertising-market evidence of scaled programmatic buying, are used to infer the five-year implementation share of the still-unautomated opportunity in 541840. Mature digital sellers may implement faster than this range, while small legacy-media representatives with fragmented data may implement slower.
caveats: Experimentation is not equivalent to operational implementation or realized labor avoidance.
The evidence does not isolate lower-middle-market independent media representatives.
rho.change_evidence: A 541840 operator survey documenting CRM coverage, programmatic penetration, AI workflow deployment, error rates, training effort, and realized hours saved after 12 months would materially move this value.
rho.rationale: Sales tools are already embedded in a receptive function: 81% of surveyed sales teams were experimenting with or had implemented AI. Implementation is not automatic, however; Salesforce reports resource, training, and data-trust barriers, and media representatives must preserve proposal accuracy, brand safety, pricing controls, and relationship accountability.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year sale or transfer plans for US employer-business owners across industries, while Capstone measures completed and announced M&A activity in the broader marketing-services sector. I haircut Gallup's 22% intent rate because intentions may not close, not every transfer conveys control to an eligible buyer, and key-person media-representation firms may be less transferable; current sector deal growth prevents an even larger haircut. The resulting proxy may still overstate completed qualifying transfers.
caveats: Gallup reports owner intentions across all employer industries, not realized 541840 control transfers.
Capstone's marketing-services category is broader than media representatives and can include more scalable digital agencies.
The small estimated LMM population makes realized five-year counts volatile.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, and broker transaction data tagged to 541840, matched to the eligible-firm roster and distinguishing control sales from internal reorganizations, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Owner succession creates a meaningful transfer pool, and adjacent marketing-services deal volume rose each year from 2023 through 2025. Yet a planned transfer is not a completed qualifying control transaction, and media-representation value can depend heavily on principal contracts and individual relationships.

records.principal_driver: A commission-based sales model lets an operator apply AI to substantial non-selling work while initially retaining much of the productivity gain.
records.principal_weakness: Programmatic and agentic buying can eliminate the independent intermediary, especially for standardized digital inventory, while legacy-media demand continues to contract.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Patient-service revenue paid through Medicaid, commercial insurance, self-pay, grants, and program funding for consultations, examinations, testing, counseling, prescriptions, procedures, fertility services, and related follow-up care.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospital departments, captive health-system units, physician offices classified elsewhere, product-only sellers, shell entities, non-control financing situations, and centers without transferable operations or normalized EBITDA in the target band.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market family planning centers providing recurring or repeat outpatient contraceptive, fertility, pregnancy, genetic, prenatal, sterilization, and medically indicated reproductive-health services to external patients.

a.bridge: Source population/quantity: BLS occupational employment for broad NAICS 621400 and O*NET tasks for medical assistants and registered nurses, interpreted with the official 621410 service definition. Transformation: assigned greater exposure to records, scheduling, billing, intake, follow-up, routine education, and documentation, and lower exposure to licensed judgment, physical examination, specimens, medication, procedures, and safeguarding. Error direction: broad outpatient-center data overweights specialties such as dialysis and surgery relative to family planning, while omission of clerical work embedded in clinical roles may bias exposure downward.
caveats: BLS reports the broader NAICS 621400 outpatient-center workforce rather than 621410 and provides employment shares rather than wage-weighted task hours.
O*NET task descriptions are cross-industry occupational profiles and do not identify tasks already automated in family planning centers.
The six-digit industry mixes counseling-led clinics, fertility centers, and procedure-intensive services with materially different task exposure.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task study from multi-site family planning operators, separating administrative, counseling, diagnostic, procedural, laboratory, and supervisory hours before and after ambient documentation and automated revenue-cycle deployment, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Scheduling, eligibility checks, intake, history capture, coding, billing, prior authorization, results communication, documentation, outreach, and routine education are exposed to AI assistance or avoidable hiring. Examinations, specimen collection, medication administration, device placement, procedures, sensitive counseling, and clinical accountability remain substantially human.

caveats: CDC measures national use and receipt of services across all provider settings, not demand at NAICS 621410 centers.
The current service basket includes contraception, fertility, counseling, and procedures with different demand paths.
Recent utilization snapshots do not establish a five-year constant-price trend, so this primitive remains an estimate.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year constant-price encounter and procedure histories from FPAR, commercial claims, Medicaid T-MSIS, and multi-site family planning and fertility operators, reconciled for site entry and policy changes, would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Family planning remains a broadly used outpatient service, with recurring needs for contraception, prescriptions, testing, counseling, fertility support, and procedures. Demographics, insurance and grant policy, state restrictions, alternative primary-care provision, long-acting methods, and changing fertility intentions create offsetting forces, so the base assumes approximately stable real service-basket demand.

caveats: No representative firm-level source measures the combined recurrence, control, transferability, concentration, compliance, and normalized-earnings screens.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a broad healthcare-facilities margin and may misclassify specialized fertility and grant-supported centers.
The industry definition combines business models with different payer, ownership, and regulatory structures.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level ownership and revenue census covering tax status, health-system affiliation, service mix, payer and grant mix, clinician concentration, licenses, normalized EBITDA, and change-of-control requirements would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The service is outsourced to external patients and often produces repeat visits, follow-up, prescriptions, and procedures. Eligibility is reduced by nonprofit or public control, grant restrictions, hospital affiliation, highly specialized founder-dependent practice, adverse payer mix, political and regulatory exposure, weak transferability, and economics outside the target band.

o.bridge: Source population/quantity: the official 621410 service definition, CDC evidence on services received from medical providers, and O*NET clinical and administrative tasks. Transformation: assigned continued operator need to prescribing, examination, specimens, diagnostics, devices, procedures, consent, and clinical escalation, while discounting for digital education, intake, follow-up, telehealth, pharmacy and primary-care substitution, and home testing. Error direction: using current clinical workflows may overstate operator need as remote care expands, while privacy, safety, licensing, and procedure requirements may keep operator dependence higher.
caveats: Service definitions and occupation tasks establish accountable clinical work but do not measure the fraction that will still require a dedicated center in year five.
Telehealth may preserve a licensed operator while eliminating some physical-site demand.
Substitution differs materially between counseling or prescription visits and device, specimen, fertility, sterilization, or other procedural services.
o.change_evidence: Patient-cohort data tracking migration from dedicated centers to digital-only contraception, primary care, pharmacy prescribing, home testing, and self-service fertility tools, together with outcomes and licensed-review requirements, would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Software can self-serve education, screening, scheduling, history collection, reminders, and some counseling, but most of the current basket still requires a licensed accountable provider for individualized prescribing, examination, specimens, testing, devices, procedures, consent, and escalation. Primary-care substitution and digital contraceptive services can bypass some dedicated-center volume.

caveats: Medicaid's coverage requirement establishes a major administered-payment channel but does not measure the payer mix or retained automation benefit of 621410 firms.
No representative source reports family planning contract repricing, self-pay competition, grant treatment of savings, or post-automation margins.
Retention varies sharply between grant-supported contraceptive clinics and self-pay fertility services.
q.change_evidence: Matched clinic-level gross-margin and reimbursement data before and after automation, separated by Medicaid, commercial insurance, self-pay, Title X or other grants, fertility services, and procedures, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Centers can retain some administrative productivity between payer-contract resets and in self-pay or grant-funded operations, particularly through additional capacity and reduced denials. Retention is limited by administered Medicaid rates, insurer negotiation, grant budgets, patient affordability, competitive fertility pricing, required staffing, and pressure to convert efficiency into access.

caveats: No representative survey measures AI implementation or realized labor-hour reduction in NAICS 621410.
Digitized forms, portal messaging, and scheduling are workflow automation and are not necessarily AI.
Large networks may deploy integrated tools faster than independent or grant-dependent clinics.
rho.change_evidence: Implementation and labor-hour data from Planned Parenthood affiliates, Title X networks, independent fertility groups, and other multi-site family planning operators using ambient documentation, automated intake, coding, and patient-communication tools would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Five years permits meaningful use of ambient notes, automated intake, eligibility and coding support, patient messaging, scheduling, and care-gap outreach. Implementation is restrained by fragmented funding, small-site integration capacity, privacy, clinical validation, state-specific rules, sensitive communications, and the need for licensed review.

s5.bridge: Source population/quantity: Gallup's five-year sale-or-transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners. Transformation: used the reported 22% intention rate as an upper anchor and discounted for noncompletion, internal or family transfers, nonprofit and public ownership, licensure, payer enrollment, grant restrictions, clinician dependence, and diligence failures. Error direction: intentions overstate completed qualifying control sales, while consolidation among transferable fertility and reproductive-health groups could make the lower bound conservative.
caveats: Gallup covers all U.S. employer-business owners and measures intentions rather than completed qualifying healthcare transactions.
Family planning ownership, tax status, and regulatory conditions may make the broad employer-business proxy systematically high.
No source measures five-year control transfers specifically for 621410.
s5.change_evidence: Closed control-sale counts and eligible-center denominators from healthcare transaction databases, family planning and fertility brokers, state change-of-ownership files, and payer enrollment records over a five-year cohort would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners report meaningful five-year sale or transfer intentions, but qualifying control transactions in family planning are reduced by nonprofit and public ownership, clinician dependence, licensure and payer enrollment, grant restrictions, reputation sensitivity, and transaction completion risk.

records.principal_driver: The principal driver is scaling recurring reproductive-health access by automating administrative and documentation work around a trusted clinical endpoint.
records.principal_weakness: The principal weakness is a heterogeneous, policy-sensitive provider pool whose most valuable services still require licensed clinical labor and whose transferable ownership base is poorly measured.

caveats: a: The cited occupation is broader than the industry lens and includes investigators employed in retail, finance, government, and other industries.
a: No source quantifies the current automation baseline or wage-weighted task mix of lower-middle-market screening and investigation firms.
a: Exposure differs sharply between high-volume background screening and surveillance- or interview-heavy investigations.
rho: Regulatory evidence establishes constraints and existing algorithmic products, not representative adoption or realized labor savings.
rho: Large technology-enabled screening providers may implement faster than lower-middle-market firms with legacy systems.
rho: Field investigation and manual source verification have lower implementation potential than standardized database screening.
e: The Census definition lists activities but provides no establishment, revenue, customer, or recurrence shares.
e: CFPB states that hundreds of companies provide employment background checks but does not map them to NAICS 561611 or the EBITDA band.
e: The injected count of 46 depends on a broad Business and Consumer Services margin assumption that may not represent either screening platforms or field-investigation firms.
s5: Gallup covers all US employer businesses and measures intentions rather than completed transactions.
s5: Licensing, data-provider contracts, customer approvals, and owner reputation may limit transferability.
s5: No industry-specific owner-age or completed-deal series was found.
q: No source directly measures five-year retention of AI-derived benefits in independent background-screening or investigation firms.
q: Pricing and pass-through differ materially between standardized checks and hourly bespoke investigations.
q: Data-source fees, cyber controls, accuracy review, and dispute handling may absorb savings that a labor-only view would overstate.
d5: BLS projects an occupation across multiple employing industries rather than outsourced 561611 service quantity.
d5: The demand outlook mixes hiring-sensitive screening with less cyclical fraud, legal, tenant, and monitoring work.
d5: Reported revenue growth could reflect data costs, inflation, or product mix rather than constant-quality service quantity.
o: FCRA obligations do not require every customer to use an independent screening firm.
o: Private-investigator licensing varies by state and applies unevenly to database-based screening.
o: Routine checks may migrate to self-service software even while regulated accountability remains with a consumer reporting agency.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Employers, property managers, insurers, law firms, financial institutions, nonprofits, and other organizations buy reports, verifications, monitoring, or investigations under per-check, subscription, retainer, hourly, or project pricing, with recurring screening volumes and repeat enterprise relationships.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 561611 combines scalable records-based personal background screening with field-heavy private detective and investigative assignments. The lens retains recurring screening and repeat organizational investigation workflows and excludes episodic personal-case work so that data retrieval, verification, exception handling, field investigation, and accountable reporting form a coherent external-service model; the narrowing is for business-model coherence rather than attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: One-off domestic, infidelity, missing-person, or other personal assignments; predominantly episodic individual-client detective work; captive employer loss-prevention or internal investigation units; credit bureaus classified in NAICS 561450; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms providing recurring or repeat outsourced employment, tenant, volunteer, vendor, or other personal background screening; continuous workforce monitoring; and repeat corporate, insurance, fraud, due-diligence, or legal investigation services to external organizations.

a.bridge: BLS and O*NET describe the broader private-detective and investigator occupation, not a wage-weighted task study of recurring 561611 firms. I map database and public-record search, cross-source comparison, routine verification, case summarization, report drafting, and client-status communication to remaining AI exposure, while leaving interviews, surveillance, fingerprint capture, source escalation, legal interpretation, identity resolution, dispute investigation, and evidentiary judgment largely unexposed. The occupation includes captive retail loss prevention and episodic field work, which may understate exposure for screening firms, while screening workflows already automate many easy searches, which may overstate exposure from the published task list; the net error direction is uncertain.
caveats: The cited occupation is broader than the industry lens and includes investigators employed in retail, finance, government, and other industries.
No source quantifies the current automation baseline or wage-weighted task mix of lower-middle-market screening and investigation firms.
Exposure differs sharply between high-volume background screening and surveillance- or interview-heavy investigations.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study from PBSA member screening firms and repeat corporate-investigation providers that separates already automated work from manual database search, verification, adjudication support, disputes, fieldwork, and reporting would materially change this value.
a.rationale: Records retrieval, data normalization, entity matching assistance, document review, and first-draft reporting are digitally tractable. The estimate is below a majority of labor because current screening platforms already automate basic searches, while difficult matches, interviews, field observation, adverse-information verification, disputes, and legally defensible conclusions require human work.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 6% employment growth for private detectives and investigators from 2024 to 2034 and says lawsuits, fraud, crime, interpersonal mistrust, and continuing background-check work support demand; CFPB describes common employer purchase of consumer reports and expansion into current-worker assessments. I translate these occupation- and regulatory-market indicators into modest five-year constant-price service-quantity growth for the narrower recurring outsourced lens, with a wide downside for hiring cycles, insourcing, and automation-driven service compression. Occupational employment includes workers outside 561611 and can move differently from purchased service quantity, so the direction may overstate or understate industry demand.
caveats: BLS projects an occupation across multiple employing industries rather than outsourced 561611 service quantity.
The demand outlook mixes hiring-sensitive screening with less cyclical fraud, legal, tenant, and monitoring work.
Reported revenue growth could reflect data costs, inflation, or product mix rather than constant-quality service quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-price US screening volumes and recurring investigation revenue from PBSA surveys, public-provider filings, major data vendors, and customer procurement records, segmented by employment, tenant, insurance, legal, and corporate use, would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: Hiring, tenant turnover, fraud, litigation, vendor risk, and workforce monitoring sustain repeat screening and investigation demand. Growth is likely moderate because employment screening is cyclical, some customers can self-serve public data, and automation reduces the labor content and price of a check even as the number and scope of checks expand.

caveats: The Census definition lists activities but provides no establishment, revenue, customer, or recurrence shares.
CFPB states that hundreds of companies provide employment background checks but does not map them to NAICS 561611 or the EBITDA band.
The injected count of 46 depends on a broad Business and Consumer Services margin assumption that may not represent either screening platforms or field-investigation firms.
e.change_evidence: A commercial roster of 561611 firms matched to tax records and coded by customer type, recurring revenue share, screening versus detective revenue, ownership independence, normalized EBITDA, and contract transferability would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition includes fingerprint, polygraph, private detective, private investigative, and personal background-check services, so the code spans both repeat organizational work and episodic personal cases. Scaled firms in the injected lower-middle-market band are more likely than sole practitioners to have employer, property-manager, insurer, legal, or corporate accounts, but no published firm-level activity or recurrence mix was found.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).
