caveats: a: No public six-digit occupation mix or wage-weighted task study exists for this heterogeneous industry.
a: The broad maintenance occupation does not capture the exact mix of pool, chimney, duct, drain, gutter, exterior-cleaning, and snow services.
a: The supplied compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
rho: General SME adoption does not measure labor implementation in property-service firms.
rho: Marketing or message generation may increase activity without reducing labor requirements.
rho: Service-line differences in route density, recordkeeping, seasonality, and safety create wide implementation variance.
e: No source measures lens eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
e: Annual or multi-year recommended maintenance is repeatable but not necessarily contractually recurring.
e: The estimated firm count may mix materially different service lines and margin structures.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than this industry or EBITDA band.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed transactions and include potentially nonqualifying transfers.
s5: Transferability likely differs sharply between dense pool routes and owner-expert inspection or project businesses.
q: No public source reports the national revenue-weighted pricing mix for NAICS 561790.
q: The direct billing evidence covers pool service and may not generalize to project-oriented subsegments.
q: Efficiency has no retained value unless paid hours fall, hiring is avoided, or released capacity can be sold.
d5: Occupational employment is not industry service quantity and includes work outside NAICS 561790.
d5: Maintenance recommendations do not prove customers purchase the recommended service or outsource it.
d5: The broad code combines weekly routes, annual or multi-year inspections, discretionary cleaning, emergencies, and weather-dependent services.
o: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the combined industry.
o: Residential customers can self-clean pools, gutters, drains, and exteriors despite safety or quality tradeoffs.
o: Robotic pool cleaners, remote monitoring, and bundled facility services may erode specialist demand unevenly.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External homeowners, property managers, associations, businesses, institutions, and public facilities buy weekly or monthly routes, seasonal programs, annual inspections, periodic maintenance, and one-time cleaning or corrective projects. Billing may be flat monthly, per visit, per asset, hourly, or fixed by quoted scope, with chemical and repair materials either bundled or separately charged.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive facilities staff; pest control, janitorial, landscaping, carpet cleaning, construction, licensed-trade installation, or remediation businesses properly classified elsewhere; software-only monitoring; one-person practices without a transferable operating organization; shells; and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced physical services to buildings and dwellings, including swimming-pool cleaning and maintenance, chimney cleaning and inspection, ventilation-duct cleaning, drain or gutter cleaning, pressure washing and other building-exterior cleaning, commercial kitchen exhaust cleaning, parking-lot cleaning, lighting maintenance, and stand-alone driveway or parking-lot snow removal.

a.bridge: BLS describes general maintenance and repair workers across many industries rather than the occupation mix and wage-weighted tasks of NAICS 561790, while SBA describes generic small-business AI uses. I map on-site cleaning, climbing, inspection, chemical testing, equipment operation, preventive service, diagnosis, and repair to low exposure; I map customer intake, reminders, route support, estimate drafting, record summarization, marketing, purchasing administration, and routine communication to higher exposure. General maintenance includes repairs outside this industry's scope and may overstate diagnostic work, while the industry's pool routes may contain more standardized recordkeeping than the proxy reveals.
caveats: No public six-digit occupation mix or wage-weighted task study exists for this heterogeneous industry.
The broad maintenance occupation does not capture the exact mix of pool, chimney, duct, drain, gutter, exterior-cleaning, and snow services.
The supplied compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
a.change_evidence: A representative NAICS 561790 payroll-weighted time study split by service line and role, measuring field execution, travel, inspection, repair, dispatch, sales, administration, current software automation, and validated AI feasibility, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The Census service basket is dominated by work on physical property. BLS describes maintenance workers inspecting and diagnosing problems, performing preventive service, ordering supplies, estimating costs, keeping records, and using tools at varied sites; only the information and coordination portions are strongly exposed to current AI. SBA identifies repeat administration, customer service, content, and business-data analysis as practical small-business uses. [S1, S2, S3]

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for general maintenance and repair workers across industries rather than constant-price service quantity for NAICS 561790. CDC, CSIA, and NADCA document recurring maintenance needs for selected pool, chimney, and duct services but do not measure market growth or the other included lines. I translate BLS's 4 percent decade employment growth and continuing need for older-property maintenance into modest five-year quantity growth, then widen for housing and commercial occupancy, pool stock, climate and snow variability, construction cycles, customer deferral, self-service, and service-line mix. Employment can understate quantity when equipment raises productivity, while general maintenance and safety guidance can overstate paid outsourcing.
caveats: Occupational employment is not industry service quantity and includes work outside NAICS 561790.
Maintenance recommendations do not prove customers purchase the recommended service or outsource it.
The broad code combines weekly routes, annual or multi-year inspections, discretionary cleaning, emergencies, and weather-dependent services.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census revenue by detailed 561790 product, combined with route counts, installed pool and fireplace bases, building age, service frequency, backlog, customer churn, and climate-normalized snow and exterior-cleaning volumes, would materially change the year-five ratio.
d5.rationale: BLS expects 4 percent growth from 2024 to 2034 for general maintenance and says older residential and nonresidential structures require more maintenance. CDC calls for frequent pool testing and preventive maintenance; CSIA cites annual chimney inspection guidance; NADCA recommends annual HVAC inspection and cleaning as needed, historically around every four to five years. These recurring physical needs support broadly stable to modestly growing demand, offset by cyclicality, weather, and deferrable household projects. [S1, S2, S7, S8, S9]

caveats: No source measures lens eligibility within the frozen EBITDA band.
Annual or multi-year recommended maintenance is repeatable but not necessarily contractually recurring.
The estimated firm count may mix materially different service lines and margin structures.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence across the supplied population showing revenue by service line, repeat-customer and contracted-route share, customer concentration, owner field hours, crew depth, licenses or certifications, and transferable agreements would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is inherently external property service, and each major line can produce repeat demand: pool care can be weekly, chimneys and HVAC systems are periodically inspected, and gutters, drains, exteriors, lots, and snow routes recur seasonally or as conditions return. Firms in the supplied EBITDA band are more likely to have crews, routes, equipment, supervisors, and transferable customer records. I exclude businesses dominated by isolated projects, misclassified construction or remediation, captive operations, and owner-personal delivery without organizational continuity. [S1, S6, S7, S8, S9]

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561790 ($5,789,061,076) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561790 ($13,714,624,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $156,878 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561790); class <5: 12,845 firms, avg revenue/firm $312,420 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $48,894 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 2,529 firms, avg revenue/firm $931,722 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $145,814 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,248 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,866,649 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $292,131 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 643 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,766,753 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $745,997 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 54 firms, avg revenue/firm $17,978,148 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,813,580 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $42,602,174 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,667,240 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 77

o.bridge: Census defines a physical service basket, BLS describes maintenance tasks, and CDC, CSIA, and NADCA specify operator activities for selected lines; none measures the share of year-five demand requiring an accountable outsourced firm. I map on-site inspection, testing, chemical handling, climbing, confined access, pressure washing, extraction, sweeping or plowing, diagnosis, preventive maintenance, and corrective work to operator-required quantity, while allowing self-service, sensors, robotic pool equipment, improved materials, bundled facilities providers, and customer in-house staff to displace some specialist demand. Safety-oriented institutional sources may overstate professional outsourcing relative to residential behavior.
caveats: No source directly measures operator-required quantity for the combined industry.
Residential customers can self-clean pools, gutters, drains, and exteriors despite safety or quality tradeoffs.
Robotic pool cleaners, remote monitoring, and bundled facility services may erode specialist demand unevenly.
o.change_evidence: A revenue-weighted customer study by service line tracking scopes retained with specialist firms, moved in-house, bundled into facilities contracts, self-served with equipment or sensors, automated, or eliminated would materially change operator-required share.
o.rationale: Most purchased outcomes require access to customer property and physical work with tools, chemicals, vehicles, or specialized equipment. CDC requires trained pool operation and frequent testing; CSIA describes complete chimney sweeping and inspection standards; NADCA defines whole-system duct cleaning. AI can support diagnosis and records but cannot deliver most field outcomes, although customers can self-serve or shift to bundled providers and more automated equipment. [S1, S2, S7, S8, S9]

q.bridge: PoolDial describes pool-service billing rather than a revenue-weighted contract mix across all NAICS 561790 service lines, while BLS and SBA identify estimation, records, and administrative tasks without measuring captured benefits. I assign relatively high initial retention to flat monthly pool routes and fixed quoted maintenance or cleaning projects, lower retention to hourly, per-visit, cost-plus-material, and competitively rebid work, then discount for renewals, customer shopping, seasonal price pressure, competitor adoption, and service-level improvements. Pool-service evidence may overstate recurring fixed-price prevalence for chimney, duct, gutter, pressure-washing, drain, and snow work.
caveats: No public source reports the national revenue-weighted pricing mix for NAICS 561790.
The direct billing evidence covers pool service and may not generalize to project-oriented subsegments.
Efficiency has no retained value unless paid hours fall, hiring is avoided, or released capacity can be sold.
q.change_evidence: A representative invoice and contract audit by 561790 service line showing revenue by pricing basis, material pass-through, renewal cadence, price changes after automation, gross margin, route density, and customer churn would materially change retention.
q.rationale: PoolDial describes flat monthly billing as standard for weekly pool service, with per-visit billing used for one-time work. Fixed monthly and quoted-scope revenue can initially preserve administrative and routing savings, whereas per-visit, hourly, material-sensitive, and rebid work passes more value to customers. Local competition and easy switching dissipate a meaningful share within five years. [S2, S3, S6]

rho.bridge: OECD measures AI adoption across SMEs in multiple sectors and countries, and SBA lists generic use cases rather than production implementation in NAICS 561790. I translate low current core-process adoption and mostly peripheral SME use into five-year implementation of this industry's exposed administrative opportunity, allowing faster uptake through embedded field-service tools but discounting fragmented data, seasonal workflows, owner attention, integration costs, technician training, and human review of safety- or property-related outputs. OECD's firms with at least 10 employees may be more digitally mature than local home-service operators, biasing implementation upward; rapid software embedding could bias it downward.
caveats: General SME adoption does not measure labor implementation in property-service firms.
Marketing or message generation may increase activity without reducing labor requirements.
Service-line differences in route density, recordkeeping, seasonality, and safety create wide implementation variance.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of LMM firms across the major 561790 service lines measuring tool deployment, workflow coverage, accepted-output rates, office and field hours, rework, integration, and avoided hiring would materially change implementation.
rho.rationale: AI can be implemented in intake, scheduling, route planning support, draft estimates, customer messages, marketing, documentation, and basic trend analysis without automating physical work. OECD reports that 11.9 percent of 10-49 employee firms used AI in 2024, core-function adoption remained below 10 percent in G7 countries, and SME generative-AI use was mostly peripheral. SBA advises firms to start small and retain human review. [S3, S4]

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year intentions among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed control transfers of eligible LMM firms in NAICS 561790. I anchor below its 22 percent employer sell-or-transfer intention after excluding plans that never close, gifts or internal succession that may not qualify, non-control events, firms outside the band, and operations that wind down with the owner. Route-based pool or seasonal businesses may be more transferable than the general population, while certification, local reputation, and owner technical dependence may reduce transferability in chimney, duct, or specialty cleaning firms.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than this industry or EBITDA band.
Owner intentions are not completed transactions and include potentially nonqualifying transfers.
Transferability likely differs sharply between dense pool routes and owner-expert inspection or project businesses.
s5.change_evidence: A transaction census for NAICS 561790 linking private and announced control changes to the eligible denominator, with service line, seller EBITDA, buyer type, ownership percentage, and post-close customer and technician retention, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years and 5 percent planned to close. Firms with documented routes, technicians, equipment, and recurring accounts can transfer, but intentions overstate completed qualifying control changes. [S5]

records.principal_driver: Recurring physical maintenance needs and fixed monthly or quoted pricing create a modest, retainable opportunity to automate coordination around field work.
records.principal_weakness: Most labor is inseparable from on-site physical execution, while the broad service mix makes the estimated acquisition pool and average economics unusually uncertain.

caveats: a: Occupation shares are headcount rather than wage shares, although reported wages inform the directional weighting.
a: The occupational mix aggregates very different staffing verticals and client work environments.
a: Client-site employees are not directly supervised by the staffing firm, so technical exposure does not imply operator-controlled deployment.
rho: Bullhorn surveys recruitment professionals globally and includes firms beyond temporary help.
rho: Purchased or experimental AI is not the same as production implementation or labor avoidance.
rho: Most on-assignment task implementation is controlled by clients, not the staffing operator.
e: No source directly measures lens eligibility within the injected firm count.
e: The code spans low-margin industrial staffing and higher-margin professional or health-care niches.
e: The injected count uses an industry-agnostic margin bridge that may misclassify staffing firms with large pass-through payroll and low reported margins.
s5: The owner survey measures intentions across industries rather than completed staffing transactions.
s5: The M&A report includes North American workforce-solutions businesses outside exact NAICS 561320.
s5: Asset purchases, contract acquisitions, and team deals may not qualify as transfers of durable operating firms.
q: Kelly is a large diversified provider rather than a representative lower-middle-market firm.
q: No source directly measures commercial sharing of AI productivity gains.
q: Retention differs materially between commodity hourly staffing, scarce professional niches, and outcome-based contracts.
d5: BLS provides exact-industry employment but output only for broader employment services.
d5: Employment is an imperfect proxy for constant-quality service quantity.
d5: Temporary-help demand is highly cyclical and recent company results may reflect a trough rather than a durable trend.
o: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share.
o: The operator role may migrate to platforms, payroll providers, or client-owned talent pools even when human temporary work persists.
o: AI-enabled candidate fraud and employment regulation may increase the value of accountable screening while also raising cost.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Clients buy contingent labor capacity, usually billed per worker-hour or per unit. The staffing firm pays assigned employees, payroll taxes, benefits where applicable, and workers' compensation, retaining a spread that funds recruiting, branch operations, compliance, and profit; some providers also earn managed-service or outcome-based fees.
lens.excluded_activities: Permanent-placement-only agencies, executive search, professional employer organizations, independent contractor marketplaces without an employer role, farm labor contractors, captive internal staffing units, shells, and firms without transferable client and worker relationships.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market temporary-help and staffing firms that repeatedly recruit, employ, administer, and supply workers to external clients for limited assignments across industrial, clerical, professional, technical, education, and health-care categories.

a.bridge: Exact-industry BLS data show that transportation and material-moving occupations are 25.28% of employment and production occupations are 17.24%, while office and administrative support is 12.92%, business and financial operations 7.59%, computer and mathematical work 4.67%, health practitioners 6.00%, construction 3.66%, and management 1.96%. I assign high AI task exposure to recruiting, matching, screening, scheduling, data entry, customer support, documentation, and portions of computer and analytical work; moderate exposure to planning, quality, and record tasks within professional roles; and low near-term exposure to material handling, assembly, physical construction, bedside care, and equipment operation. Wage weighting raises the influence of professional and computer occupations relative to their headcount, but the estimate excludes work already automated. It may overstate exposure where listed knowledge workers perform client-specific execution and understate it where robotics and computer vision reach physical assignments.
caveats: Occupation shares are headcount rather than wage shares, although reported wages inform the directional weighting.
The occupational mix aggregates very different staffing verticals and client work environments.
Client-site employees are not directly supervised by the staffing firm, so technical exposure does not imply operator-controlled deployment.
a.change_evidence: Exact payroll shares by detailed occupation, assignment-level time use, and audited AI or robotics labor-hour reductions for major temporary-staffing verticals would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Agency recruiting and administration are strongly exposed, and some professional temporary assignments contain automatable knowledge work. Most payroll, however, belongs to workers performing physical production, logistics, construction, care, or other client-site tasks that current AI alone cannot perform.

d5.bridge: BLS projects exact NAICS 561320 employment to decline 2.1% over the decade, while broader NAICS 561300 real output grows 2.5% annually; Kelly reported double-digit staffing-service declines in a major segment during 2025, with weakness at large customers and in federal-contractor demand. I use near-flat exact-industry employment as the central constant-quality demand anchor, temper the broader output growth because it includes other employment services and productivity, and widen for cyclicality, sector mix, economic recovery, customer automation, and new skill categories. Employment can understate service output when fill efficiency rises, while public-company weakness may overstate a cyclical trough.
caveats: BLS provides exact-industry employment but output only for broader employment services.
Employment is an imperfect proxy for constant-quality service quantity.
Temporary-help demand is highly cyclical and recent company results may reflect a trough rather than a durable trend.
d5.change_evidence: Exact-industry constant-price sales, billable hours, assignment counts, fill rates, temporary-help payroll by vertical, client automation plans, and a current staffing-industry forecast would materially change the range.
d5.rationale: Temporary help remains a flexible buffer for seasonal, project, vacancy, and uncertain demand, but it is an early-cycle expense that clients cut before permanent staff. Automation threatens clerical, customer-support, lower-skill production, and some knowledge assignments, while health care, education, skilled trades, and emerging technical work can offset part of the loss.

caveats: No source directly measures lens eligibility within the injected firm count.
The code spans low-margin industrial staffing and higher-margin professional or health-care niches.
The injected count uses an industry-agnostic margin bridge that may misclassify staffing firms with large pass-through payroll and low reported margins.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level product-line revenue, worker payroll, client concentration, repeat-customer share, employment-law claims, ownership, and normalized EBITDA for the supplied 2,295-firm population would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The exact industry is defined as supplying the firm's own employees to external clients for limited periods, which is inherently a repeat outsourced service. The discount covers highly concentrated contract shops, nontransferable owner-operated firms, establishments dominated by excluded permanent placement or contractor brokerage, misclassification, weak compliance infrastructure, and firms whose normalized economics do not fit the supplied margin-based size band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561320 ($126,830,216,274) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561320 ($326,341,918,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $83,060 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561320); class <5: 5,670 firms, avg revenue/firm $658,091 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $102,991 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,453 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,474,355 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $230,737 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 1,487 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,233,990 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $349,619 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 3,712 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,818,419 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $754,083 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 2,295 firms, avg revenue/firm $15,944,583 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,495,327 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 1,151 firms, avg revenue/firm $228,208,144 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $35,714,575 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 2,295

caveats: No source directly measures the year-five operator-required share.
The operator role may migrate to platforms, payroll providers, or client-owned talent pools even when human temporary work persists.
AI-enabled candidate fraud and employment regulation may increase the value of accountable screening while also raising cost.
o.change_evidence: Longitudinal data on direct-sourcing platforms, client insourcing, software-only fulfillment, employer-of-record usage, automated assignment displacement, and revenue retained by staffing operators by vertical would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: Digital marketplaces and client systems can self-serve requisitions, matching, scheduling, and some screening, while automation can remove entire categories of contingent labor. A substantial share still requires an accountable employer to recruit and verify workers, run payroll and tax withholding, provide benefits, maintain workers' compensation, manage safety and wage-hour compliance, finance receivables, address claims, and supply human labor for physical or regulated assignments. The durable share should be higher in health care, education, skilled trades, and local industrial work than in clerical, customer-support, and standardized remote knowledge work.

q.bridge: Kelly reports that staffing services are generally negotiated and invoiced per hour or per unit, that temporary-employee wages and related payroll, benefit, and workers' compensation costs are direct costs of service, and that recent demand declines were partly offset by higher bill rates while gross-profit rates remained near 20%. I map this cost-linked, competitively rebid structure to low retention of savings tied to assigned-worker hours: clients capture much of the benefit by ordering fewer hours or negotiating rates. Internal recruiting and administration savings can be retained through faster fill and lower SG&A, so retention is above zero. A large public provider facing national procurement may retain less than differentiated local specialists, while small commodity firms may have even less pricing power.
caveats: Kelly is a large diversified provider rather than a representative lower-middle-market firm.
No source directly measures commercial sharing of AI productivity gains.
Retention differs materially between commodity hourly staffing, scarce professional niches, and outcome-based contracts.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level bill and pay rates, markup renewals, fill-speed gains, internal recruiter cost, billable-hour changes, and gross-margin performance before and after AI adoption across staffing verticals would materially change the range.
q.rationale: The industry's main revenue unit is a billable worker-hour, so eliminating a worker-hour commonly eliminates both wage cost and revenue. AI that reduces agency-office effort is more retainable, but competitive markups, managed-service procurement, pay-rate transparency, and client sharing constrain the five-year benefit.

rho.bridge: Bullhorn's global recruitment-industry survey reports that 67% of firms had bought, built, or were experimenting with AI and describes use in sourcing, matching, messaging, screening, and scheduling; Kelly reports deployed AI in recruiting, applicant tracking, assessment, analytics, and talent management. I translate this to high implementation of exposed agency-office tasks but a much lower implementation share for the total wage base because client businesses control the workflows and equipment used by most assigned employees. Staffing firms may implement internal AI faster than this blended value, while customer integration, safety, bargaining, data quality, bias controls, and lack of control at client sites may make total labor implementation lower.
caveats: Bullhorn surveys recruitment professionals globally and includes firms beyond temporary help.
Purchased or experimental AI is not the same as production implementation or labor avoidance.
Most on-assignment task implementation is controlled by clients, not the staffing operator.
rho.change_evidence: A representative U.S. staffing-firm study separating internal employees from assigned workers, with verified workflow coverage, labor hours avoided, client-controlled automation, redeployment, and billable-hour effects by vertical, would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Recruitment software is already mainstream enough to automate candidate search, matching, communication, and administrative work. The larger opportunity embedded in assigned-worker payroll requires client adoption of software or robotics and often reduces billable hours rather than creating a controllable staffing-firm saving.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, while a staffing adviser tracked about 118 announced or completed North American staffing and workforce-solutions transactions in 2025. I haircut owner intentions for noncompletion, family and internal transfers, unsuitable compliance records, customer concentration, and transactions involving only contracts or teams, but use observed sector deal flow to keep the central estimate near the cross-industry intention rate. The advisory transaction count lacks a verified industry denominator and includes adjacent workforce solutions, so it may overstate exact 561320 liquidity; incomplete reporting of small private deals may understate it.
caveats: The owner survey measures intentions across industries rather than completed staffing transactions.
The M&A report includes North American workforce-solutions businesses outside exact NAICS 561320.
Asset purchases, contract acquisitions, and team deals may not qualify as transfers of durable operating firms.
s5.change_evidence: A complete U.S. transaction database mapped to 561320, matched to seller size, vertical, control status, owner age, and failed-sale outcomes, would materially change the range.
s5.rationale: Staffing firms have recognizable strategic buyers, private-equity add-on logic, recurring clients, branch networks, candidate pools, and working-capital infrastructure. Buyer appetite varies sharply by vertical, with customer concentration, workers' compensation exposure, wage-law compliance, and cyclical earnings limiting transferability.

records.principal_driver: High-volume recruiting and administration are already adopting AI, creating measurable speed and capacity opportunities across a large operator population.
records.principal_weakness: The largest labor costs are billable client-controlled assignment hours, so eliminating them often removes revenue rather than creating retainable operator savings.

sources.supports: Exact-industry scope, the limited-period supply of the provider's employees, lack of direct supervision at client sites, and exclusions for PEOs and placement agencies.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual: 561320 Temporary Help Services

sources.supports: Exact-industry employment and wage distribution across office, professional, health-care, production, logistics, construction, and other assigned-worker occupations.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 561320

sources.supports: Global staffing and recruitment AI adoption, use cases, data barriers, recruiter search time, and expected workflow savings from the GRID 2025 survey.
sources.title: Staffing Firms Using AI Are Twice as Likely to Have Increased Revenue Last Year

sources.supports: Temporary-staffing employer responsibilities, AI deployment and risks, per-hour and per-unit billing, direct worker costs, gross-profit economics, and recent demand weakness.
sources.title: Kelly Services, Inc. 2025 Form 10-K

sources.supports: Cross-industry five-year sale or transfer intentions among U.S. employer-business owners.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: North American staffing transaction volume, uneven deal timing, market softness, and buyer selectivity.
sources.title: Staffing and Workforce Solutions Industry M&A Report: Full-Year 2025

sources.title: Employment and Output by Industry

records.title: Temporary Help Services

caveats: a: The 2023 OEWS table measures occupational employment and wages, not task time or AI exposure.
a: GAO's documentation evidence spans healthcare providers and is not specific to specialty hospitals.
a: The code mixes rehabilitation, long-term acute, children's, cancer, and other specialty hospitals with different occupation and documentation mixes.
rho: ASTP's survey covers non-federal acute-care hospitals rather than specialty hospitals.
rho: Tool use is not the same as realized labor substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: Small independent hospitals adopt predictive AI substantially less often than system-affiliated hospitals.
e: Eligibility is bounded judgment rather than an observed share.
e: CMS IRF counts include hospital units, while NAICS and the dataset are organized around establishments and firms.
e: The 15.80% margin bridge may misclassify capital-intensive or low-margin specialty hospitals into the LMM band.
s5: The ASPE observation period predates the five-year forecast horizon.
s5: The hospital denominator includes general, psychiatric, rehabilitation, and long-term-care providers outside the exact firm lens.
s5: A provider change of ownership is not always a qualifying control transfer of an independent firm.
q: IRF payment is only one component of the heterogeneous code's payer and service mix.
q: Medicare rate updates can share industry productivity with the payer over time.
q: Gross workflow benefit may be reinvested in quality, compliance, or capacity rather than retained financially.
d5: The strongest volume evidence is for inpatient rehabilitation, not every specialty-hospital subtype.
d5: Medicare FFS trends may diverge from Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, and commercial demand.
d5: Nominal payment updates are not constant-price service-volume growth.
o: IRF requirements do not describe every specialty-hospital subtype.
o: Payer substitution to other care settings can reduce operator need even when medical demand persists.
o: The distinction between demand reduction and migration to a different operator is difficult to observe.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Hospitals receive prospective case or discharge payments, negotiated commercial payments, public-program reimbursement, and patient cost sharing for staffed episodes of inpatient care, often with related outpatient, diagnostic, therapy, and assessment services.
lens.excluded_activities: General medical and surgical hospitals, psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals, skilled nursing facilities, non-hospital residential care, hospital departments that are captive units rather than separately controlled firms, shells, and non-control financing or real-estate vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms operating licensed specialty hospitals that repeatedly provide inpatient and related outpatient diagnostic, medical, long-term acute, rehabilitation, restorative, or condition-specific care to patients and payers.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment and wages by occupation in specialty hospitals, while GAO documents AI use in clinical notes and coding; neither measures the wage-weighted share of current, not-yet-automated tasks in LMM specialty hospitals. I map documentation, coding, claims, scheduling, intake, discharge instructions, care-plan drafts, utilization review, record abstraction, and routine administrative analysis to exposure, while treating bedside nursing, therapy delivery, procedures, examinations, patient movement, food service, cleaning, and accountable clinical decisions as largely unexposed. This may overstate exposure where documentation remains inseparable from licensed judgment and understate it in digitally mature rehabilitation or long-term-care hospitals.
caveats: The 2023 OEWS table measures occupational employment and wages, not task time or AI exposure.
GAO's documentation evidence spans healthcare providers and is not specific to specialty hospitals.
The code mixes rehabilitation, long-term acute, children's, cancer, and other specialty hospitals with different occupation and documentation mixes.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted time-and-motion data from freestanding rehabilitation, long-term-care, children's, cancer, and other specialty hospitals separating bedside care, therapy, documentation, coding, scheduling, utilization management, billing, and already-automated work would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: BLS shows that healthcare practitioners and technical workers account for 50.97% of employment, healthcare support for 16.27%, and office and administrative support for 10.04%; registered nurses alone are 22.42% and therapists about 12%. The direct-care majority constrains substitution, but expensive clinicians perform meaningful documentation and coordination work and the administrative layer is highly information-intensive.

d5.bridge: MedPAC reports that IRF volume per FFS beneficiary rose 31% from 2015 to 2024 and that IRFs received 5.3% of hospital discharges in 2024, while CMS finalized a 2.6% nominal IRF payment-rate increase for FY 2026. These observations cover Medicare rehabilitation rather than five-year constant-price demand for the full specialty-hospital basket. I translate the sustained IRF volume trend and complex-care need into modest growth, then discount for Medicare Advantage utilization management, substitution toward SNFs and home health, hospital-referral changes, payment pressure, and non-rehabilitation service lines. This may overstate the code if recent IRF share gains plateau and understate it if aging and complex survivorship continue shifting volume into specialty settings.
caveats: The strongest volume evidence is for inpatient rehabilitation, not every specialty-hospital subtype.
Medicare FFS trends may diverge from Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, and commercial demand.
Nominal payment updates are not constant-price service-volume growth.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price panel of admissions, occupied bed days, referral acceptance, authorization denials, discharge destinations, and case mix for freestanding 622310 rehabilitation, long-term-care, children's, cancer, and other hospitals would materially change the estimate.
d5.rationale: Recent IRF volume growth supplies a concrete positive signal, and licensed specialty hospitals address complex conditions that cannot routinely move to low-acuity settings. Yet payer steering and overlap with SNFs and home health make straight-line extrapolation inappropriate, so the range includes slight contraction.

caveats: Eligibility is bounded judgment rather than an observed share.
CMS IRF counts include hospital units, while NAICS and the dataset are organized around establishments and firms.
The 15.80% margin bridge may misclassify capital-intensive or low-margin specialty hospitals into the LMM band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level linkage of SUSB entities to CMS certification, PECOS ownership, tax status, system affiliation, bed count, freestanding versus unit status, normalized EBITDA, and operating-versus-real-estate control would materially change the estimate.
e.rationale: The official code requires licensed inpatient specialty-hospital operations, which generally fit a repeat external-service model, but the supplied 117-firm count is margin-bridged and does not identify ownership, independence, normalized EBITDA, or whether a legal entity is a separately controllable operator. Hospital-based rehabilitation units, public or nonprofit restrictions, system ownership, capital intensity, payer concentration, and real-estate or operating-company structures reduce the eligible share. I estimate fewer than half of counted LMM firms are cleanly eligible, with a wide range.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 622310 ($21,910,402,880) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 622310 ($59,701,001,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $229,759 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 622310); class <5: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,664,517 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $262,994 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,184,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $187,072 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 4 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,970,250 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,049,300 -> IN $1-10M band; class 20-99: 34 firms, avg revenue/firm $14,474,324 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,286,943 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 79 firms, avg revenue/firm $36,066,443 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $5,698,498 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 174 firms, avg revenue/firm $323,282,672 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $51,078,662 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 117

o.bridge: Census defines the industry around licensed inpatient hospitals with beds and organized medical staff, CMS requires intensive rehabilitation and patient assessment for IRFs, and GAO describes AI tools as assisting notes and coding with clinician review rather than replacing care delivery. None measures the year-five operator-required share across 622310. I treat inpatient nursing, therapy, procedures, monitoring, food, infection control, safety, and accountable clinical oversight as operator-required, while allowing remote monitoring, better triage, avoided admissions, and software-only administrative work to remove a small quantity. This may be high for assessment-heavy or outpatient-adjacent services and low for high-acuity long-term-care hospitals.
caveats: IRF requirements do not describe every specialty-hospital subtype.
Payer substitution to other care settings can reduce operator need even when medical demand persists.
The distinction between demand reduction and migration to a different operator is difficult to observe.
o.change_evidence: Service-line evidence on admissions avoided through remote care, payer substitution to home or SNF settings, autonomous monitoring, virtual therapy, required onsite staffing, and the share of revenue from software-separable services would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: The defining product is licensed inpatient medical care delivered by organized clinical staff. AI can assist decisions and documentation, but the current service basket still requires a hospital operator to supply facilities, licensed care, monitoring, and accountability.

q.bridge: CMS documents predetermined per-discharge IRF payments covering routine, ancillary, and capital costs and annual market-basket, productivity, wage-index, case-mix, outlier, and quality-reporting adjustments. These rules describe Medicare rehabilitation reimbursement rather than discounted benefit retention across all specialty hospitals and payers. I infer that lower documentation, coding, and administrative cost can initially accrue under fixed case payments, then discount for annual rate updates, payer renegotiation, coding review, quality requirements, shared-risk contracts, and the use of productivity to expand capacity rather than remove cost. This may overstate retention for tightly managed commercial contracts and understate it where scarce capacity converts saved time into additional cases.
caveats: IRF payment is only one component of the heterogeneous code's payer and service mix.
Medicare rate updates can share industry productivity with the payer over time.
Gross workflow benefit may be reinvested in quality, compliance, or capacity rather than retained financially.
q.change_evidence: Payer-contract samples and hospital contribution-margin data before and after AI deployment, split among Medicare PPS, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, commercial case rates, per diem, cost-based, and value-based contracts, would materially change the estimate.
q.rationale: Prospective case payment creates a real retention channel because payment does not vary directly with each input used during a stay. However, public payment formulas update annually, quality and compliance failures can reduce reimbursement, and much AI benefit may relieve staffing pressure or improve service rather than appear as removable cost.

rho.bridge: ASTP measures predictive-AI use in non-federal acute-care hospitals and GAO reports clinician use and deployment constraints for AI documentation and coding, not five-year implementation of exposed labor opportunity in LMM specialty hospitals. I translate established EHR integration and rapidly growing administrative use into moderate implementation, then discount for independent-hospital adoption gaps, privacy, clinical validation, liability, interoperability, procurement, change management, and required human review. This may be high for small independent hospitals and low for scaled system-affiliated rehabilitation operators.
caveats: ASTP's survey covers non-federal acute-care hospitals rather than specialty hospitals.
Tool use is not the same as realized labor substitution or avoided hiring.
Small independent hospitals adopt predictive AI substantially less often than system-affiliated hospitals.
rho.change_evidence: Specialty-hospital deployment records showing eligible users, workflow penetration, validation and override rates, implementation cost, documentation minutes saved, coding productivity, staffing changes, and sustained use after one year would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Hospital AI adoption is no longer merely hypothetical: ASTP reports 71% predictive-AI use in 2024, but only 37% among independent hospitals, while GAO reports 28% clinician use of AI for documentation or coding in 2026 and continuing accuracy, cost, privacy, and oversight concerns. A little over half of exposed opportunity becoming operational within five years is plausible without assuming autonomous clinical care.

s5.bridge: ASPE's PECOS analysis found 348 hospital sales in 2016-2021, an annualized 9.8 per 1,000 hospitals and 4.6% over the study period, with sales more common among long-term-care hospitals and medium or larger hospitals. It measures provider changes of ownership across hospital types rather than qualifying control transfers of eligible LMM 622310 firms. I raise the broad hospital five-year anchor modestly for the code's long-term-care exposure and active specialty chains, while allowing downside for nonprofit, public, certificate-of-need, licensing, and system-approval constraints. Facility-level sales can overstate firm transfers when several hospitals move in one transaction and understate economic control changes not recorded as a PECOS sale.
caveats: The ASPE observation period predates the five-year forecast horizon.
The hospital denominator includes general, psychiatric, rehabilitation, and long-term-care providers outside the exact firm lens.
A provider change of ownership is not always a qualifying control transfer of an independent firm.
s5.change_evidence: Current PECOS change-of-ownership records linked to NAICS 622310, freestanding status, EBITDA band, and ultimate-parent transaction identifiers, supplemented by Irving Levin or PitchBook deal records, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Hospital ownership changes are observable but uncommon relative to ordinary small-business transfers. Specialty platforms do transact, yet licensure, payer enrollment, capital needs, regulatory review, and system ownership make the addressable control-transfer rate materially lower than generic employer-business succession rates.

records.principal_driver: Fixed episode payments and information-heavy clinical administration create a credible productivity channel while licensed inpatient care remains operator-bound.
records.principal_weakness: The code mixes materially different hospital types, and the margin-bridged firm count does not reveal which entities are independent, controllable LMM operators.

caveats: a: OEWS is available only for broader NAICS 562200 and mixes hazardous and nonhazardous treatment and disposal.
a: Occupation employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not identify current automation.
a: The EPA e-Manifest workflow demonstrates digitized information handling, not generative-AI substitutability of all compliance work.
rho: No source measures AI workflow penetration or avoided hiring in NAICS 562211.

e: The frozen n of 68 is a margin-bridged estimate and is not re-estimated here.

q: Fixed or unit pricing can retain savings initially but does not prevent repricing at renewal.
q: Treatment mix, transport distance, disposal capacity, and customer concentration can dominate labor-related pricing.

d5: Hazardous-waste tonnage can swing with a few large generators or remediation projects and may not track constant-quality service demand.
d5: Waste minimization reduces volumes while tighter rules or cleanup programs can increase treatment intensity.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Industrial, commercial, healthcare, laboratory, and government generators purchase waste profiling, manifest administration, transportation where integrated, treatment, incineration, stabilization, storage, and disposal, commonly through recurring accounts, scheduled pickups, unit-priced waste streams, and competitively bid term contracts.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal waste units, shell entities, non-control financing vehicles, hazardous-waste collection or transfer-station businesses without treatment or disposal facilities, nonhazardous landfills and combustors, sewage treatment, and remediation contractors without in-scope treatment or disposal operations.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms that operate hazardous-waste treatment or disposal facilities, including firms that combine local hazardous-waste collection or hauling with their own treatment or disposal facilities, and repeatedly serve external generators under permits and service agreements.

a.bridge: BLS reports employment rather than wage-weighted task time for the broader NAICS 562200 waste-treatment sector. I map exposure primarily to office support, dispatch, customer service, billing, manifest and compliance documentation, sales, management analysis, scheduling, and some predictive-maintenance work; I retain the physical and safety-critical work of driving, material movement, equipment operation, testing, treatment, maintenance, and hazardous-material handling. Employment shares may understate wage-weighted exposure because exposed administrative and managerial roles earn more, but the broader sector may overstate it if nonhazardous operators have more routinized dispatch and scale than 562211 facilities.
caveats: OEWS is available only for broader NAICS 562200 and mixes hazardous and nonhazardous treatment and disposal.
Occupation employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not identify current automation.
The EPA e-Manifest workflow demonstrates digitized information handling, not generative-AI substitutability of all compliance work.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted time-and-motion study of NAICS 562211 firms separating manifests, waste profiling, quoting, scheduling, billing, compliance, laboratory analysis, driving, handling, treatment, maintenance, and supervision, with observed post-AI hours by task, would materially change a.
a.rationale: In broader NAICS 562200, transportation and material-moving occupations are 44.51% of employment, construction and extraction 15.40%, installation and maintenance 8.48%, production 5.61%, and office and administrative support 9.47%. Hazardous-material workers must test, package, transport, store, and physically neutralize or remove dangerous materials under strict procedures. A 22% base therefore includes substantial document and coordination exposure but does not treat plant, transport, laboratory, maintenance, or field execution as substitutable by generative AI.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for hazardous-materials removal workers across remediation, waste treatment, construction, and other industries, while d5 is constant-price demand for the current treatment-and-disposal service basket in NAICS 562211. I use the 1% ten-year occupational growth projection as a flat-demand anchor, then widen for industrial production, waste minimization, cleanup funding, nuclear decommissioning, regulation, and changes in treatment intensity. Employment may understate service quantity if productivity rises and may overstate outsourced 562211 demand because much hazmat removal occurs in adjacent industries.
caveats: The BLS projection is occupational and cross-industry, not a forecast of NAICS 562211 output.
Hazardous-waste tonnage can swing with a few large generators or remediation projects and may not track constant-quality service demand.
Waste minimization reduces volumes while tighter rules or cleanup programs can increase treatment intensity.
d5.change_evidence: EPA Biennial Report time series through 2025 for off-site tons managed by commercial TSDFs, segmented by treatment method and deflated revenue, plus generator-industry production forecasts and permitted-capacity utilization, would materially change d5.
d5.rationale: BLS projects hazardous-materials-removal employment to grow 1% from 2024 to 2034, citing continuing cleanup needs but sporadic funding and delayed decommissioning. EPA's Biennial Report continues to collect detailed generation and TSDF management data, reflecting a persistent regulated waste stream, but it does not by itself establish growth. The base is therefore near flat with a wide cyclical, regulatory, and waste-minimization range.

caveats: NAICS establishments do not identify whether a facility is captive or serves unaffiliated customers.
Permit transferability and environmental liabilities can make an operating business economically ineligible even when its revenue model fits.
The frozen n of 68 is a margin-bridged estimate and is not re-estimated here.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census linking NAICS 562211 ownership, external-customer revenue, recurring-account share, permit status, parent-company affiliation, environmental liabilities, normalized EBITDA, and change-of-control provisions would materially change e.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition centers on operating hazardous-waste treatment or disposal facilities and integrated collection-plus-disposal operations, which ordinarily provide repeat services to external generators. Eligibility is reduced for captive facilities, highly concentrated project businesses, facilities with unresolved environmental liabilities, operations embedded in a much larger parent, and firms whose permits, land, or financial assurance cannot transfer on acceptable terms. The LMM firm count is already margin-bridged, so this estimate addresses lens eligibility rather than recomputing scale.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 562211 ($4,281,251,743) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 562211 ($9,303,910,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.2099 (margins.json[562]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Environmental & Waste Services EBITDA/Sales 20.99%); revenue/employee $310,887 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 562211); class <5: 192 firms, avg revenue/firm $726,802 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $152,556 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,504,568 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $315,809 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 52 firms, avg revenue/firm $4,508,923 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $946,423 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,813,417 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,849,936 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 20 firms, avg revenue/firm $37,467,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $7,864,365 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 37 firms, avg revenue/firm $207,873,270 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $43,632,599 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 68

o.bridge: EPA's RCRA permit requirements and BLS descriptions establish legal accountability and physical work, not the exact year-five share of demand requiring an outsourced 562211 operator. I retain demand that needs permitted receipt, testing, treatment, storage, monitoring, reporting, transport integration, and physical disposal, while deducting waste eliminated at source, recycled, treated onsite, shifted to alternative processes, or otherwise self-served. The permit evidence may overstate outsourced need because generators can minimize or manage some waste onsite, but it strongly limits full software substitution.
caveats: RCRA contains conditional permit exceptions, and state requirements differ.
Operator need is high for the remaining regulated waste, but customers can reduce the service basket through process redesign and waste minimization.
The evidence does not quantify the share of commercial 562211 volumes that generators could shift onsite or to recycling.
o.change_evidence: Generator procurement data tracking off-site versus onsite treatment, waste-minimization and recycling rates, software-only substitutions, commercial TSDF tonnage, and customer churn by waste stream would materially change o.
o.rationale: EPA states that a TSDF generally cannot operate without a legally binding RCRA permit specifying design, operations, safety, monitoring, reporting, emergency plans, insurance, financial backing, and training. BLS describes physical testing, neutralization, packaging, transport, storage, and equipment work under strict safety procedures. Software can assist decisions and records but cannot receive, treat, or dispose of hazardous material, supporting a 94% base operator-required share.

caveats: No representative source reports revenue-weighted billing methods or AI-benefit pass-through for LMM NAICS 562211 firms.
Fixed or unit pricing can retain savings initially but does not prevent repricing at renewal.
Treatment mix, transport distance, disposal capacity, and customer concentration can dominate labor-related pricing.
q.change_evidence: Five years of engagement-level quotes, waste profiles, unit prices, fuel and regulatory surcharges, contract renewal terms, bid results, realized labor hours, and contribution margins from LMM hazardous-waste operators would materially change q.
q.rationale: Hazardous-waste services are commonly sold by waste stream, container, weight, volume, pickup, treatment method, or fixed-price contract rather than by disclosed employee hours. That permits an operator to retain early administrative and coordination savings. Retention falls as competitive bids, term renewals, indexed pricing, procurement demands, customer consolidation, and scope expansion share the benefit. Regulatory differentiation, permits, transport distance, and scarce treatment capacity moderate immediate pass-through.

rho.bridge: EPA's e-Manifest system shows that receiving-facility records, signatures, invoices, corrections, and APIs can be handled in a structured digital workflow, but it does not measure AI implementation or avoided labor in hazardous-waste firms. I translate that digital readiness into adoption potential for document intake, classification assistance, scheduling, billing, customer communications, compliance retrieval, and maintenance planning, then discount for permit accountability, data validation, cybersecurity, integration with laboratory and plant systems, exception-heavy waste streams, and the cost of an error. Because registered facilities already use digital systems, the proxy may understate implementation readiness; because regulatory digitization is not autonomous AI, it may overstate realized labor savings.
caveats: No source measures AI workflow penetration or avoided hiring in NAICS 562211.
RCRA and state permit obligations constrain autonomous decision-making and require accountable personnel.
Small facilities may lack clean data, integration staff, or sufficient workflow volume to justify custom systems.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of sub-250-employee hazardous-waste TSDFs reporting AI-active seats, e-Manifest integration, administrative hours per manifest, waste-profile review time, exception and correction rates, compliance incidents, and avoided hires would materially change rho.
rho.rationale: Receiving facilities must register for e-Manifest to submit manifests and corrections, and EPA notes that site managers can administer API credentials. These are favorable conditions for assisted workflows, but RCRA permits establish detailed operating, monitoring, reporting, emergency, training, and financial-assurance requirements. A 42% base assumes meaningful adoption in administrative workflows while retaining human verification and limited substitution in safety-critical operations.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale, transfer, or public-offering plans among all US employer-business owners, not completed qualifying control transfers of LMM hazardous-waste operators. I begin with the 22% employer-owner summary, then discount gifts, internal transfers, abandoned plans, closures, minority transactions, environmental diligence failures, permit-transfer friction, and facilities whose liabilities prevent a sale. Scarce permitted assets and strategic buyer interest could make qualifying firms more transferable than the broad small-business population, while contingent liabilities could make them less transferable.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses and measures intentions rather than closings.
The source does not isolate hazardous-waste firms, the LMM EBITDA band, or control transactions.
Permit continuity can support strategic value, but environmental indemnities and closure obligations can prevent or reshape a transfer.
s5.change_evidence: PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, and EPA/state permit records matched to completed NAICS 562211 control transactions, seller EBITDA, permit transfers, environmental escrows, and failed-sale reasons would materially change s5.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 13% of employer owners planned a sale and 11% a transfer within five years, with a 22% combined sell, transfer, or public summary. Hazardous-waste facilities have durable permits and physical assets but also change-of-control, financial-assurance, cleanup, and environmental-liability diligence. A 12% base reflects an active but selective transfer market after excluding nonqualifying intentions and failed transactions.

records.principal_driver: Structured manifests, compliance records, scheduling, billing, and maintenance data create a real but bounded administrative automation opportunity around an indispensable permitted service.
records.principal_weakness: Most labor and customer value remain tied to physical transport, handling, treatment assets, safety controls, permits, and environmental liability rather than substitutable knowledge work.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as operating hazardous-waste treatment or disposal facilities, including combined local collection or hauling with treatment or disposal, and distinguishes adjacent nonhazardous and sewage activities.

sources.supports: Reports the broader NAICS 562200 occupation mix, including transportation and material moving at 44.51%, construction and extraction at 15.40%, office support at 9.47%, maintenance at 8.48%, production at 5.61%, and hazardous-materials removal workers at 5.46%.
sources.title: May 2023 OEWS: Waste Treatment and Disposal

sources.supports: Describes physical, safety, testing, packaging, transport, storage, recordkeeping, and regulatory duties and projects 1% employment growth from 2024 to 2034.

sources.supports: Explains that TSDFs generally require legally binding RCRA permits covering operating, safety, monitoring, reporting, emergency-planning, training, insurance, and financial conditions, typically for terms up to ten years.
sources.title: EPA: What a Hazardous Waste Permit Is

sources.supports: States that receiving facilities must register for invoicing, electronic submissions, and corrections and that site managers can administer API credentials, demonstrating structured digital workflows.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
