records.title: Other Waste Collection

caveats: a: BLS occupation counts do not measure task time, wages by task, or current automation within the frozen firm lens.
a: Psychiatric hospitals and substance-abuse hospitals may have materially different staffing, acuity, length of stay, and security needs.
a: AI assistance to documentation may improve care time without permitting lower licensed or observation staffing.
rho: The predictive-AI adoption source covers nonfederal acute-care hospitals, not psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals.
rho: Use of a model does not establish broad implementation, labor substitution, or acceptable safety performance.
rho: CMS quality reporting requirements add digital workflow opportunities but also preserve human validation and compliance work.
e: CMS inpatient psychiatric facility populations include psychiatric units inside acute-care hospitals that are not standalone 622210 firms.
e: Nonprofit governance and membership transfers differ from conventional equity control sales.
e: The supplied n value is a margin-bridged estimate and may misclassify facility units, system entities, or operators with atypical payer mixes.
s5: The transaction series has no denominator aligned to LMM psychiatric and substance-abuse hospital firms.
s5: Most cited counseling and psychiatric-care deals were outpatient and do not establish inpatient hospital liquidity.
s5: Publicly announced transaction counts can omit small private deals and include multi-site platforms as one event.
q: Medicare IPF payment does not describe the full Medicaid, commercial, public-contract, and self-pay mix.
q: Per-diem payment can reward lower daily cost but also creates exposure if efficiency changes length of stay or authorization behavior.
q: Payment form is not direct evidence of the discounted share of AI benefits retained.
d5: BLS employment growth is not a direct forecast of constant-quality hospital service quantity and includes replacement and staffing effects.
d5: HRSA models behavioral-health provider demand across settings rather than NAICS 622210 inpatient utilization.
d5: Unmet clinical need may remain unpaid or unserved because of workforce, bed, payer, and public-budget constraints.
o: The estimate concerns whether surviving demand needs an accountable operator, not how much labor AI can assist inside that operator.
o: Site-of-care substitution differs sharply between severe psychiatric crises, detoxification, rehabilitation, and lower-acuity conditions.
o: Remote monitoring may supplement inpatient staff but does not remove licensure, custody, medication, and safety accountability.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring admissions and patient days reimbursed by Medicare, Medicaid, commercial insurers, public purchasers, employers, and patients through prospective per-diem systems, negotiated daily or case rates, fee schedules, bundled arrangements, and limited cost-based or supplemental payments.
lens.excluded_activities: Government-owned hospitals, captive or non-control units within general hospitals or larger systems, outpatient-only mental health and substance-abuse centers, counseling-led residential facilities without hospital-level medical treatment, intellectual-disability residential facilities, shell or real-estate-only entities, and operators outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market licensed psychiatric and substance-abuse hospital operating firms repeatedly providing inpatient diagnostic, medical treatment, monitoring, nursing, psychiatric, psychological, social-work, and related services to external patients and payers through a transferable operation.

a.bridge: BLS May 2024 industry charts identify registered nurses, psychiatric technicians, counselors, psychiatric aides, social workers, practical nurses, nursing assistants, managers, and human-service assistants as the largest occupations in psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals. BLS task descriptions distinguish recordkeeping, intake and discharge support, observation reporting, care coordination, scheduling, quality reporting, billing, and administrative work from therapeutic interaction, medication administration, vital-sign monitoring, daily-living assistance, de-escalation, restraint, and continuous safety supervision. The range treats the former tasks as not-yet-automated AI opportunity and the latter as less substitutable. It may overstate exposure where EHR and coding tools are already used and understate administrative burden in less digitized standalone facilities.
caveats: BLS occupation counts do not measure task time, wages by task, or current automation within the frozen firm lens.
Psychiatric hospitals and substance-abuse hospitals may have materially different staffing, acuity, length of stay, and security needs.
AI assistance to documentation may improve care time without permitting lower licensed or observation staffing.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task census and time diary from independent psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals, segmented by nursing, technicians, aides, counselors, social work, physicians, utilization review, intake, coding, billing, and administration and reconciled to existing automation, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: The industry has meaningful documentation, utilization review, claims, scheduling, intake, discharge planning, risk review, and compliance workflows, but its core labor is unusually relational, safety-sensitive, physical, and licensed. Patient observation, therapeutic alliance, crisis response, medication, and restraint cannot be delegated broadly to software, so the remaining wage-weighted opportunity is a minority share.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 16% employment growth for psychiatric technicians and aides from 2024 to 2034, while HRSA's status-quo behavioral-health demand scalars rise to 116.92% of the 2023 level by 2028 for combined adults and children and HRSA reports large projected behavioral-health workforce shortages. These workforce measures cover broader settings and include replacement, staffing, and access effects, so they are converted conservatively into five-year constant-price inpatient service demand after allowing for unmet need, population growth, outpatient and telehealth substitution, parity and payer policy, capacity limits, and changing lengths of stay. They likely overstate inpatient growth when services shift outpatient but may understate paid demand where bed scarcity suppresses observed use.
caveats: BLS employment growth is not a direct forecast of constant-quality hospital service quantity and includes replacement and staffing effects.
HRSA models behavioral-health provider demand across settings rather than NAICS 622210 inpatient utilization.
Unmet clinical need may remain unpaid or unserved because of workforce, bed, payer, and public-budget constraints.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of case-mix-adjusted admissions and patient days for freestanding psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals, segmented by diagnosis, age, payer, geography, bed capacity, occupancy, denied admissions, and substitution to outpatient, residential, crisis, or virtual settings, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: Behavioral-health need, crisis presentations, substance-use disorders, and workforce shortages support durable demand, but need does not automatically become reimbursed inpatient volume. Outpatient treatment, crisis stabilization, residential care, telehealth, payer authorization, prevention, staffing scarcity, and public budget limits can constrain or redirect admissions, while severe acuity and safety needs preserve a core inpatient role.

caveats: CMS inpatient psychiatric facility populations include psychiatric units inside acute-care hospitals that are not standalone 622210 firms.
Nonprofit governance and membership transfers differ from conventional equity control sales.
The supplied n value is a margin-bridged estimate and may misclassify facility units, system entities, or operators with atypical payer mixes.
e.change_evidence: A record-level linkage of the frozen population to CMS inpatient psychiatric facility files, state hospital licenses, AHA ownership and system affiliation, tax filings, payer contracts, facility websites, real-estate control, and normalized EBITDA would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: The six-digit definition is centered on licensed inpatient operators with recurring external patient and payer revenue, so core business-model eligibility is plausible. Deductions cover state hospitals and other government ownership, distinct-part units or system campuses without transferable control, nonprofit entities without a conventional sale path, facilities outside the normalized EBITDA band, operators whose medical model belongs in outpatient or residential care, distressed facilities without a sustainable operation, and establishment-versus-firm duplication. AHA reports 656 nonfederal psychiatric hospitals, but that count does not resolve ownership, system affiliation, substance-abuse specialization, or LMM economics for the frozen 208-firm estimate.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 622210 ($8,334,925,927) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 622210 ($32,019,081,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: The estimate concerns whether surviving demand needs an accountable operator, not how much labor AI can assist inside that operator.
Site-of-care substitution differs sharply between severe psychiatric crises, detoxification, rehabilitation, and lower-acuity conditions.
Remote monitoring may supplement inpatient staff but does not remove licensure, custody, medication, and safety accountability.
o.change_evidence: Payer and provider data tracing current inpatient episodes over five years into outpatient, residential, crisis, home, virtual, or self-service pathways, together with licensing and evidence for safe autonomous observation or treatment, would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: The NAICS definition requires licensed hospitals providing inpatient diagnostic, medical treatment, monitoring, beds, organized medical staff, psychiatric, psychological, and social-work services. Some current demand can move to outpatient centers, residential facilities, crisis programs, telehealth, or community care, and software can support screening and monitoring. Patients who still require inpatient psychiatric or withdrawal care need an accountable operator for continuous observation, medication, vital signs, therapeutic care, de-escalation, restraint, security, emergency response, and discharge planning.

q.bridge: CMS describes the Inpatient Psychiatric Facility Prospective Payment System as using patient-level adjustments including MS-DRG, comorbidities, age, and a variable per-diem adjustment, and it updates payment rates and quality-reporting consequences annually. This Medicare structure is used as a proxy for the mixed payer book: labor and process savings can be retained within a per-diem or contracted-rate period, but length-of-stay management, payer authorization, annual public rate updates, commercial renewals, Medicaid rules, quality requirements, and outlier adjustments share or constrain benefits. Medicare may overstate standardized prospective pricing for facilities dependent on state contracts and understate retention under fixed multi-year commercial rates.
caveats: Medicare IPF payment does not describe the full Medicaid, commercial, public-contract, and self-pay mix.
Per-diem payment can reward lower daily cost but also creates exposure if efficiency changes length of stay or authorization behavior.
Payment form is not direct evidence of the discounted share of AI benefits retained.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and claim-level evidence from representative operators showing revenue by Medicare per diem, Medicaid, commercial daily or case rate, public contract, cost-based, capitation, and self-pay terms, plus authorization denials, renewal cadence, staffing clauses, and post-productivity repricing, would materially narrow the range.
q.rationale: Daily and case-based reimbursement generally does not pass through each labor input, permitting near-term retention of implemented gains. Five-year capture is moderated because payers manage admissions and length of stay, contracts reprice, public rates update, quality and staffing obligations persist, and savings may be reinvested in scarce clinicians, observation coverage, compliance, security, and access rather than distributed as margin.

rho.bridge: HealthIT.gov finds 71% predictive-AI adoption in 2024 among responding nonfederal acute-care hospitals, but only 37% among independent hospitals, with billing and scheduling among fast-growing uses. This acute-care evidence is applied to psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals after discounting for smaller scale, uneven EHR maturity, fragmented behavioral-health data, heightened privacy, limited informatics staff, clinical safety, bias, explainability, crisis risk, and the need for human review. The proxy likely overstates implementation at standalone psychiatric facilities because they were not the measured population, though it may understate future uptake of inexpensive documentation and administrative tools.
caveats: The predictive-AI adoption source covers nonfederal acute-care hospitals, not psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals.
Use of a model does not establish broad implementation, labor substitution, or acceptable safety performance.
CMS quality reporting requirements add digital workflow opportunities but also preserve human validation and compliance work.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at at least fifteen independent psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals showing model inventory, workflow coverage, clinician overrides, safety events, documentation hours, revenue-cycle outcomes, integration cost, and actual staffing effects would materially narrow the range.
rho.rationale: Administrative and documentation tools can spread within five years, and decision support may assist risk review and care coordination. Implementation across the full exposed opportunity will lag general acute-care hospitals because behavioral data are contextual, incomplete, and sensitive; false negatives in suicide, withdrawal, violence, and medication risk are costly; and frontline workflows require trust, supervision, and continuous human accountability.

s5.bridge: LevinPro HC data reported in GlobeNewswire count 104 publicly announced behavioral-health transactions in 2025, including 52 in counseling and psychiatric care, but the release says the surge chiefly reflected outpatient mental-health services and talk therapy. That broader activity is translated into a lower five-year qualifying-transfer probability for licensed inpatient 622210 operators, excluding outpatient practices, minority investments, add-on sites, internal reorganizations, closures, and uncompleted announcements. The source likely overstates inpatient hospital transfer activity because outpatient deals dominate, while public announcements may undercount small private facility sales.
caveats: The transaction series has no denominator aligned to LMM psychiatric and substance-abuse hospital firms.
Most cited counseling and psychiatric-care deals were outpatient and do not establish inpatient hospital liquidity.
Publicly announced transaction counts can omit small private deals and include multi-site platforms as one event.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census linked to licensed U.S. 622210 facilities and the frozen LMM population, separating completed control sales from outpatient add-ons, minority investments, real-estate deals, nonprofit affiliations, internal reorganizations, closures, and bankruptcies, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: Behavioral-health demand, fragmented ownership, strategic interest, payer-network value, and succession can support transfers. Hospital licensure, certificate-of-need rules, clinical staffing, real estate, nonprofit control, government ownership, compliance exposure, and patient-safety liabilities make inpatient transactions slower and less common than the broad behavioral-health deal headline implies.

records.principal_driver: Persistent high-acuity behavioral-health need and prospective daily reimbursement support selective AI gains in documentation, intake, utilization, scheduling, and discharge workflows.
records.principal_weakness: Core work depends on human therapeutic judgment and continuous safety supervision, while the true transferable LMM inpatient target pool is obscured by government, nonprofit, system-unit, outpatient, and residential boundaries.

sources.supports: Defines 622210 as licensed psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals providing inpatient diagnostic, medical treatment, and monitoring, often for extended stays, with beds, organized medical staff, and psychiatric, psychological, and social-work services; distinguishes outpatient and residential facilities.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau — 2022 NAICS Manual: Psychiatric and Substance Abuse Hospitals

sources.supports: Lists the largest occupations in psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals, including 41,760 registered nurses, 30,420 psychiatric technicians, 17,530 substance-abuse and mental-health counselors, 14,180 psychiatric aides, 8,640 mental-health and substance-abuse social workers, and major nursing, management, and human-service roles.
sources.title: BLS — May 2024 OEWS Industry Employment Charts

sources.supports: Describes observation and recording, therapeutic activities, medication, intake and discharge, vital signs, daily-living assistance, safety monitoring, escort, cleaning, and restraint duties; projects 16% employment growth from 2024 to 2034.
sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook — Psychiatric Technicians and Aides

sources.supports: Reports 71% predictive-AI adoption among responding nonfederal acute-care hospitals in 2024, 37% among independent hospitals versus 86% among system-affiliated hospitals, growing billing and scheduling uses, and incomplete evaluation across all models.
sources.title: HealthIT.gov — Hospital Trends in the Use, Evaluation, and Governance of Predictive AI, 2023–2024

sources.supports: Reports 656 nonfederal psychiatric hospitals and provides ownership and system-affiliation context for the wider U.S. hospital population.
sources.title: American Hospital Association — Fast Facts on U.S. Hospitals, 2026

sources.supports: Reports 104 publicly announced behavioral-health transactions in 2025 versus 73 in 2024, including 52 counseling and psychiatric-care deals, while identifying outpatient mental-health services and talk therapy as the main driver.
sources.title: LevinPro HC / GlobeNewswire — Behavioral Health Care M&A Activity Increases 42% in 2025

sources.supports: Describes IPF prospective payment, patient-level adjustments for MS-DRG, comorbidities, age, and variable per diem, annual rate updates, outliers, quality reporting, cost data, and payment refinements.
sources.title: CMS — FY 2025 Inpatient Psychiatric Facility PPS Final Rule Fact Sheet

sources.supports: Publishes behavioral-health demand adjustment scalars rising from 100% in 2023 to 116.92% in 2028 for the combined adult-and-child population and explains status-quo and unmet-need workforce demand scenarios, including a starting psychiatrist shortfall.
sources.title: HRSA Bureau of Health Workforce — Behavioral Health Care Provider Model Components

records.title: Psychiatric and Substance Abuse Hospitals

caveats: a: O*NET provides task descriptions rather than time or wage weights for NAICS 621391 practices.
a: The AMA survey covers physicians broadly and does not isolate podiatrists or independent lower-middle-market practices.
a: Clinical case mix varies between routine office care, diabetic wound care, orthotics, and surgery.
rho: The AMA result is self-reported physician use or awareness, not verified end-to-end implementation or realized labor savings.
rho: Podiatry-specific adoption may differ from the physician sample because of practice size and procedural workflow.
rho: Privacy, safety, efficacy, liability, and training constraints can delay clinical uses more than administrative uses.
e: The Census definition establishes industry scope but does not measure eligibility among LMM firms.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify practices with unusually high clinician compensation, owner add-backs, or ancillary revenue.
e: A practice can be independently classified yet remain economically inseparable from a founder or facility relationship.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and not restricted to healthcare or the lower-middle market.
s5: The survey measures owner intentions rather than completed qualifying transactions.
s5: No cited source measures podiatry practice deal frequency or transfer completion.
q: No public source measures the share of AI-enabled gross benefit retained by independent podiatry practices.
q: Retention differs between Medicare-heavy, commercial insurance, cash-pay, and facility-based practices.
q: The estimate excludes demand displacement and implementation failure, which belong in d5, o, and rho.
d5: Occupational employment is not a direct measure of constant-price service demand or independent-practice revenue.
d5: CDC disease-burden evidence supports need but not the paid volume captured by LMM podiatry firms.
d5: Payer policy, scope-of-practice changes, and substitution by physicians or other caregivers may change demand independently of disease prevalence.
o: The sources describe occupations and broad outlook rather than direct buyer substitution in independent podiatry practices.
o: Routine nail care, education, screening, wound care, surgery, and complex diabetic care have very different operator requirements.
o: Operator-required does not necessarily mean the same practitioner type; some care can shift to another licensed provider.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Practices receive fee-for-service, contracted payer, Medicare or Medicaid, and patient cash payments for visits, diagnostics, procedures, surgery, supplies, and follow-up care; chronic disease and post-procedure cases create repeat utilization while reimbursement is commonly tied to coded clinical services.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospital-owned or other captive departments, physician practices classified outside 621391, product-only footwear or durable-medical-equipment sellers, nonclinical foot-care businesses, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market independent podiatry practices providing recurring or repeat diagnosis and treatment of foot, ankle, and lower-leg conditions to external patients, including examinations, imaging review, diabetic foot care, wound care, routine procedures, surgery, orthotics-related clinical work, and follow-up care in offices or facilities of others.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET describes podiatrist clinical and recordkeeping tasks, while the AMA surveys U.S. physicians across specialties about AI use cases rather than measuring a wage-weighted task share in podiatry offices. Transformation: documentation, chart and research summarization, patient-message drafting, coding support, scheduling, intake, billing follow-up, and portions of image or history review are mapped to exposed clinician and administrative labor, while physical examination, procedures, surgery, device fitting, and accountable diagnosis remain unexposed. Direction of error: physician-wide adoption and task descriptions may overstate substitutability in small podiatry practices with hands-on case mix, but may understate exposure in centralized billing, call handling, prior authorization, and documentation teams.
caveats: O*NET provides task descriptions rather than time or wage weights for NAICS 621391 practices.
The AMA survey covers physicians broadly and does not isolate podiatrists or independent lower-middle-market practices.
Clinical case mix varies between routine office care, diabetic wound care, orthotics, and surgery.
a.change_evidence: A wage-linked time study from a representative sample of independent podiatry practices separating physicians, medical assistants, front desk, billing, documentation, imaging review, procedures, surgery, and already-automated work would materially change this range.
a.rationale: Podiatry combines a substantial physical clinical core with repeatable digital administration and documentation. AI can reduce clerical work and assist clinical preparation, but most direct treatment time and final medical judgment remain difficult to substitute.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS projects podiatrist employment to grow 2 percent from 2024 to 2034 and attributes continuing need to population aging and chronic diseases while noting substitution by other caregivers; CDC documents recurring diabetic-foot risks and preventive foot care. Transformation: the occupational outlook and disease-linked need are translated into a five-year constant-price demand ratio for the present podiatry service basket, with a broad range for utilization, reimbursement, referral, and caregiver-substitution uncertainty. Direction of error: employment can understate service quantity if productivity or delegation rises, but can overstate independent-practice demand if hospitals, physicians, or other caregivers take share or reimbursement constrains visits.
caveats: Occupational employment is not a direct measure of constant-price service demand or independent-practice revenue.
CDC disease-burden evidence supports need but not the paid volume captured by LMM podiatry firms.
Payer policy, scope-of-practice changes, and substitution by physicians or other caregivers may change demand independently of disease prevalence.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price series of 621391 visits, procedures, unique patients, diabetic-foot episodes, referrals, payer-authorized utilization, reimbursement-adjusted collections, and market share by independent versus integrated practices would materially change this range.
d5.rationale: Aging, diabetes, obesity, mobility problems, and recurrent foot conditions support a stable service need, but BLS expects only limited occupation growth and explicitly identifies substitution by non-podiatrist caregivers. The current basket is therefore modeled near flat rather than at broad healthcare spending growth.

caveats: The Census definition establishes industry scope but does not measure eligibility among LMM firms.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged and may misclassify practices with unusually high clinician compensation, owner add-backs, or ancillary revenue.
A practice can be independently classified yet remain economically inseparable from a founder or facility relationship.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of LMM-band 621391 businesses using ownership records, payer enrollment, practice websites, clinician rosters, facility affiliations, revenue mix, owner production, and normalized EBITDA would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The official industry is already defined around independent podiatry practices serving patients, so most scaled firms should fit the recurring external-service lens. Eligibility is reduced by hospital-controlled arrangements, unusually owner-dependent practices, weak transferable management, nonrecurring surgical concentration, and economics distorted by ancillary product sales or facility contracts.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621391 ($2,024,495,626) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621391 ($5,518,101,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $150,001 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621391); class <5: 4,196 firms, avg revenue/firm $340,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $53,737 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,601 firms, avg revenue/firm $837,348 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $132,301 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 554 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,879,892 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $297,023 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 222 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,010,027 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $791,584 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 25 firms, avg revenue/firm $19,523,880 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,084,773 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,649,268 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $418,584 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 25

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: O*NET describes physical treatment, diagnosis using examinations and tests, patient advice, recordkeeping, surgery, and device-related work; BLS describes medical and surgical foot care and notes that some patients may use other appropriate caregivers. Transformation: hands-on examination, procedures, surgery, wound management, fitting, and accountable diagnosis are treated as operator-required, while routine education, intake, monitoring, documentation, navigation, and some low-acuity follow-up can be self-served, software-supplied, or shifted to other providers. Direction of error: current task descriptions may overstate operator need as remote monitoring and AI improve, but may understate it where licensure, liability, physical intervention, and patient trust preserve human clinical delivery.
caveats: The sources describe occupations and broad outlook rather than direct buyer substitution in independent podiatry practices.
Routine nail care, education, screening, wound care, surgery, and complex diabetic care have very different operator requirements.
Operator-required does not necessarily mean the same practitioner type; some care can shift to another licensed provider.
o.change_evidence: Patient-episode data showing which podiatry visits are eliminated, self-managed, remotely managed, handled by software, or shifted to physicians, nurses, and other caregivers, including outcomes and payer acceptance, would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Most podiatry value depends on a licensed practitioner or supervised clinical team physically examining or treating a patient. Software can reduce information and follow-up work but is unlikely to eliminate most core episodes within five years.

caveats: No public source measures the share of AI-enabled gross benefit retained by independent podiatry practices.
Retention differs between Medicare-heavy, commercial insurance, cash-pay, and facility-based practices.
The estimate excludes demand displacement and implementation failure, which belong in d5, o, and rho.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and practice-level diligence on payer mix, fee schedules, capitation or value-based terms, staffing ratios, AI vendor costs, claim-denial effects, visit capacity, wage savings, patient pricing, and renewal repricing would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Coded fee-for-service and patient cash pricing do not automatically pass each minute of administrative productivity to payers, so practices can retain savings from lower documentation, scheduling, billing, and intake effort. Retention is reduced by software fees, payer fee schedules, value-based contracting, competitive pressure, compliance costs, and the likelihood that clinical time saved becomes capacity or service improvement rather than immediate payroll removal.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: the AMA's 2026 survey of U.S. physicians measures self-reported incorporation or awareness of AI use cases and identifies documentation, summarization, privacy, safety, training, and liability considerations. Transformation: the observed momentum in assistive use is applied to the exposed podiatry workflow over five years, with discounts for small-practice integration, EHR interoperability, protected health information, clinical validation, staff training, payer rules, and the need for licensed review. Direction of error: physician awareness or use may overstate reliable production implementation and labor release, while rapid vendor embedding in EHR, revenue-cycle, and patient-communication tools may make implementation faster than this estimate.
caveats: The AMA result is self-reported physician use or awareness, not verified end-to-end implementation or realized labor savings.
Podiatry-specific adoption may differ from the physician sample because of practice size and procedural workflow.
Privacy, safety, efficacy, liability, and training constraints can delay clinical uses more than administrative uses.
rho.change_evidence: Practice-level cohort data from podiatry EHR and revenue-cycle vendors showing active use, error rates, clinician override, minutes saved, claim denials, staffing changes, patient complaints, and sustained adoption by practice size would materially change this estimate.
rho.rationale: Administrative and documentation tools can be added without redesigning physical care, and mainstream clinical software is likely to embed more assistance. Reliable deployment still requires workflow integration, privacy controls, quality review, and practitioner accountability.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. Transformation: the intention rate is discounted for plans that do not close, internal or family transfers without qualifying control, and podiatry practices whose licenses, referral relationships, payer enrollment, or owner production do not transfer cleanly. Direction of error: the broad employer sample may overstate completed qualifying podiatry deals, while the retirement and succession needs of clinician-owners may make scaled group practices more transaction-prone than the general sample.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and not restricted to healthcare or the lower-middle market.
The survey measures owner intentions rather than completed qualifying transactions.
No cited source measures podiatry practice deal frequency or transfer completion.
s5.change_evidence: Five-year completed control-deal counts and eligible-practice denominators from Irving Levin Associates, PitchBook, Axial, healthcare M&A advisers, lender files, state licensure records, and payer-change-of-ownership filings would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Podiatry practices can transfer when multiple clinicians, payer contracts, referral sources, systems, and management outlast the owner. Clinical owner dependence and credentialing make stated succession intentions substantially more generous than completed control-transfer probability.

records.principal_driver: Repeat, information-heavy clinical workflows offer administrative AI leverage while physical foot care preserves an accountable operator role.
records.principal_weakness: Most direct clinical labor is hands-on and licensed, while the eligible transaction pool is small and practices may depend heavily on producing owners.

sources.supports: Defines 621391 as independent D.P. practices diagnosing and treating diseases and deformities of the foot in offices or facilities of others.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Sector 62 Definitions: Offices of Podiatrists

sources.supports: Describes podiatrist tasks including physical treatment, diagnosis from histories, examinations, imaging and tests, patient advice, surgery, device work, and medical-record maintenance.
sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Podiatrists

sources.supports: Projects podiatrist employment to grow 2 percent from 2024 to 2034; cites aging and chronic disease as demand supports and substitution by other caregivers as a limit.
sources.title: Occupational Outlook Handbook: Podiatrists

sources.supports: Reports AI use and awareness among U.S. physicians, leading documentation and summarization use cases, and adoption constraints involving privacy, safety, efficacy, liability, and training.
sources.title: AMA 2026 Physician Survey on Augmented Intelligence

sources.supports: Reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years and describes the survey population.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Describes podiatrists' role in diabetic foot care, recommends recurring foot examinations, and reports that about 12 percent of people with diabetes develop diabetic foot ulcers during their lifetime.

records.title: Offices of Podiatrists

caveats: a: No public source reports a six-digit occupation mix or wage-weighted task distribution for NAICS 561740.
a: The BLS occupation is broader than carpet and upholstery cleaning and includes duties outside the industry definition.
a: The supplied compensation ratio has a 2024-wage and 2022-receipts vintage mismatch.
rho: General SME adoption is not implementation intensity in NAICS 561740.
rho: Use of AI for marketing or messages does not necessarily reduce payroll or avoid hiring.
rho: Small operators may lack consistent customer, route, and job-history data needed for deeper automation.
e: No source measures lens eligibility among firms in the frozen EBITDA band.
e: Residential customers may repeat only every several years and are not contractually recurring.
e: The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and small, making eligibility sensitive to classification and normalization error.
s5: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than this industry or EBITDA band.
s5: Owner intentions are not completed transactions and include transfers that may not qualify.
s5: Local reputation, lead channels, and technician retention may deteriorate when an owner exits.
q: No source measures the national revenue mix by pricing model for NAICS 561740.
q: Published pricing guides are advisory and not realized transaction data.
q: Administrative time savings create value only if owners reduce paid hours, avoid hiring, or redeploy capacity productively.
d5: Occupational employment is not industry output and includes many non-carpet cleaning activities.
d5: No source quantifies the installed carpet and upholstery base or cleaning frequency nationally.
d5: Residential cleaning can be deferred, while commercial demand varies with occupancy and flooring substitution.
o: No source measures operator-required quantity directly for this industry.
o: Commercial facilities may bundle carpet work into broader janitorial contracts rather than use a specialist.
o: Advances in autonomous floor equipment or stain-resistant materials could reduce operator need faster than assumed.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: External households and organizations buy on-site or plant-based cleaning through per-room, per-square-foot, hourly, quoted-project, or scheduled maintenance pricing. Residential demand is commonly episodic but repeatable; commercial and institutional accounts can be scheduled under recurring maintenance plans or service agreements.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house cleaning departments, self-service equipment rental, janitorial firms without substantive carpet or upholstery work, new-product manufacturing or installation, repair-only operations, one-person practices without a transferable operating organization, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced cleaning, spotting, dyeing, maintenance, and related care for used carpets, rugs, and upholstery at residential, commercial, institutional, or plant locations.

a.bridge: BLS describes the adjacent janitor and building-cleaner occupation rather than a wage-weighted task census for NAICS 561740, and SBA describes generic small-business AI uses rather than this industry's payroll. I treat on-site vacuuming, extraction, spotting, equipment movement, setup, inspection, and customer-premises work as minimally exposed, while mapping customer intake, reminders, routing support, quote drafting, marketing, review responses, purchasing administration, and routine office communication into the exposed share. The broader BLS occupation includes non-carpet duties and may misstate the industry's field-versus-office mix; generic SBA use cases may overstate feasible exposure in firms with limited digitized data.
caveats: No public source reports a six-digit occupation mix or wage-weighted task distribution for NAICS 561740.
The BLS occupation is broader than carpet and upholstery cleaning and includes duties outside the industry definition.
The supplied compensation ratio has a 2024-wage and 2022-receipts vintage mismatch.
a.change_evidence: A representative NAICS 561740 payroll-weighted time study separating field technicians, dispatch, sales, estimators, customer service, and administration, with current automation and AI-feasibility testing by task, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: The industry's core output is cleaning physical surfaces. BLS describes cleaners spending most of the day standing, using vacuums, sprayers, buffers, and other equipment, while SBA identifies repeat administration, content, customer service, and business-data analysis as practical small-business AI uses. AI can reduce office and coordination labor but does not currently replace most technician hours. [S1, S2, S3]

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for the broader janitor and building-cleaner occupation, not constant-price service quantity for NAICS 561740. I use its 2 percent 2024-2034 employment growth and continued need for clean and healthy spaces as a stable-demand anchor, convert the decade outlook into a roughly flat five-year quantity base, and widen for housing turnover, commercial occupancy, floor-covering mix, consumer budgets, and productivity. Employment can understate quantity if equipment improves output per worker, while the broader occupation's recurring janitorial demand may overstate specialized carpet-cleaning durability.
caveats: Occupational employment is not industry output and includes many non-carpet cleaning activities.
No source quantifies the installed carpet and upholstery base or cleaning frequency nationally.
Residential cleaning can be deferred, while commercial demand varies with occupancy and flooring substitution.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-price Census service revenue for NAICS 561740 combined with carpet-installed-base data, commercial occupancy, customer cleaning frequency, backlog, and same-location job counts would materially change the year-five quantity ratio.
d5.rationale: Physical carpets, rugs, and upholstery continue to soil and require maintenance, while commercial programs and repeat residential jobs provide replacement-like demand. BLS expects 2 percent employment growth over 2024-2034 for broader cleaners and says continued need for clean and healthy spaces supports demand, though high-tech cleaning methods limit employment growth. The service is discretionary enough for postponement in weak consumer or property markets. [S1, S2, S6, S7]

caveats: No source measures lens eligibility among firms in the frozen EBITDA band.
Residential customers may repeat only every several years and are not contractually recurring.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged and small, making eligibility sensitive to classification and normalization error.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence across the supplied population showing revenue by repeat customer, scheduled-maintenance share, customer concentration, technician depth, owner field hours, equipment ownership, and transferable contracts would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: NAICS 561740 is inherently an outsourced service to external property owners and occupants, and carpet or upholstery care can recur as soils and wear accumulate. Firms in the supplied LMM band are more likely than sole operators to have crews, equipment, routes, commercial accounts, and an organization that can transfer. I exclude operations dominated by nonrepeat restoration events, a single owner's personal labor or relationships, captive cleaning, and adjacent janitorial or repair activities. [S1, S6, S7]

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561740 ($1,656,368,233) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561740 ($4,396,373,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $116,825 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561740); class <5: 4,924 firms, avg revenue/firm $209,051 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $32,717 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 1,020 firms, avg revenue/firm $633,106 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $99,081 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 481 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,525,081 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $238,675 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 272 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,863,772 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $604,680 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 31 firms, avg revenue/firm $18,598,161 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,910,612 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $120,061,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,789,599 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 31

o.bridge: BLS describes physical cleaning work in a broader occupation and Census defines the service basket, but neither measures what share of year-five carpet-cleaning demand will require a specialized external operator. I map extraction, spotting, chemical selection, equipment transport, furniture handling, premises access, inspection, and accountable remediation to operator-required quantity, while allowing some demand to shift to customer self-cleaning, rental equipment, improved materials, bundled janitorial providers, or more automated cleaning equipment. The broader BLS task list may overstate similarity to specialist technicians, but the physical-location requirement makes the direction robust.
caveats: No source measures operator-required quantity directly for this industry.
Commercial facilities may bundle carpet work into broader janitorial contracts rather than use a specialist.
Advances in autonomous floor equipment or stain-resistant materials could reduce operator need faster than assumed.
o.change_evidence: A revenue-weighted customer study tracking current scopes that remain with specialist firms, move to in-house or rental self-service, bundle into janitorial contracts, adopt robotic equipment, or disappear would materially change this share.
o.rationale: The purchased outcome requires work on customer-owned physical surfaces with powered equipment, chemicals, setup, movement, and quality judgment. AI can improve booking and support decisions but cannot supply most of the physical service; self-service equipment and bundled cleaners can displace a limited share. [S1, S2, S6]

q.bridge: Housecall Pro and ISSA describe current quoted pricing practices for carpet and broader commercial cleaning, not a revenue-weighted contract mix or observed retention of AI benefits in NAICS 561740. I assign higher retention to per-room, per-square-foot, quoted-project, and fixed maintenance-plan revenue than to hourly jobs, then discount for competitive rebids, renewal repricing, customer shopping, recurring-plan discounts, and the need to spend some savings on faster response or marketing. Vendor-facing guidance may overrepresent organized software-using firms and commercial pricing, which could overstate retention across the full lens.
caveats: No source measures the national revenue mix by pricing model for NAICS 561740.
Published pricing guides are advisory and not realized transaction data.
Administrative time savings create value only if owners reduce paid hours, avoid hiring, or redeploy capacity productively.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and invoice audit showing revenue by pricing basis, renewal cadence, discounting, post-automation price changes, gross margin, lead-source fees, and technician utilization would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Residential work is commonly priced per room or square foot, and commercial work is generally priced by square foot or hour; scheduled work can be standardized and recurring plans use discounts. Fixed job or area prices let operators retain office-efficiency gains initially, but low switching costs, visible local competition, plan renewals, and hourly commercial work share benefits with customers over five years. [S6, S7]

rho.bridge: OECD measures AI adoption across SMEs in multiple countries and sectors, while SBA lists generic small-business applications. I translate their adoption and use evidence into five-year implementation of this industry's already-limited exposed office opportunity, raising implementation for low-cost embedded tools but discounting fragmented systems, weak data, owner attention, training, quality review, and the very small number of scalable LMM firms. OECD's 10-49 employee firms are broader and often more digitally mature than carpet cleaners, which could overstate implementation; rapid diffusion of embedded scheduling and communications features could make the estimate conservative.
caveats: General SME adoption is not implementation intensity in NAICS 561740.
Use of AI for marketing or messages does not necessarily reduce payroll or avoid hiring.
Small operators may lack consistent customer, route, and job-history data needed for deeper automation.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of LMM carpet and upholstery cleaners measuring production deployment, accepted-output rates, labor hours by workflow, software integration, rework, and avoided hiring would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Low-cost tools can plausibly become routine for customer response, marketing, reminders, job documentation, and administrative analysis within five years. Yet OECD reports that only 11.9 percent of 10-49 employee firms used AI in 2024, that core-function use was below 10 percent in G7 countries, and that SME generative-AI use is mostly peripheral. SBA advises small firms to start small and retain human review. [S3, S4]

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year plans among US employer-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers of LMM carpet cleaners. I use its 22 percent sell-or-transfer intention as an upper anchor, then discount transfers to family or employees that may not qualify, plans that do not close, non-control events, owner-dependent wind-downs, and industry classification or EBITDA-band mismatch. LMM scale and tangible equipment or route density may improve transferability relative to microbusinesses, while local brand and owner dependence may reduce it.
caveats: Gallup covers all employer businesses rather than this industry or EBITDA band.
Owner intentions are not completed transactions and include transfers that may not qualify.
Local reputation, lead channels, and technician retention may deteriorate when an owner exits.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census for NAICS 561740 linking private and announced control changes to the eligible-firm denominator, with seller EBITDA, ownership percentage, buyer type, and post-close continuity, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22 percent of employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or go public within five years and 5 percent planned to close. A carpet-cleaning firm with crews, equipment, repeat accounts, and documented operations can transfer, but stated intentions exceed completed qualifying control changes. [S5]

records.principal_driver: Fixed-price physical jobs leave a small but implementable back-office efficiency layer that can be retained when systems reduce coordination labor or avoided hiring.
records.principal_weakness: Most wages fund on-site manual execution that current AI cannot substitute, and the estimated LMM acquisition pool is very small.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments primarily engaged in cleaning and dyeing used rugs, carpets, and upholstery.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS 561740 Carpet and Upholstery Cleaning Services

sources.supports: Describes physical cleaning duties and equipment, physical stamina requirements, 2 percent projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034, continued demand for clean and healthy spaces, and employment restraint from high-tech cleaning methods.
sources.title: Janitors and Building Cleaners

sources.supports: Identifies practical small-business AI uses in repeat administration, data analysis, content, customer service, phone routing, and review responses, while advising firms to start small and apply human review and data safeguards.

sources.supports: Reports lower AI adoption among SMEs, including 11.9 percent of 10-49 employee firms in 2024, below-10-percent core-business adoption in G7 countries, and predominantly peripheral generative-AI use among SMEs.

caveats: a: BLS publishes an occupation mix for NAICS 561500, not Convention and Visitors Bureaus alone, and that parent is dominated by travel agencies and reservation services.
a: Destinations International describes functions and examples but does not report a wage-weighted task inventory or current automation baseline.
a: Public-facing marketing workflows may be more exposed than convention sales, stakeholder governance, and destination-development work, so organization mix matters materially.
rho: The 2025 Futures Study has 537 participants across 36 countries rather than a representative U.S. six-digit sample.
rho: The industry blog emphasizes a handful of named adopters and does not publish the survey response count or realized payroll savings.
rho: Public records, accessibility, procurement, brand safety, and community-trust obligations can slow implementation even when tools are technically available.
e: No source measures the legal-form and transferability distribution of the 101 frozen LMM-band firms.
e: The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a commercial-services margin that may be misleading for nonprofit and budget-consuming destination organizations.

e: Public funding does not by itself prove public ownership, and some nonprofit organizations contract with governments while remaining operationally independent, but neither structure creates ordinary equity control.
s5: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures plans rather than completed qualifying transfers.
s5: Destination-organization governance changes, executive succession, mergers of nonprofits, and public contract rebids usually are not purchases of equity control.
s5: The tiny eligible subset makes deal-frequency estimates especially unstable.
q: No source measures the five-year share of implemented gross benefit retained by U.S. lower-middle-market CVBs.
q: Retention as distributable margin differs sharply from retention as additional mission capacity in nonprofit organizations.
q: Funding structures vary among dedicated taxes, improvement-district assessments, municipal contracts, memberships, sponsorships, advertising, and grants.
d5: Travel spending is a demand driver, not a direct measure of CVB service quantity or constant-quality output.
d5: The forecast extends through 2030, one year short of a full five-year horizon from 2026, and travel forecasts can change materially.
d5: Funding risk is global survey evidence, while travel forecasts are national U.S. aggregates that mask destination-specific winners and losers.
o: No source quantifies the operator-required share for CVBs over five years.
o: AI-powered search is already reducing traditional website traffic, but its effect on convention wins, partner value, and public mandates is not measured.
o: Large convention destinations and small visitor-information bureaus have different exposure to self-service substitution.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The economic customers and funders are commonly municipalities, tourism improvement districts, lodging and hospitality members, and other destination stakeholders, while travelers and meeting planners receive many services without a direct charge. Revenue commonly comes from dedicated occupancy or sales taxes, district assessments, government service contracts, memberships, sponsorships, advertising, and grants rather than transaction fees from visitors.
lens.excluded_activities: Government tourism-development offices, captive municipal departments, entities without independently transferable control, shells, convention and trade-show organizers, venue operators, travel agencies, tour operators, and software-only travel-information products.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market operators repeatedly providing destination marketing, convention and meeting sales support, visitor information, itinerary content, local-partner promotion, and related destination-management services to external municipalities, tourism districts, lodging stakeholders, event organizers, businesses, and travelers.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS reports employment shares and wages for the broader NAICS 561500 parent, while Destinations International documents destination-organization workflows and current AI uses. Transformation: the parent occupation mix is reweighted conceptually toward CVB marketing, research, sales, visitor-information, and administrative work, then the not-yet-automated portions of content production, research synthesis, inquiry handling, localization, CRM upkeep, reporting, and sales preparation are mapped to wage-weighted exposure. Direction of error: the parent industry's large travel-agent and reservation occupations likely overstate routine transactional exposure, while its very small published creative share likely understates CVB-specific marketing and content work; current unmeasured tool use may make residual exposure lower.
caveats: BLS publishes an occupation mix for NAICS 561500, not Convention and Visitors Bureaus alone, and that parent is dominated by travel agencies and reservation services.
Destinations International describes functions and examples but does not report a wage-weighted task inventory or current automation baseline.
Public-facing marketing workflows may be more exposed than convention sales, stakeholder governance, and destination-development work, so organization mix matters materially.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit payroll-and-time study from Destinations International's reporting platform that separates destination marketing, content, convention sales, visitor services, research, partner relations, advocacy, finance, and already-automated work would materially change the wage-weighted exposed share.
a.rationale: CVB work contains a large digital information and marketing layer that AI can draft, translate, summarize, personalize, classify, and analyze. Exposure is constrained by convention selling, government and board relations, local-partner coordination, destination strategy, community engagement, brand judgment, and on-site visitor or event work that still depend on accountable people and local knowledge.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: U.S. Travel Association forecasts inflation-adjusted travel spending, including domestic leisure, business, and group travel, while Destinations International measures funding risk and expanding destination-organization mandates. Transformation: forecast real travel activity through 2030 is used as a demand anchor and tempered for the weaker link between visitor spending and bureau budgets, international volatility, public-funding diversion, and local political choices to estimate year-five constant-price demand for the current CVB service basket. Direction of error: national travel spending growth may overstate bureau demand where funding is cut or marketing shifts to platforms, but may understate demand where organizations add destination development, events, community engagement, and data responsibilities.
caveats: Travel spending is a demand driver, not a direct measure of CVB service quantity or constant-quality output.
The forecast extends through 2030, one year short of a full five-year horizon from 2026, and travel forecasts can change materially.
Funding risk is global survey evidence, while travel forecasts are national U.S. aggregates that mask destination-specific winners and losers.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year panel of constant-dollar U.S. destination-organization budgets, service volumes, convention leads, bookings, visitor inquiries, funding cuts, and mandate expansions, matched to local travel demand, would materially change this ratio.
d5.rationale: The spring 2026 forecast shows continued real growth in domestic leisure, business, and group travel through 2030, supporting ongoing destination promotion and convention work. Demand for bureau services is less secure than travel demand itself: 42 percent of destination organizations reported near-term funding at risk, international demand remains volatile, and public budgets can diverge from visitor volumes. Expanded mandates partly offset those risks.

caveats: No source measures the legal-form and transferability distribution of the 101 frozen LMM-band firms.
The frozen firm count is margin-bridged using a commercial-services margin that may be misleading for nonprofit and budget-consuming destination organizations.

Public funding does not by itself prove public ownership, and some nonprofit organizations contract with governments while remaining operationally independent, but neither structure creates ordinary equity control.
e.change_evidence: A firm-by-firm audit of the frozen population using IRS status, secretary-of-state records, municipal contracts, governing documents, beneficial ownership, funding concentration, change-of-control clauses, and normalized distributable earnings would materially change the eligible share.
e.rationale: The activity is recurring and externally oriented, but the transferable-firm subset is unusually narrow. Destinations International reports that 92 percent of destination-organization funding comes through public-sector channels, and a large operating example, Visit Denver, is a private nonprofit governed by a board and contracted by the city. Many bureaus are therefore public, quasi-public, nonprofit, or contract-dependent organizations whose control cannot be purchased like an ordinary company. The eligible minority consists of genuinely private, independently transferable operators with durable service contracts, stakeholder relationships, and economics separable from a public body or nonprofit charter.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: Census defines the CVB service basket, and Destinations International documents both self-service AI capabilities and the industry's expanding coordination, advocacy, destination-development, brand, event, and community roles. Transformation: routine information, itinerary, translation, content, search, and first-line inquiry volumes are treated as increasingly self-served, while convention representation, official brand accountability, local partnerships, funding advocacy, community alignment, and destination stewardship remain operator-required. Direction of error: rapid AI-agent adoption could disintermediate more visitor discovery and meeting research than assumed, while the industry's quasi-public mandate and local convening role could preserve more operator-required demand.
caveats: No source quantifies the operator-required share for CVBs over five years.
AI-powered search is already reducing traditional website traffic, but its effect on convention wins, partner value, and public mandates is not measured.
Large convention destinations and small visitor-information bureaus have different exposure to self-service substitution.
o.change_evidence: Buyer and funder research measuring planner use of CVBs versus AI and platforms, chatbot containment, website referral losses, convention lead sourcing, municipal renewal decisions, and which services stakeholders still require from an official operator would materially change this share.
o.rationale: Travelers and planners can increasingly obtain basic destination information, itinerary ideas, translations, and comparisons directly from platforms and AI agents. An accountable local organization remains important for official representation, convention bids, venue and hotel coordination, stakeholder access, public funding, local-data validation, crisis communication, brand stewardship, and community legitimacy. The operator role therefore contracts at the informational edge but persists in relationship-heavy and civic functions.

q.bridge: Source population and quantity: Destinations International documents public-sector funding concentration, funding risk, diversified levies and assessments, and current AI-enabled capacity in destination organizations. Transformation: budgeted tax, assessment, membership, sponsorship, advertising, and contract revenue is treated as less mechanically tied to labor hours than cost-plus billing, allowing implemented benefits to be retained as capacity, faster service, broader language coverage, or operating margin, then discounted for budget reviews, procurement, stakeholder scrutiny, and mandated reinvestment. Direction of error: dedicated funding may protect retained capacity more than assumed, while nonprofit restrictions and public funders may capture nearly all savings through added deliverables or lower allocations.
caveats: No source measures the five-year share of implemented gross benefit retained by U.S. lower-middle-market CVBs.
Retention as distributable margin differs sharply from retention as additional mission capacity in nonprofit organizations.
Funding structures vary among dedicated taxes, improvement-district assessments, municipal contracts, memberships, sponsorships, advertising, and grants.
q.change_evidence: Contract, budget, and audited-financial cohorts showing funding formulas, renewal resets, labor expense, AI spend, service levels, unrestricted surplus, and changes in sponsor or public allocations before and after automation would materially change commercial retention.
q.rationale: Visitor and planner services are often free at use, so labor savings are not passed through transaction by transaction. Dedicated taxes and assessments can preserve an approved budget, and AI can expand output within it. Retention is limited because public and member funders scrutinize performance, 42 percent of surveyed organizations reported funding at risk within three years, and nonprofit operators may be expected to reinvest savings rather than distribute them.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: Destinations International's global study and industry blog document active use of generative AI for itineraries, multilingual content, creative workflows, research, optimization, chatbots, and internal work, alongside uneven adoption and policy development. Transformation: demonstrated uses in destination organizations are translated into the portion of remaining exposed work a scaled U.S. operator can operationalize within five years after training, review, integration, brand, data, accessibility, and public-accountability constraints. Direction of error: highlighted adopters and association participants likely overstate typical readiness, while five years of product integration and the labor pressure identified in the industry study could make current examples understate eventual implementation.
caveats: The 2025 Futures Study has 537 participants across 36 countries rather than a representative U.S. six-digit sample.
The industry blog emphasizes a handful of named adopters and does not publish the survey response count or realized payroll savings.
Public records, accessibility, procurement, brand safety, and community-trust obligations can slow implementation even when tools are technically available.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after deployment audits across U.S. CVBs measuring eligible workflows, active-seat adoption, human-review minutes, factual correction rates, integration cost, multilingual quality, and realized labor hours would materially move this estimate.
rho.rationale: The core tools already operate in workflows central to the industry, and destination organizations report leadership mandates, staff training, chatbots, expanded language coverage, and new content formats. Implementation remains short of straight-through automation because local facts change, public brands require review, staff adoption is uneven, data quality and privacy matter, and convention or stakeholder commitments need accountable approval.

s5.bridge: Source population and quantity: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell, transfer, or take public within five years. Transformation: the intention rate is applied only to the small eligible private subset and discounted for plans that do not close, nonmarket gifts, internal changes, public-contract novations, and ownership changes blocked by funding or change-of-control terms. Direction of error: broad employer data may overstate completed qualifying transactions, while the eligible subset's scale, contracts, and staff may make it more transferable than the average small employer.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and measures plans rather than completed qualifying transfers.
Destination-organization governance changes, executive succession, mergers of nonprofits, and public contract rebids usually are not purchases of equity control.
The tiny eligible subset makes deal-frequency estimates especially unstable.
s5.change_evidence: Completed five-year control-transaction counts for private destination marketing and visitor-services operators from PitchBook, Capital IQ, Axial, municipal contract disclosures, and specialist brokers, matched to an eligible-firm denominator, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A genuinely private, contract-backed destination-services operator can transfer, but stated succession intentions are not completed control deals. The dominant nonprofit, public, and board-governed structures belong in eligibility rather than being counted as acquisitions; leadership turnover and municipal rebids are not qualifying control transfers.

records.principal_driver: Marketing, information, translation, research, and reporting workflows are digitally intensive and already show practical AI adoption.
records.principal_weakness: Publicly funded nonprofit and board-governed structures sharply limit the pool of independently transferable firms.

caveats: a: The BLS occupational mix covers all outpatient care centers rather than dialysis centers alone.
a: CMS requirements identify workflows and accountable tasks but do not measure time, wages, or current automation.
rho: HTI-1 governs certified-health-IT transparency and interoperability rather than facility adoption or realized savings.
rho: No target-band dialysis-center study links AI deployment to verified labor reductions over multiple years.
e: CMS reports facilities, while the frozen n input estimates firms; multi-site ownership and chain concentration can create a large unit mismatch.
e: The frozen n estimate relies on a broad healthcare-facilities margin rather than dialysis-specific normalized EBITDA.
s5: Gallup intentions are not dialysis-specific and include non-arm's-length transfers.
s5: Facility ownership changes may be internal reorganizations rather than qualifying firm-level control transfers.
q: The Medicare bundle is directly observed, but the payer mix and commercial contract reset cadence of target firms are not.
q: No source directly measures the long-run share of AI-enabled gross benefit retained by dialysis operators.
d5: CDC burden and starts are patient indicators rather than a constant-price revenue or treatment-volume forecast.
d5: BLS NAICS 621400 includes several outpatient industries and cannot isolate dialysis demand.
o: Operator survival is not the same as survival of the current number of in-center stations or visits.
o: The estimate assumes no near-term technology eliminates the need for certified clinical accountability.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Centers deliver repeated, medically necessary treatments and support to chronic kidney-failure and some acute-kidney-injury patients. Medicare pays predominantly through a regulated bundled per-treatment prospective payment, with case-mix and facility adjustments; Medicaid, Medicare Advantage, and commercial contracts supplement the payer mix.
lens.excluded_activities: Hospital departments that cannot transfer as standalone going concerns; nephrology physician practices without dialysis-center operations; equipment manufacturers; laboratories; transplant centers; captive units; shells; and businesses outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Transferable lower-middle-market operators providing recurring outpatient hemodialysis, peritoneal-dialysis support, home-dialysis training and monitoring, interdisciplinary care planning, and related renal-dialysis services to patients and payers.

a.bridge: Source population: broad NAICS 621400 Outpatient Care Centers employment, national O*NET nurse and medical-secretary tasks, and CMS dialysis-care requirements, rather than a 621492 wage-weighted task study. Quantity transformation: assign scheduling, eligibility, billing, documentation, record review, care-plan drafting, quality reporting, lab trending, patient communication, and decision support to the exposed share while reserving machine setup, cannulation, direct monitoring, infection control, medication administration, emergency response, counseling, and accountable clinical decisions. Error direction: the broad outpatient mix may overstate exposure because dialysis is technician- and nursing-intensive, or understate it if repetitive treatment data and documentation dominate more staff time than in other outpatient settings.
caveats: The BLS occupational mix covers all outpatient care centers rather than dialysis centers alone.
CMS requirements identify workflows and accountable tasks but do not measure time, wages, or current automation.
a.change_evidence: Facility payroll and time-and-motion data by nurses, patient-care technicians, dietitians, social workers, billing staff, and managers, linked to task-level automation already in use, would materially move a.
a.rationale: Dialysis generates structured, repeated data and extensive documentation that AI can summarize, reconcile, code, prioritize, and monitor. The treatment itself requires physical equipment, direct patient contact, infection control, vascular-access work, and licensed oversight, keeping most current labor outside substitutable scope.

d5.bridge: Source population: national CKD burden and dialysis starts, CMS's current facility and payment baseline, USRDS incidence/prevalence monitoring, and BLS's broader outpatient-care output projection. Quantity transformation: infer modest five-year growth in constant-quality dialysis demand from the large chronic-disease pipeline and recurring starts, while discounting broad outpatient growth for mortality, transplantation, prevention, new medicines that slow progression, and shifts among in-center and home modalities. Error direction: may be high if prevention and disease-modifying drugs reduce starts, or low if diabetes, hypertension, aging, and survival expand the treated population.
caveats: CDC burden and starts are patient indicators rather than a constant-price revenue or treatment-volume forecast.
BLS NAICS 621400 includes several outpatient industries and cannot isolate dialysis demand.
d5.change_evidence: USRDS age-adjusted incident and prevalent dialysis counts through 2026, modality-specific patient-years, transplant rates, mortality, payer mix, and treatment volumes linked to 621492 revenue deflators would materially move d5.
d5.rationale: Dialysis is life-sustaining for kidney failure, CDC reports a large CKD population and hundreds of daily treatment starts, and CMS expects roughly 7,600 facilities to serve Medicare beneficiaries under the current system. Demand is durable but not guaranteed to match broad outpatient growth because incidence, mortality, transplantation, and home modality adoption all affect center volumes.

caveats: CMS reports facilities, while the frozen n input estimates firms; multi-site ownership and chain concentration can create a large unit mismatch.
The frozen n estimate relies on a broad healthcare-facilities margin rather than dialysis-specific normalized EBITDA.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level roster reconciling CMS-certified facilities to ultimate owners, EBITDA, hospital affiliation, payer enrollment, medical-director contracts, accreditation, and change-of-ownership history would materially move e.
e.rationale: Dialysis centers naturally provide recurring services to external patients and payers, and a licensed facility with staff, equipment, payer enrollment, records, and referral relationships can transfer as a going concern. Eligibility is reduced by hospital-based units, facilities owned inside large chains, nontransferable payer or medical-director arrangements, licensing and certification issues, and the possibility that the frozen margin bridge misclassifies firm size.
