Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $215,888 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 621492); class <5: 158 firms, avg revenue/firm $643,899 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $101,736 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 71 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,278,845 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $202,058 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 92 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,086,196 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $329,619 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 118 firms, avg revenue/firm $6,187,881 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $977,685 -> out of $1-10M band; class 100-499: 26 firms, avg revenue/firm $23,230,423 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $3,670,407 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 42 firms, avg revenue/firm $658,654,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $104,067,385 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 26

o.bridge: Source population: CMS coverage, payment, assessment, care-plan, quality, and professional-contact requirements for Medicare dialysis, rather than an observed five-year bypass share for lower-middle-market firms. Quantity transformation: treat physical dialysis, recurring clinical assessment, interdisciplinary plans, safety standards, and monthly professional contact as operator-required, while allowing home treatment, transplant, prevention, and software-enabled self-management to remove some in-center activity. Error direction: may overstate survival if home technologies sharply reduce facility involvement or understate it because home dialysis still requires a certified facility's training, monitoring, supplies, and accountable clinical support.
caveats: Operator survival is not the same as survival of the current number of in-center stations or visits.
The estimate assumes no near-term technology eliminates the need for certified clinical accountability.
o.change_evidence: Modality-specific CMS and USRDS data on patient-years, facility support hours for home dialysis, fully remote care models, transplant transitions, and provider-of-record requirements would materially move o.
o.rationale: Software can change where dialysis occurs and automate monitoring or administration, but it cannot eliminate the need for equipment, sterile processes, prescription management, clinical escalation, quality systems, and a certified accountable provider for most treated patients. Even home modalities retain facility support and oversight.

caveats: The Medicare bundle is directly observed, but the payer mix and commercial contract reset cadence of target firms are not.
No source directly measures the long-run share of AI-enabled gross benefit retained by dialysis operators.
q.change_evidence: Payer contracts, CMS cost reports, quality-payment adjustments, technology costs, staffing expense, and renewal economics from automated and comparable nonautomated centers over five years would materially move q.
q.rationale: A bundled per-treatment Medicare payment gives facilities a near-term incentive to lower administrative and care-delivery cost while maintaining quality, so some implemented benefit can be retained. Annual market-basket and productivity updates, quality incentives, commercial rebidding, labor standards, technology expense, clinical reinvestment, and payer scrutiny limit durable capture; improved outcomes can also benefit payers through avoided complications rather than facility revenue.

rho.bridge: Source population: national health-IT certification rules and CMS dialysis documentation, assessment, care-planning, and quality requirements, not measured AI deployments at target facilities. Quantity transformation: treat structured longitudinal labs, treatment records, certified health IT, and repeated protocols as implementation enablers, then discount exposed tasks for system integration, clinical validation, privacy, workflow redesign, model monitoring, and required professional sign-off. Error direction: may be high for small facilities with legacy systems or low where a multi-site operator can spread integration and governance costs.
caveats: HTI-1 governs certified-health-IT transparency and interoperability rather than facility adoption or realized savings.
No target-band dialysis-center study links AI deployment to verified labor reductions over multiple years.
rho.change_evidence: Audited multi-site deployments reporting hours per treatment, documentation time, claim denials, quality outcomes, alert overrides, adverse events, and sustained staffing changes would materially move rho.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is plausible for scheduling, documentation, coding, denial prevention, treatment-data review, quality reporting, patient outreach, inventory, and decision support. Safety-critical alerts, care-plan changes, and treatment decisions require conservative validation and human accountability, while small operators face meaningful fixed integration costs.

s5.bridge: Source population: Gallup's U.S. employer-business owners across industries, of whom 22% planned to sell or transfer within five years. Quantity transformation: use that as an intention prior, then lower the base for chain-owned facilities, regulated change of ownership, payer and medical-director dependencies, and the gap between intentions and completed qualifying control sales. Error direction: may overstate completed sales or understate dialysis availability if consolidation, reimbursement pressure, succession, and capital needs accelerate exits among remaining independents.
caveats: Gallup intentions are not dialysis-specific and include non-arm's-length transfers.
Facility ownership changes may be internal reorganizations rather than qualifying firm-level control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from CMS change-of-ownership records, state facility licenses, certificate-of-need filings where applicable, and healthcare M&A databases, resolved to ultimate owners, would materially move s5.
s5.rationale: Independent dialysis operators may sell for succession, scale, purchasing, technology, or compliance reasons, but many facilities already belong to larger organizations and are not separate control opportunities. A meaningful but below-benchmark probability is appropriate for the eligible firm rather than the facility.

records.principal_driver: The principal driver is a highly recurring, life-sustaining service with structured longitudinal data and substantial administrative and clinical-support workflows that can be improved by AI.
records.principal_weakness: The principal weakness is that most labor remains tied to physical treatment and accountable clinical care, while regulated bundled reimbursement limits durable margin capture.

rho: Stated adoption intentions do not establish production deployment or labor savings.
rho: Implementation will vary materially by discipline, customer software stack, drawing standard, and tolerance for error.

d5: The industry spans cyclical construction, industrial, infrastructure, and patent-related end markets with different trajectories.
d5: Downstream growth may be served by in-house teams, offshore providers, or software rather than independent U.S. drafting firms.
o: There is no direct forecast of the operator-required share for independent drafting services.
o: Professional licensure, code responsibility, and contractual accountability vary by discipline and jurisdiction.
o: A firm that moves upstream into coordination, model management, or checking may retain demand that a pure production-drafting shop loses.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Business customers are primarily architects, engineers, fabricators, manufacturers, contractors, developers, and asset owners. Revenue is project-based or task-order work billed through hourly rates, fixed fees, unit rates, or recurring capacity arrangements; repetition comes from continuing drawing packages, revisions, standards conversion, detailing, and as-built work rather than subscriptions.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive in-house drafting departments, firms principally providing architecture or engineering design, blueprint reproduction, graphic illustration without drafting responsibility, shells, one-off practices without repeat outsourced service, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms whose principal business is outsourced CAD, BIM, steel-detailing, mechanical, electrical, civil, structural, architectural, as-built, drawing-revision, or closely related drafting work for external customers, with recurring engagements or repeat project work.

caveats: No source directly measures wage-weighted AI task exposure for NAICS 541340.
Occupational evidence includes drafters employed outside independent drafting-services firms.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input is six-digit and rated medium evidence, but its wage and receipts vintages differ and its harmonization factor adds measurement error.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study from a sample of independent drafting firms, split by discipline and coding each task as already automated, technically automatable, or human-dependent, would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The industry converts engineering and architectural specifications into detailed drawings, and the Census product list confirms a broad mix of machine, system, steel, civil, structural, architectural, as-built, checking, and revision work (S1, S10). BLS and O*NET describe computer-centered tasks such as preparing plans, entering dimensions and materials, revising drawings, retrieving information, and checking compliance, which are exposed to generative CAD, rules-based validation, document extraction, and automated redline workflows (S2, S3). The estimate excludes work already automated by CAD and BIM and reserves substantial labor for interpreting incomplete specifications, spatial reasoning, constructability, exception handling, customer coordination, and final checking.

d5.bridge: BLS projections measure employment in adjacent occupations, engineering services, and professional services, not constant-price demand for the current NAICS 541340 service basket. The cited growth rates are converted into a modest five-year demand ratio and triangulated across downstream architecture and engineering activity. Employment can understate service demand when productivity rises and can overstate it if staffing intensity increases, so the range spans mild contraction through moderate growth.
caveats: Employment projections are an imperfect proxy for constant-quality service quantity.
The industry spans cyclical construction, industrial, infrastructure, and patent-related end markets with different trajectories.
Downstream growth may be served by in-house teams, offshore providers, or software rather than independent U.S. drafting firms.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted NAICS 541340 receipts and project-volume data by drafting discipline, ideally paired with construction starts, engineering-services backlog, and customer outsourcing rates, would separate end-market demand from productivity and pricing effects.
d5.rationale: Underlying customers continue to require plans, system layouts, fabrication details, revisions, and as-built records. BLS projects positive growth for architecture and engineering occupations, professional, scientific, and technical services, engineering services, and architects, while architectural and civil drafting grows modestly but the aggregate drafter occupation is roughly flat as software raises productivity (S2, S8, S9). Those signals support slightly higher real demand for drawing outputs despite uneven discipline-level conditions and automation-related employment pressure.

caveats: Neither Census source measures recurring-customer behavior, independence, or transferability.
The frozen LMM firm count is an estimate derived from size classes and a margin bridge rather than a direct count, so the eligible-firm count inherits margin and classification error.
Patent drafting and small one-person practices may have economics unlike CAD and BIM production firms.
e.change_evidence: A complete establishment roster enriched with service mix, customer-repeat rate, owner dependence, EBITDA, and independent-versus-captive status would directly identify the eligible share.
e.rationale: The NAICS definition is already centered on an externally supplied professional service, and the Census survey form shows that classified firms sell recognizable drafting outputs such as detailing, system drawings, as-builts, checking, revisions, and consulting (S1, S10). Most firms of sufficient size should therefore meet the outsourced-service criterion. The excluded share allows for one-off patent or blueprint work, mixed firms whose principal economics sit outside drafting, customer concentration that prevents a transferable repeat business, and owner-dependent practices lacking durable delivery capacity.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

caveats: There is no direct forecast of the operator-required share for independent drafting services.
Professional licensure, code responsibility, and contractual accountability vary by discipline and jurisdiction.
A firm that moves upstream into coordination, model management, or checking may retain demand that a pure production-drafting shop loses.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews with architecture, engineering, fabrication, and construction buyers should quantify current outsourced volume, expected insourcing, software-only production, offshore substitution, required professional sign-off, and willingness to retain an accountable drafting vendor for each deliverable type.
o.rationale: Customers will still need accountable conversion of specifications into coordinated, standards-compliant deliverables, especially where inputs are incomplete, disciplines conflict, or revisions affect constructability. However, BLS explicitly notes that improved CAD and BIM let architects and engineers perform work formerly done by drafters, and architects themselves prepare scaled drawings and direct document production (S2, S9). AI-enabled self-service, software-generated commodity drawings, and offshore supply can therefore remove a material share of quantity from independent drafting operators even when underlying drawing demand persists.

caveats: No public source provides the billing-method mix or observed pass-through for NAICS 541340.
Retention may differ sharply between commodity redraw work and specialized steel, process-piping, or regulated deliverables.
This estimate addresses pricing and benefit sharing only; quantity loss and implementation friction are handled elsewhere.
q.change_evidence: A contract-level sample should classify revenue by hourly, cost-plus, fixed-fee, unit-rate, and retained-capacity terms and compare renewal pricing, gross margin, win rate, and customer sharing before and after workflow automation.
q.rationale: Fixed-fee, unit-priced, and capacity-based projects can initially preserve more of an implemented productivity benefit through higher throughput or avoided hiring. Hourly and cost-plus work pass time savings to customers more directly, while repeat procurement and competitive bidding eventually reprice even fixed-fee work. Because drafting outputs are readily specified and compared and upstream architects or engineers can sometimes perform them internally, the estimate assumes that less than half of the discounted gross benefit is retained in the base case.

rho.bridge: Autodesk surveyed a global, cross-industry Design and Make population rather than U.S. independent drafting firms, and measured current or planned incorporation of machine-learning models rather than the implemented share of exposed drafting labor. The adoption signal is translated downward to allow for workflow integration, training, quality assurance, file interoperability, customer standards, and liability review. The proxy can overstate implementation because respondents include larger and more digitally mature organizations, while its focus on machine-learning models can understate automation delivered through ordinary CAD, BIM, scripting, and rules engines.
caveats: The Autodesk survey is global, vendor-sponsored, and spans AECO, manufacturing, and media rather than NAICS 541340.
Stated adoption intentions do not establish production deployment or labor savings.
Implementation will vary materially by discipline, customer software stack, drawing standard, and tolerance for error.
rho.change_evidence: Firm-level interviews with owners, CAD managers, and production leads should measure deployed tools, percent of drawing hours affected, rework rates, human-review requirements, training cost, and realized staffing avoidance after at least two completed projects.
rho.rationale: Drafting starts from digital source files and is already performed in CAD or BIM, lowering the data-capture barrier. Autodesk reports that only 13 percent of its broad survey population had incorporated machine-learning models, while 67 percent expected to include large language models within five years; early adopters reported operational improvements, but the study also notes skills and implementation struggles (S11). BLS separately says improved BIM, measuring technology, and AI are making architectural design more efficient (S9). The base case therefore assumes meaningful but incomplete conversion of exposed tasks into production workflows.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age and ownership-count data cover U.S. employer businesses broadly, while the ACEC evidence covers engineering and broader design or environmental firms rather than NAICS 541340. Older and concentrated ownership is treated as a pressure toward succession, and observed adjacent-sector transactions as evidence that control transfers can clear. The bridge is biased upward if owner retirement produces closure rather than sale or if larger engineering firms are more marketable than drafting shops; it may be biased downward if small drafting owners have unusually concentrated succession timing.
caveats: Broad owner age is not a measure of intent to sell or of business transferability.
ACEC evidence is adjacent-sector and likely represents larger, more institutionalized firms than independent drafting services.
Announced deal counts can omit small private transactions and do not provide a denominator of eligible firms.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of closed transactions and failed or withdrawn sale processes for U.S. drafting firms, linked to owner age, EBITDA, customer concentration, and internal succession outcomes, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: Census reports that 51 percent of responding owners of employer businesses were at least age 55 and separately finds single-owner firms common, both of which support succession pressure but not a sale probability (S4, S5). In ACEC and FMI's survey of engineering-firm owners, 62 percent reported an ownership plan, but smaller firms were less likely to have one; adjacent design and environmental deal tracking also shows an active transaction market (S6, S7). A qualifying transfer is therefore plausible for a meaningful minority, with a substantial haircut for firms that close, transfer internally without a control sale, or cannot support a buyer.

records.principal_driver: A digitally native, labor-intensive production workflow offers many repeatable drawing, revision, extraction, and checking tasks that can be automated while retaining accountable human review.
records.principal_weakness: The same tools can let architects, engineers, fabricators, and customers self-produce commodity drawings, while a small and poorly observed transferable-firm pool limits consolidation certainty.

caveats: a: The occupation table includes ambulance employers of all sizes and ownership types, not only eligible LMM firms.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not measure current automation.
a: Ground emergency, nonemergency interfacility, specialty transport, and air ambulance workflows differ materially.
a: The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
rho: Neither source measures representative AI adoption or realized labor savings.
rho: CMS ground-ambulance evidence includes government, nonprofit, volunteer, and public-safety organizations unlike the acquisition lens.
rho: Medical-direction rules, dispatch integration, payer audits, and liability constraints vary by state and service type.
e: The NAICS definition does not measure recurring relationships, ownership eligibility, transferability, or normalized EBITDA.
e: CMS data include government, public-safety, nonprofit, volunteer, and institutional organizations outside the lens.
e: The injected count of 188 uses a broad healthcare-facilities margin bridge that may misclassify thin-margin ambulance firms into or out of the LMM band.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed external control deals.
s5: No source measures five-year transfer probability for eligible private ambulance firms.
s5: Licensing, certificates, municipal contracts, payer enrollment, and billing liabilities can block intended sales.
q: No source directly measures retained AI-derived benefits for lower-middle-market ambulance firms.
q: Retention differs across Medicare fee schedule, Medicaid, managed care, commercial insurance, facilities, municipalities, and self-pay.
q: CMS ground-ambulance cost and revenue data combine ownership and operating models and therefore do not establish private-firm profitability.
d5: BLS forecasts occupations across private ambulance, government, hospital, and other settings rather than purchased 621910 service quantity.
d5: Employment can diverge from service quantity as deployment and documentation productivity change.
d5: Local public funding, hospital patterns, population density, disasters, and payer policy create substantial geographic variation.
o: The sources establish current duties and payment definitions, not a measured year-five operator-required share.
o: Low-acuity and scheduled transports may face more substitution than emergency advanced-life-support calls.
o: Air ambulance, specialty care transport, urban ground response, and rural response have different automation constraints.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Providers respond to emergency calls and scheduled medical transports under recurring payer enrollment, facility, health-system, municipal, managed-care, or event relationships; reimbursement commonly combines a service-level base payment, loaded mileage, geographic adjustments, negotiated rates, and patient cost sharing.
lens.excluded_activities: Government-owned or captive ambulance units, nonmedical special-needs transportation, vehicle-only services without medical care, one-off operations without a repeat payer or customer relationship, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent ambulance firms providing recurring or repeat ground or air emergency response, interfacility transport, standby, and medically necessary patient transport with clinical care to external patients, facilities, payers, municipalities, and other customers.

a.bridge: BLS's May 2023 industry table covers all ambulance-services employers rather than a wage-weighted task study of lower-middle-market independent firms. It reports EMTs and paramedics as 70.56% of employment, ambulance drivers and attendants as 4.25%, and office and administrative support as 8.65%, including 3.63% dispatchers. I map dispatch triage assistance, routing, deployment, documentation, coding, billing, supply prediction, shift scheduling, quality review, and portions of clinical decision support to remaining AI exposure while leaving driving, lifting, scene safety, assessment, treatment, monitoring, equipment handling, and patient handoff largely unexposed. Employment shares underweight higher-paid managers and clinicians, while mature CAD, ePCR, navigation, and billing systems may mean some mapped work is already automated.
caveats: The occupation table includes ambulance employers of all sizes and ownership types, not only eligible LMM firms.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not measure current automation.
Ground emergency, nonemergency interfacility, specialty transport, and air ambulance workflows differ materially.
The injected compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study across independent ground and air ambulance firms separating response readiness, dispatch, driving, scene care, transport care, documentation, coding, billing, scheduling, vehicle work, and already automated tasks would materially change this estimate.
a.rationale: The industry's dominant labor is mobile, physical, time-critical, and clinically accountable. AI can reduce documentation and coordination effort and improve deployment, but only a minority of current wage-weighted work is plausibly substitutable or avoidable hiring within five years.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 5% growth in EMT and paramedic employment from 2024 to 2034 across ambulance services, local government, hospitals, and other employers and cites persistent emergencies plus population aging. I translate that occupation-wide ten-year employment forecast into modest five-year constant-price demand growth for the current private outsourced ambulance service basket, allowing downside from alternative transport, treat-in-place programs, public budgets, payer controls, and efficiency gains. The occupation forecast may overstate private ambulance quantity if government or hospital employment grows faster, or understate quantity if productivity offsets staffing shortages.
caveats: BLS forecasts occupations across private ambulance, government, hospital, and other settings rather than purchased 621910 service quantity.
Employment can diverge from service quantity as deployment and documentation productivity change.
Local public funding, hospital patterns, population density, disasters, and payer policy create substantial geographic variation.
d5.change_evidence: Five years of constant-price private ambulance revenue, emergency responses, transports by level and payer, loaded miles, nontransport calls, interfacility volumes, treat-in-place outcomes, response-time compliance, and lost-call data would materially change this value.
d5.rationale: Crashes, acute illness, disasters, overdoses, falls, strokes, cardiac events, and interfacility needs preserve demand, with aging providing a modest tailwind. Payer medical-necessity rules, alternative care pathways, public funding, and staffing constraints keep the outlook from being high growth.

caveats: The NAICS definition does not measure recurring relationships, ownership eligibility, transferability, or normalized EBITDA.
CMS data include government, public-safety, nonprofit, volunteer, and institutional organizations outside the lens.
The injected count of 188 uses a broad healthcare-facilities margin bridge that may misclassify thin-margin ambulance firms into or out of the LMM band.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level 621910 roster matched to ownership type, state ambulance licenses, certificates of need, CMS enrollment, payer and facility contracts, transport mix, normalized EBITDA, municipal dependence, fleet ownership, billing audits, and change-of-control terms would directly establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: Private ambulance firms generally supply repeat services through payer enrollment, facility contracts, dispatch arrangements, and recurring emergency or interfacility demand, fitting the default lens. Ineligibility remains for owner-dependent operators, air-ambulance models outside the LMM band, firms reliant on nontransferable municipal awards or certificates, weakly documented billing, public or captive units, volunteer-dependent operations, and businesses without normalized transferable earnings.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 621910 ($10,182,295,458) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 621910 ($20,846,045,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: The Census definition requires patient transportation with medical care in equipped vehicles operated by trained personnel, and BLS describes scene response, assessment, treatment, safe transport, monitoring, documentation, and handoff, often with one clinician driving while another cares for the patient. I translate those defining duties into the share of year-five quantity still requiring an accountable ambulance operator. Occupational descriptions may overstate operator need for low-acuity calls redirected to alternative transport or treat-in-place, but such avoided calls belong primarily in d5; autonomous emergency transport and unsupervised clinical care are unlikely to replace more than a small share of the remaining service within five years.
caveats: The sources establish current duties and payment definitions, not a measured year-five operator-required share.
Low-acuity and scheduled transports may face more substitution than emergency advanced-life-support calls.
Air ambulance, specialty care transport, urban ground response, and rural response have different automation constraints.
o.change_evidence: Regulatory approvals, dispatch protocols, autonomous-vehicle safety data, treat-in-place and alternative-destination outcomes, staffing models, and service-level studies showing which transports can occur without an accountable ambulance crew would materially change this share.
o.rationale: AI can support triage, navigation, dispatch, documentation, and clinical decisions, but it cannot broadly secure a scene, lift a patient, drive under emergency conditions, establish an airway, administer treatment, monitor deterioration, or transfer responsibility at a hospital. Nearly all surviving ambulance quantity therefore remains operator-required.

caveats: No source directly measures retained AI-derived benefits for lower-middle-market ambulance firms.
Retention differs across Medicare fee schedule, Medicaid, managed care, commercial insurance, facilities, municipalities, and self-pay.
CMS ground-ambulance cost and revenue data combine ownership and operating models and therefore do not establish private-firm profitability.
q.change_evidence: A representative payer and contract dataset showing reimbursement by service level and mileage, payer mix, assignment and pass-through terms, municipal subsidies, rebid cadence, denial and collection rates, technology costs, and gross-margin changes after automation would materially change this estimate.
q.rationale: Medicare pays through a national fee schedule based on service level, mileage, and geographic adjustments and requires participating suppliers to accept allowed charges, so a provider can retain some cost productivity while rates remain fixed but has little pricing discretion. Commercial, Medicare Advantage, Medicaid, facility, and municipal contracts add negotiated rates, rebids, cost sharing, and bad-debt exposure. Competition, public subsidies, annual fee updates, software and integration costs, wage pressure, and payer scrutiny limit durable retention.

rho.bridge: BLS documents the industry's dispatch, administrative, and clinical occupation mix, and CMS's ground-ambulance data collection documents administration, management, dispatch, labor, vehicle, cost, and revenue workflows but does not measure AI adoption. I translate those structured operating workflows into five-year implementation of AI-assisted dispatch, deployment, reporting, coding, billing, scheduling, and quality review, then haircut for high-acuity clinical validation, fragmented public-safety interfaces, connectivity, cybersecurity, payer audit, medical-direction, labor, and liability constraints. The structured data may overstate implementation in small operators with legacy systems, while acute staffing pressure may accelerate tools that reduce paperwork and optimize deployment.
caveats: Neither source measures representative AI adoption or realized labor savings.
CMS ground-ambulance evidence includes government, nonprofit, volunteer, and public-safety organizations unlike the acquisition lens.
Medical-direction rules, dispatch integration, payer audits, and liability constraints vary by state and service type.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year panel of independent ambulance firms reporting workflow-level AI deployment, override rates, response times, documentation time, coding accuracy, denials, clinical incidents, staff adoption, integration cost, and discontinued tools would materially change this value.
rho.rationale: Computer-aided dispatch, electronic patient-care records, fleet telemetry, and claims systems provide a digital base for incremental AI. Implementation will be slower for autonomous decisions affecting triage or care than for documentation and logistics because mistakes carry immediate safety and regulatory consequences.

s5.bridge: Gallup reports five-year sale or transfer intentions for US employer-business owners across industries, not completed external control acquisitions of ambulance firms. I haircut the 22% intention rate for plans that do not close, family or internal transfers, state licensure and certificate requirements, payer enrollment, municipal consent, fleet and liability diligence, billing exposure, and medical-director dependence. The broad survey may overstate qualifying external transfers, although succession pressure and consolidation could raise activity among established private operators.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures intentions rather than completed external control deals.
No source measures five-year transfer probability for eligible private ambulance firms.
Licensing, certificates, municipal contracts, payer enrollment, and billing liabilities can block intended sales.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from state EMS license and certificate records, CMS changes of ownership, PitchBook, Capital IQ, Irving Levin, municipal award notices, and ambulance brokers, matched to the eligible LMM roster, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: A private ambulance platform can transfer its fleet, workforce, payer enrollment, facility relationships, dispatch capacity, and operating licenses, but regulatory approvals, public contracts, clinical liabilities, and local market structure make completed qualifying transactions materially less common than stated owner intentions.

records.principal_driver: AI-assisted dispatch, deployment, documentation, coding, and scheduling can improve scarce-crew productivity across repeat payer and customer relationships.
records.principal_weakness: Physical transport and emergency clinical responsibility dominate the work, while regulated fee schedules and payer contracts limit retained upside.

sources.supports: Defines 621910 as ground or air patient transportation with medical care in lifesaving-equipped vehicles operated by trained personnel and distinguishes nonmedical special-needs transportation.

sources.supports: Describes response, assessment, treatment, transport, monitoring, documentation, supply, and handoff duties; reports substantial ambulance-industry employment and projects 5% occupation growth from 2024 to 2034, citing persistent emergencies and population aging.

sources.supports: Explains that Medicare Part B uses a national ambulance fee schedule across private, municipal, volunteer, independent, and institutional providers and requires providers and suppliers to accept allowed charges, with patient coinsurance and deductible.

records.title: Ambulance Services

caveats: a: O*NET importance scores describe an occupation, not wage shares or time shares inside NAICS 561622.
a: The home-services AI survey is vendor-sponsored and is concentrated in HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and general contracting rather than locksmithing.
a: The frozen compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and an IPS harmonization factor.
a: Electronic-access work mixes physical installation, software configuration, cybersecurity, and customer training in proportions not measured here.
rho: The survey is an online sample of active platform customers and industry contacts and is explicitly directional.
rho: Reported use of AI is not the share of exposed work implemented or a verified labor saving.
rho: Small owner-operated locksmith shops may lack standardized data and workflows needed for reliable automation.
e: The frozen count of 10 LMM-band firms is margin-bridged using a broad services margin and is not a verified target list.
e: The NAICS code permits device and safe sales when combined with service, so product intensity can vary materially.
e: Industry commentary indicates that many firms remain break-fix businesses with limited recurring monthly revenue.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and its transfer category includes outcomes that do not qualify as outside control sales.
s5: One consolidator announcement establishes buyer activity but not an industry transaction rate.
s5: Owner-held technical knowledge, customer trust, and licenses can make an intended sale nontransferable.
q: The pricing survey is non-scientific and does not report revenue-weighted billing shares.
q: Electronic-access work often uses hourly and trip fees, which can pass labor savings to customers through fewer billable hours.
q: Recurring revenue is described as new and limited for many locksmiths rather than an established industry-wide model.
d5: Occupational employment is not service quantity and includes locksmiths working outside NAICS 561622.
d5: The electronic-access survey is a subscriber sample and does not measure constant-price industry demand.
d5: Direct-to-consumer smart locks can create installation and support work but also displace traditional service calls.
o: Operator need differs sharply between consumer smart locks, automotive work, commercial master-key systems, electronic access, and safes.
o: Electronic access shifts work toward software and integration without necessarily keeping it inside NAICS 561622.
o: O*NET describes today's occupation and cannot resolve five-year self-service adoption.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers buy emergency and scheduled service calls, labor, travel, parts and hardware, project installation, and sometimes maintenance, software, monitoring, or access-control subscriptions. Pricing commonly combines a service or trip fee with hourly, per-unit, materials, or quoted-project charges; recurring contracts are emerging but are not yet the dominant model.
lens.excluded_activities: Pure lock or safe retail and wholesale without installation or maintenance, stand-alone key duplication, security-alarm monitoring businesses classified elsewhere, captive facilities staff, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market locksmith firms supplying repeat or recurring installation, repair, rekeying, emergency opening, master-key, safe, door-hardware, and electronic-access services to external residential, commercial, institutional, and automotive customers, with associated locking-device sales where service remains integral.

a.bridge: O*NET describes the locksmith occupation rather than the wage-weighted occupation mix of private NAICS 561622 firms, while Housecall Pro surveys a broader home-services population. I treat customer intake, scheduling, dispatch, quoting, recordkeeping, invoicing, follow-up, business reporting, technical information retrieval, and parts of access-system configuration as AI-exposed, then discount the large share of wages tied to driving, drilling, cutting, installation, manipulation, diagnosis at the opening, and security-sensitive judgment. This can overstate exposure because trade-platform users and office-heavy firms are more digital, or understate it by omitting managers, dispatchers, and sales staff from the occupational task profile.
caveats: O*NET importance scores describe an occupation, not wage shares or time shares inside NAICS 561622.
The home-services AI survey is vendor-sponsored and is concentrated in HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and general contracting rather than locksmithing.
The frozen compensation ratio combines 2024 wages with 2022 receipts and an IPS harmonization factor.
Electronic-access work mixes physical installation, software configuration, cybersecurity, and customer training in proportions not measured here.
a.change_evidence: Payroll-weighted time studies from multi-technician U.S. locksmith firms showing hours by field, dispatch, sales, and administration role; job-level handle times; current field-service software use; and pre-AI versus AI-assisted time for calls, quotes, documentation, and access-control configuration.
a.rationale: The core occupation is dominated by physical work on locks, safes, vehicles, and door hardware. AI can nevertheless absorb meaningful work around each job and assist with documentation, estimates, routing, customer communication, knowledge retrieval, and electronic-access setup, leaving a bounded but minority current labor opportunity.

d5.bridge: BLS projects national locksmith-occupation employment rather than constant-price U.S. 561622 service quantity, while Locksmith Ledger surveys subscriber expectations for electronic access control. I translate the occupational decline into pressure on traditional mechanical and routine work, offset by growth in connected locks, retrofits, commercial access projects, and associated service. Employment can fall faster than demand through productivity, and the trade survey can overstate growth because respondents invested in electronic access are more engaged.
caveats: Occupational employment is not service quantity and includes locksmiths working outside NAICS 561622.
The electronic-access survey is a subscriber sample and does not measure constant-price industry demand.
Direct-to-consumer smart locks can create installation and support work but also displace traditional service calls.
d5.change_evidence: A constant-price U.S. revenue series for NAICS 561622 split among emergency, mechanical, automotive, safe, commercial door-hardware, and electronic-access work, plus service-call volumes, installed access points, project backlog, churn, and price deflators through 2031.
d5.rationale: BLS projects occupational contraction, consistent with automation, simpler consumer products, and substitution away from some traditional work. Against that, the industry survey reports electronic access as a major or complementary driver for most respondents and rising expected implementation. The combined current service basket is therefore likely to be roughly stable, with material downside and upside.

caveats: The frozen count of 10 LMM-band firms is margin-bridged using a broad services margin and is not a verified target list.
The NAICS code permits device and safe sales when combined with service, so product intensity can vary materially.
Industry commentary indicates that many firms remain break-fix businesses with limited recurring monthly revenue.
e.change_evidence: Firm-level diligence on the supplied LMM candidates using primary NAICS verification, service-versus-product revenue, repeat-customer share, contract and software revenue, technician count, customer concentration, owner dependence, licensing, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The official industry definition is service-centered and usually fits the repeat outsourced-service lens, including mechanical and electronic installation and repair. The discount covers firms dominated by one-time retail or project sales, owner-personal goodwill, subcontractor networks, weak records, nonassignable customer relationships, unsuitable normalized economics, or adjacent security activities outside the code.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561622 ($993,633,183) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561622 ($2,597,692,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: The official industry definition, O*NET tasks, and locksmith trade sources describe current work and technology adoption rather than the share of year-five quantity requiring an accountable locksmith operator. I classify routine intake, simple guidance, credential administration, some diagnostics, and standardized consumer replacement as self-serviceable or software-supplied, while retaining an operator for physical opening, drilling, cutting, installation, repair, safe work, site assessment, code and life-safety judgment, secure handoff, and electronic-access integration. This may overstate operator need if smart hardware becomes reliably self-installed, or understate it if connected systems increase integration, cybersecurity, and service obligations.
caveats: Operator need differs sharply between consumer smart locks, automotive work, commercial master-key systems, electronic access, and safes.
Electronic access shifts work toward software and integration without necessarily keeping it inside NAICS 561622.
O*NET describes today's occupation and cannot resolve five-year self-service adoption.
o.change_evidence: Service-platform data on remote versus onsite resolution, smart-lock self-install success, warranty and callback rates, autonomous credential-management adoption, insurer and code requirements, customer acceptance of software-only support, and technician hours per installed opening.
o.rationale: The industry's defining work is performed on physical openings, vehicles, safes, and access systems at customer sites. Smart products can remove some low-complexity calls and move administration into software, yet locks remain cyber-physical security devices whose installation, repair, override, and accountable access decisions usually require a trusted local professional.

q.bridge: Locksmith Ledger surveys reader pricing practices rather than the discounted share of AI benefits retained by U.S. LMM locksmith operators. I map fixed service calls, trip charges, per-unit charges, quoted projects, hardware margin, and emerging recurring contracts to retention of reduced dispatch and administrative cost, while treating hourly labor, transparent bids, customer comparison, software fees, and competitive repricing as pass-through channels. The reader sample may overrepresent professionalized firms and pricing discipline, so it can overstate retention for fragmented local operators.
caveats: The pricing survey is non-scientific and does not report revenue-weighted billing shares.
Electronic-access work often uses hourly and trip fees, which can pass labor savings to customers through fewer billable hours.
Recurring revenue is described as new and limited for many locksmiths rather than an established industry-wide model.
q.change_evidence: Contracts, estimates, invoices, renewal records, and job-cost data from U.S. locksmith firms showing revenue by service-call, hourly, fixed-unit, project, hardware, and subscription model, together with price changes, win rates, gross margins, and AI software expense after deployment.
q.rationale: Much of the exposed work is nonbillable intake, routing, quoting, records, and follow-up, so reducing it can raise capacity without directly cutting a customer invoice. Fixed call and unit charges support capture, but hourly billing, project competition, hardware costs, AI and access-control license fees, and faster customer response by competitors share the benefit over five years.

rho.bridge: Housecall Pro measures current AI use and reported use cases among 248 U.S. home-service professionals, not implementation of exposed tasks in NAICS 561622. I translate the reported adoption of customer communication, estimating, reporting, and built-in trade software into five-year production use for the exposed locksmith workflow, then discount for fragmented small shops, limited process documentation, security and privacy concerns around key records, inaccurate quotes, variable emergency calls, integration costs, and owner resistance. The online vendor-linked sample likely overstates adoption, while rapid embedding in field-service platforms could make the five-year estimate conservative.
caveats: The survey is an online sample of active platform customers and industry contacts and is explicitly directional.
Reported use of AI is not the share of exposed work implemented or a verified labor saving.
Small owner-operated locksmith shops may lack standardized data and workflows needed for reliable automation.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of U.S. locksmith firms and field-service vendors measuring AI-feature activation, weekly active use, automated call and quote share, override rates, booking errors, credential-data controls, labor hours per job, and realized office-headcount avoidance.
rho.rationale: Nearly half of the broader trade sample already reported active AI use, and current use cases align closely with locksmith back-office work. Implementation should expand through existing scheduling, phone, estimating, and accounting tools, but unattended decisions involving access rights, security credentials, or job pricing will require controls and human review.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans among U.S. employer-business owners across industries, while Cobalt documents one active U.S. security-services acquisition program and a locksmith transaction. I start below Gallup's employer intention rate to remove gifts, internal transfers, unrealized plans, and businesses too dependent on the owner to transfer, then allow upside for a trade facing succession concerns and for demonstrated strategic buyer activity. Gallup may overstate completed qualifying sales, while sparse disclosed transactions may understate private add-on activity.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and its transfer category includes outcomes that do not qualify as outside control sales.
One consolidator announcement establishes buyer activity but not an industry transaction rate.
Owner-held technical knowledge, customer trust, and licenses can make an intended sale nontransferable.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of U.S. locksmith and physical-security transaction data from business brokers, Axial, PitchBook, BizBuySell, and strategic acquirers, linked to a verified eligible-firm denominator and coded for control status, seller age, revenue model, technician retention, and closed versus withdrawn listings.
s5.rationale: Employer-business owners show meaningful transfer intent, and an active consolidator has acquired a U.S. lock-and-door company as part of a broader acquisition program. Locksmith firms can transfer technicians, routes, commercial accounts, equipment, and access-control relationships, but small target count, owner skill, licensing, reputation, and limited contracts reduce the probability of a qualifying close.

records.principal_driver: AI can remove avoidable coordination and office work while electronic access expands higher-value installation and service needs that still require a trusted local operator.
records.principal_weakness: Only 10 LMM firms are modeled, and the core trade is physically intensive, owner-dependent, and often break-fix rather than contractually recurring.

sources.supports: The official definition covers sales of mechanical or electronic locks, safes, and vaults with service, plus installation, repair, rebuilding, and adjustment of those products.
sources.title: 2022 North American Industry Classification System Manual

sources.supports: The 2026 occupation profile documents physical key cutting, lock manipulation, drilling, door-hardware and access-system installation, safe repair, vehicle opening, and lock-and-key recordkeeping tasks.

sources.supports: BLS projects national locksmith and safe repairer employment from 18,800 in 2024 to 17,200 in 2034, a decline of 8.3 percent, while projecting 1,700 annual openings.

sources.supports: A spring 2026 survey of 248 U.S. home-service professionals reports 48 percent active AI use, leading uses in customer communication, estimates, and reporting, and 84 percent of users expecting to increase use.

sources.supports: The trade survey describes combinations of service, trip, hourly, fixed-service, per-unit, project, and materials pricing and reports average regular-hour service-call and labor charges.

sources.supports: Gallup reports that 22 percent of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, versus 9 percent of nonemployers.

sources.supports: The 2025 trade survey reports that 85 percent of locksmith respondents saw electronic access control as a major driver or complement and 40 percent expected to implement more in the following year.

sources.supports: Industry participants describe most locksmiths as starting with break-fix service, recurring revenue emerging through projects and access control, one operator at 14 percent recurring revenue, contract scarcity, and installation expertise as essential.

records.title: Locksmiths

caveats: a: The occupation source is five-digit and heavily reflects guard and patrol services rather than armored-car services alone.
a: Employment shares are not wage shares, and no public source measures current not-yet-automated task time in six-digit 561613.
a: Large-operator technology disclosures may not represent the installed systems or workflows of LMM firms.
a: The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a wage and receipts vintage mismatch and a harmonization adjustment.
rho: No source measures five-year AI implementation for U.S. LMM armored-car firms.
rho: Firm-level AI use may be confined to office functions and does not establish labor realization.
rho: Brink's is a large global operator with more capital, data, and technology infrastructure than the screened firms.
rho: Security, insurance, customer controls, labor agreements, and chain-of-custody requirements can delay deployment.
e: NAICS membership does not reveal contract recurrence, captive status, customer concentration, owner dependence, or normalized EBITDA.
e: Some valuable-item transport is episodic, and some firms may combine armored work with adjacent guarding, courier, or systems activities.
e: The frozen LMM firm count is margin-bridged rather than a directly observed roster.
s5: Broad owner age does not measure transfer intent or timing in armored-car services.
s5: The principal U.S. transaction is large and old relative to the LMM lens.
s5: Private small-company transactions and failed sale processes are not observed with a common denominator.
s5: The small frozen firm universe makes a probability estimate especially sensitive to a few firms.
q: No source directly measures the discounted share of implemented benefit retained by U.S. LMM armored-car firms.
q: Contract structure, escalation rights, route density, and customer bargaining power vary materially.
q: A large global operator's managed-services mix may not represent smaller regional carriers.
q: Cash-volume loss is addressed in d5 and operator displacement in o, not in this retention estimate.
d5: BLS publishes no six-digit constant-quality output forecast for armored-car services.
d5: The five-digit employment projection is dominated by guard and patrol services and is not a direct demand quantity.
d5: Consumer payment count is not the same as commercial cash value, route stops, ATM demand, or valuables transport.
d5: Managed-services growth at a global leader may reflect share gains and acquisitions rather than market growth.
o: No source directly forecasts the operator-required share for six-digit 561613.
o: Operator need differs between high-density cash routes, ATM programs, valuables transport, and occasional pickups.
o: Integrated banks, retailers, ATM platforms, or national carriers may internalize or absorb work currently served by LMM firms.
o: This primitive excludes demand-volume decline, which is handled in d5.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Customers include financial institutions, retailers, government entities, jewelers, and other businesses that pay recurring service charges or per-action fees for scheduled pickup, secure transport, ATM work, cash handling, vaulting, reconciliation, and related managed services. Economics depend on route density, labor utilization, fleet and facility costs, insurance, loss control, and contract pricing.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive bank or retailer cash operations, ordinary courier or trucking businesses without protected valuables custody, standalone guard and patrol companies, standalone cash processors without armored transport, security-system businesses, one-off operators without repeat outsourced service, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms providing repeat outsourced armored transportation of cash, receipts, precious items, or other valuables, including ATM replenishment, cash processing bundled with transport, vault services, deposit verification, computer-safe service, coin handling, and mobile security escort work.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for five-digit NAICS 561610 rather than six-digit 561613, and its mix is dominated by guard and patrol services. The bridge maps the documented protective, dispatch, clerical, supervisory, and systems work to the armored-car workflow, assigning exposure to route planning, cash forecasting, dispatch support, reconciliation, exception review, report writing, customer service, billing, and administration while preserving the physical custody, driving, loading, observation, and emergency-response share. The broader occupation mix likely overstates generic stationary guarding and understates armored transport, cash-room, and route work; use of a large operator's technology mix may overstate digitization already achieved by smaller firms.
caveats: The occupation source is five-digit and heavily reflects guard and patrol services rather than armored-car services alone.
Employment shares are not wage shares, and no public source measures current not-yet-automated task time in six-digit 561613.
Large-operator technology disclosures may not represent the installed systems or workflows of LMM firms.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts input has a wage and receipts vintage mismatch and a harmonization adjustment.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time study at independent U.S. armored-car operators should separate driving, armed custody, loading, cash-room processing, ATM work, dispatch, route design, reconciliation, customer service, sales, compliance, and administration, and should identify which tasks are already automated.
a.rationale: The five-digit occupation profile reports protective-service occupations at 92.87 percent of employment and office support at 3.17 percent, while computer occupations are only 0.18 percent (S2). BLS describes guard work as patrol, alarm monitoring, emergency response, access control, security checks, observation, and report writing; the judgment and physical-presence duties are hard to substitute, while reports and monitoring support are more exposed (S3). Census product definitions also show that the service includes ATM settlement, balancing, deposit verification, cash vaulting, computer safes, coin handling, and bundled cash management, creating a meaningful but minority digital and administrative task layer (S1). The range excludes automation already embedded in routing, counting, ATM, and safe systems.

d5.bridge: BLS projects employment for five-digit investigation, guard, and armored-car services rather than constant-quality demand for six-digit armored-car output, and Federal Reserve consumer-payment shares capture only one source of cash movement. The bridge starts from modest broader-industry employment growth, then reduces the base for continued substitution from cash toward electronic payments while allowing ATM outsourcing, smart-safe services, valuables logistics, nominal economic activity, and a recent cash-use floor to stabilize service demand. The broader BLS series likely overstates armored-car growth if guard demand is stronger; consumer cash share may overstate decline in serviced cash value because payment count, cash recycling, commercial deposits, ATMs, and valuables follow different patterns.
caveats: BLS publishes no six-digit constant-quality output forecast for armored-car services.
The five-digit employment projection is dominated by guard and patrol services and is not a direct demand quantity.
Consumer payment count is not the same as commercial cash value, route stops, ATM demand, or valuables transport.
Managed-services growth at a global leader may reflect share gains and acquisitions rather than market growth.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted U.S. 561613 receipts and physical activity data should track route stops, cash value and pieces handled, ATM replenishments, smart-safe deposits, vault transactions, valuables shipments, customer locations, contract wins, and cash-payment trends by end market.
d5.rationale: BLS projects employment in NAICS 561610 to increase 4.8 percent over the decade, indicating modest broader demand rather than rapid contraction (S6). The Federal Reserve reports cash at 16 percent of participant payments and says its use has fallen substantially since 2016 but appears to have reached a floor in recent years (S7). Brink's simultaneously reports a continuing shift toward managed ATM and digital retail solutions, which can support outsourced cash handling even as traditional route demand faces pressure (S5). The base therefore assumes a moderate five-year decline in the current constant-quality service basket, with a wide range for payment substitution and outsourcing.

caveats: NAICS membership does not reveal contract recurrence, captive status, customer concentration, owner dependence, or normalized EBITDA.
Some valuable-item transport is episodic, and some firms may combine armored work with adjacent guarding, courier, or systems activities.
The frozen LMM firm count is margin-bridged rather than a directly observed roster.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit firm roster should classify independent ownership, revenue by recurring armored transport and bundled cash service, customer contract recurrence, route density, captive status, ordinary courier exposure, customer concentration, and normalized EBITDA.
e.rationale: The industry products inherently involve external pickup, protected transport, ATM service, cash handling, vaulting, and escort work, and these services normally repeat for banks, retailers, and other organizations (S1). Most independent firms in the frozen LMM band should therefore fit the outsourced-service lens. Exclusions remain for captive operations, ordinary couriers, one-off valuable-item moves, businesses dominated by standalone guarding or systems work, thin owner-operated practices, and firms without transferable route density or controls.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 561613 ($1,619,895,217) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 561613 ($3,486,001,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1565 (margins.json[56]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Business & Consumer Services EBITDA/Sales 15.65%); revenue/employee $132,623 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 561613); class <5: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $412,931 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $64,624 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 10 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,302,200 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $203,794 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 16 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,288,375 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $201,631 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $8,083,579 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,265,080 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,619,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,974,874 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $522,562,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $81,781,005 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 31

caveats: No source directly forecasts the operator-required share for six-digit 561613.
Operator need differs between high-density cash routes, ATM programs, valuables transport, and occasional pickups.
Integrated banks, retailers, ATM platforms, or national carriers may internalize or absorb work currently served by LMM firms.
This primitive excludes demand-volume decline, which is handled in d5.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with bank cash managers, large retailers, ATM operators, insurers, regulators, and independent carriers should quantify which current stops and cash-handling services will be eliminated, self-served, centralized, or still require an independent armored operator at year five.
o.rationale: For demand that remains at year five, physical pickup, protected custody, loading, transport, ATM access, vault handling, and incident accountability still require an insured and controlled operator. Census product definitions center the service on armored vehicles, cash and valuables custody, ATM handling, and security escorts (S1). Software, smart safes, better cash forecasting, remote ATM monitoring, customer cash recycling, and fewer route stops can eliminate some engagements or move work in-house, but they do not fully replace the accountable carrier for the residual physical movement. The estimate therefore preserves most remaining quantity while allowing standardized low-frequency service to be self-served or absorbed by banks, retailers, or larger integrated platforms.

caveats: No source directly measures the discounted share of implemented benefit retained by U.S. LMM armored-car firms.
Contract structure, escalation rights, route density, and customer bargaining power vary materially.
A large global operator's managed-services mix may not represent smaller regional carriers.
Cash-volume loss is addressed in d5 and operator displacement in o, not in this retention estimate.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract sample should measure revenue and gross profit by fixed route fee, per-stop, per-item, ATM, vault, cash-processing, equipment, and subscription terms, then compare productivity savings, renewal pricing, route frequency, churn, win rates, and customer concessions after automation.
q.rationale: Armored-car economics combine recurring routes and per-action service with route density, fleet, facility, insurance, and labor costs. Faster forecasting, dispatch, reconciliation, and office work can initially reduce internal cost without changing customer service units, favoring retention. At renewal, banks and retailers can seek lower prices or fewer stops, and competing carriers can adopt similar tools. Technology-enabled smart-safe and ATM offerings may deepen integration and preserve value, but they can also make service use more transparent and reduce physical visits. Brink's reports that managed ATM and digital retail services now form a material share of its business, but this does not measure retained automation benefit at LMM firms (S5).

rho.bridge: Census measures AI use across U.S. firms and business functions rather than implementation inside armored-car firms, while Brink's describes a large global operator already shifting toward managed, technology-enabled services. The bridge treats mature digital dispatch, forecasting, reconciliation, and customer-data workflows as favorable implementation infrastructure, then applies a haircut for LMM capital limits, legacy systems, cybersecurity, labor practices, regulated custody, and the need to validate any change that affects route safety or cash control. Broad firm data may understate readiness at sophisticated operators but overstate implementation at small local fleets.
caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation for U.S. LMM armored-car firms.
Firm-level AI use may be confined to office functions and does not establish labor realization.
Brink's is a large global operator with more capital, data, and technology infrastructure than the screened firms.
Security, insurance, customer controls, labor agreements, and chain-of-custody requirements can delay deployment.
rho.change_evidence: A matched operator study should report deployed AI tools, route and dispatch adoption, forecast accuracy, cash-room exception rates, administrative hours, training and integration cost, audit findings, loss incidents, customer restrictions, and realized hiring avoidance before and after deployment.
rho.rationale: Census found AI in a business function at 18 percent of firms and 32 percent on an employment-weighted basis, but 66 percent of users relied on augmentation only and AI-related employment decreases occurred at 2 percent of firms (S4). Brink's states that it is moving from traditional cash logistics toward managed services and reports technology-linked ATM and digital retail offerings as 28 percent of 2025 revenue, showing that operational data and software can be embedded in this service model (S5). Implementation should be strongest in planning, forecasting, customer administration, document work, reconciliation triage, and support, but slower where outputs affect physical routing, custody, safety, audit trails, or loss responsibility.

s5.bridge: Census owner-age data covers responding owners of U.S. employer businesses across industries, while the Brink's transaction evidence concerns one large U.S. cash-management acquisition and several other transactions rather than LMM 561613 firms. Older ownership is translated into succession pressure and the observed transactions into evidence that route networks and customer relationships can transfer. The broad age data may overstate sale probability because retirement can lead to family succession or closure; large strategic acquisitions may overstate marketability and valuation of small operators, while private local deals are often undisclosed.
caveats: Broad owner age does not measure transfer intent or timing in armored-car services.
The principal U.S. transaction is large and old relative to the LMM lens.
Private small-company transactions and failed sale processes are not observed with a common denominator.
The small frozen firm universe makes a probability estimate especially sensitive to a few firms.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year U.S. database of armored-car control sales, failed processes, closures, internal successions, and recapitalizations should be linked to eligible-firm counts, owner age, route density, fleet condition, customer concentration, loss history, licenses, and EBITDA.
s5.rationale: Census reports that 51 percent of responding owners of employer businesses were at least age 55, establishing broad succession pressure without measuring sale intent (S8). Brink's acquired Dunbar's U.S. cash-management operation, including its branch network, armored vehicles, and small-to-medium customer focus, and separately disclosed acquisitions of U.S. secured trucking and foreign cash-in-transit businesses (S9). The range recognizes demonstrated strategic demand and route-density synergies but allows for a small eligible pool, licensing and insurance constraints, family succession, customer concentration, and closures.

records.principal_driver: Dense recurring routes and accountable physical custody allow AI-enabled planning and administration to improve productivity without removing the core outsourced service.
records.principal_weakness: Persistent migration from cash to electronic payments can reduce stops and route density faster than digital workflow savings can improve economics.

sources.supports: Defines 561613 armored-car products as protected cash and valuables transport, ATM services, bundled cash management, deposit verification, vaulting, computer-safe service, coin handling, and mobile security escort work.

sources.supports: Describes patrol, monitoring, emergency response, deterrence, access control, security checks, observation, report writing, licensing, armed-guard requirements, and remote-monitoring pressure.

sources.supports: Provides nationally representative firm AI adoption, functional breadth, augmentation, and reported employment-effect evidence.
sources.title: The Microstructure of AI Diffusion: Evidence from Firms, Business Functions, and Worker Tasks

sources.supports: Describes the transition from traditional cash logistics toward managed services and reports the scale and growth of ATM managed services and digital retail solutions alongside cash and valuables management.
sources.title: The Brink's Company 2025 Annual Report

sources.supports: Projects five-digit investigation, guard, and armored-car services employment to increase 4.8 percent over the decade.
sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024 to 2034

sources.supports: Reports declining consumer cash use, a 16 percent payment share in the 2024 Diary, and evidence that cash use had reached a recent floor.

sources.supports: Reports that 51 percent of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey.

sources.supports: Documents the acquisition of Dunbar's U.S. cash-management network and other secured-transport and cash-in-transit businesses, demonstrating transferability and strategic route-network value.
