caveats: a: OEWS is for NAICS 624200 and includes housing and emergency-relief establishments outside 624210.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares, and no cited source measures current AI exposure or existing automation.
rho: No source measures five-year AI implementation in this six-digit industry.
rho: A national network's technology capacity is unlikely to represent small local operators.
e: No source measures the share of 624210 establishments meeting the fixed lens.
e: The frozen firm-count input is margin-bridged from an 8% social-assistance placeholder; donated food and nonprofit accounting can make receipts-to-EBITDA inference materially misleading.
e: IRS restrictions apply to section 501(c)(3) organizations, not every establishment in the NAICS code.
s5: There is no observed control-transfer rate for eligible 624210 firms.
s5: The estimate depends strongly on the unresolved legal-form mix and on whether asset transfers preserve the operating platform.
q: The Feeding America financial statements describe one large nonprofit network, not the eligible lower-middle-market subset.
q: Contract economics likely vary sharply between reimbursed programs, fixed grants, and fixed-fee meal delivery.
d5: Food insecurity prevalence is need, not paid or funded service demand.
d5: The 2024 measure is one national observation and cannot identify local funding or capacity constraints.
o: The source establishes current activities, not a five-year substitution rate.
o: Operator requirement may remain high while the specific local operator type changes through network centralization or retailer partnerships.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recipients often pay little or nothing; operating resources commonly come from contributions, donated food, grants, member-network support, public programs, or service contracts. The narrower investable subset may have recurring fixed-fee or reimbursement arrangements with governments, health plans, institutions, or sponsors.
lens.excluded_activities: Volunteer-only programs, entities without transferable operating control, captive programs, one-off disaster responses, food retail, institutional cafeterias, and social-assistance activities not primarily centered on community food collection, preparation, distribution, or delivery.
lens.included_activities: U.S. operators classified in community food services that repeatedly collect, prepare, distribute, or deliver food to external recipients and that can plausibly support lower-middle-market normalized operating earnings.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for the broader four-digit NAICS 624200, not wage-weighted AI task exposure in 624210. I map office, financial, fundraising, management, eligibility, recordkeeping, dispatch, and planning work to partial AI exposure while assigning little exposure to cooking, driving, material handling, cleaning, and direct human service. The broader group includes housing and emergency-relief work, so it may overstate non-food casework and understate food logistics in 624210; both errors make the direction uncertain.
caveats: OEWS is for NAICS 624200 and includes housing and emergency-relief establishments outside 624210.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares, and no cited source measures current AI exposure or existing automation.
a.change_evidence: Obtain payroll-weighted occupation files and a task-time study from at least 20 food banks, meal-delivery programs, and community kitchens, separating already-automated tasks from remaining intake, grant, donor, routing, procurement, and reporting work.
a.rationale: The broader industry has 11.64% of employment in office and administrative support, 8.54% in business and financial operations, and 13.26% in management, alongside smaller communications and planning roles. These functions contain draftable, classifiable, schedulable, and reconcilable tasks. However, the service itself includes physical food preparation, delivery, driving, and inventory movement, constraining substitution.

d5.bridge: USDA measures household food insecurity, not constant-price demand for community food-service output. I use the large and recently persistent food-insecure population as a demand-need indicator, then temper it because funding, donated supply, public benefits, demographics, and service capacity mediate the conversion from need to delivered service. This proxy can overstate funded demand when resources contract and understate it when outreach or capacity expands.
caveats: Food insecurity prevalence is need, not paid or funded service demand.
The 2024 measure is one national observation and cannot identify local funding or capacity constraints.
d5.change_evidence: Obtain five-year same-program meal and food-pound volumes, waitlists, unique-client counts, and constant-quality contract demand from a stratified panel of food banks, community kitchens, and meal-delivery operators, alongside committed funding through year five.
d5.rationale: USDA reports that 13.7% of U.S. households were food insecure in 2024, statistically similar to 2023, indicating a large persistent need base. A roughly stable to modestly growing five-year service basket is plausible, with a wide range for economic conditions, public policy, donations, and food costs.

caveats: No source measures the share of 624210 establishments meeting the fixed lens.
The frozen firm-count input is margin-bridged from an 8% social-assistance placeholder; donated food and nonprofit accounting can make receipts-to-EBITDA inference materially misleading.
IRS restrictions apply to section 501(c)(3) organizations, not every establishment in the NAICS code.
e.change_evidence: Match the 229 modeled establishments to IRS exempt-organization records, state entity filings, Form 990s, and private-company databases; then verify revenue model, ownership, normalized EBITDA, recurring external customers, and control-transferability firm by firm.
e.rationale: The industry definition centers on food banks, soup kitchens, and meal-delivery programs for people in need, while the observed network economics rely heavily on contributions and donated goods. A material share of establishments therefore may be charitable or otherwise lack transferable equity and normalized EBITDA. The eligible subset is likely limited to scaled, recurring contracted operators with genuine transferable control.

o.bridge: The Census industry definition identifies inherently physical collection, preparation, distribution, and delivery activities, rather than measuring the future operator-required share. I infer that software can alter access and coordination but cannot itself acquire, handle, prepare, and deliver food. This may overstate operator persistence if cash benefits, grocery credits, direct-to-recipient logistics, or retailer fulfillment displace community operators.
caveats: The source establishes current activities, not a five-year substitution rate.
Operator requirement may remain high while the specific local operator type changes through network centralization or retailer partnerships.
o.change_evidence: Measure substitution in markets adopting electronic grocery benefits, direct retailer delivery, centralized procurement, and self-service intake by comparing community-operator volumes and contract awards before and after adoption.
o.rationale: Most of the service basket requires accountable custody of food, food-safety processes, facilities, local distribution, delivery, or in-person handoff. Digital intake and benefits tools may reduce some intermediary work, but they do not eliminate the core physical operator.

caveats: The Feeding America financial statements describe one large nonprofit network, not the eligible lower-middle-market subset.
Contract economics likely vary sharply between reimbursed programs, fixed grants, and fixed-fee meal delivery.
q.change_evidence: Review a representative sample of government, health-plan, institution, and sponsor contracts plus grant agreements, recording pricing basis, allowable-cost rules, rebid cadence, gain-sharing, restricted-fund terms, and actual treatment of operating underspends.
q.rationale: Savings under fixed grants, donations, or fixed-fee contracts can fund more service or strengthen operating reserves, but they do not automatically become distributable owner economics. Competitive rebids, reimbursement rules, donor restrictions, and mission commitments can share benefits with funders and recipients. Retention is better where a transferable operator has multi-year fixed pricing and measurable service-level obligations.

caveats: No source measures five-year AI implementation in this six-digit industry.
A national network's technology capacity is unlikely to represent small local operators.
rho.change_evidence: Collect pre/post labor-hour and error-rate results from production AI deployments at 15 community food operators, including implementation cost, integration time, staff adoption, privacy incidents, and the share of suggested actions requiring human correction.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation should be feasible in donor communications, grant drafting, intake triage, scheduling, reporting, procurement support, and route planning, but small-operator budgets, fragmented systems, sensitive client data, food-safety accountability, volunteer workflows, and the need for human review limit realized deployment.

caveats: There is no observed control-transfer rate for eligible 624210 firms.
The estimate depends strongly on the unresolved legal-form mix and on whether asset transfers preserve the operating platform.
s5.change_evidence: Build a five-year transaction census from PitchBook, Capital IQ, state charity filings, Form 990 Schedule R, and press releases, then classify each event as equity control sale, asset sale, nonprofit affiliation, internal reorganization, or closure.
s5.rationale: Qualifying control transfers should be rare because many archetypal operators are charitable organizations without private shareholders, and nonprofit affiliations or board changes do not necessarily create a transferable control transaction. The small eligible for-profit subset can still experience owner succession or strategic sale.

records.principal_driver: Persistent food need supports recurring physical service demand that software alone cannot fulfill.
records.principal_weakness: Much of the category may be nonprofit, donation-funded, and outside a conventional transferable-control investment model.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue commonly comes from government grants, cooperative agreements, cost-reimbursement programs, competitively or urgently awarded service contracts, nonprofit subcontracts, donations, and restricted program funding. Delivery spans preparedness and grant administration, emergency deployment, shelter and commodity operations, case management, resettlement, and long-tail recovery support.
lens.excluded_activities: Government emergency-management departments; captive internal response units; shells and non-control vehicles; spontaneous volunteer groups without an operating enterprise; direct medical providers, transportation carriers, restoration contractors, and logistics vendors classified outside NAICS 624230; and organizations outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market firms supplying recurring or repeat outsourced disaster relief, emergency food or shelter, material-aid logistics, survivor counseling, case management, or refugee and immigrant resettlement services to governments, institutions, nonprofit prime contractors, or affected populations.

a.bridge: O*NET and BLS describe emergency-management directors and social-and-human-service assistants rather than the wage-weighted occupation and task mix of NAICS 624230. I map plan and report drafting, resource and regulation research, grant applications, case-note work, needs triage, translation support, scheduling, and routine communications to exposed work; I treat command judgment, field assessment, shelter operations, counseling, transport, commodity handling, and relationship work as largely unexposed. The proxy may overstate exposure for field-logistics-heavy operators and understate it for resettlement and grant-administration specialists.
caveats: The occupation sources span government and adjacent social-service settings rather than this six-digit industry.
Disaster response, material relief, and refugee resettlement have materially different labor mixes.
Exposure excludes already automated work and does not imply safe autonomous use during crises.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary covering emergency planners, grant and compliance staff, call-center and intake workers, case managers, shelter staff, logisticians, counselors, interpreters, drivers, and warehouse personnel at at least 25 NAICS 624230 operators.
a.rationale: The code contains a meaningful information-processing layer before, during, and after an incident. AI can accelerate document-heavy and coordination-support tasks, but most service units still require human judgment, trusted communication, physical delivery, or on-scene execution under uncertain conditions.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 3% ten-year growth for emergency-management directors, NOAA and GAO measure disaster frequency, cost, and federal workload, and the FY 2026 refugee ceiling measures a policy-controlled resettlement input; none directly measures five-year constant-price demand for the NAICS 624230 service basket. I translate sustained disaster need into modest central growth while allowing refugee-policy contraction, appropriations limits, event volatility, and insourcing to drive the low case. Disaster losses can overstate addressable relief-service volume, while a single quiet or catastrophic year can make any point estimate misleading.
caveats: Billion-dollar disaster counts measure loss events, not purchased relief-service units.
Refugee ceilings are policy limits rather than realized admissions and can change within a fiscal year.
Five-year demand is unusually sensitive to disasters, wars, migration policy, appropriations, donations, and government delivery choices.
d5.change_evidence: A segment-weighted forecast using inflation-adjusted FEMA and state obligations, disaster case counts, shelter nights, commodity units, active recovery projects, USRAP arrivals and per-arrival service contracts, private donations, and operator backlogs mapped specifically to NAICS 624230.
d5.rationale: Recent disaster frequency and open recovery workload support continued need, but funded service demand is episodic and budget-constrained. The resettlement component is especially policy-sensitive: the May 2026 determination raised the FY 2026 ceiling from 7,500 to 17,500, still far below the prior FY 2025 target of 125,000. A near-flat central case with a wide range reflects offsetting segments rather than extrapolating recent disasters.

caveats: No observed legal-form or control-eligibility denominator was found for the estimated LMM band.

For-profit vendors serving disasters may be recorded outside this industry, while tax-exempt relief organizations can appear in private-employer data.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census linking SUSB establishments to IRS Form 990 status, SAM.gov registrations, USASpending awards, state charity records, ownership databases, primary NAICS verification, and normalized EBITDA for the dataset-injected 137-firm population.
e.rationale: FEMA describes voluntary agencies as central across the emergency-management cycle, while the IRS requires charitable assets to remain dedicated to exempt purposes. Some commercial relief coordinators, case-management contractors, and operating service companies may meet the lens, but many private-sector disaster vendors are classified in logistics, construction, healthcare, or professional-services codes rather than 624230.

caveats: This is a bounded judgment, not an observed displacement rate.
Information-only navigation and routine status updates are more substitutable than shelter, logistics, counseling, or field response.
Mutual aid and government insourcing could displace a lens operator without eliminating the underlying service.
o.change_evidence: Program-level evidence comparing digitally self-served versus operator-delivered relief on completion, safety, fraud, equity, survivor outcomes, and cost, including the share of service units delivered without credentialed personnel or accountable organizations.
o.rationale: Emergency shelter, food and material distribution, survivor safeguarding, field assessment, volunteer management, counseling, resettlement, credentialing, and accountable custody of funds and supplies require an operator. AI can enable self-service information and automate coordination support, but it is unlikely to replace the organization responsible for physical delivery and crisis outcomes.

caveats: The federal cost-share and urgent-award sources describe public funding structures, not operator retention of AI benefits.
Donation-funded, grant-funded, cost-reimbursement, and fixed-price programs have very different retention mechanics.
The estimate excludes implementation and demand effects.
q.change_evidence: Contract-level review of at least 150 grants and service agreements across disaster relief and resettlement, coding fee basis, allowable costs, indirect-cost treatment, underspend rules, budget rebasing, renewal pricing, surge clauses, audit recoveries, and post-automation contribution margin.
q.rationale: Cost-reimbursement grants, eligible-cost rules, public audits, restricted funding, competitive renewals, and mission commitments can pass savings to funders or beneficiaries. Retention can be higher on fixed-price subcontracts, capacity-constrained programs, or urgent awards where avoided hiring and faster response create value, but post-event scrutiny and rebidding constrain durable capture.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures present and expected near-term AI use across all nonfarm businesses, not the five-year implemented share of exposed work in relief services. I extend the horizon but discount for small-operator budgets, intermittent surge workflows, sensitive survivor data, multilingual quality needs, government-system integration, credentialing, auditability, and the high cost of crisis errors. The economy-wide proxy likely overstates adoption by volunteer-dependent nonprofits but may understate implementation by professional grant and logistics operators.
caveats: BTOS changed its AI-use wording in late 2025 to include any business function.
No six-digit industry adoption series or relief-specific implementation benchmark was found.
Disaster surges can expose tools to novel conditions that are absent from normal-period pilots.
rho.change_evidence: A longitudinal survey of NAICS 624230 operators measuring production AI use by workflow, offline and degraded-network performance, multilingual error rates, human-review time, security incidents, and verified labor-hour avoidance over five years.
rho.rationale: Implementation is most plausible in preparedness, knowledge retrieval, grant administration, document processing, routine casework, and after-action reporting with explicit human approval. Incident command, eligibility determinations, safeguarding, medical or mental-health escalation, and live public communications require tighter controls.

caveats: No industry-specific control-transfer rate was found.
Emergency contract awards and nonprofit affiliations are not themselves qualifying control transfers.
The interval is conditional on eligibility and excludes dissolution without transferable operations.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census using PitchBook, CapIQ, Form 990 Schedule N, state merger filings, federal award novations, and owner interviews, restricted to the eligible firms identified in a legal-form and EBITDA census.
s5.rationale: Eligible privately controlled operators can transfer through succession or acquisition, and urgent-response capabilities may attract strategic buyers. The industry's nonprofit prevalence, mission restrictions, grant novation requirements, and reliance on relationships or volunteers reduce the share of organizational changes that qualify as transferable control transactions.

records.principal_driver: Rising disaster complexity and a large planning, reporting, grant, casework, and coordination burden create recurring opportunities for human-supervised AI support.
records.principal_weakness: The commercially eligible firm pool and retained economics are poorly observed, while demand is unusually exposed to event timing and abrupt public-policy changes.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is typically a blend of competitively awarded government grants or subcontracts, reimbursable program funding, institutional service agreements, donations, and limited participant or homeowner payments. Work may combine housing operations, case management, compliance reporting, referral coordination, and field repair delivery.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code combines transitional housing, volunteer-assisted low-cost housing construction or repair, and repair for elderly or disabled homeowners. The lens therefore retains only recurring or repeat service operators with transferable control economics and excludes non-transferable charitable programs and passive housing assets so that operating-workflow primitives remain coherent.
lens.excluded_activities: Government agencies; charitable organizations without transferable control economics; donation-only or volunteer-only programs; passive property ownership; non-control financing vehicles; captive units; emergency shelters classified in NAICS 624221; and pure construction contractors classified elsewhere.
lens.included_activities: Privately controllable operators providing recurring or repeat transitional-housing, low-income home-repair, housing-stabilization, or closely associated support services to governments, nonprofit prime contractors, institutional partners, or households, within the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.

a.bridge: O*NET and BLS describe the adjacent social-and-human-service-assistant occupation rather than the wage-weighted task mix of NAICS 624229. I map paperwork, service research, eligibility support, recordkeeping, scheduling, referral, and routine follow-up to exposed work, while treating in-person assessment, care, safety monitoring, transport, repair, and relationship work as largely unexposed. The proxy likely overstates exposure for repair-heavy programs and may understate it for case-management-heavy operators.
caveats: The occupational sources cover a broader social-services population and do not isolate this NAICS code.
The code's housing-repair and transitional-housing segments have materially different occupation mixes.
Exposure denotes technically addressable work, not realized implementation or headcount reduction.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary or time-and-motion study across transitional-housing case managers, residential staff, grant administrators, maintenance workers, and home-repair crews at at least 20 NAICS 624229 operators.
a.rationale: A material administrative layer can use AI for intake summarization, case-note drafting, resource matching, document checks, reporting, and communications. The service core still includes face-to-face judgment, resident support, safety oversight, site work, and physical home repair, limiting substitution.

d5.bridge: BLS projects 2024-2034 employment for the broader social-and-human-service-assistant occupation, while HUD reports homelessness and housing-bed trends rather than constant-price demand for the current NAICS 624229 service basket. I translate the 6% ten-year occupational growth, severe housing need, a recent 4% decline in transitional-housing beds, and current federal support for transitional housing into a modest five-year service-demand increase. Need can exceed funded demand, so homelessness growth likely overstates purchasable volume; program redesign or appropriations cuts could push demand below current levels.
caveats: The 2024 point-in-time count measures need and system use on one night, not paid demand or annual service volume.
HUD reported transitional-housing beds fell 4% from 2023 to 2024 even as total homelessness rose, showing that program mix can diverge from need.
The July 2026 CoC competition was still subject to a proposed modification at the research date.
d5.change_evidence: A cohort forecast using five years of HUD Housing Inventory Count beds by project type, CoC and HOME award obligations, state and local contract budgets, waitlists, and inflation-adjusted service units specifically mapped to NAICS 624229.
d5.rationale: Underlying housing instability and aging-related support needs are durable, but realized service volume is budget-constrained and program mix can shift between transitional housing, permanent housing, rapid rehousing, and repair. A central case near broad social-service growth is more defensible than extrapolating the recent homelessness spike.

caveats: No observed denominator of LMM firms by legal form or transferability was found.
The dataset-injected n=0 is described as an estimate based on a placeholder margin and should not be interpreted as observed absence of operating organizations or eligible firms.
NAICS establishment classification may place mixed housing-and-services organizations in another primary code.
e.change_evidence: A business-level census combining IRS Form 990 status, SAM.gov and USASpending awardees, state charity registries, PitchBook or CapIQ ownership records, and normalized EBITDA for every establishment mapped to NAICS 624229.
e.rationale: The principal federal housing programs identify nonprofits and public entities as core applicants, and charitable assets generally remain dedicated to exempt purposes. A minority of establishments may nevertheless be privately controlled service contractors or commercially transferable operating subsidiaries that meet the recurring-service and EBITDA lens.

Retention can be higher under fixed-price service subcontracts or where productivity supports more clients without proportional hiring, but direct grants often reward documented service delivery rather than margin expansion.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures current and near-term AI use across all nonfarm businesses, not the five-year implemented share of exposed work in community housing. I increase the horizon from current adoption to five years but reduce implementation for small-operator budgets, fragmented systems, sensitive client data, grant compliance, and human review. The broad-business proxy may overstate implementation in nonprofit-heavy programs but understate eventual adoption of low-cost administrative tools.
caveats: BTOS changed its AI-use question in late 2025 to cover any business function, complicating historical comparison.
No sector-specific adoption series was found for this six-digit industry.
Use of a drafting tool does not imply that the exposed task share is operationally implemented.
rho.change_evidence: A 2026 baseline survey and annual follow-up of NAICS 624229 operators measuring production AI use by workflow, user adoption, exception rates, human-review time, and realized labor-hour avoidance.
rho.rationale: Implementation should concentrate in bounded administrative workflows with human approval. Small scale, procurement limits, interoperability with funder systems, privacy, uneven data quality, and the consequences of eligibility or safety errors constrain deployment into client-facing decisions.

caveats: No industry-specific transfer-rate series was found.
The range is conditional on eligibility and should not be applied to public or non-transferable charitable entities.
Asset transfers and nonprofit consolidations may look like deals but fail the qualifying-control definition.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from PitchBook, CapIQ, state nonprofit-merger filings, Form 990 Schedule N, and federal grant novation records, hand-coded for qualifying changes of control among eligible operators.
s5.rationale: For the small privately transferable subset, owner succession, sponsor recapitalization, or strategic acquisition can produce a control transfer. However, the code's nonprofit prevalence means many mergers, affiliations, grant novations, and asset transfers do not confer transferable private control economics and are excluded.

records.principal_driver: A sizable documentation and coordination layer can be augmented while underlying housing and support needs remain persistent.
records.principal_weakness: Nonprofit/public dominance and grant-constrained economics leave very few clearly transferable LMM firms and weak evidence on retained savings.

caveats: S1 is reachable but did not expose the registered six-digit definition in fetched page content, so that particular activity-definition citation could not be verified from the cited URL.

The a, rho, and d5 bridges rely on a broad adjacent occupation, all-business AI adoption, and homelessness or bed trends rather than an industry-specific task mix, implementation study, or constant-price service-demand series; these limitations are disclosed and the states are honestly PROXY.

The numerical levels for e, s5, q, and o remain bounded judgments rather than measurements. Their cited sources support program structure and transfer constraints, not the selected point estimates; the ESTIMATE labels, ranges, and change-evidence requests make this transparent.

The dataset-injected n=0 is derived from broad employment-size-class averages using an 8% placeholder margin. It is not observed absence of LMM firms, and holding that anchor fixed forces F to zero and the entire published interval to STRUCTURAL_OUT; the packet discloses this weakness and the resulting MODEL_CONDITIONED action appropriately requires validation.

sources_audited.note: The exact Census URL opened an official 2022 NAICS search page, but the fetched page content did not display the 624229 title or definition registered in the packet.

BLS describes the cited client-service duties and reports 6% projected employment growth from 2024 to 2034 with about 50,600 average annual openings.
Census reports overall business AI use of 17% to 20% from December 2025 to May 2026 and explains the November revision to cover AI use in any business function.
HUD lists competitive CoC awards, eligible costs and project components, the 25% non-leasing match, and eligibility for nonprofits and public entities.
HUD states that the FY 2026 competition offers more than $4 billion for supportive services and housing and records the July 16 notice of a not-yet-effective proposed modification.
The HUD report gives 771,480 people experiencing homelessness on one night in 2024, an 18.1% year-over-year increase, and a 4% decline in transitional-housing beds.
IRS states that a 501(c)(3) organization's assets must be permanently dedicated to exempt or public purposes and describes the required dissolution distribution.
HUD states its intent to increase investment in transitional housing and supportive services and lists nonprofit and public bodies as eligible applicants.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Individuals, property participants, financial institutions, and recurring professional-referral channels pay statutory or negotiated per-instrument fees plus permissible preparation, research, consultation, certification, and related legal-service fees; repeat business is relationship- and channel-driven rather than subscription-based.
lens.excluded_activities: Law firms primarily practicing law (541110); ordinary notaries limited to oaths, affidavits, depositions, or signature witnessing without power to draw and approve legal documents (541199); title abstract and settlement offices (541191); captive government or corporate notarial units; shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market offices, including Puerto Rico practices, whose recurring or repeat outsourced work is drafting, approving, executing, receiving, indexing, or storing legal instruments such as real-estate instruments, wills, powers, trusts, and contracts, with the legally empowered notary retaining professional accountability.

a.bridge: BLS reports that an external 2023 study estimated 44% of legal-profession tasks susceptible to automation; that source covers the broader legal profession, measures task rather than wage shares, and is not specific to 541120. The base is reduced slightly because this industry's highest-accountability work includes impartial advice, legal warnings, authentication, and public faith, while drafting, document review, intake, indexing, reporting, and storage are more exposed. The proxy likely overstates wage-weighted substitution when licensed notary-lawyer time dominates, but could understate exposure in staff-heavy offices.
caveats: The 44% benchmark is broader legal work, not a 541120 occupation mix or wage-weighted measure.

The frozen compensation ratio is LOW quality and measured at ancestor 5411 using 2024 wages over 2022 receipts before IPS harmonization; broad legal services may have a materially different labor mix from licensed notarial offices.
Puerto Rico rules are a concrete fit for empowered civil-law notarial practice but may not describe every U.S. establishment classified in 541120.
a.change_evidence: A time-and-wage study of 541120 offices separating licensed-notary hours from document intake, drafting, review, indexing, monthly reporting, billing, and storage, paired with task-level AI benchmark results on anonymized notarial files.
a.rationale: Census defines the production process around legal-document drafting, approval, execution, and records. BLS identifies document review and preparation as meaningful GenAI use cases, while Puerto Rico's professional rules require the notary to advise requesters, explain consequences and warnings, and personally stand behind authenticity. The range therefore treats much preparatory and administrative time as exposed but preserves a large human-accountability core.

d5.bridge: Census observed Puerto Rico population declining 0.6% from July 2024 to July 2025; compounding that one-year geographic trend for five years gives about 0.97, used as the base demand proxy. Population is not notarial transaction volume: aging can raise wills, estates, powers, and trust matters per capita, while property, credit, migration, and disaster-recovery cycles can move instruments independently. The population proxy therefore likely understates demand if aging and formalization dominate, and overstates it if transaction activity or out-migration weakens faster.
caveats: A single-year population change is a weak proxy for constant-price, constant-quality notarial demand.
The geographic mix of all U.S. 541120 establishments is not observed; Puerto Rico evidence may dominate the conceptual fit without representing every establishment.
The estimate holds service quality constant and excludes AI-driven price changes.
d5.change_evidence: ODIN monthly-index and annual-report counts by instrument type and value for 2019-2026, then a five-year forecast tied to property transfers, mortgage originations, deaths, business formations, trusts, powers, and non-contentious matters in the relevant jurisdictions.
d5.rationale: Core demand is attached to legally consequential property, estate, power, trust, contract, and non-contentious matters rather than discretionary paperwork alone. Puerto Rico's shrinking population is a headwind, but the continued statutory role for public instruments and an older population support a roughly stable rather than collapsing five-year service basket; the wide range captures property-cycle and migration sensitivity.

caveats: There is no defensible n-band value, and this percentage must not be used to infer an absolute eligible-firm count.
Census explicitly excludes 541120 from County Business Patterns, limiting establishment-level size and geography evidence.
The general employer-firm sole-ownership evidence is not industry-specific and only informs the breadth of owner dependence.
e.change_evidence: A 541120 firm census linking five years of customer invoices, repeat-client concentration, external versus captive revenue, EBITDA, professional ownership, and services by NAICS-coded legal entity.
e.rationale: The NAICS production definition is an outsourced professional service, and repeat referral channels can support recurring volume. Eligibility is reduced because many matters are episodic, some practices are personally identified with one licensed professional, and mixed law/notary offices may fall outside 541120. The estimate applies only to firms already in the LMM band and does not replace the declared missing firm-count input.

caveats: The operator requirement is strongest in Puerto Rico's civil-law notarial regime and may be weaker elsewhere.
Legal reform could narrow mandatory notarization or authorize new digital public-service models within five years.
The estimate allows software to remove ancillary document work even when the final act still requires a notary.
o.change_evidence: A jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction legal review of instruments that can be validly self-executed without a notary, combined with ODIN matter counts and customer-channel evidence on end-to-end software substitution versus software-assisted licensed execution.
o.rationale: The current 541120 basket is defined by authority to draft, approve, and execute legal instruments, not mere commodity signature witnessing. Puerto Rico rules treat notarial public faith as delegated state power, require licensed admission and a notarial exam, and obligate the notary to explain, advise, authenticate, and preserve protocols. Software can eliminate or self-serve portions of intake, drafting, retrieval, and reporting, but most surviving public-instrument quantity still needs an accountable licensed operator.

caveats: The statutory fee text is presented by Justia and carries its warning that official sources may be more current.
Fee rules are jurisdiction-specific; the national revenue mix and share subject to Puerto Rico's tariff are not observed.
This estimate addresses commercial pass-through only and does not treat implementation difficulty or demand loss as leakage.
q.change_evidence: Five years of matter-level invoices and labor cost from target offices, tagged by statutory-tariff versus negotiated fees, renewal or referral-channel repricing, discounts, win/loss data, and post-automation realization.
q.rationale: Puerto Rico's notarial fee schedule establishes minimums and percentage bands for many instruments and restricts discounting, while allowing negotiated fees for some work and additional reasonable charges for preparatory legal effort. Fixed or value-based instrument fees let an operator retain near-term labor savings instead of automatically passing them through as fewer billable hours. Over five years, competition, negotiated non-schedule fees, customer demands, and shifts toward lower-cost standardized workflows still share part of the benefit.

caveats: No industry-specific adoption or realized-hours study was located.
The Integrated Notarial System already digitizes some reporting, so estimates must exclude work already automated at the current baseline.
Implementation varies with office size, document standardization, legacy records, Spanish-language tooling, and local professional rules.
rho.change_evidence: A five-year cohort study or vendor telemetry from Puerto Rico notarial practices showing the share of exposed hours actually removed, avoided hires, exception rates, review time, malpractice incidents, and disciplinary findings after deployment.
rho.rationale: Within five years, offices can implement AI for intake, clause and form drafting, checklisting, document comparison, indexing, deadline prompts, and monthly-report preparation. Implementation is capped well below full exposure because licensed notaries must independently advise parties, verify intent and legal efficacy, preserve confidentiality and protocols, and remain professionally responsible for errors; fragmented practice systems and limited clean training data add workflow friction.

s5.bridge: The Census 2019 Annual Business Survey found 51% of responding owners of employer businesses were age 55 or older, a broad owner-age proxy rather than a transfer measure for 541120. The estimate applies a large downward haircut from owner aging to a five-year qualifying control transfer because retirement can produce closure, internal succession, or archive handoff rather than sale, and the notarial authority is personal and licensed. The broad age proxy likely overstates qualifying transfers; it could understate them only if 541120 owners are materially older or consolidation accelerates.
caveats: Owner age is from 2018 responding employer businesses across industries, not 541120 firms or the LMM band.
A protocol's transfer to an archive is not a qualifying business control transfer.
No observed five-year deal cohort for this NAICS code was located.
s5.change_evidence: Puerto Rico bar and ODIN microdata on active notary age, cessations, protocol transfers, professional-corporation ownership changes, and arm's-length practice sales, supplemented by legal-services M&A databases and interviews with notarial archivists.
s5.rationale: Aging creates succession pressure, but transferability is constrained: Puerto Rico requires admission to law practice plus a notarial examination, and ODIN receives and safeguards the protocols of notaries who die, are suspended, become incapacitated, or cease practice. A buyer can acquire staff, systems, brand, and referral relationships, but not the seller's delegated public faith, making qualifying control transfers less frequent than generic small-business succession events.

records.principal_driver: Document-heavy workflows plus regulated per-instrument pricing create a plausible path from AI-enabled labor compression to retained gross benefit.
records.principal_weakness: Licensed-person dependence, protocol custody rules, and a missing defensible LMM firm count make scalable control-transfer supply highly uncertain.

caveats: a: OEWS covers NAICS 624200, including community food, other housing, and emergency-relief services outside 624221.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares, and the source does not measure AI exposure or existing automation.
a: AI assistance in casework does not imply substitution for professional judgment or resident supervision.

rho: Federal ESG rules do not capture additional state licensing, local requirements, or protections for domestic-violence shelters.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Residents generally are not the economic customer. Funding commonly flows through government grants or subrecipient awards, philanthropy, and institutional sponsorship; a potentially investable subset would require recurring outsourced operating contracts or other reliable third-party payment.
lens.excluded_activities: Government-run captive units, volunteer-only programs, non-transferable entities, one-off disaster shelters, permanent supportive housing, transitional housing classified elsewhere, residential treatment facilities, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: U.S. temporary-shelter operators that repeatedly provide short-term emergency or residential shelter to external clients and could plausibly meet the lower-middle-market normalized EBITDA band under the fixed lens.

a.bridge: BLS measures employment and wages for the broader four-digit NAICS 624200, not wage-weighted AI task exposure for temporary shelters. I map portions of management, business, office, intake, documentation, scheduling, compliance, and case-support tasks to AI exposure while assigning low exposure to supervision, crisis response, counseling, security, cleaning, food service, and facilities work. Housing and relief activities outside 624221 may make the proxy overstate or understate the true mix, so the error direction is uncertain.
caveats: OEWS covers NAICS 624200, including community food, other housing, and emergency-relief services outside 624221.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares, and the source does not measure AI exposure or existing automation.
AI assistance in casework does not imply substitution for professional judgment or resident supervision.
a.change_evidence: Obtain wage- and time-weighted task diaries from at least 20 temporary shelters, separated by role and shift, and audit current software use to distinguish not-yet-automated documentation, intake, reporting, scheduling, referral, and case-support work.
a.rationale: In broader NAICS 624200, management accounts for 13.26% of employment, business and financial operations 8.54%, office and administrative support 11.64%, and community and social service occupations 29.91%. AI can draft notes, summarize records, support eligibility and referral searches, schedule beds and staff, and prepare grant or compliance reports, but the large client-facing and facility-operating component remains human work.

d5.bridge: HUD's point-in-time count measures people experiencing homelessness, not constant-price demand for the full 624221 service basket, and does not cover every domestic-violence, abuse, runaway-youth, or medical-crisis shelter user. I use the large current count and longer-run increase as a need indicator, while allowing a wide range because funding, prevention, permanent housing, immigration, housing costs, policy, and shelter capacity determine how need converts to shelter service. The count can understate annual flow and non-HUD shelter populations, but can overstate funded shelter demand when people remain unsheltered.
caveats: A one-night homelessness count is not annual shelter utilization or funded demand.
The total includes unsheltered people and excludes some populations served by temporary shelters.
Policy and appropriations can cause service quantity to diverge sharply from underlying need.
d5.change_evidence: Use HUD HMIS annual-flow and Housing Inventory Count data plus comparable domestic-violence, youth, and medical-crisis shelter datasets to build same-service volumes, occupancy, turnaways, length of stay, and funded-bed commitments by market through year five.
d5.rationale: HUD counted 745,652 people experiencing homelessness on one night in January 2025, down 3% from 2024 but up 27% from 2013. That supports persistent need rather than a simple extrapolation of one year's decline or prior growth. Five-year service quantity could fall if prevention and permanent housing succeed, or grow if housing instability and funded capacity expand.

caveats: A zero modeled firm count does not prove that no qualifying operator exists; the dataset uses an estimated 8% social-assistance margin bridge.
Grant recipients can use subrecipients or contractors, so recipient eligibility does not reveal the operator ownership mix.
No defensible LMM-firm denominator was found.
e.change_evidence: Match all 624221 establishments to IRS Tax Exempt Organization Search, Form 990 filings, SAM.gov and USAspending awards, state corporation records, and private-company databases; then verify ownership, normalized EBITDA, external recurring contracts, and control-transferability entity by entity.
e.rationale: The frozen dataset estimates no firms in the target EBITDA band, and available sources do not identify a denominator of LMM temporary-shelter firms from which an eligible share can be estimated. HUD funding structures explicitly include public and private nonprofit recipients, while section 501(c)(3) economics are not transferable shareholder earnings; neither fact quantifies the remaining for-profit, recurring outsourced, control-transferable population.

Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

o.bridge: The Census definition and ESG shelter standards establish that current services involve physical lodging or subsidized housing plus accountable safety, sanitation, privacy, access, and facility obligations; they do not measure the future operator-required share. I infer high persistence because software cannot itself supply safe shelter, while recognizing that prevention, rental assistance, self-service intake, hotels, landlords, or permanent-housing programs can bypass a dedicated shelter operator. The proxy may therefore overstate persistence of the current operator form.
caveats: The sources describe current activities and compliance, not a five-year substitution rate.
Physical shelter can remain necessary while provision shifts from dedicated facilities to hotels, apartments, or government operation.
o.change_evidence: Compare market-level shelter nights and operator contracts before and after expansion of prevention, rental assistance, hotel placement, centralized intake, and permanent housing; classify which service volumes cease to require a dedicated temporary-shelter operator.
o.rationale: Temporary shelter requires control or coordination of real space and accountable compliance with physical safety and sanitation standards. AI can change intake, records, referrals, and administration, but cannot eliminate the need for an organization responsible for beds, access, resident safety, privacy, maintenance, and incident response.

caveats: No source measures retained AI benefit for temporary-shelter operators.
The estimate is conditional on an eligible operator existing despite the frozen zero firm count.
Federal program terms do not represent every state, local, philanthropic, medical-crisis, youth, or domestic-violence funding arrangement.
q.change_evidence: Review a representative sample of shelter operating contracts, ESG subrecipient agreements, grant budgets, and renewal files for pricing basis, allowable-cost reconciliation, administrative caps, performance incentives, rebid cadence, gain-sharing, and treatment of underspends.
q.rationale: Government grants and subrecipient arrangements can restrict allowable costs, cap administration, require reporting, and reallocate benefits through renewals or rebids. Philanthropic operators may use savings to expand service rather than create distributable economics. A hypothetical transferable operator on a multi-year fixed-price management contract could retain more, but that contract model is not established for the industry population.

caveats: No source measures five-year implementation of exposed AI tasks in 624221.
Federal ESG rules do not capture additional state licensing, local requirements, or protections for domestic-violence shelters.
rho.change_evidence: Collect implementation cost, adoption, labor-hour, error, privacy, and client-outcome data from production AI deployments at 15 shelters, including separate results for HMIS intake, case notes, referral matching, staff scheduling, incident reports, and grant compliance.
rho.rationale: Administrative copilots and workflow automation are implementable within five years, but fragmented grant-funded technology, sensitive personal data, domestic-violence confidentiality, uneven records, staff turnover, human-review requirements, and high-consequence safety decisions constrain deployment. Facility and direct-care work also cannot be converted merely because documentation around it is digitized.

caveats: The frozen dataset estimates zero firms in the target band.
IRS restrictions apply to section 501(c)(3) organizations, not every possible temporary-shelter operator.
Observed operating-control changes could occur without a transferable equity transaction.
s5.change_evidence: Construct a five-year event census using PitchBook, Capital IQ, state charity and corporate filings, Form 990 Schedule R, procurement award changes, and operator press releases; identify any eligible firms first, then classify equity sales, asset sales, nonprofit affiliations, internal reorganizations, closures, and contract reawards.
s5.rationale: No eligible LMM-firm population was established and no source measures qualifying control transfers for temporary-shelter operators. Nonprofit affiliations, board changes, grant reawards, and facility-management contract changes are not automatically equity control transfers and cannot be converted into a five-year probability without transaction-level classification.

records.principal_driver: Emergency lodging remains a physical, safety-critical service with persistent underlying need.
records.principal_weakness: No defensible population of transferable LMM temporary-shelter firms was established.

sources.supports: Defines temporary shelters as establishments providing short-term emergency or temporary residential shelter and notes that operators may use their own shelters or subsidize existing housing, hotels, or motels.
